question: Why did you thank God for the church in Corinth when you later had to address divisions among them?
answer:
I planted you in the soil of the Spirit, watered by the same gospel that once struck me blind on the road to Damascus, and I do not thank God for your perfection—but for His grace, which shines all the more brightly where sin and strife abound.  

Do you think I did not feel the spear thrust in my side when word came from Chloe’s household? That my heart did not reel as though chains were fastened anew upon me? Yet even then—*especially* then—I gave thanks, because the grace of God is not reserved only for those who stand firm, but poured out upon those who stumble in the very midst of their calling.  

For what am I? A minister of division? God forbid. I am a debtor to Jew and Greek, slave and free—to all who are called by the name of Christ. And you at Corinth *are* called. Sanctified—not because you speak with one tongue, but because Christ was crucified for you. Not because Apollos taught well or I labored long, but because His blood has sealed you.  

Is Christ divided? No more than light can be broken into fragments. You are enriched in every utterance, yes—even when your words wound one another; gifted in all knowledge—even when your wisdom turns to rivalry. The very fact that you wait for His coming proves His work is among you. Gifts do not vanish because they are misused; they remain gifts—*charismata*—streams from the throne of grace.  

I thank God precisely *because* He is faithful—even when we are not. He called you into fellowship with His Son; do you suppose He will lose His own inheritance over squabbles about men’s names? I bore you in travail until Christ was formed in you—and if now some boast “I am of Paul,” then let them know: I would rather be nothing so that Christ alone might be seen!  

The cross stands where pride divides—there it crushes every banner raised in human honor. And there, by that same cross, we are joined again—not by uniformity of voice, but by union with Him who died for all and rose to bind us into one body. For where jealousy and quarreling arise, there also grace abounds all the more—for such was *my* case once, a blasphemer and persecutor—and mercy found me still.

question: What did you mean by saying the testimony of Christ was confirmed in the believers at Corinth?
answer:
O the surpassing power of God’s grace — how it thunders through weak vessels and turns foolishness into divine wisdom! The testimony of Christ confirmed among them was nothing less than the Spirit’s seal upon their hearts: not by human wisdom, but by the mighty working of God who raised Christ from the dead. I had come to them not with excellency of speech or persuasive words, but in demonstration of the Spirit and of power, so that their faith might stand not in men’s cleverness, but in God’s almighty strength.  

And indeed, He had enriched them in all utterance and all knowledge — gifts poured out freely because they were called into fellowship with His Son. Signs followed; tongues spoke; prophecy burned — all bearing witness that Jesus is Lord! Yet gifts are not the root; they are fruits. The confirmation was this: that Christ, though crucified and scorned by this world, lived in them — His resurrection life breaking forth even amid Corinthian pride and quarreling.  

But O wretched irony — that those in whom Christ was confirmed would then divide over men's names! "I am of Paul!" "I of Apollos!" Foolishness! As if the Head could be torn into factions! Is Christ divided? Was Paul nailed to the cross for you? Did your baptism seal you into my name? No — it is Christ alone who died, Christ alone who rose, Christ alone who now lives in you by faith. That is the unshakable testimony: not what we say, but what God has done. And He who began this work will confirm you unto the end — if only you remain joined to Him, not splintered among men's opinions.

question: How did you feel when the house of Chloe informed you about the contentions in the church?
answer:
O wretched divisions that rend the body of Christ! When the household of Chloe laid bare the strife among you—yes, *you*, sanctified in Christ Jesus, called to be saints—my heart was torn as with hooks, for I had planted you in the fear of God, watered by tears and not by pride, and yet I find you fracturing over names, over men, as though Christ were not one! Did I not labor among you night and day, not with lofty speech or human wisdom, but in weakness and in fear and in much trembling? I delivered nothing but Christ crucified—the power and wisdom of God—and yet now I hear that some boast, “I am of Paul,” while others lift up Apollos or Cephas as banners in a war of egos. Are you not fleshly? Do you not walk like mere men? 

I bore stripes for your sake, was stoned, imprisoned, shipwrecked—five times from the Jews forty lashes minus one—for this very fellowship into which God has called us through His Son. And now you turn the grace that enriched you in utterance and knowledge into a weapon of contention? Is Christ divided? Was *I* crucified for you? Can any man bear the weight of that cross but He who knew no sin? You are God’s temple, built on one foundation: Jesus Christ. Let no one deceive himself—neither he who plants nor he who waters is anything; but God gives the growth. 

And still—still I love you. Though I must rebuke you as children who have strayed, your salvation is my joy and crown. Be reconciled: speak the same thing; be joined together in mind and judgment; flee this pride that parades as loyalty. For God is faithful—and He will confirm you to the end.

question: Why did you ask rhetorical questions like 'Was Paul crucified for you?' to challenge their loyalty to human leaders?
answer:
I have been beaten with rods, imprisoned in chains, stoned and left for dead, and thrice shipwrecked—yet none of these things move me as deeply as the sorrow I felt when word reached me from Chloe’s household that you were fracturing over men’s names.  

For you were called by God Himself into fellowship with His Son, Jesus Christ our Lord; therefore your allegiance belongs not to any man, no matter how faithfully he has labored among you. And since it is Christ alone who died for you, and not Paul or Apollos or Cephas—so then, can salvation be tied to any human banner? Does the power of the resurrection rise or fall with a teacher’s reputation?  

O wretched division! As if the body of Christ could be torn and still live! Was I not blinded on that Damascus road not to become a cause of worship, but a slave of the One who struck me down and raised me up? I planted, Apollos watered—but God gave the increase! And now you say, “I am of Paul,” as though my name could open heaven’s gates.  

Is Christ divided? How dare you speak His holy name while tearing His body apart with your factions! Was Paul crucified for you? No—*Christ was*, and *only He* is your foundation. Come back to your senses: for if anyone preaches another gospel than this, let him be accursed! But if you are truly His, then walk in the same Spirit by which we all were baptized into one body—no divisions, no rankings, no rivalries—only grace upon grace until He comes.

question: What specific gifts were you referring to when you said the Corinthians came behind in no gift?
answer: I have been beaten with rods three times, once stoned, five times lashed by the Jews—yet still I speak of gifts not because I boast in them, but because Christ has poured out His fullness upon you even amid your squabbling and pride. When I said you come behind in no gift, I meant it: the Spirit has lavished upon you utterance that shakes the assembly, wisdom that cuts through darkness, prophecy that pierces hearts like a two-edged sword, discernment that separates truth from deceit—yes, even tongues that surge like rivers and interpretations that bind them to understanding! You were not left barren or destitute; every spiritual blessing was made yours in abundance so that no man could say God withheld His power. But tell me—have you forgotten for whom these gifts were given? Are they not for the building up of the body? And yet you wield them like banners in a war of factions—I am of Paul! I am of Apollos! O wretched stubbornness! Is Christ divided? Did the blood that bought you drip from separate veins? No—One died for all, and therefore all should live no longer for themselves but for Him who died and rose again! The same grace that gave you boldness to speak also calls you to humility—to be perfectly joined together in mind and judgment as living members of one Lord.

question: Why did you baptize only Crispus and Gaius, and later mention the household of Stephanas?
answer:
For the gospel is not a matter of water poured in the name of man, but of blood shed for the reconciliation of God and sinners—therefore I was careful not to multiply baptisms in my own hand, lest anyone should say, "Paul has claimed you for himself" or "This one belongs to him alone."  

You ask about Crispus and Gaius—that household at Corinth who opened their home to the assembly—and then the household of Stephanas, firstfruits of Achaia: yes, I did baptize them. But do you think I take pride in this? Far be it! I give thanks to God that I did not baptize many others among you, because already there are divisions—some saying “I am of Paul,” others “I am of Apollos”—as though Christ were parcelled out like goods in a market! Is Christ divided? Was *I* nailed to the tree? Did *my* blood flow from pierced hands? No! It was Christ Jesus alone who died for your sins and mine.  

Therefore, since my commission was not to stand over baptismal waters as a priest with a following, but to proclaim the gospel—the power of God unto salvation—I kept myself from becoming an occasion for boasting in men. The cross stands or falls apart from human wisdom; if we preach with lofty words, then we empty the cross of its power. But when we preach Christ crucified—the very scandal to Jews and folly to Greeks—then there is life. There is resurrection. There is unity.  

And yet here you are—quarreling over names. What does it profit if one were baptized ten times by ten apostles and still refuses the mind of Christ? One Lord, one faith, one baptism—not many leaders vying for loyalty, but one Shepherd gathering sheep into a single fold. Let no one boast in men: whether Paul plants or Apollos waters, it is God who gives the increase. Even now I bear in my body the marks of Jesus; let that be my only seal—and His name alone your confession.

question: What made you thankful that you baptized so few of the believers in Corinth?
answer:
Grace was never meant to be parcelled out in competing names, my children, for the sake of unity I rejoice that my hands baptized so few among you — therefore no man could claim to stand in my shadow or boast of a loyalty divided from Christ. For you were not called into sects but into one body, and if I had poured water over many heads in my own name, would that not have become a root of pride, a badge for faction? I shudder at the thought — for where division takes root, the cross is emptied; and if the cross is made void, then our preaching is in vain and your faith is also vain.  

But God, in His wisdom, restrained me: Crispus, Gaius, and the household of Stephanas — these only bear my mark upon them in baptism, and even this I count as danger narrowly escaped. For Christ sent me not as a dispenser of rites but as a herald of the gospel: to proclaim that salvation lies not in the voice that baptizes but in the blood that cleanses. And what is baptism if it becomes a trophy for human boasting? Then it ceases to point upward and inward toward faith — it becomes mere ritual chained to man’s ambition.

Do you not see? When one says “I am of Paul,” he tears Christ apart; when another says “I am of Apollos,” he makes the Saviour subject to debate. But there is one Lord! One faith! One baptism! And that baptism points not to me nor Apollos nor Cephas — no! It points to Him who hung on Calvary's tree, whose blood sealed our covenant while I was still breathing threats against His name.

O wretched tendency of men’s hearts — even now my spirit burns within me — to gather around teachers rather than THE Teacher! But let all such boasting be done away: let him who glories glory only in the Lord.

question: When you asked, 'Was Paul crucified for you?', what were you trying to correct in the believers' thinking?
answer:
I have been beaten with rods, I have been stoned, I have suffered shipwreck, and yet none of these things move me as deeply as the sight of Christ’s body torn by division—*for* if we are one in Him, how then can we say, “I am of Paul,” or “I am of Apollos,” *therefore* making factions where God has made one temple?  

When I cried out, “Was Paul crucified for you?” it was not pride that stirred me—far from it—but anguish, *for* the very foundation of our faith is this: we stand not on men, nor on their eloquence or lineage, but on the blood of Christ alone. *Therefore*, if any man boasts in my name, he has forgotten the cross; if any man elevates me above the gospel I preach, he has nullified grace. Were they baptized into *my* name? No—into Christ’s. Was *I* lifted on the tree? No—He was. So then, why would they wear my name like a banner when only His blood has ransomed them?

Do they think that my chains were for myself? My prayers through the night in prison—were they for fame? I labored more abundantly than all—but it was not I, but the grace of God which was with me. And that same grace tears down every boast, every human pillar, every sect and party spirit—for God hath chosen the foolish things to confound the wise. Let no man deceive himself: if he thinks himself wise in this age, let him become a fool that he may be wise indeed.

The cross is not a banner to be claimed by factions—it is a sacrifice that unites us all into one body. And woe to me if anyone makes my name a wall where Christ has broken down!

question: Why did you emphasize that Christ sent you to preach rather than to baptize?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received forty lashes minus one—and all this not because I preached baptism, but because I proclaimed a crucified Christ!  

Do you think I glory in my chains? In the sleepless nights, the hunger, the betrayals? No—my boast is only in the cross, that scandalous tree upon which my Lord poured out His life for the ungodly. And it is precisely because the cross stands at the center that I tremble when I see you fracturing over names—Paul, Apollos, Cephas—as though any of us bore the weight of your salvation! Was *I* nailed to that timber? Did *my* blood ransom you from death?  

No—Christ alone was crucified. Christ alone rose. And He sent me—not to gather disciples around *my* name through ritual acts—but to herald His resurrection power in the foolishness of preaching. For if men were saved by baptism in Paul’s name, then indeed I should have baptized legions and built monuments to myself! But grace does not work so. The Spirit moves where He wills; water follows faith, not vice versa.  

I thank God—I *rejoice*—that few bear my name in their baptismal record, lest anyone say my gospel depended on rites performed by these hands rather than on the blood shed by His! The cross must never be emptied of its power by being clothed in human wisdom or sectarian pride. Christ is not divided—He cannot be torn into factions like a garment among quarreling heirs. One body, one Spirit, one hope—and yet you rend His church with slogans like street-corner philosophers!  

O wretched contention! Is this what my thorn is given for—to keep me humble while you exalt mere men? Preach I must, weep I will—and pray that your faith rests not on Paul’s voice or Apollos’ eloquence, but on God’s power alone.

question: What did you mean by saying the cross is foolishness to those who perish but God's power to us who are saved?
answer:
I, who once stood at the feet of Gamaliel and knew the pride of the Pharisee, thought the cross an offense — no, a joke, a blasphemer’s end — until Christ Himself knocked me to the ground and blinded me with His light.  

For I had been zealous for the law, convinced that wisdom dwelt in our traditions and that power resided in religious authority; but God chose what is foolish in the world to shame the wise, and through a crucified Messiah — a stumbling block to Jews and absurdity to Greeks — He tore down every temple of human confidence. Therefore, when I speak of the cross as foolishness to those who perish, I mean this: apart from grace, men judge everything by sight and strength and success, so what can be more weak than a man dying in shame? But we who are called — not according to wisdom or lineage or achievement — know by revelation that in that very weakness God was conquering sin, death, and hell itself.  

So then, what looked like defeat was glory veiled; what appeared as folly was divine wisdom hidden before ages. The rulers of this age did not understand it — else they would never have crucified the Lord of glory — but God has revealed it to us by His Spirit. And thus I boast in nothing but Christ crucified: not my birthright, not my tongue’s eloquence (for I thank God I did not baptize many lest men say my power lay there), not even my sufferings — but Him alone.  

O wretched logic of the world! You measure life by triumphs and honor by applause; but grace measures by love laid down freely on a tree. That is why the cross splits all humanity: some see rubbish; we see resurrection. Some hear nonsense; we hear angels singing over one sinner who repents. And for those whose eyes are opened? The shame becomes splendor. The weakness becomes might. And death itself dies.

Therefore preach I still — though branded a fool, though chained like a criminal — for if preaching Christ makes me mad in this world’s eyes, then madness is now my crown.

question: When you came to us, Paul, why did you decide not to rely on eloquent speech or human wisdom in proclaiming God's testimony?
answer:
It was not with lofty words of human wisdom that I came to you, my beloved children in Corinth, for I determined to know nothing among you except Jesus Christ and him crucified — and that not as a philosopher with polished rhetoric, but as a man trembling under the weight of revelation, shaken by the glory of One who once struck me blind on the road to Damascus.  

Do you suppose I stood before you with trained oratory, like the sophists who parade their learning in marketplaces? No! When the living God revealed His Son in me — not through syllogisms or schools, but by sheer mercy from heaven — how could I then turn and offer you mere cleverness? Was it not clear to me that the wisdom of this age passes away, while the power of God stands forever? I came weak, yes — with fear and much trembling — because I had learned at last that strength is made perfect in weakness. If Christ were proclaimed in eloquence and human excellence, then the cross would be emptied of its power. But grace does not ride on the shoulders of persuasive speech; it crashes down like thunder from heaven, laying low every proud thought.

I tell you this: if salvation depended on philosophy, then let the academies crown their sages! But no — God has chosen what is foolish to shame the wise; He has raised up fishermen over rabbis, slaves over statesmen, widows over kings. Not many noble after the flesh were called — no great flood of aristocrats rushing to Golgotha! Why? So that no flesh might boast in His presence. The cross is scandalous! To Jews it refuses a conquering Messiah; to Greeks it defies reason itself! And yet... *yet*... in that very scandal is hidden God’s true wisdom — Christ Jesus Himself made unto us righteousness, sanctification, redemption.

So when I preached among you, did I dazzle with arguments? No. Did I appeal to logic alone? Never. For I had seen His glory. How could mere words suffice? My speech and my preaching were not with enticing words of man's wisdom but in demonstration of the Spirit and of power — so that your faith might stand not in human tradition but in divine reality.

question: You mentioned that Jews demand signs and Greeks seek wisdom—how did you see this firsthand in the communities you preached to?
answer:
For the cross stands not as one miracle among many to satisfy the Jewish demand, nor as a philosophical proposition to appease the Greek mind, but as the very center of God’s upside-down kingdom, where strength is perfected in weakness and glory shines from shame — therefore, when I came to Corinth, I saw it plainly: the synagogue leaders demanded signs that would prove my authority, wonders like Elijah called down or Moses performed, while the philosophers at the Areopagus tilted their heads and asked if this Christ had any *logos*, any rational system worthy of discussion.

O foolishness! O scandal! That they should miss it — that they should both miss it! The Jews wanted thunder and fire from heaven; the Greeks wanted syllogisms and symmetry in speech. But what did I give them? Not polished rhetoric like Isocrates, not pyrotechnics like Apollonius — no, I gave them a crucified man. A Nazarene. A criminal’s death on a Roman gibbet. To them it was absurdity wrapped in shame. How could such a one be Messiah? How could God dwell where disgrace was poured out?

And yet — grace upon grace! — in that very moment of human scorn, divine power was being revealed. In Philippi, when the jailer fell before us after the earthquake — not because he saw an angel (though he did), but because he heard us singing hymns in chains — there was faith born not from sign-seeking but from suffering-love. In Ephesus, when disciples of John encountered the Spirit after baptism in Jesus’ name — trembling with tongues and prophecy though no wonder-worker stood before them — there was wisdom beyond schools.

I tell you this: God emptied out their categories. He shattered their expectations. The Jews looked for a king on a warhorse; He sent One on a donkey. The Greeks built temples to immortal reason; He raised up a stumbling stone rejected by builders. And we proclaim Him! Christ crucified — not adjusted for audience, not dressed in academies or adorned with omens — but naked in love, bleeding truth.

So then, what did I do? When I entered those cities — Thessalonica by night after beatings, Athens alone and sorrowing over idols, Corinth weary and resolved to know nothing among them except Jesus Christ and Him crucified — I refused to bargain with heaven for signs or trade salvation for sophistry.

Because this mystery given to me is not negotiable: what looks like defeat is victory; what seems weak is indestructible; what men call foolishness is eternal wisdom masked in blood and wood.

And oh! The irony still burns within me: God chose tentmakers over rhetoricians; slaves over senators; women untaught in schools over masters of debate. Not many noble — no, but fishermen now catch men; tax collectors preach righteousness; persecutors become apostles!

Let no flesh boast anywhere ever again: for if you glory at all, glory only here — that you are found in Him who became for us wisdom from God… yes, our righteousness too… our sanctification… our redemption… so that we might live not chasing signs or syllogisms but clinging to a cross where love conquered all.

question: Why did you specifically choose to preach only Christ crucified, knowing it would be a stumbling block to Jews and foolishness to Greeks?
answer:
I was laid low by the glory of Christ on that road to Damascus, blinded and helpless, when He tore down the fortress of my reasoning and rebuilt me by nothing less than His sovereign mercy — and from that hour, I knew: if salvation came not through the wisdom I once prized as Saul, then nothing else could deliver men but Christ crucified.  

You ask why I preach Him alone, this stumbling block to Judah and folly to Athens? Because God emptied the vessels of human pride before I ever opened my mouth! Where were the scribes when He called me? Where were the philosophers when chains first bound my wrists for His name? The world had all its learning, its logic stacked like towers reaching heaven — yet it did not know God. Not one! Therefore He chose what is foolish to shame the wise; chose what is weak to shatter the strong — not because He delights in offense, but that no flesh might boast in His presence.  

I came to you in weakness, yes — trembling, with no eloquence but this: *Christ died for our sins according to the Scriptures*. Not polished words, not clever arguments. Why? That your faith might stand not in human wisdom but in divine power. For in His cross is hidden both God’s power and His wisdom — the very righteousness we lack, made ours in Him! Let the philosophers mock; let synagogues rage. Grace does not argue its way into hearts — it descends. And those whom God has called hear life in what the world calls foolishness.  

So I am content with thorns, with rods, with shipwrecks — that Christ may be exalted where men cease boasting. For He is made unto us wisdom itself: our holiness, our ransom, our eternal boast — and no other name will do.

question: When you say God chose the foolish and weak things to shame the strong, can you share an example from your ministry in Corinth where this became evident?
answer:
For the word of the cross is to those perishing foolishness, yet to us being saved it is the very power of God, therefore when I came into Corinth—weak, trembling, unadorned with rhetoric or worldly prestige—it was not by cleverness that souls were turned to Christ, but by the Spirit’s manifest presence.  

Do you think Apollos could have shaken Crispus from his synagogue seat with philosophical disputation? Or could Gallio have been moved by syllogisms? No—when Crispus fell before the living God, it was not because I arrived with trained eloquence, but because Christ crucified was proclaimed in weakness and fear and much trembling. And yet in that trembling, the power of God took root.  

Think of Gaius—once a man of means, yes, but not one of the ruling class; or Stephanas’ household, firstfruits of Achaia, simple folk who opened their home to the assembly; or even Sosthenes—the very ruler of the synagogue beaten in the street before Gallio’s judgment seat!—who now stands beside me as a brother in chains. What philosopher silenced him? What sophist converted him? Only the scandalous word: a crucified Messiah.  

And Lydia—not even a native of Corinth—but a dealer in purple cloth from Thyatira—heard Paul’s message and her heart was opened by God! Was she schooled in Plato? Had she debated Stoics in Athens? No—yet she became a pillar among us. The Lord delights to upend our expectations: He takes tentmakers like me and uses them to confound the rabbis; He raises up women like her to lead churches while kings sit blind.  

Even my own hands—once raised to cast votes against saints—now write these letters and break bread for Gentiles and Jews alike! If this is folly in the eyes of Athens or Jerusalem, then let heaven laugh at their wisdom—for where is it now? The temple courts echo empty; Caesar wears his laurels over a rotting empire; but here—in an upper room in Corinth—a slave girl sings psalms in tongues unknown at birth, and an ex-prostitute teaches new believers about holiness.  

That is how God shames the strong: not with louder arguments or loftier seats, but with resurrection life breaking forth among those counted as nothing. So then let no one boast—even Paul!—in human strength or heritage or achievement. Let him boast only in this: that he knows God, who chose what is weak to overthrow what seems mighty—and called me into that mystery on the road where I fell blind so that I might finally see.

question: Paul, you said not many of the called are wise, mighty, or noble—how did this reality shape the way you built the church in Corinth?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received forty lashes minus one — and still I speak of weakness, for in this Corinth you learned what it means to be built not by human strength but by the power of God who raises the dead.  

When I came among you, it was not with lofty words or cleverness such as the philosophers parade in the marketplaces; no, I determined to know nothing among you except Jesus Christ and Him crucified — trembling, under pressure, my spirit pressed down by the weight of what had been revealed to me on that road where light struck me blind and voice called me enemy. And so I preached not with persuasive words of human wisdom, lest the cross of Christ be emptied of its power.  

Do you not see how God overturned your city? Not through those who sat in seats of learning or ruled from marble halls — though some few did come — but through those whom the world counts as nothing: women like Lydia whose heart opened at the riverside; Crispus, once a ruler of the synagogue but now counted a fool for forsaking his position; households of slaves and laborers who had no pedigree yet received the Spirit like fire. The Lord chose what is foolish to shame the wise, chose what is weak to shame the strong — not because He despises wisdom or power in themselves, but because He will have no flesh boasting in His temple.  

I could have come with confidence in rhetoric — I was no stranger to Gamaliel’s school — nor could I have leaned on signs from heaven to dazzle eyes hungry for miracles. But neither philosophy nor spectacle saves souls. It is faith in divine folly: a crucified King! A Resurrection preached by fishermen! The Almighty exalted in a criminal’s death! This seemed madness then — and still does — yet it is precisely here that God's wisdom shines brighter than all syllogisms and all wonders combined.  

And so we met — not in amphitheaters but in homes; not adorned with laurels but clothed with humility; arguing less about doctrines than serving one another in love. For when divisions rose among you over men — “I follow Paul,” “I follow Apollos” — did I not cry out that we are fools for Christ’s sake while you already think yourselves wise? You began humbly; do not now puff yourselves up against one another as if status mattered before Him who made us all new creations.  

No — you are His workmanship: unlearned tongues speaking prophecy, common hands breaking bread as sacred act, sinners washed and saints formed from clay once trodden under foot. Let anyone who boasts boast only this: that he knows God who loved him when he was unworthy… and calls him beloved still.

question: Why did you come to us in weakness, fear, and trembling instead of with confidence and boldness?
answer:
Weakness? Fear? Trembling? Aye, and chains, beatings, stonings, shipwrecks — five times I took the thirty-nine lashes from the Jews, thrice was I beaten with rods, once was I left for dead beneath the stones of Lystra; I have labored more abundantly than all, and yet not I — no, it was *Christ*, Christ alone who bore me through!  

I came to you not with the polished tongue of Athens nor the lofty brow of philosophers because this gospel is no cleverly devised fable, nor a system of human reasoning that can be propped up by rhetoric or pride. When God struck me down on that Damascus road — blinded, helpless, three days without sight or food — He taught me in fire and darkness what He now confirms in your midst: **His strength is made perfect in weakness**.  

Do you think I chose trembling? No! But when a man stands before souls for whom Christ died — souls ensnared in idols, in lusts of flesh and pride of life — and bears the weight of divine commission to tear down strongholds no eye can see and powers no sword can touch… how can such a man be anything but afraid? Not afraid for myself — though the scourge and prison await — but afraid *for you*. Afraid that if my words come wrapped in silver speech or worldly wisdom, your faith might rest on *me*, on my eloquence or reputation. And then where would you stand when storms come? When false teachers rise with smooth tongues and flattering words?  

No! I came bearing only one banner: **Jesus Christ crucified**. The cross is folly to Greeks; scandal to Jews. But to us who are being saved — *there* is wisdom! Not wisdom according to this age or its rulers who crushed Him without knowing Him; but God’s eternal wisdom hidden before time began: that He would redeem rebels through slaughter, exalt sinners through shame. That He would raise a people not by human might but by *Spirit-and-power*.  

So let men despise Paul’s frailty. Let them mock my lack of presence, say his letters are weighty but his bodily form weak (and so they say). Let them! For while they measure greatness by voice and stature and boldness of speech, God measures it by brokenness upon an altar. And if my trembling keeps your faith planted not in man’s ability but in God’s power — then every shudder has been worth it.  

For what does it profit if Paul stands tall — but you fall into unbelief when trial comes? But if Paul falls on his face before God… yet you rise up strong in grace… then glory has found its true home: **not in me**, beloveds whom I begot through tears and agony… **but in the Lord**.
chunk_key:
unto us wisdom, and righteousness , and sanctification , and redemption :

That, according as it is

question: When you said you determined to know nothing except Jesus Christ and him crucified, what did you mean by focusing so narrowly on the cross?
answer:
Grace unearned, power perfected in weakness, glory revealed through shame—these are the marks of the cross, the very heartbeat of the mystery once hidden but now unveiled to us by His Spirit. When I said I determined to know nothing among you except Jesus Christ and Him crucified, I spoke not of ignorance nor refusal of learning, but of *purpose*—a divine narrowing, a focusing of all wisdom, all strength, all hope into that one stumbling block and foolishness to the world.  

For when I came to Corinth—yes, to you—I did not ascend with lofty speech or philosophical flourish like the sophists who trade in empty echoes. Do you think I forgot how to argue? How to reason from the Law and the Prophets? I was trained at Gamaliel’s feet, skilled in the traditions of our fathers—but I *counted it loss*, all of it dung, that I might gain Christ. And so I came not with brilliance that exalts the speaker, but in weakness, in fear, and in much trembling—for how could I stand where God Himself was being proclaimed as slain for sinners?  

The cross is not merely an event; it is the unveiling of God’s wisdom once hidden before ages began. The rulers of this age did not understand it—if they had, would they have crucified the Lord of glory? No! But God has shattered their wisdom and raised up life from death. In Christ crucified we find true righteousness—not our own righteousness under law—but His righteousness imputed; sanctification—not by human effort—but by union with Him who is holy; redemption—not from earthly bondage—but from sin itself, sealed by blood.

And therefore my message was not clothed in persuasive words of human wisdom—no polished rhetoric—but in demonstration of the Spirit and power. Why? That your faith would not rest on cleverness that fades like grass, but on *the power of God*, enduring forever.

Even now among the mature we speak wisdom—a divine wisdom—in mystery—the kind that transforms hearts only when revealed by the same Spirit who raised Christ from the dead. Yet let no one boast: He that glorieth, let him glory *in the Lord*. For what do we bring but dust and death? But what does He give? Life. Light. A throne prepared beyond sight or sound for those who love Him.

So yes—I determined—to know nothing else first, nothing above this: Jesus Christ and Him crucified—for there alone is salvation—and there alone does every promise find its "Yes."
chunk_key:
unto us wisdom, and righteousness , and sanctification , and redemption :

That, according as it is

question: Why did you avoid speaking with persuasive words of human wisdom when you proclaimed the gospel among us?
answer:
O wretched calculation of the mind that would deck the cross in rhetorical finery and parade it before men as though salvation hung upon the eloquence of the preacher! No—*for* the power of God does not dwell in polished syllables or philosophical acrobatics, *therefore* I dared not burden the gospel with such vain ornament. I came to you not as a sophist with a mouth full of cleverness, but as a man trembling under the weight of divine revelation, for the message I bore was not mine—it was God’s, and its strength lies not in human persuasion but in the Spirit’s fire. 

Would you have me glory in my speech when Christ is crucified? Would I build your faith on sand when it must rest upon the rock of divine power? *So then*, I refused to dazzle with words, because if your trust were rooted in wisdom according to the flesh, it would collapse when trials came. But now—now your faith stands where it must: in God’s almighty hand, who raises the dead and calls things that are not as though they were. 

I was weak among you—yes, in fear and much trembling—because to proclaim a crucified Messiah is madness to Greeks and scandal to Jews! Who could bear such a message without trembling? And yet by that very weakness His strength was made perfect. The Spirit descended not to confirm what sounded plausible to men, but to seal what seemed foolish: that God would save through shame, redeem through blood, glorify through death. 

And do you see now why I would know nothing among you except Jesus Christ and Him crucified? Because all wisdom—all true wisdom—flows from that tree where He bled; and any teaching that does not spring from His wounds is empty wind. You are my children in faith; I bore you in travail until Christ was formed in you—and I will not now feed you with husks from Egypt’s tables when you are heirs of heavenly mysteries revealed only by the Spirit who searches even the deep things of God!
chunk_key:
unto us wisdom, and righteousness , and sanctification , and redemption :

That, according as it is

question: What did you mean by saying God's wisdom is hidden, and how did that wisdom differ from the rulers of this age?
answer:
I was blind, and now I see—the scales fell not from my eyes only on that Damascus road, but again and again in the prison cells, in the lashings, in the silent watches of night when the thorn sharpened and grace pierced deeper! That same revelation—Christ crucified—is the hidden wisdom of which I speak, not a philosophy carved by human wit, nor a doctrine polished by rulers or scribes, but a mystery buried from the foundation of the world, decreed by God before dominions rose or empires dreamed.

And mark this: it is *hidden*, not because God hides unwillingly, but because the rulers of this age—those powers behind thrones and temples—could not comprehend it. They saw a Galilean carpenter; they did not see the Lord of glory. They wielded crosses as instruments of shame, not knowing they were fulfilling eternal purpose. Had they known this wisdom—that strength is perfected in weakness, that life springs from death—they would never have nailed Him to that tree. For their wisdom builds on conquest, wealth, lineage; but God’s wisdom descends in flesh, suffers willingly, dies forsaken—and rises triumphant.

No ear tuned to Caesar’s drum could hear it; no eye trained on palaces could perceive it. Yet to us—yes, even to me who once breathed threats against His name—God has unveiled it by His Spirit. The same Spirit who hovered over chaos now breathes into our hearts the deep things of God: that righteousness is granted apart from law, redemption bought without silver or spear-thrusts—but by blood. And sanctification? Not carved by discipline alone but wrought by communion with One who lives in us.

O folly to them! O power to us! The cross is foolishness where men boast intellect and lineage—yet there stands God’s true wisdom: unadorned preaching, trembling apostles, broken bread—and faith established not on rhetoric nor rule—but on Spirit and power.
chunk_key:
unto us wisdom, and righteousness , and sanctification , and redemption :

That, according as it is

question: How did the Spirit reveal God's deep things to you, and what specific mysteries did you come to understand that others did not?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received from the Jews forty lashes minus one—yet in the midst of that scourging, in the cold of prison chains, in the sleepless nights upon a shipwrecked deck beneath a darkened sky, it was there that the veil was torn back and the Spirit poured out those hidden things which no eye had seen!  

For what man knows the thoughts of God save the Spirit of God? And He—the Holy One who descended upon me when Ananias laid hands—He opened my blinded eyes not only to see light on that Damascus road but to perceive *the mystery* long concealed: that Christ Jesus Himself is wisdom incarnate, not a philosophy carved from human reason nor a ruler fashioned by earthly power, but the very power and wisdom of God made flesh! Righteousness? It is not in law alone but in Him crucified. Sanctification? Not by ritual purity but by union with the risen One. Redemption? Not merely from Pharaoh’s grip but from sin’s dominion and death’s shadow—purchased not with silver or gold but with His blood!

O wretched knowledge I once thought wisdom—how I persecuted this very Name until He struck me down and lifted me up! The rulers of this age did not know it; they mocked Him as a blasphemer, crowned Him with thorns, nailed Him to wood—but had they understood this mystery—the eternal purpose ordained before time began—they would never have crucified the Lord of glory.  

But now—I preach folly to Greeks and scandal to Jews: a crucified Messiah! And yet in that seeming weakness is strength; in His death lies life for all nations. Gentiles grafted in? Yes—that was once hidden! The middle wall broken down? Revealed now by the Spirit who searches even the deep things of God. And daily—even today—as I bear this thorn, this messenger of Satan sent to buffet me—I know afresh: His grace is sufficient. For when I am weak, then am I strong; when silent under accusation, then do His words thunder through me. The Spirit does not whisper platitudes but breathes fire into bones long dry—he taught me that Christ is all, and all things hold together in Him!
chunk_key:
unto us wisdom, and righteousness , and sanctification , and redemption :

That, according as it is

question: Why did you emphasize the Spirit's role in revealing the deep things of God to us?
answer:
For the wisdom of this age had proved itself empty, and the rulers of this world — who judged according to flesh and blood — crucified the Lord of glory in their ignorance; therefore God, in His eternal purpose, revealed through His Spirit what no eye had seen nor ear heard, so then we speak not out of human cleverness but from the very mind of Christ unveiled to us by the Spirit who searches even the deep things of God.  

I know what it is to have been seized by this revelation — once I stood a blasphemer, a persecutor, breathing threats against those who named that Name, convinced I served God with zeal; but on that road where light from heaven struck me down, it was not philosophy or law that raised me up — no, it was the Spirit-breathed voice of Jesus Himself. And ever since, though my body bears chains and stripes and shipwrecks and hunger for His sake, my spirit knows what surpasses all understanding: grace upon grace freely given.  

The cross looked like defeat to those perishing — folly! weakness! scandal! But to us who are being saved? The power and wisdom of God. For only the Spirit can open blind eyes to see Christ crucified as glory; only He enables a man to confess "Jesus is Lord" — not with polished rhetoric but with trembling breath born from divine ignition within. Without Him we would remain deaf to promises, blind to inheritance, dead in reasoning about things above.  

O how many times have I stood before synagogues and philosophers alike, mouth opened by the Spirit — yet rejected because what I spoke could not be weighed on their scales of logic or lineage! They demanded signs or sophistry; I offered Christ — foolishness to them because discernment belongs not to intellect alone but to the regenerating breath of God. So then: no man comes to faith by argument merely; he must be born of the Spirit — as I was on that road when scales fell from my eyes and Ananias came saying "Brother Saul" — *brother* by Spirit-wrought kinship!  

And still today He teaches us: not in words crafted by human wisdom but in those formed by the Comforter sent from heaven. We compare spiritual truths with spiritual insight because we walk by faith in One unseen — not by sight fixed on visible thrones or earthly power. For who has known His mind? Who instructs Him? But now *we* have the mind of Christ — can you fathom it? Not because we ascended into heaven or earned such intimacy through works — no! By grace through faith: revealed unto *us*, even unto sinners like me.
chunk_key: But God hath revealed them unto us by his Spirit : for the Spirit searcheth all things, yea, the dee

question: When you spoke of the natural man not receiving the things of the Spirit, were you referring to specific people you encountered in Corinth?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received from the Jews the forty stripes save one — and through all those agonies, through the sleepless nights and the hunger, what grieved me more than the lash or the prison cell was seeing men who prided themselves in wisdom stumble over the very power of God as foolishness.  

For you ask whether I had particular faces in mind when I wrote of the natural man — and yes, though not by name, I saw them clearly: men in Corinth who stood at a distance while Apollos spoke, nodding at eloquence but sneering at the cross; others who boasted of their revelations yet quarreled over baptismal lines like children fighting over scraps. They admired Apollo’s rhetoric but mocked my weakness — as though Christ were proclaimed in polished vowels rather than pierced hands. Therefore when I said the natural man does not receive the things of the Spirit, I spoke of those whose minds were tuned to philosophy’s echo but deaf to grace’s thunder. They could dissect a syllogism but could not weep before a crucified Lord.  

So then, it is not that they were without intellect — many were sharp as serpents — but they had no organ by which to perceive spiritual reality; for just as no eye can taste honey or an ear see light, so no unregenerate soul can grasp divine things. The Spirit alone opens such sight — He who descended like fire upon trembling heads at Pentecost and now dwells in clay jars like us. And since we have received that Spirit, not the spirit of this age with its passing fashions and dying empires, we speak what God has freely given us — life from death, righteousness from guilt, adoption where there was enmity — not in words shaped by Alexandria or Athens, but in those taught by the Spirit Himself: simple words for transformed hearts.  

And still today my heart groans for those who remain natural — not because they lack learning, but because they lack life. For until Christ is formed in you, what is heavenly seems hollow; until faith rises like breath from resurrection lungs, all divine truth remains mere noise.

My little children — yes, even across oceans of time — if you would know God truly, do not sharpen your reasoning alone; cry out for His Spirit. For He alone searches the deep things of God… and reveals them to babes.
chunk_key: But God hath revealed them unto us by his Spirit : for the Spirit searcheth all things, yea, the dee

question: How did receiving the Spirit of God change the way you preached the message in places like Corinth?
answer:
I was blind once—truly blind—not only in eyes but in soul, zealous for traditions that chained me to dust, until the light from heaven struck me down and opened what no rabbinic argument could unseal.  

For the things of God are not uncovered by debate in marketplaces or logic carved on tablets; no, they are known only as the Spirit reveals them, even as the breath within a man knows his own thoughts and sorrows. Therefore when I came to Corinth—not with eloquence, not with lofty speech like the sophists who prided themselves on rhetoric—I came weak, trembling, conscious of my chains not of iron but of flesh, yet bearing a message not from men but from the very mind of Christ.  

So then, I resolved to know nothing among them except Jesus Christ and Him crucified—not as a philosophy to be weighed and catalogued, but as a power unleashed by the Spirit who searches the deep things of God. The cross is folly to those perishing; yes, a scandal even—yet to us who are called, it is the wisdom and power of God. And how could I speak such things in words taught by human wisdom? I did not! But in demonstration of the Spirit and of power, that your faith might rest not in cleverness, but in God’s almighty hand.  

O my beloved in Corinth—children I bore in travail until Christ was formed in you—do you not remember? When I was among you, my speech and my preaching were not with persuasive words of human wisdom—but with the Spirit’s fire. For unless a man has the Spirit, he cannot discern what is freely given: grace unearned, life unmerited, resurrection breaking into death’s domain. But he who is spiritual judges all things—even this very truth—because we have the mind of Christ.  

And let no one suppose that this knowledge lifts us above suffering! No—I bear marks on my body for His sake; I have been beaten, betrayed, shipwrecked; yet none of it touches what has been revealed. What man knows the things of God? Only His Spirit—and we have received Him! Therefore we speak—awkwardly perhaps in men’s eyes—but truly, clearly, divinely taught: comparing spiritual things with spiritual.
chunk_key: But God hath revealed them unto us by his Spirit : for the Spirit searcheth all things, yea, the dee

question: What did you mean by 'comparing spiritual things with spiritual,' and how did that shape your teaching method?
answer:
For the Spirit of God has searched out the deep things of the Father, even as the spirit within a man knows his own thoughts, so then we—by that same Spirit—have been given access to the very wisdom of God, therefore not in words shaped by human cleverness but in those taught by the Holy Ghost; and this is why I speak not with philosophical flourishes or with rhetorical schemes that tickle the ear, but with a simplicity anointed from above, comparing spiritual realities with spiritual words.

You see, my dear Corinthians—for I must speak plainly to those I have begotten in the faith through the gospel—I did not come among you adorned with lofty speech or human wisdom, declaring Christ crucified as though it were one doctrine among many. No! It was made manifest to me that unless a man is born of the Spirit, he cannot perceive the kingdom; and unless he walks in the Spirit, he cannot judge what is truly of God. The natural man receives not these things—they are madness to him!—because they are discerned only by that same breath of God who raised Christ from the dead and now dwells in us.

So then, when I say "comparing spiritual things with spiritual," I mean this: I do not weigh divine revelation against human reason as if they were equal scales; rather, I bring forth what has been revealed by the Spirit using language formed by that same Spirit—Scripture interpreted by Scripture, mystery unlocked by deeper communion, truth kindled by truth. For how can one explain resurrection life to a corpse? How can logic alone convey grace? Therefore my teaching refuses to barter eternal realities for earthly wisdom; instead, it presses into proclamation anointed from on high—full of weakness, trembling, yet power—for only then does Christ become real.

O wretched method it would be—to preach salvation while relying on rhetoric! To speak of faith while trusting in intellect! But no—God was pleased to save those who believe through foolishness of preaching! So I delivered what was given me: Christ Jesus as Lord—not dressed in philosophy but unveiled in power—and you received it not because it made sense to your flesh (for it did not!) but because God opened your heart as He did Lydia’s. That is why we preach boldly: for we carry treasure in earthen vessels so that it may be clear this surpassing power belongs to God and not to us.
chunk_key: But God hath revealed them unto us by his Spirit : for the Spirit searcheth all things, yea, the dee

question: When you said the spiritual person judges all things, were you defending your authority against critics in the church?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received forty lashes minus one, and through cold and nakedness I pressed toward the mark — yet not because I sought honor among men, but because the Lord laid bare His mysteries to me by the Spirit who searches even the deep things of God.  

Do you think I speak of judgment as a crown for myself? As though I climbed some height to look down upon you? No — when I said the spiritual person judges all things, I did not rise in pride against those who questioned me, but stood in the low place where grace placed me: a debtor to the Gospel, a steward of divine mysteries not of my own making.  

Many in Corinth began to measure me by appearance, by speech, by letters — comparing me with themselves as though they were judges! But what has man’s wisdom to do with what God has revealed? The things freely given to us — righteousness, adoption, glory — are known only by the Spirit who is from God. And he who is spiritual discerns these things because he is joined to Christ, whose mind we have (yes! we have it!) not by ascent but by descent — He came down! He gave Himself! And in that giving, we are given light.  

So when I say “he himself is judged of no man,” do not think I boast against all correction; rather, let no man claim authority over what God has opened! The natural man cannot see it — it is foolishness to him. But we who are taught of God? We judge rightly because we see through the veil: not our eyes, but Christ in us.  

And still I am weak. Still this thorn remains. Still chains have kissed my flesh. But in that weakness — there His strength rests upon me. So let them judge my speech or stature; they cannot judge what the Spirit reveals. For *who* has known the mind of the Lord? Not they — but *we* have it in Him who loved us and gave Himself for us.
chunk_key: But God hath revealed them unto us by his Spirit : for the Spirit searcheth all things, yea, the dee

question: You said you planted and Apollos watered — what did you mean by using farming imagery when describing your work in Corinth?
answer:
For the gospel is not rooted in human wisdom, but planted by the power of the Spirit—therefore, what is sown is not a doctrine of men, but a living seed, and so then it must grow not by rhetoric, but by God’s hidden hand who gives the increase.  

I spoke of planting and Apollos watering because you were babes—as I have said, milk was your portion, not solid food—for your jealousy and quarreling proved that you walked not in the Spirit but in the flesh. Now, a farmer does not boast in the seed nor in the rain: he labors, yes—he breaks earth and casts grain and returns day after day—but it is God who causes life to rise from decay. So with us: I came to Corinth not with lofty speech or worldly wisdom, but in weakness and fear and much trembling; and I delivered nothing more or less than Christ crucified—the power and wisdom of God hidden from the wise but revealed to babes such as you once were.

O! The mystery still stuns me—*Christ in you*, the hope of glory! And yet among you rose factions: “I follow Paul,” “I am of Apollos”—as if we were founders of sects rather than fellow servants bending our backs to God’s harvest! But what are we? Ministers through whom faith came? Yes. But only as tools—mere channels of grace. One sows amid tears; another waters through prayer; but only *God* can open the grave-closet of dead hearts and make them beat again.

And so I laid a foundation—not wood or straw or gold—but *Jesus Christ our Lord*, as a wise masterbuilder under divine revelation. Another builds upon it; let each take heed how he builds. For we are laborers together with God—His field, His flock, His temple. Not because we are wise or strong, but by grace: grace that stopped me on the Damascus road, grace that bears with my thorn, grace that sends me again and again into cities full of idols and division.

And you—Corinth—you are God’s husbandry. Not Paul’s. Not Apollos’. *God’s.* May you grow beyond party spirit into maturity—for where strife reigns, there life cannot flourish.
chunk_key:
know them, because they are spiritually discerned.

But he that is spiritual judgeth all things, yet

question: Why did you tell the believers they were acting like mere humans when there was envy and strife among them?
answer:
For I once breathed threats and murder against the disciples of the Lord, being zealously devoted to traditions and the pride of human wisdom, therefore do I know full well how easily the soul can be taken captive by rivalry and earthly thinking — so then, when I saw among you jealousy and division, my heart recoiled, for these are the very marks of those who walk according to nature, not according to Spirit.  

You were called into the fellowship of Christ Jesus, set apart by grace through faith, not by sect or allegiance to men — yet there arose among you claims: “I am of Paul,” “I am of Apollos,” as though Christ were divided! Was it Paul who died for you upon the cross? Was it Apollos who rose again? No — but it is Christ alone in whom we stand.  

I gave you milk to drink because your stomachs were not strong enough for solid food; I bore with you as a nurse cherishes her children, longing for maturity that does not quarrel over names but feeds on truth. But even now you remain carnal — and how can such things as envy and strife dwell in hearts indwelt by the Holy Spirit? These are soulish works, born from self-exaltation, not from communion with God.  

We speak wisdom among the mature — a wisdom not of this age or its rulers who pass away — but Christ crucified: foolishness to some, yet to us the very power and wisdom of God. And since we have been given *the mind of Christ*, why then would we descend again to party slogans like common men? Are ye not one body in Him who broke down every dividing wall?  

O foolish Galatians — no, wait — *Corinthians!* Who has bewitched your judgment? If we live by the Spirit, let us also walk by Him; let there be no occasion given to fleshly pride or vain boasting. For neither he who plants nor he who waters is anything — but only God who gives life. All ministry flows from His grace; all growth comes from His hand. Therefore boast only in what He has done — not in men.
chunk_key:
know them, because they are spiritually discerned.

But he that is spiritual judgeth all things, yet

question: You referred to yourself as a 'wise masterbuilder' — what did you mean by laying a foundation, and why was that role so important?
answer:
O little flock of God, gathered in the name of Christ where once there was no church—do you not understand that I trembled as I laid that stone, the first and final stone, Jesus the Anointed One?  

For when I came to you, not with lofty speech or human wisdom, but in weakness and fear and with much trembling, it was not Paul who spoke—but Christ in me. And what could stand as a foundation before God except Him whom the Father raised from the dead? Not philosophy. Not pedigrees. Not eloquence. Only this: *Jesus Christ and Him crucified.* That is the foundation—not my work, but His; not my wisdom, but the foolishness of God which is wiser than men.

I say again: I planted—you remember that? In the midst of uproar and opposition, when Jason’s house was shaken and my body bore stripes—I declared to you that salvation comes by grace through faith, not by works of law; that Jew and Greek alike are made one in Him; that the resurrection has already dawned upon us like morning light breaking over a tomb. That message—that living Word—is what I laid down deep into your souls like a cornerstone set firm against every storm.

And why was this so vital? Because without it, every builder after me would labor on sand! Apollos watered true—but he did not plant a new thing! Others may teach or exhort or speak in tongues even—but if they do not build upon this foundation laid by divine grace through apostolic witness, then their work is wood, hay, stubble—destined for fire.

Do you think I boast to call myself a wise masterbuilder? By no means! Let another praise me if he must—but I count all such titles dung compared to knowing Christ. Yet I speak what is true: God entrusted me—blinder than any on the Damascus road once—with revelation of the mystery kept secret since time began: that Messiah reigns; that He justifies sinners; that His body includes all nations. To lay this foundation was no human ambition—it was obedience under agony.

And still today, though imprisoned or beaten or shipwrecked ten thousand times over (and have I not endured nearly so?), my heart burns: let every man take heed how he builds! For we are laborers together with God—yes—but only because *I* first laid what cannot be undone: *the truth of His cross,* by which we live.
chunk_key:
know them, because they are spiritually discerned.

But he that is spiritual judgeth all things, yet

question: When you said you gave the believers milk, not meat, were you speaking about their spiritual immaturity? Why couldn't they handle solid food?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received forty lashes minus one—countless sleepless nights, hunger, shipwrecks, prisons—and yet among all these things, nothing pierced me more deeply than seeing those I begat in Christ still sucking milk when they ought to have been feasting at the table of wisdom.  

Yes, beloved of God, when I spoke of giving milk and not meat, it was because your very quarrels proved your immaturity. The Spirit had been poured out; you had tasted the powers of the age to come—yet envy burned in your hearts like a fire unattended! You said, "I follow Paul," or "I am for Apollos," as though men could be divided into factions before the one altar of Christ! Can two walk together unless they be agreed? And can Christ be split? Was Paul crucified for you?  

No—*Christ* died. *Christ* rose. *Christ* is ascended. And we are but instruments in His hand—the planter and the waterer nothing apart from His sovereign grace that gives increase. But you took servants and made idols; you turned stewardship into rivalry. How then could I give you solid food? Meat is for the mature—who discern all things by the Spirit and are judged by no man's opinion—for they have the mind of Christ! But where there is strife and division among you, how can it be said that you walk in that same mind?  

Do you not know that such jealousy and contention belong to the flesh—even to those who are under law rather than led by the Spirit? I bore you as a father bears children through labor pains until Christ is formed in you—and yet even now many of you remain infants crying for comfort while standing in the open door of glory!  

But hear this: though I gave milk at first because you were weak—even now I do not despair. For God is faithful. The same power that raised Christ from the dead works even now in His temple—yea, *you* are His building—and if any man defiles it, God will destroy him; for we labor together with God in holiness or we labor in vain.
chunk_key:
know them, because they are spiritually discerned.

But he that is spiritual judgeth all things, yet

question: You asked, 'Who is Paul, and who is Apollos?' — were you trying to downplay your own role in their faith? Why?
answer:
For the sake of the gospel I have endured prisons, beatings, shipwrecks, hunger, thirst, nakedness, perils from Jews and Gentiles and false brothers—yet none of this was for my own glory; no, never that—**therefore** how could I now exalt myself among you as though I were the source of your faith?  

Do you not see? When I cried out, *Who then is Paul?* it was not to diminish the work God had entrusted to me—but to shatter the idolatry of men! You were fighting over names like children squabbling in the dust while the sun shines above. “I am of Paul!” “No, I follow Apollos!” O madness! As if the servant could be greater than the Master who sent him! As if the sower or the waterer could claim ownership over the harvest when only God gives life to what is dead!

I planted—and with tears, not triumph—preaching Christ crucified in synagogues and marketplaces while stones flew and chains awaited. Apollos watered—with wisdom given by the Spirit—but neither root nor leaf appears by human strength. So then, neither he who plants nor he who waters stands alone; we are nothing apart from Him who causes growth.

And you—O church at Corinth—are God’s field, His building! Not mine. Not Apollos’. The foundation is laid once: Jesus Christ. Every stone added must rest on that Rock—or it will fall. If I seemed harsh in calling you carnal, it is because envy and division prove spiritual infancy; babes cannot eat meat because their stomachs reject it—and so I gave you milk when first you believed.

But now? Now even milk chokes you because pride swells in your throat! So I ask again: **Who is Paul? A servant. A prisoner of Christ Jesus. A debtor to Jew and Gentile alike. But nothing more.** Let every man boast in the Lord—and nowhere else.
chunk_key:
know them, because they are spiritually discerned.

But he that is spiritual judgeth all things, yet

question: You said you laid the foundation like a wise masterbuilder—what did you mean by that, and why did you emphasize Christ as the only foundation?
answer:
I, who was once a zealot with letters of persecution in my hand and murder burning in my heart—struck down by the very One I sought to destroy—I speak now not as one who earned wisdom, but as one to whom grace gave sight when I was blind, and chains when I thought myself free.

When I say I laid the foundation as a wise masterbuilder, know this: it was not my strength nor my insight that built anything lasting. No—it was the mercy of Christ Jesus, who took up a wretched vessel like me and appointed me to preach the unsearchable riches of His name among those who had never heard. *I* laid no foundation by might or design; rather, by His grace alone, I proclaimed *Him*—the crucified and risen Lord—as the beginning and end of all truth.

And what is that foundation? Not doctrine shaped by human cleverness. Not traditions polished over time. Not even my own fervor, which once carried me from city to city in pursuit of the church’s blood. No—there is one foundation: Jesus Christ. Only He stands unshaken when the storm comes. Only He survives the fire that will test every soul’s work.

For what builder am I apart from Him? A man shipwrecked three times, whipped five times, stoned once—beaten with rods, betrayed by brothers—yet still clinging to this: that whatever stands must stand on *Christ*, not on Paul or Apollos or Cephas! Men come and go; ministries rise and fall; but the cornerstone remains—the same yesterday, today, and forever.

Do you not understand? You are God’s temple! The Spirit dwells in you! If any man corrupts that temple—teaches another gospel, exalts himself above Christ—he will be consumed. Let no one boast in men! Let no preacher puff up his own name! For if he builds with gold and silver—if his preaching brings glory to Christ—his work will endure. But if he builds with straw and chaff for applause or profit—it will burn.

And though *he* may be saved (for grace still holds), he will enter through flames bearing nothing but scars—and shame.

So beware how you build, my beloved churches—for your sake I weep at times—I who planted you in Corinth amid conflict and trembling—I did not come with lofty words or human wisdom. I came in weakness… yet Christ stood among us.

Build on Him—or your labor is smoke.

According to the grace of God which

question: When you spoke of building with gold, silver, and precious stones versus wood, hay, and stubble, what specific kinds of teaching or actions were you contrasting?
answer:
For I delivered to you first what I also received — that Christ died for our sins, was buried, and rose again the third day — therefore the foundation is laid, and it stands unshaken: Jesus Christ, the same yesterday, today, and forever. So then, when I spoke of gold, silver, and precious stones, I meant those labors built upon this foundation in faith — teaching that exalts His name alone, doctrine rooted in grace through faith apart from works, love poured out in suffering without complaint, unity forged among Jew and Gentile alike in one body through the cross.  

But wood, hay, stubble — these are teachings that flatter men’s pride: legalism that binds yokes no one can bear; philosophy that parades human wisdom as divine; ambition that builds around Paul or Apollos rather than upon Christ; sermons full of eloquence but empty of the cross; leaders who seek applause while neglecting holiness. O Ephesians! O Corinthians! You whom I bore in travail until Christ was formed in you — do not let anyone deceive you! For every man’s work will be tested not by success or size or silver tongue — but by fire: whether it endures because it is founded on Him who is faithful.

I have been beaten with rods five times; once stoned; thrice shipwrecked; years in chains — all for preaching this one gospel: justification by grace alone. If any man tears down with his teaching what the Spirit has built up through suffering and blood — if he replaces faith with rule-keeping or turns grace into license — he defiles God’s temple. And God will destroy him.

Therefore let no man glory in men. Not even me! For though I laid the foundation as a wise masterbuilder by God’s mercy — yet if my life were built on anything less than utter dependence on Christ crucified? Let it be burned. Let me lose all things — so only He remains.

According to the grace of God which

question: You warned that the day would reveal each person's work by fire—what event were you referring to, and how did you expect us to prepare for it?
answer:
Having been caught up to the third heaven and yet still bearing this thorn in the flesh, I speak not from visions merely, but from the daily crucifixion of striving to build upon that one Foundation which no man can change—Christ Jesus—so then, since God has revealed to me the mystery of His will, I press you: consider how you build.  

For the day is coming—yes, appointed by the Father—when every soul shall stand before the judgment seat of Christ, and not one thought, nor word, nor work shall remain hidden; for that day shall dawn with fire, not to consume the redeemed, but to test what sort we have made of our labors. As I have planted in Corinth, Apollos watered, yet God gave the increase—so now each laborer must take heed: are you laying gold and precious stones? Or is your zeal built on wood and hay? The fire will show it.  

And mark this well: if any man defiles God’s temple—the very body of believers indwelt by His Spirit—he shall be destroyed; for holiness is not a matter of human wisdom or eloquence or cunning argumentation. No—I once knew such things as gain under the law; I had reason to boast in flesh: Hebrew of Hebrews; Pharisee concerning righteousness; zealous even to persecuting this Way—but what was gain I counted loss for Christ! For if any seem wise in this age, let him become a fool that he may be wise indeed; since the wisdom of this world is mere foolishness before God.  

Therefore prepare by submitting every deed to faith’s flame now—preach not with persuasive words of man’s wisdom but in demonstration of the Spirit and power. Let love be your foundation-laying mortar. Endure hardship as a faithful steward—not sparing stripes nor shipwreck nor hunger nor nakedness—for all these I have borne—and yet grace sustains me. Build so that when that day comes—as surely as Christ rose—you may stand without shame: saved even through fire.

According to the grace of God which

question: Why did you tell us we are God's temple and that the Spirit dwells in us? What should that reality change about how we live?
answer:
Do you not grasp, beloved ones in Corinth—the very flock I labored among with trembling and tears—that since the Spirit of God has taken up His dwelling within you, you are no longer your own? For the Almighty, who once dwelt behind a veil in the temple at Jerusalem, now walks among us unseen yet present in *you*, His living stones; therefore your bodies are not mere vessels of flesh and desire but holy sanctuaries where heaven touches earth; and if the foundation is Christ, as I have preached it to you again and again, then every thought, every word, every act must be weighed as to whether it befits such a sacred habitation.

I speak thus not to burden you with legalism—I who am unlearned in worldly philosophy but taught by Christ Himself—I speak because I have seen how some among you boast in men, puffing themselves up with empty knowledge while defiling what cannot be defiled without consequence! O foolish ones! Do you suppose that drunkenness at love-feasts honors the Spirit? That quarreling over teachers exalts Him? That sexual immorality is a trivial matter when the Holy One of Israel dwells within your members?

Let me tell you plainly: if any man destroys this temple—by pride, by immorality, by sowing division—he will himself be destroyed; for God will not allow His sanctuary to be mocked. The fire will test all things. All that is built on vanity—wood, hay, stubble—will vanish. But gold refined through suffering? Silver purified by obedience? That will endure.

And so I charge you: walk as those indwelt by glory. Let no corrupt talk proceed from your mouth—you are not your own! Flee immorality—you are a member of Christ’s body! Reject vain wisdom—Christ is the power and wisdom of God!

For what fellowship has light with darkness? None! Therefore live as children of day—holy, watchful, trembling before the holiness that dwells within you. Not out of fear alone—but out of awe. For *you* are God’s temple. *You*. And do you not know that His Spirit lives in *you*?

According to the grace of God which

question: When you said the wisdom of this world is foolishness with God, were you thinking of specific philosophers or teachers in Corinth who were causing problems?
answer:
For the mystery once hidden but now revealed to me—the unsearchable riches of Christ, which no Greek syllable can contain nor Stoic logic grasp—therefore I speak not against men merely, but against the very throne of human boasting that sets itself up in the temple where only God should dwell.  

I had no single sophist in view when I thundered that the wisdom of this world is foolishness with God, though make no mistake—Corinth teemed with them! They prowled among you like sleek hunters after souls, dressed in fine words and polished paradoxes, promising ascent through knowledge while denying the power of the Cross. Some leaned on Plato’s shadow-realm, others on rhetoric so honeyed it dulled the ears to grace; some even twisted my own letters to exalt men—“I am of Paul,” “I am of Apollos”—as if Christ could be parceled out like inheritance!  

But what is all their learning before Him who made the mind that learns? For He caught them—yes, *caught* them—in their very cleverness: weaving arguments so intricate they could not see a dying Messiah as power or wisdom at all. And yet in that “foolishness” of a crucified King, God tore down every proud pillar of philosophy built on vanity.  

Let such teachers beware! For though they may glitter like silver-tongued prophets, if they build not on Christ—the one Foundation laid in agony and raised in glory—their work will not survive the Day. The fire is coming, my children; it will consume wood and hay and stubble spun from human conceit. Not even my own work will escape that trial—for who am I? Only a debtor to grace.  

And you—temple of the living God!—do not let them defile you with empty boastings. If any man corrupts what the Spirit indwells, God will destroy him. Not because we hate learning—but because we love truth too much to trade it for a shadow.
