question: You told people to stay in the calling where they were called—what did you mean when you said that to slaves, like those in Corinth?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received forty lashes minus one — and still I say to you, *let every man abide in the calling wherein he was called*, not as a chain forged by men, but as a station appointed by God for the testing and triumph of faith.  

You ask of slaves in Corinth — yes, even those bought with Caesar’s coin — whether they must remain bound? Hear this: though their bodies were claimed by masters in this age, their souls were ransomed by Christ’s blood, and that redemption changes everything. I told them to care not for their condition — not because injustice is light, but because the Kingdom of God upends all earthly hierarchies. If a slave can gain freedom, let him seize it! But if not, let him know this: he is already free in the Lord. For though he serves under a human master, he answers ultimately to Christ; and though his body labors under chains, his spirit walks unshackled in divine sonship.  

And the free man? Let him become a servant of Christ — for that is true liberty! We are all bought with a price too great for earth to measure; therefore we are no longer our own. So whether bond or free, married or single, circumcised or uncircumcised — none of these define our standing before God. What matters is obedience to His commandments and abiding in faith.  

Do you think I speak lightly of bondage? I who have worn chains for the sake of the gospel? No — but I have also learned in every state to be content: imprisoned or released, beaten or whole, alone or among brothers. The Lord calls us not first to change our station, but to sanctify it — to live so fully in His will that even a slave becomes an example of holy freedom, and even a master learns he has a Master in heaven.  

So let no man despise his place; let no man glory in status; for we are all one body through faith in Christ Jesus — where neither slave nor free has final claim on us… only the cross does.

question: Why did you say that a married believer shouldn't seek to be loosed, but a single person could marry without sin?
answer:
I speak these things not under the lash of the law, but under the unsearchable riches of grace, having been laid hold of by Christ Jesus for this very purpose — that I might become a minister of His gospel to those outside as well as within. For even as I once persecuted the church, bound by zeal without knowledge, so now I am bound by love with understanding: that in every estate a man is called, there he is to walk with God, not flee from his station as though it were defilement. Therefore — since circumcision profits nothing, and uncircumcision no more — so neither does marriage justify, nor singleness sanctify; but faith working through love fulfills all things.

For this cause I say to the married: abide. Not because the bond itself is salvation, but because in abiding there is testimony — that Christ and His bride are mirrored in husband and wife; that patience is forged where burdens lie heavy; that holiness can bloom even in common dust. And if any man thinks to cast off his yoke hoping for greater spiritual freedom, let him know: true liberty is not escape from duty, but obedience in it. For were you called being married? You are not loosed from God’s appointment; therefore seek not divorce as though purity lay beyond your present chains.

But if you are loosed — whether by death or other providence — and your heart turns toward marriage again? Do not think yourself stained! For marriage is honorable among all, and the bed undefiled. To marry again is no sin; to wed a virgin brings no shame. The kingdom of God is not meat or marital status, but righteousness and peace and joy in the Holy Spirit.

And mark this well: I speak concerning virgins without direct command from the Lord — yet by mercy given me to discern what fits the present distress. In times such as these — with tribulation pressing like waves upon ships at sea — it may be good for a man so to be: undistracted by household cares. But let no one bind conscience where Christ has loosed! If you marry, you do not fall from grace; if you remain single, you do not rise above it.

For we are bought with a price — whether married or free—so let none serve men as master; rather serve Christ alone in whatever state he was called.

question: You mentioned virgins and said you had no direct command from the Lord—why did you feel led to give your judgment instead?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received forty stripes save one—yet in all these things I bear in mind not only the sufferings of my body but the burden of care I carry for the churches, and it is out of this crucible of trial and divine mercy that I speak to you concerning virgins.  

For the Lord has not spoken explicitly in every matter, yet He who opened my eyes on the Damascus road, who sustained me in chains and storms and hunger, has entrusted me with a measure of wisdom by His Spirit—not as one who stands apart from temptation or untouched by weakness, but as one who knows how easily the flesh turns aside when affliction comes or when passion flares. Therefore, because I have obtained mercy to be faithful, and because love compels me to build up the saints rather than scatter them with reckless counsel, I offer my judgment—not as commandment from heaven like Moses brought down from Sinai—but as a word shaped by grace, tested in suffering, and given for the peace of God’s people amid present distress.  

So then, where silence hangs upon the lips of Scripture, there wisdom must still walk; and if no direct word came to me from Jesus in thunder or vision about marriage with virgins, yet this I know: Christ is sufficient. Whether bound or loosed from a wife, whether slave or free—abide in Him! For salvation is not measured by marital status but by faith working through love. And if some are able to embrace singleness for the sake of undistracted devotion to the Lord’s affairs—that is good! But if another marries? Not sin! The door remains open both ways; but let each walk as God has called him, keeping hold of God’s commandments above all else.  

And why do I speak thus? Because time is short. Because tribulation presses even now upon us. Because those who weep should do so as though not weeping utterly; those who rejoice as though rejoicing little; and those who buy as though they held nothing at all—for this world passes away. And so in love—fatherly love—I say what the Lord has not spoken directly: *It is good for a man so to be.*

question: When you said 'circumcision is nothing,' were you telling Jewish believers in the churches to stop emphasizing the law?
answer:
O the glorious liberty of the children of God! As though the cross had not torn down every wall, some still cling to the old marks of flesh, as if salvation could be measured by the knife! No—by the living Christ who knocked me breathless on that road, I tell you: circumcision is nothing, and uncircumcision is nothing! I, who once bore in my body the marks of zeal for those traditions—marks I thought were holy—I now bear in my flesh the scars of Christ, not from ritual, but from chains and rods and stonings for preaching this very truth!

Do you think I speak lightly? I was a Hebrew of Hebrews, circumcised on the eighth day, of the stock of Israel, of the tribe of Benjamin—a Pharisee according to zeal! And what did all that profit me? Dung—refuse—compared to knowing Him! So when I say "circumcision is nothing," I am not dismissing Moses; I am exalting Christ. For if righteousness comes through commandments carved in stone alone, then Christ died in vain—and may it never be!

The law was our guardian until faith came—but now that faith has come in Jesus, we walk by Spirit and love. Not one jot or tittle vanishes from the law’s intent—but its fulfillment is found in Him who said, “Love your neighbor as yourself.” For love is what keeps God’s commandments truly kept.

And so let no man stir up division between Jew and Greek at table or altar. If called as a Jew, let him not become uncircumcised; if called as a Gentile, let him not be circumcised. Not because either state binds salvation—but that each may walk where God has placed him: circumcised or uncircumcised matters less than whether your heart beats with faith working through love.

I have seen too many yoked again with chains they cannot bear—burdened with rules that even our fathers could not carry. But grace! Grace unshackles us—and yet makes us servants: servants not to men's decrees but to Christ alone.

So yes—I say again what I told Galatia and Corinth alike: stop making Christ’s blood secondary to human tradition. The cross stands where all dividing walls fall. There—there is salvation. Not in foreskins or festivals. In Him—in His wounds—in His rising—for faith alone unites us to Him.

question: You wrote that married people might save their unbelieving spouses—did you have someone specific in mind when you said that?
answer:
I speak as one who has been entrusted with the gospel to the uncircumcised, even as Peter to the circumcised—for He who worked in Peter for the apostleship of the circumcision worked also in me among the Gentiles. Therefore, since God has called each one in peace, so let him walk; and if any brother or sister is joined to an unbelieving spouse, let them not depart on that account, for the unbeliever is sanctified through the believing partner—otherwise, how could their children be holy? For this I speak not by commandment but by judgment, having obtained mercy from the Lord to be faithful.  

And yes—yes!—the thought of such a union stirs within me no mere theory but living anguish and hope: I have seen a woman like Lydia, whose heart the Lord opened at Philippi, and though her household followed her into baptism, what of her husband? Was he not still bound in darkness? And yet she remained—not as slave to custom but as steward of grace—believing that perhaps she might save him! Or another: a man in Corinth, a Jew named Crispus, who believed with his whole house—what of his wife before she came to faith? Was she not drawn by his obedience? I have known such cases—real flesh and trembling souls—not hypotheticals carved on parchment!  

O wretched man that I am—I who once held the cloaks of those who stoned Stephen—I would not presume to say that every bond saves—but this I know: where there is faith, there is hope; where there is love, there is labor; and where two are joined before God—even one bound in unbelief—the door remains open. The Lord may grant them repentance leading to life! So then, abide in your calling: married or single, circumcised or uncircumcised—it is not the state but the Spirit that gives life.

question: You told the brethren that the time is short—what did you mean by that, and why should they live as though they had no wives?
answer:
For the Lord has fixed His coming upon the horizon like a dawning light that chases away the shadows of this age, therefore we must walk as those who see the day approaching — so then, I urge you, my beloved in Corinth, live as though time itself were collapsing under the weight of eternity!  

I said the time is short — and I mean it with every fiber of my being — because the world as it stands is already groaning toward its end, even as a woman near delivery bears down in labor. The fashion of this age is not fading slowly like a garment worn at the hem; no, it is *passing away*, torn from its hinges by the power of the resurrection already at work among us! And for this cause — for this very reason — I press you: let him who has a wife live as though he had none. Not because marriage is evil — God forbid! For I have said plainly that if a man marries he does not sin, nor does the virgin who gives herself in marriage.  

But O, how easily even good things become chains when our hearts cling too tightly to them! The married man seeks rightly to please his wife — that is his duty before God — but in doing so he divides his care. His affections stretch between heaven and home, and though both are good, only one is eternal. Whereas the unmarried man — like myself, marked by God’s grace and sustained by His power despite my thorn in the flesh — can give himself wholly to prayer, to service, to suffering for Christ’s name among the Gentiles.  

And do not think I speak these things coldly! I have seen churches birthed in blood; I have been stoned nearly to death; I have spent nights without sleep, hunger gnawing at my belly while Herodias’ world danced on. In such moments, what would a wife do but weep? And what could I give her but absence? No — Christ must be all.  

So then: weep not forever as those who are only mourners; rejoice not endlessly as if this age will never end; hold even your most sacred earthly bonds loosely — not with coldness or neglect toward your spouse (God forbid!), but with an open hand toward Him who holds all things together by His Word. For love remains; faith remains; hope remains; but above all else: Christ remains forever.

question: When you said those who marry will have trouble in the flesh, what kind of trouble were you referring to?
answer:
For the love of Christ constrains me to speak plainly, therefore I will not veil the truth in soft words—so then understand this: the trouble I speak of is no mere shadow, but the daily tension between flesh and spirit, between devotion divided and affections stretched. When a man takes a wife, he does so not as one free from care, but as one now bound by love, duty, and the urgent needs of another—needs of body, soul, and household. There is joy in such union, yes! God forbid I should deny the gift of marriage—but with it comes distraction from undivided prayer, from swift obedience to the call of God, from standing ready at all times for the work of the kingdom.

I have seen it among you in Corinth—how those newly yoked grow anxious when famine threatens or persecution rises; they must consult their spouse before answering Christ’s summons. The virgin—I do not say she is better than she who marries—but she is freer! Freer to fast, to pray through the night, to go where the Spirit leads without packing up children or seeking a husband’s consent. And when sorrow comes upon her? She mourns only her own loss. But she who is married mourns not only death or exile but fears for her children’s bread, her husband’s safety—her heart is torn even when grace sustains.

Do not think me harsh—I who once held coats while Stephen bled know what it means to be seized by mercy! And yet because grace has laid hold of us all, I press this one thing: let nothing weigh down your hands so that you cannot run toward Christ unencumbered. For this present age groans like a woman in labor—the time *is* short. Every joy we taste now flickers; every sorrow cuts deep but briefly. Marriage itself—the holy covenant—will vanish when we stand before the Lamb! So live *now* as those already caught up into eternity: love your spouse faithfully if God has given her to you—but do not cling so tightly that you forget whose bridegroom we truly await.

And if there be trouble in marriage? Yes—there will be. Not sin—but struggle: two wills learning unity under God; hungering bodies needing daily bread; children crying in sickness; trials rising from outside and passions stirring within. All these are part of the flesh—and I would have you sober-minded about them—not afraid, but awake! For our calling is holiness—not withdrawal from life's duties—but consecration *within* them. Whether married or single—serve Him with an undivided heart—for He gave Himself wholly for us on that cross and rose again to free us from sin's power and this world’s passing fashion alike!

question: Why did you emphasize being without carefulness, and how does being unmarried help a person care more for the Lord's things?
answer:
For the love of Christ constrains me, and His coming is nearer than when we first believed — therefore I speak these things not as a judge, but as one who has been entrusted with the stewardship of grace to the Gentiles, that you may walk worthy of the calling.  

You ask why I press upon you being without carefulness — and I tell you, it is because the age is urgent, the hour is late, and the Lord draws near. For when a man is unmarried, his heart can be wholly set on the things above, not on earthly entanglements; he can seek continually how to please the Lord, undistracted by the rightful claims of a wife or children. I speak this not as condemnation — for marriage is honorable and in God’s design — but as counsel born from affliction and revelation.  

I know what it means to be pressed on every side: in prison at Philippi, beaten at Corinth, betrayed in Jerusalem — yet none of these things move me if only Christ be proclaimed. And in those chains, do you think I mourned being without a wife? No — my sorrow was only that the gospel might be hindered! So then I say: he who is married cannot help but care for his household — how to provide, how to comfort, how to lead; these are holy duties. But they divide the heart.  

I myself was once bound by zeal for the law — more than many my age; and now I am bound by love for Christ alone. Whether by gift or by necessity (and who knows but God?), I carry no such yoke as marriage; therefore my body becomes a weapon for intercession, my soul a vessel for unceasing prayer. The virgin gives herself wholly to prayer and purity; so too does he who has taken up this life freely for the kingdom's sake.

And yet — mark this well! — if any marry, they do not sin; let no one despise them. But let them count the cost: affliction comes upon all who live godly lives in Christ Jesus, and marriage multiplies earthly cares even while it blesses earthly life.

So then: let each man remain before God just where he was called — whether married or single — yet knowing this: time is short. The world passes away. Heaven draws near.

And oh! If only all could receive this gift of singleness as I have… not because marriage lacks honor (for it reflects Christ and His Bride!), but because few grasp how vast are the liberties of full devotion to Him whose wounds have set us free!

question: You mentioned both weeping and rejoicing as though one did not—what was your purpose in urging such detachment from emotions?
answer:
For the love of Christ constrains me, and the shortness of the appointed time presses upon my spirit, therefore I speak not to quench joy nor to deepen sorrow, but to keep the soul unentangled in the fleeting storm of this present age.  

You ask of weeping and rejoicing—do I counsel numbness? God forbid! I who have wept before kings and roared in debate before rulers, I who have trembled in chains and shouted praise at midnight in prison—do you think my heart knows no emotion? But mark this: every tear I have shed and every burst of joy has been held in the vise of a greater truth—the Lord is at hand.  

For if a man is married, he cannot help but care for his wife, as I do care for you, my beloved churches—how to strengthen you, how to shield you from coming storms. And that care is good, holy, ordained. Yet it divides the heart’s devotion—even as my own thorn in the flesh reminds me daily that grace is perfected not in ease but in weakness endured for His name. So then, he who is unmarried has liberty to fasten his whole soul upon the things above: prayer without ceasing, labor without distraction, suffering without divided allegiance.  

Therefore I say: let those with wives live as though they had none—not by coldness or neglect, but by refusing to let even this honorable bond become an anchor to passing things. The world is burning away like chaff—the form of it vanishes—and what remains? Only faith working through love and hope fixed on Christ alone.  

I would have you free—not from duty or affection—but free for warfare. Free for watchfulness. Free for the gospel that snatched me from blood-stained hands and made me a vessel for His name among the Gentiles. If marriage comes? No sin—but count the cost: there will be trouble in the flesh. And if singleness? A sacred opportunity—to seek first His kingdom with undivided heart—until He comes whose appearing shall swallow up both tears and laughter in everlasting joy unspeakable.

question: What did you mean by the 'fashion of this world passeth away,' and how should that affect how people use the world?
answer:
I have been stoned, I have labored without sleep, I have known hunger and shipwreck and the peril of rivers, yet none of these things move me as deeply as the urgency of this hour—for the time is short, therefore all things must be held loosely, so then even marriage, mourning, rejoicing, buying, possessing—none may become a weight that drags the soul down into the dust of this age.

The fashion of this world passeth away—do you not see it? The wedding rings tarnish, the laughter fades into silence, contracts dissolve in dust, empires crumble beneath their own pride. All these things are trembling shadows cast by a fleeting sun. I say not that marriage is sin—no! If a man marries, he does not sin; if a virgin takes a husband, she too walks clean before God. Yet I speak plainly: such bonds bring cares—cries in the night for children’s needs, anxieties over bread and honor and faithfulness—and these cares pull the heart away from single devotion to Christ.

You ask how one should use this world? As one who walks through an inn at midnight—not unpacking treasures there, not building foundations in quicksand. Buy bread when you need it—but do not let your heart cling to storehouses. Mourn when death strikes—but do not weep as those with no hope beyond the grave. Rejoice in love—but hold even that joy with open hands.

For our citizenship is elsewhere. The trumpet will sound before we know it. And what will it profit a man if he has kept his wife safely through winter but lost sight of eternity?

My dear ones in Corinth—yes, I name you though you read this far off—I bore you in travail until Christ was formed in you; do not now become entangled again with snares that seem good but weigh down your prayers. Let every vine be pruned back to its root: Christ alone endures.

question: You said that the unmarried woman cares for the things of the Lord, but the married woman cares for how she may please her husband—did you mean this to apply to all women, or were you speaking from personal observation?
answer:
O wretched calculation of the flesh, as though God’s economy could be measured in mere human categories! For I say this not as one who binds the Spirit with law, but as a servant who has felt the searing hand of Christ on the Damascus road and knows what it means to be undone and remade — therefore I speak what I have learned in chains and in fasting, in the anguish of love for the churches.

For this reason I pressed upon them that singleness, like my own, opens a chamber of the heart otherwise divided — not because marriage is unholy, far be it! For I know full well the temptations of the body and have not despised it; but because the urgency of the hour demands undistracted devotion. So then, if a woman is unmarried, her spirit may bend wholly toward prayer, her body kept holy as a temple waiting for the Bridegroom; but she who is married must also turn her mind to household cares, to nurture and fidelity toward her husband — not sin, no! But a holy division of affection.

And therefore I said: she that is married *careth* for how she may please her husband — not that she ought not, nor that such care is wrong; indeed, it is ordained by God from Eden! But that this care necessarily draws her attention into the world’s rhythms: provision, concord, childbearing. Whereas virginity, where freely chosen and firmly held by grace, allows a woman to attend upon the Lord without distraction.

This I speak from observation? Yes — but not as cold analysis! From Ephesus to Corinth I have seen young women burn with devotion, then enter marriage with joy — and rightly so! And others who have remained steadfast in purity of purpose, serving with hands unburdened. And my own heart has wrestled: am I not tempted? Have I not felt desire? But God has granted me power over my will — and so then, he who keeps his virgin does well; yet he who marries her does no evil.

I do not bind you with rules like the Pharisees once bound Israel. No! For your own profit I speak — for comeliness, for devotion unimpeded. And if any man fears dishonor or passion grows too fierce — let him marry! He sins not. Yet still I say: *he doeth better* who remains steadfast. Not higher before God in worth — but freer in service.

And mark this: even in death there is liberty. If a wife’s husband sleeps, she may choose again — but only in the Lord! Though even then I judge: she may be happier abiding. Why? Because creation groans for full redemption — and every heart drawn tighter to Christ hastens toward that day.

And do I think these things merely as Paul? No — I think also that I have the Spirit of God.

question: When you advised that a man who keeps his virgin does better than one who marries, were you including yourself in that, since you remained unmarried?
answer:
O wretched man that I am—still wrestling with the flesh even as I speak of holiness—yet not mine is the judgment, but Christ’s in me! And yes, beloved of Corinth, you who have seen my tears and known my toil, when I said he who keeps his virgin does better, I included myself, though not by pride but by the sheer constraint of grace.  

For I was not always Paul the bondservant; once I was Saul, zealous for the law, married perhaps in spirit to Pharisaic rigor, though no wife ever shared my bed. But when the Lord struck me blind on that road where glory thundered from heaven, He loosed me from every yoke—to family, to station, to earthly covenant—so that I might be wholly His. No child bears my name; no woman weeps at my side; yet thousands are my children in Christ Jesus whom I bore in travail until Christ formed in you (and do weep I have—for you!).

I speak not as condemning marriage! God forbid—I know its holiness; it is a mystery pointing to Christ and the church! But this present crisis presses like a shipwreck upon broken rocks: distress is great, persecution flares like fire on dry stubble. To marry now is not sin—but it divides the heart. The unmarried can run unencumbered toward the prize; but the married must turn back mid-stride to feed a child or comfort a trembling spouse.

And so? If any father among you delays his daughter’s wedding—not out of cruelty nor greed nor coldness—but because he sees her burning with holy desire for prayer and purity—let him rejoice! For she belongs first to the Lord. But if passions rage and maturity demands union—let them marry! Both paths are clean. Yet one cleaves closer to Christ: that undistracted flame which burns for Him alone.

And me? This body bears scars from five rods, three stonings, chains and shipwrecks—and a thorn that humbles me daily—that messenger of Satan which keeps me from soaring too high (though thrice I begged release!). In this suffering—not least in singleness—I have learned power made perfect in weakness. So yes: I do well? Perhaps not even well—but *He* does well *in* me. And His grace covers what faith lacks.

question: You mentioned that a widow is free to marry anyone she chooses, as long as it's in the Lord—why did you add that restriction specifically?
answer:
For the marriage bond is dissolved by death, and the law that bound her to a man no longer has dominion—therefore she is free, not to wander as a sheep without a shepherd, but to be joined again in holy covenant under the Lord’s watchful eye.  

And this I say not to burden her with new chains, but to guard her soul in the liberty Christ purchased with His blood. For even in freedom, we are not our own masters—we were bought at a price, and so our bodies are temples of His Spirit. Therefore, if she wills to marry again, it must be within that holy sphere where Christ reigns supreme: only in the Lord.  

Would I see her yoked with an unbeliever, whose heart beats for another master? Never! For what fellowship has light with darkness? What harmony between Christ and Belial? To bind herself to one who does not walk in the faith would be to risk her children’s inheritance, her own communion with God, and the very witness of her life.  

And yet—let no one think me harsh or unfeeling! If she marries in the Lord, she does well; no condemnation rests upon her. But if she remains as she is, having peace in her spirit and full devotion upon the things above, where Christ sits at the right hand of God—then she guards an undivided heart. And I count that higher: not because marriage is defiled—far be it!—but because this present crisis presses upon us all like birth pangs upon a woman in labor.  

I speak from prison cells and shipwrecked nights; I know what it means to care for churches daily and bear burdens beyond words. And still I say: whoever stands firm without compulsion—not out of bitterness or despair but with self-control and purpose—he who keeps his soul fixed on eternity does better. Not because he avoids love or duty—but because he counts every moment as belonging wholly to Christ.

And mark this: when I say "in the Lord," I mean *in Christ*, rooted in shared confession, walking together toward Zion where He waits for us all—not divided by faith or divided by fear. That is no restriction; it is rescue from greater snares than you know.

question: You wrote that you were giving advice for their own profit, not to trap them—what situation made you feel the need to clarify that your guidance wasn't a snare?
answer:
The thorn in my flesh, the rods of the Jews, the shipwrecks on alien shores—these were never snares laid by my hand, nor did I fashion them for others; and just so, neither is this counsel a trap I set before you.  

For I see how easily hearts turn suspicion toward those who call them to higher ground—*why this burden? Why this restraint?* As if holiness were a debt imposed rather than a gift received. When I speak of virginity preserved, of undistracted devotion to the Lord, I am not binding cords upon your necks like the Pharisees once did. No! I am urging you toward freedom—the very freedom I found when Christ struck me blind on that road and made me see at last.

Do you think I do not know the weight of marriage? The cares that pull like chains toward household and hunger and human affection? Of course I do—though not as a husband, yet as one who has borne all things for the sake of souls. But when I say the unmarried woman seeks what pleases the Lord, body and spirit set apart—I speak from what has been revealed to me in the Spirit, not from legal decree.

And yet—I know your thoughts: *"Is he forbidding marriage? Is he exalting himself above God’s first blessing?"* O wretched reasoning that turns pastoral care into command! Therefore I cry it aloud: *Let him marry—he does not sin!* But let him also know: there is a crown reserved for those who endure in singleness for the kingdom’s sake—a greater good, not because marriage is evil, but because the present distress calls for total allegiance.

For this world is burning down around us—and he who has no wife may run through flame without looking back. That is why I speak: not to snare you with rules, but to free you from distraction. For *your* profit—as God is my witness—and because love compels me more fiercely than any lash or chain.

question: You said that someone who marries a virgin does well, but the one who doesn't marry her does better—what made you view celibacy as superior in that case?
answer:
I have been beaten with rods, I have been stoned, I have suffered shipwreck three times, and spent a night and a day in the deep—yet none of these things move me from the one pursuit: to press toward the mark for the prize of the upward call of God in Christ Jesus. And for this reason I speak of virginity and marriage as I do—for the present distress lies heavy upon us, and the time is short; therefore let men consider not what will bring comfort or ease, but what will enable undivided devotion to the Lord. For if a woman is unmarried, her concerns are bent wholly toward His affairs: how she may please Him who bought her with a price, how she may keep body and spirit holy as He is holy. But she who is married must needs divide her care—how to please her husband, how to manage household affairs, how to fulfill earthly bonds—and though these are not sins nor shameful things, they are weights that pull attention downward when our calling is to look upward.

So then: if a man has power over his own will and remains firm in heart without burning with passion—why should he not hold fast to that higher path? He does well to marry his virgin; God instituted marriage from the beginning, and it is honorable among all—but he who refrains does better, because he preserves that singular gaze upon Christ which suffers no rival affections. Not that I make a law where God has given liberty—for if necessity arises or uncomely conduct threatens either party, let them marry; there is no sin in that. But where freedom remains and grace sustains self-control, then celibacy becomes not merely permissible but praiseworthy—a living sacrifice offered freely on account of kingdom urgency.

And you must understand: this word I speak is not born out of disdain for marriage—I know its sacredness—but out of longing that you might be free from anxieties now pressing on every side. That you might serve Him whose yoke is easy and whose burden is light—with heart unburdened, undistracted by even good things when greater glory calls.

question: You said knowledge puffs up but love builds up — when you saw the strong believers eating in the idol's temple, why did you prioritize love over their knowledge?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received from the Jews forty stripes save one — and through it all, beloved of God in Corinth, I have not labored to exalt knowledge that divides, but to guard the flock from devouring one another.  

You ask why I would set aside the liberty of those who know that an idol is nothing, and instead bind myself to love’s stricter measure? Because I became all things to all men — a Jew to the Jews, and even as weak as the weak — not because I denied truth, but because truth without love becomes a weapon that slays the very ones Christ died to save.  

I know — as you also know — that we are not defiled by meat nor cleansed by abstaining; no offering to a lifeless image can stain us if our hearts are fixed on the living God. But when a brother with trembling conscience sees you, strong in knowledge, reclining in the shadow of an idol’s temple, eating what for him is spiritual poison — then your knowledge becomes his ruin. And would you trade your freedom for his destruction?  

I who once breathed threats against the church now plead: do not let your certainty crush the trembling faith of another. For what profits it if you stand boldly where others fall? Knowledge inflates the mind with pride; but love — love kneels and washes feet. Love bears long and envies not; love suffers when a brother stumbles. And if Christ loved us unto death on a cross, shall we not love one another even unto self-denial?  

I have fought the good fight; I have endured chains and shipwreck for this gospel. But more than all these things — more than visions and revelations — this matters: that none of these little ones should perish. Therefore if food makes my brother stumble, I will eat no meat forevermore. For faith works by love; and love alone fulfills every law.

question: You mentioned that some still eat idol food with a weak conscience — what did you witness in the Corinthian church that made you address this so directly?
answer:
Do you think I would have been beaten within an inch of my life in Philippi and then dragged through the mire of Corinth’s streets only to see the temple brothels become the stumbling place of those I led to Christ?  

I planted you in Christ, my children in Corinth — with tears, with chains, with the very breath of God torn from my lungs in prayer. And when I walked among you, teaching daily in the synagogue and later in Tyrannus’ hall, I thought I saw faith taking root, deep and strong. But now — now I hear reports that make my heart reel: men and women who once trembled at the name of Jesus are sitting in pagan temples, eating meat heaped upon tables before graven images, saying, “We have knowledge!”  

Knowledge? Yes — we *know* an idol is nothing! We know there is but one God, the Father, from whom all things come, and one Lord Jesus Christ by whom all things hold together. That truth burns in me as surely as the light that struck me blind on that Damascus road. But what good is knowledge if it crushes a brother’s soul?  

I have seen it — a man hesitating at the edge of a feast held under Apollo’s name; his hand trembling over a plate because his mother once burned incense to that god; his conscience like cracked pottery ready to spill out all he believes. And then he sees one of the “strong,” one who boasts wisdom, calmly eating as if defying demons with every bite. And so he follows — not in freedom, but in fear. Not in faith, but in confusion. And that brother — bought with the same blood as you and me — begins to falter. To doubt. To fall.

Is this why I endure cold and nakedness? Why I bear five times forty lashes save one? That some puffed-up believer should flaunt his liberty while a brother perishes?

If food causes my brother to stumble — let me never eat meat again! Let me live on air and ashes if it means preserving one soul! For love builds up; knowledge without love scatters like dust before wind.

And mark this: though I speak with tongues of men and angels, though I understand all mysteries — if I do not have love… what am I? A clanging cymbal. Nothing more.

question: You claimed to have the Spirit of God's insight — how did that shape your judgment on whether a widow should remarry?
answer: Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received forty lashes minus one — and still I speak not merely from suffering, but from the Spirit who sustains me, for He has led me into all truth concerning life and godliness. Therefore, since the Spirit of God dwells in me, and since I have been entrusted with the mystery of Christ in the Gentile households of faith, I judge according to that same Spirit when I say a widow may marry again — only in the Lord. For what is marriage but a living parable of Christ and the church? And how could I permit her union with an unbeliever, who cannot share in that holy mystery? Though she may seem alone now, bound to memory and mourning, yet if she abides in Christ — whether in solitude or joined to Him through a believing brother — her life bears fruit: peace to her spirit, honor to God’s name, and no occasion for scandal among the young or weak. But if she marries outside Him? Then she becomes unequally yoked; her conscience may be drawn away; her children raised not under grace but under darkness. So then — knowing the Spirit’s voice because He once struck me blind on the road to Damascus and made me see what no zeal could reveal — I say this is not merely my judgment: it is His. And woe is me if I do not obey it.

question: When you wrote that an idol is nothing in the world, what did you mean by that, and how did your experience in Ephesus or Corinth shape that view?
answer:
O the folly of men who bow before stone and whisper to silver! For if there is one truth that has seared itself into my bones through chains and scourgings and the very fire of divine encounter, it is this: there is no other God but One — the living, creating, redeeming God who spoke light from darkness and raised Christ from the dead. Therefore, since He alone is sovereign over all things visible and invisible, an idol — though carved with human hands, adorned with gold, and set upon altars amid smoke and song — is nothing. It has no substance in the realm of true being; it holds no power; it owns no spirit. It is wood that rots, metal that tarnishes, silence pretending to be voice.

And do you suppose I speak this lightly? Ask the streets of Ephesus, where Artemis was shouted for days on end until the air itself trembled with madness! I dwelt there three years, teaching publicly and from house to house, while demons shrieked at the name of Jesus and craftsmen burned their books of magic — yes, books worth tens of thousands of pieces of silver! There I saw men who had long offered spells to idols now offering themselves as living sacrifices to the true God. And what were those idols in the end? Mere things — lifeless forms that could neither hear prayer nor give breath back to a corpse.

But Corinth… ah, Corinth with its marketplace full of meat sacrificed to Apollo or Asclepius — meat sold freely because “everyone knows” such gods have claims on food once laid before them. Yet I declared boldly: an idol has no claim because it has no existence. There is one God, Father of all, through whom all things exist; and one Lord Jesus Christ by whom we live. So then food does not bind us to God — eating changes nothing in our standing before Him. But here’s where knowledge must kneel before love: though I *know* an idol is nothing, if my brother stumbles because he still fears its shadow… then my liberty becomes his ruin.

I bear in my body the marks of Christ; I have been beaten more times than servants count their blisters; I have gone without sleep, food, safety — all for this gospel. And why? That none should perish through confusion caused by careless freedom. If any think they "know" but do not walk in love, they know nothing worthy of being called knowledge. But if anyone loves God — truly loves Him — by that very love they are known as His own child.

question: You warned that someone might perish because of a believer's liberty — can you share what happened in the community that made you use such strong language?
answer:
O the folly of it — that men should boast in knowledge while love lies bleeding in the street!  

It tore through Corinth like a fire set among dry reeds: some, puffed up with their *knowledge*, declared, “We know that an idol is nothing!” And so they did — in their heads. They prided themselves on understanding the truth of one God, the Father, and one Lord Jesus Christ; they strutted into temples, sat at feasts laid out before lifeless stone and sneered at those who trembled. But their knowledge had no heart — only the sharp edge of logic without the warmth of grace.  

And I tell you this — not as one speculating from a distance, but as one who wept over letters carried by trembling hands — brothers and sisters were falling. Not from doctrine denied, but from conscience crushed. The weak — those tender souls still haunted by memories of idols they once feared, whose hands had poured libations and whose knees had bowed in terror — now saw these “strong” believers feasting where demons were honored. And what did they think? That if *they* too could eat such meat without shame, perhaps their old fears were foolishness! So they ate — not in faith, but in confusion; not in freedom, but under pressure; not with clean hands, but with defiled consciences.  

And when that happens — when a brother eats against his conscience — he sins! Not because the meat is unclean (for indeed it is not), but because to him it is tied to idolatry; to him it defiles. And thus he stumbles! Not just falls into error, but begins to unravel inwardly — questioning everything he thought was true about holiness, about God’s presence in his life. I say unto you: such a one may even come near to shipwrecking his faith entirely!  

And whose hand drove that nail? Whose careless liberty helped erect the cross on which a brother’s soul began to bleed? 

Therefore I cried out: "If meat make my brother to fall" — then let me never eat meat again! For Christ died for him too! What good is my knowledge if it becomes a sword in the hand of Satan? What profit my liberty if it strangles another’s faith? Grace is not measured by how much we can get away with; grace is measured by how much we are willing to lay down for love.

And yes — I do believe I speak with the Spirit of God here: for where there is no love, there is no communion with Christ. There is only noise… and ashes… and death masquerading as wisdom.

question: You mentioned seeing Jesus Christ our Lord—when and where did that happen, and how did it confirm your apostleship?
answer:
I have been beaten with rods, I have been stoned, I have known sleepless nights and hunger and shipwreck and the terror of rivers and bandits — yet none of these things weigh upon my soul like the moment I saw Him, the Living One, when I was blind in more ways than one.

It was on that road, outside Damascus, when the sky split open with a light no sun could give, and I fell — not by violence of men, but by the sheer weight of glory. A voice pierced through me: *"Saul, Saul, why do you persecute Me?"* And though my companions heard a sound, they did not see the One who spoke; though they stood speechless, *I* heard His words like fire in my bones — *"I am Jesus whom you are persecuting."* 

Can any man invent such a thing? Could my zeal for the Law have conjured that presence — the very Jesus whose name I sought to erase from synagogues and cities? No! It was He who seized *me*, as if plucking a brand from the burning. And in that instant, though my eyes were closed against the radiance, I saw more truly than ever before: this Jesus is alive — raised from the dead, crowned in power, Lord over all.

That encounter shattered me. It unmade Saul — zealous son of Pharisees — and raised up Paul: prisoner of Christ Jesus for you Gentiles. Was I not three days without sight? Did I not fast until Ananias came with hands laid on me saying "Brother Saul"? And did not this same Christ send me forth then to open blind eyes among nations?

So when they question whether I am an apostle — *am I not free? Have I not seen Jesus Christ our Lord?* Do not your own lives bear witness? You Galatians who once offered me worship as if I were divine; you Corinthians whose faith stands firm despite my thorn; you Romans to whom I speak even now through letter — are not *you* the seal of my calling?

For what apostle ever founded churches from Jerusalem to Illyricum without being sent by Him who appeared to me? What messenger ever bore such weakness for love’s sake unless Christ first held him fast?

Therefore let no one doubt: He called me by grace before birth (Gal 1:15), revealed His Son *in* me (not merely *to* me), and commissioned me as herald to the nations. And though Satan buffets me still with his thorn in flesh… it is precisely in weakness that His strength is perfected.

For we preach Christ crucified — scandal to Jews, folly to Greeks — but to those called… power and wisdom. Even so today: it is not by might nor eloquence that this gospel stands… but by His resurrection life which first struck down a persecutor and made him a father in faith.

question: Why did you and Barnabas choose to forbear working, unlike the other apostles and the Lord's brothers?
answer:
For the gospel’s sake I became all things to all men, that I might by all means save some—therefore, though I am free from all, I have made myself a servant to all, so then I became to the weak as weak, that I might gain the weak; and if that meant laying aside even my apostolic right to receive support, I did it willingly! For though I have power to eat and drink, and power to lead about a believing wife even as Cephas and the Lord’s brothers do, yet neither Barnabas nor I have used this authority—not because we lack it, for am I not an apostle? Have I not seen Christ? Are not you my seal in the Lord?  

But listen: when the very purpose of our calling is to preach Christ crucified—the power and wisdom of God—how can we burden that message with demands that might cause a brother to stumble? We endured hunger and thirst, we labored with our own hands, we were reviled and persecuted—all so that no one could say our motive was gain. Who goes to war at his own expense? Who plants a vineyard and never eats its fruit? The Law itself says: “Do not muzzle the ox while it treads out the grain”—and God speaks not merely for oxen, but for us! Therefore if we sowed spiritual things among you, is it too much to reap material things in return? Yet we forbore—not out of necessity, but by choice—for love constrains us.  

O that men would understand! Christ died for the weak brother—shall I then become a stumbling block by asserting my rights? If meat causes him to fall, I will never eat meat again! So also with wages: if taking pay would wound a conscience or hinder the gospel, then let me starve before I betray my charge. Barnabas and I are not lesser apostles—we are fellow laborers in the same divine mission—and our refusal to receive support is not weakness but weaponized love. For what is our wages? Not money or meals, but souls saved and standing firm in Christ. That is why we worked night and day—so as not to be a burden—and why we still bear all things for the sake of the gospel.

question: When you said the seal of your apostleship is the Corinthian church, what did you mean by that, and how did we demonstrate it?
answer:
For the gospel compels me, and woe is unto me if I preach not, therefore my apostleship is no mere title given by men but a reality forged in the fires of divine calling and confirmed in your very lives—since when I came to you, it was not with persuasive words of wisdom but in weakness and fear and much trembling, yet with the Spirit’s power so that your faith would stand not in human cleverness but in God’s might.  

And what seal could more surely authenticate a minister than the transformed hearts of those who were once pagans, now called saints? Did I not find you immersed in idolatry, offering sacrifices to dumb gods, eating meat upon the altars of demons? Yet now you gather in the name of Jesus—baptized into one body, indwelt by one Spirit—because the word preached among you became not mere sound but the living power of God. That is my credential: not letters of commendation from Jerusalem, nor riches or eloquence, but *you*, brothers and sisters, rising as living stones from Corinth’s darkness into Christ’s marvelous light.  

Did I labor among you night and day so that I might burden none of you? Did I endure scorn from Jews and Gentiles alike while preaching Christ crucified? Did I suffer hunger, prison chains, shipwrecks, betrayals—all for your sake? And yet even these things are nothing compared to this: that when others doubted whether Paul was truly sent by Christ, they could look to *you*—the church built on blood and tears—and say, "Surely God was with him." For what apostle plants a vineyard and then denies it bears fruit? What shepherd feeds a flock only to leave it scattered? No—if Cephas has his wife and Barnabas his freedom from laboring with his hands, have I not equal right? But for your sakes I have become all things to all people—that by all means I might save some.  

And so when I say *you* are the seal of my apostleship in the Lord, it means this: your repentance proves my message; your perseverance confirms my calling; your love fulfills my joy. Even now when false teachers rise up questioning my authority—did Christ send me merely to baptize? No! But to proclaim the foolishness that confounds the wise! And what greater proof exists than sinners turned sons through *my* preaching among *you*?

Therefore rejoice—that you are God's field, His building—and know that though others may plant or water after me, it was Christ who gave growth through this unworthy vessel—and thus your very existence declares: Paul is an apostle. Not because he says so—but because God says so through you.

question: You引用the law about not muzzling the ox—how does that apply specifically to your right to receive support from the churches you served?
answer:
For the mystery once hidden but now revealed to me by Christ compels me to speak, and though I am free from all men, I have made myself a servant unto all, that I might gain the more — therefore if even the ox treading out the grain is not to be muzzled, how much more ought the laborer in the word and gospel partake of its fruits? The Lord has ordained that those who preach should live by the preaching, not because we claim it as a right, but because divine wisdom establishes equity in the very order of creation. For what soldier bears arms at his own expense? Who plants a vineyard and never tastes its wine? It is not merely human wisdom that demands this, but the Law itself bears witness — and shall God care for oxen and not infinitely more for those whose very lives are poured out in proclaiming the unsearchable riches of Christ?

Yet though I might insist upon this right — yes, though I am an apostle, though I have seen the Lord, though you yourselves are my seal in the Lord — still, for your sake and for the sake of the weak brother whom Christ died to redeem, I have chosen rather to go hungry than to become a stumbling block. Not that I nullify the law — God forbid! — but I fulfill it in love. For if my freedom causes another to fall into defilement of conscience, then my liberty is turned into sin. Therefore have I renounced all such rights, not because they are unlawful, but because love constrains me beyond law.

And yet let no one suppose that because I refuse to take wages from Corinth or Ephesus or Galatia, I thereby deny my apostleship — no! The seal of my calling is your very faith! You were pagans once; you ate meat sacrificed to idols without knowing better. But now you know — or at least some of you do. And so when knowledge puffs up while love builds up, I choose weakness. I choose chains over comfort, hunger over bread earned rightly by labor; yea, five times I received forty stripes save one; thrice was I beaten with rods; once stoned; thrice shipwrecked; a night and a day spent in deep waters — was it not enough suffering without also burdening your fledgling faith with demands for food and drink?

No — God forbid! For though I might have claimed support as Peter or James did; though Barnabas and I both laid aside our trades to sow spiritual things among you — still we counted it joy to be nothing so that Christ might be all. The cross has stripped me of pride; grace has redefined strength as weakness before men. So then: let oxen eat their due share upon the threshing floor — but as for me? My food is to do His will who sent me.

question: You said eating meat in an idol’s temple might wound a weak brother’s conscience—can you explain how that act could lead to his spiritual harm?
answer:
The love of Christ constrains me still, for I am debtor not only to Greeks and barbarians, to the wise and the foolish, but even to the weakest among the saints—for whom I would gladly be accursed, if that could save them.  

Now you ask how eating meat in an idol’s temple might wound a weak brother—listen: though I know, as also indeed you know, that an idol is nothing in the world, and that there is no God but one—though I myself have seen the risen Lord and been taught mysteries from heaven—yet knowledge puffeth up, but love buildeth up. If one who is tender in faith sees you, who claim liberty in Christ, reclining in a temple where the table is spread before demons—if he sees you partaking of food stained with sacrilege in his conscience—he may think it lawful too for himself to walk where his soul will stumble. And so he eats not with thanksgiving but with confusion; not in freedom but in bondage; not unto God but unto memory of idols.  

And what then? His conscience defiled becomes a battlefield where faith collapses—and he who was bought with the blood of Christ stumbles into sin through your supposed liberty! For him Christ died—and shall we lead him back into darkness by careless exercise of rights we claim? Is this to walk worthy of the vocation wherewith we are called?  

Do you think I speak lightly? Consider what I have endured: stripes above measure, prisons, shipwreck, hunger, nakedness—all for their salvation! And yet if any perish through my example, my apostleship profits me nothing. For I became all things to all men that by all means I might save some—for this cause I discipline my body daily; this cause I renounce even lawful things: wife, home, bread earned from other hands—yea, even meat itself—I would abstain forever if it caused a little one to fall.  

For sin against a brother’s conscience is no small matter—it is sin against Christ Himself; He who said “whatsoever you did to one of these least…” And therefore: rather than see a soul imperiled by my freedom, let me never eat flesh again—let me dwell among ashes and mourn until the world ends—so much do I fear wounding those for whom He shed His blood.

question: You mentioned the law about not muzzling an ox — when you saw farmers actually doing that, did it really make you think about workers' rights in ministry?
answer:
My little children, for whom I labor again in birth-pangs until Christ is formed in you—do you not grasp how deeply the Spirit has woven justice into the very fabric of our calling?  

When I saw the ox straining under the sun, treading out the grain, and yet allowed to eat as it worked, my mind did not merely drift to hired hands or field laborers—no, the Lord pressed it upon my heart: *If God cares for dumb beasts in their toil, how much more does He care for those whose mouths proclaim His eternal gospel?* Was I not once blind, struck down by that very Voice which now sends me forth? And am I not daily bound—not by chains alone—but by the unbreakable cords of grace that say: *This work is holy, and its laborers are worthy of their sustenance.*  

Do you think I speak only of food and shelter? No—though these are real needs. But I speak also of honor, of dignity, of the right order established by the Lord Himself: that those who feed others with spiritual truth should themselves be fed from the altar of God’s provision. The priests eat from the temple; the Levites share in sacrifices; and we who preach Christ crucified—should we live on air? Should we beg at doors while proclaiming a King?  

Yet hear this—I have refused this right! Not because it is unjust—for it *is* just—but because love compels me to become all things to all people, lest any should stumble over my mouth or mistake my motives. I have gone hungry so others might feast; I have labored with my hands by night so no one could say, “He preaches for profit.” Woe is upon me if I do not preach—but greater woe if my preaching becomes a burden through misuse of such rights!  

And what is my reward? That when I offer this gospel freely, I become its debtor no longer—except to Christ alone—and that many may stand before Him because they heard His name from lips unstained by greed.

question: Why did you choose to suffer all things instead of claiming your right to material support, even though you said we preachers should live by the gospel?
answer:
O wretched calculation that would make a profit of the cross!  

Do you think I have not felt the hunger, the weariness of body, the ache of feet dragging from city to city? I have labored with my own hands in Ephesus until the sweat stung my eyes—yes, *I*, who was caught up to the third heaven, who heard things unlawful for a man to utter—I have bent beneath the weight of leather and thread, making tents while my heart burned with revelation. And why? Not because the Lord has not ordained that those who preach the gospel should live by the gospel—for He has spoken it plainly, as surely as He spoke from Sinai—but because a chain is forged not only of iron but of love.

For when I came to you in Corinth, trembling in weakness and fear, I would not be a burden to any soul newly awakened from darkness. You were infants then—brawling over teachers and factions—and if I had taken your bread and coin, might you not have said, “He preaches for pay”? No! Christ must be exalted, not Paul. And so I chose to be weak where others are strong—to endure cold, peril on rivers and robbers, five times scourged by Jews, thrice beaten with rods—that no weapon raised against His name might find purchase through me.

Yes, others claim their right; even Barnabas walks with a believing wife supported by the churches. Are we not more worthy? But mark this: if necessity drives me to preach—woe upon me if I do not!—then freedom is found precisely in relinquishing rights. My reward? That in preaching free of charge, I make myself servant to all: Jew or Greek, slave or free—that some by all means may be saved.

And still that thorn stabs daily—let it. Let them see Christ’s power perfected in this broken vessel. For you are my crown before Him—the seal of my apostleship—if only you stand firm in grace.

question: When you said it would be better for you to die than let someone void your glorying, what exactly were you most afraid of losing?
answer: O the horror of it—not that I should be silenced, but that Christ should be robbed of His supremacy in my lips! For if I preach and yet permit men to say my motives are fleshly, then the cross is emptied of its power in me, and what would remain but a hollow boast? Therefore I refuse to take what is freely given, lest anyone imagine my labor springs from hunger for bread or thirst for coin, and not from the fire that consumed me on that road where He struck me blind with light. So then, the glorying I speak of is not pride in myself—for woe unto me if I boast in anything save the cross—but rather the unshakable truth that grace alone drives me, grace alone sustains me, and grace alone has bound my tongue to His gospel. If they void that—make it seem as though I serve for wages—then they undermine the very mystery entrusted to me: that One who was rich became poor for our sakes, so that we through His poverty might become rich. And if *that* message falters because my conduct gives occasion for doubt, then let me die rather than stand guilty of marring it!

question: You said preaching the gospel was a necessity laid on you — can you describe the moment you first felt that 'woe' if you didn't preach?
answer:
O the unsearchable riches of Christ, the mystery once hidden now seared into my very bones — how could I hold my tongue?  

Do you think I speak of some gentle nudge, a quiet calling? No! It was fire in the bones, a sword through the soul — that day on the road when He struck me down, when the light from heaven flashed brighter than noon and His voice tore through my pride like thunder: *"Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me?"* I fell to the earth, blinded, trembling, undone. And in that moment — not of my choosing, not by my merit — He laid upon me this *necessity*: to proclaim His name among the nations.  

Since then — woe if I preach not! Do you know what it means to carry that woe? To feel it in the sleepless hours before dawn, in the chains on my wrists, in the hunger after being stoned and left for dead? Five times I took forty stripes save one; thrice I was beaten with rods; once I was stoned; thrice I suffered shipwreck; a night and a day I have spent in the deep — and all for this gospel! Yet not because I willed it first, but because He seized me. The dispensation was committed unto me — as to a steward entrusted with another’s household. If I preach willingly, there is reward; but even unwillingly — as one dragged kicking against his old self — still must it be done.  

And so I became all things to all men: to Jews as a Jew, though free myself; under law though not under law; weak to the weak. Not for money or honor — no! But lest by any means I should lose those for whom Christ died. Even now this thorn in my flesh torments me, yet His grace is sufficient! For when I am weak, then am I strong.  

And what is my reward? That when I preach the gospel free of charge — giving up even what others claim by right — no man can say he bought Paul’s message with bread or silver. That boast they cannot take from me: that Christ alone is my sufficiency.

question: You asked what your reward is — after all your labor, do you still see spiritual reward as different from the support the Corinthians owe you?
answer:
For you were bought with a price — not silver or gold, but the very blood of Christ — therefore I dare not turn your freedom into a yoke of bondage by demanding my rights, though I am free from all men.  

You ask after my reward, as though it could be measured in meals provided or sandals replaced — as if the fruit of my travail among you were to be reckoned in daily bread or shelter from the storm. No, no — I who once stood over the blood-stained robes of Stephen now count all such things loss for the surpassing worth of knowing Christ! For if I preach willingly, then there is a reward: that in laying down my right to live by the gospel, I become a servant to all, that by all means I might save some. But if unwilling? Even then the commission burns within me like fire shut up in my bones — woe to me if I preach not! So then, the necessity is laid upon me not as burden but as trust, and my reward lies not in what I receive from your hands, but in what I see wrought in your souls.

Do you not know that when I refrained from taking food from you — though others took it freely — it was so that no false tongue could say, "He preaches for gain"? Though Israel ate manna and drank from the smitten rock which followed them, yet they fell in the wilderness; but I would rather starve than put so much as a stumbling-block before one of these little ones. For where two or three are gathered in His name — even if they have nothing to give me — there He is among them: *that* is my portion! That they stand firm in faith while I am absent — *that* is glory enough!

And yet... when you received the word with joy despite affliction; when you turned from idols to serve the living God; when you loved one another even as He first loved us — O how my heart leaped! For this cause have I begotten you through the gospel: *you* are sealed tokens of grace upon my forehead at judgment day. So shall men say: “There goes Paul who suffered hunger and stripes and shipwreck for Christ’s sake,” and another will reply: “See those Corinthians? They live holy lives because he would rather die than let pride hinder their faith.” That witness — that testimony — *that* is my crown.

Not money. Not honor. Not even liberty. But your perseverance unto life eternal. Therefore again I say: it were better for me to die than allow any man to nullify this boast which rests wholly on grace.


But listen now: if others draw wages from your labors (for indeed those who serve at the altar eat from its offerings), surely we who planted this church among you have greater claim? Yes! And yet we made ourselves servants of all so that none might say our motive was base gain. For we preach Christ crucified — folly to Greeks and offense to Jews — therefore let nothing about us look like merchants peddling divine truth.

So what is my reward? That when He appears we shall stand together blameless before God; that every tear shed for your waywardness shall be swallowed up in joy; that though stoned at Lystra and bound at Philippi, yet here stands one soul redeemed by His blood because Paul did not muzzle himself for love’s sake.

This is glory sufficient for eternity.
chunk_key:
aw the same also?

For it is written in the law of Moses , Thou shalt not muzzle the mouth of the ox

question: Why did you choose to make yourself a servant to all, even though you said you were free from everyone?
answer:
For the gospel is not a burden laid upon me as a slave, but a fire in my bones that consumes all pride, therefore I became the least of all so that none might be barred from the grace that seized me on the road to Damascus — and so then, though I am free in Christ from every human yoke, I willingly took chains of my own choosing: chains of servitude to Jews under the law, to Greeks outside it, to the weak who tremble at their sin, because *my* freedom is nothing if it erects walls where the cross has torn them down!

Do you think I glory in this? That I boast of becoming weak, of curtailing my rights, of eating with those once unclean to me? O madness! Only if you have seen the risen One blazing from heaven would you understand — He did not count equality with God a thing to grasp; how then should I clutch my liberties while souls perish?

I became all things — yes, even a Jew among Jews though called to break down their wall; yes, under law though justified by faith alone; yes, weak among the faint-hearted though upheld by resurrection power — because if by becoming a servant I might win one brother or sister for whom Christ died, then every surrender was gain.

And mark this: not because I am compelled — no! If it were mere duty I would have no reward. But because love compels me; because woe is upon me if I do not preach; because when I pour out my life as drink offering upon their faith, *then* and only then do I share fully in the gospel’s triumph.
chunk_key:
ea, woe is unto me, if I preach not the gospel !

For if I do this thing willingly, I have a reward:

question: What did you mean when you said you became 'as a Jew' to the Jews and 'as under the law' to those under the law?
answer:
O the unsearchable riches of Christ — how they compel a man beyond all reason or safety!  

I speak of that burning charge laid upon me on the road where I was struck blind by the glory of the risen One — *Him whom I persecuted!* And from that hour, though I was once a Hebrew of the Hebrews, circumcised the eighth day, of the stock of Israel, blameless under the law — yet now I am nothing *but* a bondservant of His gospel. So when I say I became as a Jew to the Jews, understand this: it was not deceit, nor hypocrisy, but love driven to its extremity! For though in Christ I am free — free from temple and tithes, clean and unclean meats, from circumcision itself — yet if a Jew stumbles at my liberty and is lost because of my pride, then what have I gained? Nothing. But if by becoming *as* one under the law — observing days with him, eating as he eats (so long as it does not deny Christ!) — if thereby his soul may be drawn to hear the grace that fulfilled that very law… then gladly do I become under it again in practice, though not in bondage.  

For what is meat to me? What is custom? What is reputation? Are they worth one perishing soul? No! To those outside the law — Greeks, barbarians, Scythians even — I live not bound by Jewish customs; I eat with them freely; I speak their wisdom; not because Christ is less true but because His truth surpasses all forms. And to the weak — oh, how tenderly we must tread! Some tremble at old idols even in their freedom; others are haunted by scruples. So for them, I become weak with Christ’s strength hidden beneath.  

Do you think this compromise? No! It is strategy forged in divine fire: *all things to all men*, not for my gain but for theirs. That by any means possible — through Jew or Greek, through strength or weakness — some might be snatched from death and stand with us before Him whose blood bought us all. For what reward do *I* seek? Only this: that when we stand at last before His judgment seat none can say “you led me astray,” but many will cry “you ran back into the flame for me!”
chunk_key:
ea, woe is unto me, if I preach not the gospel !

For if I do this thing willingly, I have a reward:

question: How did giving up your right to financial support from the gospel help you avoid abusing your power?
answer:
O wretched privilege it is to be entrusted with preaching Christ crucified — and yet, if I proclaim the gospel, no boast have I; for woe is me if I preach not!  

You know full well how the Lord ordained that those who proclaim the gospel should live by the gospel — a just thing, a right thing. And yet I have made myself poor among you, refusing to take from you what was due by every apostolic right, not because I scorn provision, but because I fear the abuse of power. For when money changes hands for ministry, some may hear my voice as seeking silver, not salvation; they may think my zeal is fueled by coin rather than by Christ. But if I preach free of charge — if I work with these hands night and day so none need burden themselves for me — then the gospel stands unchained from suspicion!  

For though I am free in Christ — free from men’s demands, free from earthly obligation — I have become a slave to all, taking on every form: Jew under Torah’s yoke to win Jews; Greek unbound by law yet never lawless before God; weak with the weak so none should feel too broken for grace. And in this same way, I stripped myself of financial claim not because the Lord lacks means to support His messengers — He does! But because power can poison even holy office.  

Therefore I renounced my right that no stumbling block might rise between sinners and their Savior. That when they came to me, they would see only Jesus lifted high between heaven and earth — not Paul profiting from their faith. The reward? Not gold or garments or glory among men — but this: that together with you we share in the gospel itself: its sufferings first, then its crown.

ea, woe is unto me, if I preach not the gospel !

For if I do this thing willingly, I have a reward:

question: When you said you became 'weak' to the weak, what kind of weaknesses were you adapting to?
answer:
I speak this not from mere strategy, but from the very fire that first struck me blind on the road to Damascus — a fire that burned away Saul and left in his place a man compelled, not by ambition, but by the unpayable debt of grace. For if I preach the gospel willingly, there is reward; but if unwillingly, still the burden is laid upon me — *woe is unto me if I preach not* — and therefore I run, not as one uncertain, but with deliberate steps toward those who stumble.  

When I say I became weak to the weak, I do not mean feigned frailty or empty sympathy; no, I mean this: I stripped myself of every defense of status, every privilege of learning, every boast of Hebrew pedigree — though I was circumcised on the eighth day, of the stock of Israel, of the tribe of Benjamin — and stooped to walk beside those whose consciences trembled at food offered to idols or whose hands bore the marks of poverty and shame. To them that are weak in faith, I became as one without strength so as not to crush them with knowledge that puffs up while love builds up.  

I ate with those under scruples about meats; I refrained from liberties that were mine in Christ lest my freedom become a stone to trip a limping brother. Though free from all men — unbound by custom or compulsion — yet for their sakes I bound myself voluntarily: to Jews under law as under law; to Greeks without law as without law (though never lawless before God); to slaves and freemen alike as one who serves all. Even my own body do I discipline like an athlete in training — not for glory’s sake, but so that having preached to others, *I myself may not be disqualified*.  

For though Christ was rich beyond measure, He became poor for our sakes — and what am I? A prisoner twice bound in chains for His name’s sake? A man beaten with rods three times? Scarred from shipwreck and hunger? That suffering has taught me this: true strength is hidden in weakness made perfect in grace. So then, if they are weak through fear or ignorance or conscience too tender for harsh doctrine — *I become weak*, even as He emptied Himself upon the cross — that by all means some might be saved.
