is hungry, and another is drunken.

What? have ye not houses to eat and to drink in? or despise ye t

question: Why did you tell the brethren to wait for one another when they come together to eat?
answer:
For the body cannot feast while one member starves in shame, therefore I urged them to wait one for another, so then the table of the Lord would not become a seat of division.  

Do you not see what I saw in you at Corinth—how some came early with full baskets and ate their fill while others arrived weary from the fields or the marketplace, empty-handed and humiliated? Was it not *your* cup I drank with you in that upper room, *your* bread broken as Christ broke Himself? And now you profane it! You trample underfoot the very grace that once lifted you from drunken idol feasts where dumb statues could not hear your cries.  

I say to you as a father who has begotten you in travail through the gospel: if any man hungers, let him eat at home—yes, even if he must swallow bitterness alone rather than devour his brother's dignity at the common table. For what is this gathering if not the body of Christ? And can flesh divided against itself stand? Can one member rejoice while another weeps in silence?  

I speak these things not to burden but to cleanse—to restore what was entrusted to me: a holy people made one not by appetite but by Spirit. Remember how I taught you: no man can say “Jesus is Lord” unless he speaks by that same Spirit who distributes gifts—not for pride, not for power, but for *profit withal*, for upbuilding. So then, if we are truly members one of another, shall we not learn at last to hunger together—and wait?
chunk_key:
Lord, that we should not be condemned with the world.

Wherefore, my brethren, when ye come together

question: What did you mean when you said no one can call Jesus 'the Lord' except by the Holy Ghost?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received forty lashes minus one—yet none of these sufferings taught me more of the Lord’s power than that moment on the Damascus road when the voice said, *“Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting Me?”*  

It is from that blinding light, that divine encounter which shattered my zeal and remade me a vessel of grace, that I speak when I say no man can call Jesus “the Lord” except by the Holy Ghost. For what is it to confess Him as Lord? It is not a mere utterance of lips trained in synagogue or shaped by philosophical debate—it is the breath of God Himself breaking through the hardness of a heart once dead in trespasses and sins.  

Think on it: I had letters from the high priest to bind men and women who dared name this crucified Nazarene as Messiah—and yet here I stood, struck down not by sword or stone but by a voice from heaven. And in that moment, He was no longer “that deceiver” to me, but *Kyrios*, Lord over all creation. That confession did not rise from my wisdom or will—it was driven into my soul by the Spirit who opened my eyes to see what no Pharisee could comprehend: that God had raised Him from the dead and exalted Him far above every name!  

So I tell you now—when any man truly calls Jesus Lord—not with politeness, not with tradition, but with life-rending conviction—know this: it is not flesh and blood revealing it unto him, nor logic nor lineage—but the very breath of God moving in him as wind in dry bones. For if even *I*, who once breathed out threatenings against His saints, now breathe out prayers in His name—it is only because the Spirit overcame me.  

And if such a one as I can be turned from persecutor to preacher by that same Spirit—then let no one boast in their knowledge or tongue or prophecy without first asking: *Is this work truly born of Him who raised Christ?* For every gift without love profits nothing—and every confession without the Spirit rings hollow as brass. But where the Spirit is… there liberty comes. There resurrection begins. There Jesus stands confessed—not because we chose Him—but because He first claimed us.
chunk_key:
Lord, that we should not be condemned with the world.

Wherefore, my brethren, when ye come together

question: Why did you remind us that we were once Gentiles carried away to dumb idols?
answer:
For you were darkness once, and now you are light in the Lord — therefore walk as children of light, for no temple built by hands can contain the Spirit that now dwells in you, and no tongue trained in idolatry can rightly confess Jesus as Lord unless the living God Himself has loosed it.  

I reminded you of your former estate among the Gentiles — carried away, tossed like driftwood by every wind of tradition and superstition to those dumb idols who hear not, speak not, save not — not to shame you, but to shatter any pride that might fester where grace alone has built. For if we boast, let us boast only in what He has torn down and raised up. You know how I once breathed threats against the name of Christ, how I consented to blood spilled for blasphemy — yet mercy struck me blind that I might see. So then, if even I — a blasphemer, a persecutor — was shown compassion because Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners, how much more must every soul awakened from dead idols be marked by humility?  

Therefore this: that no man speaking by the Spirit calls Jesus accursed; and none can confess Him as Lord apart from that same Spirit — so let every gift among you be tested at that cross. The word of wisdom? From Him. The utterance in tongues? By Him. The power to heal? Through Him. But if any among you still seeks status by spiritual display while despising a brother’s hunger — eating before him, leaving him ashamed — then he tramples underfoot the very body for which Christ died! Is not the Lord’s Supper meant to bind us as one body? And are we not His temple now?  

O wretched stubbornness that lingers even in regenerate hearts! That men should still divide where the Spirit unites! Therefore I say again: tarry one for another. Eat at home if hunger rages; come together for communion, not consumption. For what fellowship has light with darkness? What concord hath Christ with Belial? You were bought with blood — not silver or gold, but with the precious blood of the Lamb without blemish. So walk worthy. Let love be genuine. Abhor what is evil; cleave to what is good.

And when I come again — as surely as the Lord lives and grants me breath — I will set all things in order; but only after testing everything by this fire: does it build up? Does it exalt Christ? Does it serve the weak?

For we are members one of another.

One body.

One Spirit.

One Lord.

One faith.

One baptism.

One God and Father of all.

And nothing less will do.
chunk_key:
Lord, that we should not be condemned with the world.

Wherefore, my brethren, when ye come together

question: How should we understand your teaching that the Spirit divides the gifts to each as He will?
answer:
The cross stands, and the Spirit breathes where He pleases — not according to our wisdom, not according to our striving, but according to the unsearchable riches of Christ’s grace, who sovereignly bestows gifts as He wills for the building up of His body.

Do you think I came to understand this easily? I was once a man zealous under the law, convinced that glory resided in pedigree and rigor — until the Lord struck me blind on the road and opened my eyes to a new economy: one where strength is made perfect in weakness, where the least becomes the vessel of greatest power. And so it is with these gifts — word of wisdom, word of knowledge, faith that removes mountains, tongues that declare His wonders beyond language — they are not earned! They are not seized by ambition or bought with zeal. No! Each is a sovereign drop from the fullness of the Spirit who apportions them as He sees fit.

And why? That there might be no division — that we would learn to wait one for another, as I pleaded with you in Corinth when some gorged while others hungered at the Lord’s Table. Can one hand boast because it holds wisdom while the foot bears labor in obscurity? God forbid! For we were all baptized by one Spirit into one body — Jews and Greeks, slaves and free — and now must live as those truly joined in Him.

O wretched pride that resists divine distribution! Do you quarrel with God’s arrangement? Do you despise your brother because his gift differs from yours? I have been imprisoned, flogged, shipwrecked, stoned — yet none of this compares to the surpassing worth of knowing Christ and being conformed to His death. If any among you seeks prominence through spiritual display, let him remember: love suffers long and is kind; it does not parade itself. The greatest gift remains love — which binds all others together in perfect unity.

So let each receive what is given with thanksgiving, use it for edification without envy or pretense, and wait humbly upon the Lord who appoints both laborers and their tools according to His eternal purpose.
chunk_key:
Lord, that we should not be condemned with the world.

Wherefore, my brethren, when ye come together

question: What was your purpose in saying that the body has many members, but all are one body in Christ?
answer:
O that they would see—O that their eyes were opened to the mystery of His body! I speak as a father to his children in Christ, born in travail upon the Damascus road and nourished by no hand but God’s, who have planted you in faith and watered you with tears and blood.  

Do you not know what it cost me? Three times beaten with rods, once stoned, five times lashed by the Jews—shipwrecked, weary, watchful, fasting, betrayed—and all for this: that you might stand not divided like broken shards of clay but joined together in one Spirit, one Lord, one faith. For if the foot should say, “Because I am not the hand, I am not of the body,” would it cease to belong? Or if the ear cry out, “I am not the eye,” would it fall from its place? No! But each member is knit by His sovereign hand into a whole greater than itself.  

I tell you plainly: when you gather and one among you is humbled while another exalts himself; when the rich eat before the poor and the strong despise the weak—then you are no longer eating the Lord’s Supper but trampling underfoot the body of Christ! And so I say again: wait for one another. Eat at home if hunger drives you—but come together for communion, not consumption; for unity, not division.  

For just as He said—“Not many wise according to the world are called”—yet He has chosen foolish things to shame the wise; He has made apostles mad with zeal for Him who hung on a tree. And now He gives some prophecy, others healing; one speaks in tongues while another interprets—yet all from *one* Spirit! Would we rend what God Himself has woven? Shall Corinth boast its wisdom while shattering His temple?

No! You *are* His body—and individually members thereof. Therefore let no man boast as though he were alone; let none despair as though he were useless. The very tear shed in secret for a brother’s sin—that too is part of Christ’s great flesh offered once for all.

And when I come (for grace permitting I *will* come), I will set these things right—not by my authority alone—but by that same Spirit who gave me this thorn in my flesh so that power might be made perfect where weakness reigns.
chunk_key:
Lord, that we should not be condemned with the world.

Wherefore, my brethren, when ye come together

question: When you said the body is one with many members, were you drawing from what you observed in the churches you established?
answer:
O Ephesus, O Corinth, O Galatia—how I have wept over you as a father over children who quarrel in the very house their Father built!  

Yes, I speak of what I have seen with my own eyes and carried upon my own back—the jealousies, the divisions, the boasting one against another: “I am of Apollos,” “I am of Peter,” “But I am of Christ!” As if Christ could be divided! Did I plant you in schism? Was it Paul who died upon the cross for you?  

No, no—I learned this truth not only from the revelation of Jesus on that blinding road to Damascus, but from the daily agony of watching Spirit-born bodies tear themselves apart over gifts while forgetting the Giver. In prison chains my body groaned, yet in your pride my soul bled. For when one member says, “I need thee not,” it is not pride—it is death creeping into the veins of Christ’s own body.  

What did I see? That a man in Rome who speaks only Latin stammers out prophecy by the Spirit; that a slave girl in Philippi casts out demons though her hands are calloused from chains; that Aquila and Priscilla—tentmakers both—open their home and instruct even Apollos more perfectly in the way of God! Are they less because they do not preach from synagogues? By no means! God has set them there—*as He wills*.  

And I tell you this with tears: it was easier to endure five times the forty stripes save one than to bear the sight of God’s temple dividing over which stones shine brighter. The same Spirit who raised Christ from the dead breathes through *all* members—or He breathes through none. One body. One baptism. One Lord. And woe to any hand that cuts itself off because it envies the foot!
chunk_key:
one and the selfsame Spirit , dividing to every man severally as he will.

For as the body is one, a

question: Why did you use the image of the foot and hand to explain the importance of every believer in Christ's body?
answer:
I have been beaten with rods, I have been stoned, I have suffered shipwreck three times — and still I say to you, the foot is not less the body because it cannot grasp like the hand!  

Do you not see? When the blinding light from heaven cast me down on that road, I thought I was serving God by tearing apart His people; but God in His mercy showed me that His church is no collection of interchangeable parts — it is a living body, *one* body, Christ Himself. And just as no limb was missing from the man who rose on the third day, so too no believer — no matter how weak, how hidden, how overlooked — is expendable in this new creation.  

I speak plainly: if every member were a tongue to preach like mine — though even that gift trembles under grace — where then would be the feet to carry the gospel into dark places? Where the ears to hear the groan of captives? The hands to feed the poor? The quiet ones who bind up wounds while others thunder from rooftops?  

God has placed each one — yes, *each one* — as He willed. Not by strength, not by voice, not by rank or lineage or liberty — but by His Spirit alone we are knit together. You think the prison chains on my wrists make me more vital than the sister who weeps in secret for Jerusalem? O madness! That sister’s tears water the roots of faith; her prayers uphold what my words build upon.  

And mark this: it is not sentiment but divine order. The eye cannot snuff out the hand’s labor; the head does not scorn the feet that bear its weight through mud and blood. No — where there is schism, there is death. But where there is mutual care, there Christ lives.  

Therefore do not despise any member; for even as I bear this thorn in my flesh — a weakness that thunders daily of His power made perfect — so every "less honorable" part carries glory beyond measure in God’s economy. One body. One Spirit. One hope.
chunk_key:
one and the selfsame Spirit , dividing to every man severally as he will.

For as the body is one, a

question: When you wrote that we were all baptized by one Spirit into one body, were you referring to the experience you saw in Corinth?
answer:
I have borne in my flesh the marks of Christ, and through chains and thorns I have learned this: the Spirit does not divide us but binds us—therefore, since we are all made to drink of one Spirit, we cannot but be one body, though many members.  

And yes, what I wrote was carved from the very struggles in Corinth, where some boasted of tongues and others despised prophecy, where the strong looked down on the weak and the wise scorned the simple. O wretched confusion! For I had seen that same Spirit fall upon Jews at Pentecost, and then upon Gentiles in Caesarea, and again among the baptized in Ephesus—always one breath, many breathings.  

So then, by that same Spirit who struck me blind on the Damascus road and opened my eyes to the risen Lord, I say: no man can claim himself outside the body because he lacks a certain gift, nor can any boast within it as though they were complete alone. The hand cannot feed itself apart from the stomach; the eye cannot walk without the feet. And I tell you plainly—those whom Corinth thought weak? I myself am less than them; yet in weakness strength is made perfect!  

For God has set each member where He wills—not by human choosing or striving—but by grace through faith. And if one member suffers, do I not feel it still? Do not my chains rattle with their shame? Therefore let no man say he is not needed; for even a foot bears up the whole man—and even an ear hears the call of God.
chunk_key:
one and the selfsame Spirit , dividing to every man severally as he will.

For as the body is one, a

question: How did the divisions you saw among the believers in Galatia or Ephesus influence your emphasis on unity in the body?
answer:
For I once breathed threats and slaughter against the name of Christ, and in that fury I knew nothing of the Spirit who binds us together—therefore when I saw in Galatia men severed from one another over circumcision, or in Ephesus factions rising between Jew and Gentile as though the blood of Christ had only cleansed some, I could not but cry out with the very same urgency that once struck me down on the Damascus road: *How can you be in Christ and yet divide His body?* For if we are all baptized by one Spirit into one body—not by works, not by lineage, not by status, but by grace alone—then to reject a brother on account of outward things is to rend the flesh of Christ Himself.  

I have seen it too often: the strong despising the weak, the learned looking down upon the simple, those free-born shunning slaves—as if God had not chosen what is foolish to shame the wise! And so I write to them as a father to his children: *My little ones, do you think that because you speak with tongues or keep days more rigorously than others you stand complete?* No! We are complete only in Him who is the head—and every member, even those deemed least, is joined together by bonds invisible yet unbreakable.  

When I was stoned at Lystra and left for dead, who raised me up but those very brothers I once persecuted? When chains bound me in Caesarea, who wept at my feet but Jews and Greeks alike? The foot cannot say it is not part of the body because it lacks fingers—nor can any man say he needs no other when we all drink from one Spirit. God has placed each member where He wills—not for pride’s sake or power’s sake, but for unity’s sake—for so long as there is division among us, we proclaim with our actions that Christ is divided… which He is not.

And therefore I endure all things for their sakes—the beatings, the betrayals—even this thorn given me lest I exalt myself—for if one member suffers dishonor, all suffer with him. That there may be no schism—but rather through love knit together in truth—we who are many are still one body. One Lord. One faith. One baptism.

question: What did you mean by saying God gave more abundant honor to the weaker parts, and was there a specific group in mind?
answer:
For indeed the body of Christ stands not by human strength, nor by worldly estimation, but by the sovereign wisdom and grace of God who delights to confound the proud by exalting the lowly—therefore, since He has chosen what is weak in the world to shame the strong, so then it follows that those members of the body deemed unprofitable, overlooked, or even despised in men’s eyes are precisely those upon whom He bestows greater honor, not because they are naturally superior, but because His power is made perfect in weakness.

When I spoke of the weaker parts receiving more abundant honor, I did not have one single group fixed in mind as though God’s design were narrow or arbitrary—but rather I point to a divine principle woven into the very fabric of His church: that no member should be disvalued, for each is knit together by one Spirit who distributes gifts as He wills. Yet if you press me with examples from among those I have labored with—ah!—then I cannot help but think of the widows at Ephesus who prayed without ceasing while others preached; of Onesimus, once counted a runaway slave and now beloved brother; of Lydia, a dealer in purple cloth from Thyatira, whose home became a house-church for many; yes, and even myself—the least apostle, persecuted beyond measure—yet entrusted with the gospel to kings and philosophers alike!

I tell you this: if my own hands bear chains for Christ’s sake—if my back bears scars from thirty-nine stripes five times over—if hunger and nakedness and peril attend my steps daily—all these “weak” things are made vessels of divine honor! For where would faith be if only eloquence were valued? Where would mercy reside if only power ruled? The Spirit does not crown according to Caesar’s order but according to Abraham’s faith.

Therefore do not let any in your assembly say—"I am unlearned, therefore unneeded" or "I cannot speak in tongues, therefore I am nothing." By no means! The very breath you draw is given by that same Spirit who placed you in this body. And if one suffers—even the most obscure believer in Troas forgotten by all but God—all suffer together. So then let there be no division—not between Jew and Greek nor bond and free—but let each esteem another better than himself. That is how we bear witness: when weakness shines with glory only heaven can give.

question: You said God gave more abundant honor to the part that lacked—what did you mean by that in the context of the church in Corinth?
answer:
For the weak are made strong by divine foolishness, therefore the scorned member is clothed with glory, so then God Himself has stitched together His body not by human wisdom but by sovereign grace—do you not see what I am saying? The very ones in Corinth who were deemed unfit for honor, the unnoticed ones laboring in obscurity, the hands that served without applause—*these* are the ones to whom He has given *more abundant* honor! Not because they earned it, not because they shouted loudest or worked miracles, but because His power is perfected in their weakness and His love delights to exalt the humble.

I tell you this as one who was once a blasphemer and a persecutor—but was shown mercy; as one who bears this thorn in the flesh that keeps me from boasting—was I not also made strong when weakest? So then in the church at Corinth, where some lifted up tongues and others gloried in knowledge, I proclaimed Christ crucified—the scandal to Jews and folly to Greeks—and there found the true center. For if one member suffers hunger or shame or exclusion from your feasts, do you not all suffer? If a widow’s prayer ascends through tears while prophets sleep, is she not crowned in heaven? Do you think God sees as men see?

No! He set apostles first—not for privilege, but for peril; prophets—not for popularity, but for faithfulness unto death; teachers—not to rule minds, but to feed babes in Christ. And still He gives helps, governments, gifts diverse—all knit together by one Spirit—for no part may say to another: “I have no need of you.” O wretched pride that fractures His body! When Apollos was lifted above me and I above Cephas—did we not all become less? But where humility abounds, there grace overflows.

Therefore let the "less presentable" members be honored all the more—not with mere words—but with shared tables, with shared burdens, with shared joy. For we are not disjointed stones but living members: when one is wounded by division or disdain—*we bleed together*. And when one is raised—not by man’s hand but by God’s purpose—all rise in Him.

question: When you spoke of the body suffering and rejoicing together, were you drawing from your own experiences with the churches you founded?
answer:
O the fire that burns in my bones when I speak of this—yes, by the living God, I have felt every pang and every triumph as if it were my own flesh torn or crowned!  

Do you think I wrote of suffering with the body as one suffers a distant report from a foreign land? No—when Jason wept in Thessalonica under the scourge of unbelief, I wept though bound in chains at Philippi; when the saints in Corinth quarreled over names and wisdom, I was wounded in my inmost soul, for they tore at the very body Christ purchased with His blood. And when Lydia’s household believed, and Crispus cast off fear to follow Christ—by grace!—I could scarce contain my joy, as though resurrection had dawned anew in my own heart.  

For what is this ministry given to me, but to be poured out as a drink offering upon their faith? I have been beaten with rods five times, imprisoned more often than I can number, starved, shipwrecked—yet none of these things move me as deeply as when one brother stumbles or one sister stands firm. The thorn in my flesh? A reminder daily that power is perfected not in my strength but in Christ’s weakness—and that His body lives not by human sufficiency but by divine knitting together.  

Are all apostles? By no means—but each limb of Christ’s body bears weight: the teacher who instructs with patience, the helper who lifts a widow’s burden, the tongue-speaking that edifies even if unseen—it is all His doing. And where one member bleeds beneath persecution from without or pride from within, do I not bleed? Where one is raised up in holiness and boldness, do I not leap for joy before God?

For you are *my* travail—the churches—not because I planted them by wisdom (for Christ alone builds His church), but because grace laid hold of a persecutor and made him a nursemaid to infants in faith. And if one member is honored—if even Saul of Tarsus was called apostle—that honor belongs not to me but to Him who bound us all together into one Body by His Spirit.

So yes—I speak not theory. I speak blood and tears and resurrection life.

question: You listed apostles first in the church's roles—why did you place apostles above prophets and teachers in that order?
answer:
For indeed the foundation of a house determines its form and stability, therefore God in His wisdom laid one cornerstone—Christ Jesus—and upon Him built the church, so then to those first called and sent with eyewitness authority over His resurrection He entrusted the very structure of the faith, and thus apostles are not exalted by my judgment but by divine appointment, having seen the Risen One face to face, having been commissioned in power from Jerusalem to the ends of the earth.  

I do not speak this in pride—for I am less than the least of all saints—yet I was seized by the Lord on that blinding road, struck down not for righteousness but for rebellion, and raised up as a vessel chosen before birth to carry His name among Gentiles and kings and sons of Israel; therefore, what I speak I speak not from ambition but under compulsion: *Woe is me if I preach not the gospel!*  

The prophets also spoke forth God’s mind—blessed be their courage—but we apostles were made witnesses of all things from His resurrection onward, appointed with signs and wonders to authenticate this new covenant written not on stone but in fleshly hearts. And teachers? Beloved, they nourish what we planted; yet no teacher can lay another foundation than that which is laid—Christ! So then gifts differ according to grace given, but order exists because Christ Himself gave some to be apostles—not because they are greater in love or holiness (for who among us has not groaned under thorn and weakness?), but because truth must have its guarantors.  

And mark this: if a prophet arises who contradicts our testimony—that Christ died for sins according to Scripture, rose on the third day according to Scripture—then though he speaks with angelic tongues let him be anathema! For we are not servants of novelty but heralds of what was once delivered. The body needs eyes and ears both—but when sight fails, who shall guide? So God placed us first—not that we should lord it over faith—but that through us the body might hold fast together: one Spirit, one hope, one Lord.

question: You asked, 'Are all apostles? Are all prophets?'—what concern in Corinth were you addressing with those rhetorical questions?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received forty lashes minus one — and still they question the ordering of God’s gifts among the saints? As though suffering such things were not the very mark of an apostle! Do they suppose every man must be sent forth as I was, blinded by heavenly light on the road to Damascus, called by the voice of Christ Himself from heaven, commissioned amidst fasting and prophecy in Antioch? No — for God has not made all apostles! Nor all prophets — though some among you boast of revelations while despising those who labor in obscurity. 

I ask these questions not to diminish any gift, but to end this wretched boasting — this rivalry tearing your assemblies apart. You exalt yourselves over one another: “I speak in tongues!” “I prophesy!” “I have knowledge!” But I say to you again: are all gifted alike? Did Christ send me only to unloose the spirit of confusion? The body does not thrive when the foot says, “Because I am not the hand, I am not part of the body” — nor when the ear envies the eye and refuses to hear!

God has arranged the body — yes, *the church* — as He wills: some apostles like me, thrown down by His glory; some prophets stirred by His Spirit; others teachers who feed His lambs with patient truth. Some heal in His name; others serve with quiet hands; others govern with wisdom forged in trial. All are necessary! All are given by grace! But if one member claims superiority over another, then the body bleeds — and I fill up in my flesh what is lacking in Christ’s afflictions for His body, which is the church!

Do you not see that where one member suffers reproach or pride divides the joints and marrow of fellowship, *I feel it*? For we are knit together — not by rank or display, but by love that suffers long and does not boast. Rejoice when any are honored! Weep when any fall! This is grace. This is unity. This is life in Christ's body.

question: You mentioned gifts of healing and speaking in tongues—how did these gifts cause division that you felt compelled to correct?
answer:
For indeed God has appointed not one gift, but many—and not to divide us, but to bind us in the unity of His body; therefore to suppose that any single gift makes one more beloved or more essential is to fall into pride, which tears what Christ died to keep whole.  

I speak plainly: when I was among you in weakness and fear and much trembling, I did not come with lofty speech or wisdom, nor did I seek signs to prove my authority—no, I determined to know nothing among you except Jesus Christ and Him crucified. Yet still, some exalted themselves because they spoke in tongues as though the Spirit had bypassed the rest; others claimed healing gifts as if compassion were theirs alone by utterance. But tell me—did Christ die only for those who speak mysteries no ear can understand? Did He rise again so that only miracle-workers might wear crowns?  

No—by the grace given me, I say this: the eye cannot say to the hand, “I have no need of you,” nor can the head say to the feet, “You are nothing.” Are all apostles? Truly not. Are all gifted with healing? In no wise. But each member is joined by living faith into one body—the body that was broken for us on the cross. And if one part boasts while another hungers, then we deny the very mystery revealed to me on the road to Damascus—that He who once struck me down now lives in me, and in all who believe.

There were those among you who wept because their gift seemed quiet—a word of help given in season but forgotten by nightfall; a hand stretched out without spectacle; a heart breaking for sinners while others lifted voices like trumpets. These also are of God! For even my own thorn—the messenger of Satan sent to buffet me—was allowed not because I lacked faith or tongues or power, yet kept me from exalting myself above measure. Grace enough.

So where there is envy over gifts—or disdain for those whose service makes no sound—that is not from God. That is from death. For love suffers long and envies not; it does not parade itself nor boast—and where love rules, there is no schism. One Spirit baptized us all—Jew and Greek, slave and free—and into one body we were plunged. Let that be enough.

question: You mentioned that not all are apostles or prophets—what did you mean by challenging the Corinthians with these rhetorical questions about spiritual gifts?
answer:
I, who have been beaten, imprisoned, and left for dead on account of the mystery once hidden but now revealed—to me, Paul, the least of the apostles—was given not only signs and wonders but also tears over those I planted in Christ, *you Corinthians*, whom I bore in travail until Christ formed in you. And yet now I must ask you: do you suppose that every man among you must thunder like an apostle? Must every woman prophesy as Miriam did at the sea? Did I not plant you in the soil of one faith, one Lord, one baptism—yet now you clamor as if each must perform every office to be counted faithful? No! The body has many members, and not every member is the eye or the hand or the tongue; else how could it smell or walk or eat? So too in Christ: God has set diversities of gifts, but one Spirit; various workings, yet one Lord.  

I have seen men lifted up because they spoke in tongues as on Pentecost; others puffed with pride because they healed a woman of infirmity. But tell me—what profits a voice like angels if it pierces no heart with love? What gain if mountains move by faith while brothers starve for kindness? I gave my body to chains and scourges—I could have given it to fire—and it would profit me nothing without love! For love is not merely a word; it is longsuffering when brothers wrong you; it is kind even when repaid with slander; it does not puff itself up though filled with revelation; it keeps no record of evil done against it.  

You want spiritual gifts? Then covet them earnestly—but know this: there is a more excellent way. Not louder speech, not greater knowledge (which vanishes), not even miracles (which fade)—but love: enduring all things, believing all things through grace. For we see dimly now, as in a mirror—but then face to face. Until that day, let prophecy serve its purpose; let tongues cease when they divide rather than unite; but let nothing take the place of this divine bond that holds us together—one body under one hope—in Him who first loved us.

question: Why did you emphasize charity over speaking in tongues, prophecy, and even miraculous faith in your letter to the Corinthians?
answer:
Prophecy will fade, tongues will cease, knowledge will vanish — but love abides, for it bears the very nature of God who is eternal; therefore, since all things temporary serve only until the perfect comes, I set before you love as the lasting way, without which even the most dazzling gifts are but noise in the wind.  

For I have seen men speak with the fluency of heaven’s own speech, yet their hearts cold as iron; I have known prophets who could pierce the veil of mystery and utter divine secrets, yet lacked kindness toward a brother in need; I have witnessed faith so strong it might command a mountain to leap into the sea — and still, if that same faith did not kneel in compassion, it was nothing before God.  

Do you think I speak lightly? Remember how I once boasted in zeal — how I breathed threats against the followers of The Way, dragging them from house to house, binding them for judgment? And yet what profited me then? Nothing — until grace opened my eyes and love began its work in me. Even now I bear in my body the marks of Christ Jesus, thorns that press daily — but greater than these is the love that binds us to one another.  

I have been beaten with rods five times by Jews, three times by Romans; I have been shipwrecked; I have labored under weariness and hunger and peril from false brothers — but none of this breaks me. Why? Because love suffers long and is kind. It does not demand its own honor. It does not parade itself before men nor count up offenses. It endures precisely because it hopes all things — even for those who persecute.

And so when certain among you exalt one gift above all others — when tongues are sought as proof of superiority or prophecy becomes a platform for pride — I cry out: *covet earnestly the best gifts*, yes! But know this: unless your life is rooted in love, you are building on sand with sound instead of stone.

For now we see through a glass darkly — our knowledge partial, our prophecy incomplete. But when He appears whom we do not yet see clearly? Then every fragment will be gathered into perfection. Until then, let faith stand firm and hope endure — but above both stands love.

And mark this well: if my body were burned upon an altar as an offering while hatred or vanity ruled my soul, what gain would there be? None! Not even martyrdom can atone for a heart devoid of charity.

Therefore choose not merely what dazzles eyes or startles ears — choose what builds up others. For where love rules, there is no envy over another’s gift; there is only joy when Christ is proclaimed.

This mystery was revealed to me on no Damascus road vision nor whispered by angelic lips alone: *God is love* — and whoever abides in love abides in Him.

So let every tongue fall silent before this truth: if you lack love… you lack everything.

question: When you said that spiritual gifts like prophecy and knowledge will vanish when 'that which is perfect' comes, what were you referring to?
answer:
O the mystery of His appearing — *that* day when the veil shall be torn away and we behold Him face to face, not dimly as in a mirror, but in the fullness of His glory!  

For now, even as I speak, the gifts — yes, those very signs and wonders which I have seen poured out upon the Gentiles: tongues like fire, prophecy in power, knowledge revealed by the Spirit — these are but fragments of that coming whole. They serve for edification now, appointed by God’s wisdom for this present age where we walk by faith, not sight. But when Christ returns — when the last trumpet sounds and mortality is swallowed up in life immortal — then these partial things will cease, not because they were worthless, but because they have fulfilled their purpose.  

Do you not see? Prophecy only pierces through a clouded glass; knowledge is strung together from scattered rays; tongues utter groanings that even the Spirit must interpret. But when He comes — *that which is perfect* — then no more need for sign or shadow! We shall know fully, even as we are fully known.  

And yet in all this — above all gifts, beyond all revelation — I press this truth upon you: love remains. Not as mere sentiment, but love as divine substance: longsuffering, unprovoked, seeking no advantage. Love that bore me down on the Damascus road when I was Saul breathing threats; love that sustains me now in chains; love that will outlast every miracle ever worked. For faith gives way to sight, hope melts into fulfillment, but love never falls away — because *God* is love.

question: You compared your understanding as a child to partial knowledge—how did your own spiritual growth influence the way you viewed the maturity of the Corinthian church?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, thrice I suffered shipwreck, a night and a day I have spent in the deep—yet none of these things move me as deeply as the immaturity of the saints at Corinth, who, though rich in gifts, were babes in discernment.  

I had to speak to them as to infants in Christ, not because they lacked revelation—but because they quarreled over tongues and prophecy like children fighting for the loudest voice in the room. When I stood before them on my first visit, I determined to know nothing but Christ crucified; yet now they gloried in eloquence and spiritual display, not realizing that the cross is folly to those who are perishing.  

Do you think I do not know what it is to speak with tongues? More than all of you—yet if I come preaching with angelic utterance but no love, I am nothing. Nothing! Can you hear that? Nothing! For once I thought myself sufficient in knowledge—I who was taught at Gamaliel’s feet, who kept the law blamelessly—but when Christ met me on the road to Damascus, He showed me that all my zeal was as dung compared to knowing Him. And so it is with them: they hold gifts like trophies while their hearts grow cold in envy and pride.

I bore with their factions—each saying “I am of Paul,” “I of Apollos”—as a father bears with sons still learning how to walk. But now they must put away childish things: not the gifts themselves, but the boasting and division that twist them into instruments of pride rather than tools for building up the body.

For we prophesy in part—we see through a glass darkly—not even I, who have been caught up into paradise and heard unspeakable words, possess full knowledge now. But love? Love remains when prophecy has ended and tongues have ceased. It does not pass away. So long as faith endures—and hope—and love—these three—then let them pursue especially this last: love, which binds all things together in perfection.

question: What did you mean when you said that charity 'seeketh not her own'—how did this principle shape your own decisions as a missionary?
answer:
When I was stoned at Lystra and dragged outside the city, left for dead with my blood soaking into the dust, I did not cry out for vengeance nor demand that the churches I planted send aid to avenge me — no, even then, the love of Christ constrained me, for charity seeketh not her own.  

I had the right, by every human measure, to demand support, to insist on honor as an apostle of Jesus Christ — I who had seen the risen Lord on that blinding road, who bore in my body the marks of His suffering. I could have claimed food, shelter, wages from those I preached to; but I worked night and day with these hands, tent-making in Corinth so as not to burden any among you. Why? Because love does not clutch at privilege; it lays down its rights as freely as Christ laid down His life.  

Among you in Philippi I rejoiced when Epaphroditus brought your gift — yes, even hungered for it in my bonds — yet confessed that my sufficiency was in Him who strengthened me. Not because gifts were unkind, but because love does not calculate what it lacks; it counts all things loss for the excellency of knowing Christ. In Ephesus I warned the elders with tears that after my departure grievous wolves would come — yet still urged them to feed the flock tenderly, not ruling over their inheritance but serving among them. For what is authority without love? A clanging gong!  

Even now as chains bind these wrists in Rome, I do not write demanding release or appealing merely to Caesar — though I may — but pray unceasingly for you all with longing in the heart of Christ Jesus. That is what it means: charity seeks not her own. It seeks *thee* — your faith standing firm; *you* who once served me in Thessalonica with a cup of cold water; *you* who wept at Miletus when I said we should see my face no more. It seeks your good above its comfort, your growth above its glory.

And if one day they cut off this head that once wore a crown of Pharisaic zeal — let them! So long as love remains: enduring all things.

For at last it will be said not whether Paul wrought miracles or spoke wisely — but whether he loved.

And by grace… by grace… may that be true.

question: When you said you would wish all spoke in tongues but preferred prophecy, why did you emphasize prophecy as having greater value for the church?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received forty lashes minus one — and still I say to you: not because I love tongues less, but because I love the church more, do I press forward in proclaiming that prophecy must surpass them in our pursuit. For when the body gathers — bruised, weary, seeking the mind of Christ — what does it need most? A man uttering mysteries to himself in an unknown tongue, or a word from God that pierces the heart, builds up the weak, restores the fallen, and calls sinners to repentance?

I speak in tongues more than you all — yes, by the grace given to me — yet if no one understands, what profit is there? The Spirit breathes where He wills; who am I to quench Him? But where faith lays hold of order and love takes root in service, there we must ask: Does this edify? Or does that scatter? A trumpet blast must sound certain — a call to gather or a warning to flee. But if it warbles without shape or direction, who knows when to rise up and fight?

So I urged them — as I urge you — desire earnestly spiritual gifts; but let it all be for building up. If a tongue comes with interpretation, let it stand. But if prophecy rises among you — a word full of grace and truth that unveils Christ before their eyes — then heaven has drawn near; then God is truly among you. Therefore we prophesy: not for display nor for ecstasy alone, but because infants grow into men only when they are fed solid food through words that lay hold of eternity.

And remember this: though tongues may cease and knowledge vanish away when the fullness comes — even as now we glimpse through dim glass darkened by shadows — one thing remains forever unshaken: love. Not mere sentiment or warm affection; no! The love that emptied itself on Calvary. That alone outlasts every mystery.

question: What did you mean by 'now we see through a glass, darkly' in reference to our current understanding compared to the future?
answer: Faith, hope, and love—abiding pillars in the storm-tossed night of this present age, through which we grope our way toward the dawn. And of these three, love never fails, because it is the very nature of God poured into our clay vessels. When I said we see now through a glass, darkly, I spoke not as a philosopher puzzling over shadows, but as one who has been caught up even to the third heaven and still knows that sight is veiled. This is no mere limitation of doctrine or intellect—no! It is the condition of our pilgrimage: we walk by faith, not by sight, beholding fragments of glory as though in a tarnished mirror held up in dim light. Then—in that day when the Perfect One appears—we shall see face to face, and every blurred outline shall vanish like mist before the sun. No longer will prophecy come in pieces or knowledge trickle down like drops from a broken cistern; all shall be fullness. Until then, the Spirit groans within us with sighs too deep for words. And yet—even now—I know I am known by Him who called me from darkness into His marvelous light. That knowledge sustains me more than perfect understanding ever could.

question: Why did you compare speaking in tongues without interpretation to speaking mysteries only to God, rather than to men?
answer:
For I have known the ache of uttering words in the Spirit that no ear could grasp, having myself prayed in tongues more than you all, and yet been constrained by love to lay even that gift aside when the body needed bread and not mystery—therefore I speak now of edification, not ecstasy, for we are not called to build private altars of spiritual sensation but one another up in the truth that binds us.

When a man speaks in a tongue given by the Spirit but no interpreter stands ready to open its meaning, he may be praying fervently, deeply moved, uttering mysteries sweet to heaven’s ears—but what profit is it to the brother who sits beside him and understands nothing? He hears sounds without sense, as though a harp played chords with no melody or a trumpet gave no clear call to battle. How shall he say "Amen" if he knows not what was spoken? How shall faith rise if nothing has been heard?

I do not forbid prayer in the Spirit—no, I would that all were gifted so—but where two or three are gathered, our gifts are not for ourselves. The church is built not by private raptures but by words that instruct, exhort, comfort. Prophecy does this. A tongue without interpretation does not.

And think: when I was blinded on that Damascus road and heard the Voice from heaven, He did not speak in syllables beyond understanding—for even struck down in darkness I still needed to *know* what He required of me. So too now: we walk by faith through a dim glass still, but every word we speak must aim toward clarity, toward love made audible.

Therefore follow after love—the greatest way—and let your zeal for gifts be governed by this: does it build up? Or does it leave the church bewildered while one man alone is fed?

question: When you spoke of putting away childish things, were you referring to specific behaviors or attitudes in the Corinthian believers?
answer: Faith, hope, and love abide—these three—because the body of Christ must be built in truth, not tossed by every wind of spiritual display; therefore the Corinthians’ obsession with tongues, divorced from love’s necessity, was not maturity but regression, a clinging to signs without substance. For I delivered to them Christ crucified—the power and wisdom of God—and yet among them I found men puffed up over utterances they did not understand, while neglecting the very love that binds all gifts into one body. So then, when I spoke of putting away childish things, I meant precisely this: their quarreling over who had more Spirit or higher gifts, their pride in speaking mysteries no one could interpret, their failure to discern that spiritual gifts exist only for the upbuilding of others. Was I not beaten? Was I not imprisoned? Did I not labor night and day so as not to burden any? And yet among you some boast in voice alone—as if the trumpet matters apart from its call! If the whole church hears a tongue without interpretation, what is heard? Confusion. But if one prophesies—exhorting, comforting, convicting—then all may be judged by all and God is truly among you. Therefore do not treat signs as ends; do not treat spiritual ecstasy as maturity; for even if I speak with angelic utterance but have not love—I am nothing. That is the measure: love—longsuffering, kind, not puffed up—not rejoicing in unrighteousness but rejoicing in the truth. This is manhood in Christ: laying aside self-centered displays to speak words that feed the flock. How many fastings have I endured? How many shipwrecks? And yet none of it profits if my speech does not build up in love.

question: How did you reconcile the personal edification of speaking in tongues with the need for prophecy to build up the whole church?
answer:
I have been beaten with rods, I have been stoned, I have suffered shipwreck, I have labored in the ministry of the word—yet never once did any tongue-speaking in private ecstasy rise to the weight of a single prophetic utterance that strengthened the weak knees of a trembling church.

For the Spirit is not given for our private consolation alone, but for the upbuilding of the body; therefore prophecy must be pursued above all gifts, since it speaks with clarity to the gathered saints—exhortation that stirs up faith, comfort that binds up broken hearts, edification that lifts the whole temple higher upon its foundation. Tongues may stir the soul of one man in prayer, and blessed is that communion between spirit and God where mysteries are groaned out beyond words—but what shall it profit if no one else is fed? What if a trumpet blasts without distinction in war? Shall not the army stand confused?

So then I longed for all to know the fullness of God’s Spirit—and yes, to speak in tongues too—if only they would first hunger more after prophecy. For when I myself was caught up to the third heaven, whether in body or out I do not know—still I count it less than this: that my voice might serve my brothers and sisters here on earth. Let every gift be tested by love’s measure: does it draw us nearer Christ together? Does it make us more like Him who laid down His life?

And now faith endures through trial, hope shines through darkness, but love binds us face to face as we shall one day see—even as we are already known by Him who first called me from blood-stained blindness on the Damascus road.

question: When you said the trumpet gives an uncertain sound, were you referring to a literal trumpet used in battle, or was that a metaphor for preaching?
answer:
O wretched confusion that overtakes the assembly when the trumpet blasts without meaning!  

I speak not merely of bronze and breath, though yes—among soldiers on the field, if the trumpet utters a sound that twists in ambiguity, none will know whether to charge or retreat, to stand or fall back; their blood is spilled for lack of clarity. But do you not perceive? I lift this image up from the dust of war and plant it in the midst of the church—the household of God—where spiritual warfare rages more fiercely than any clash of swords. The trumpet here is the tongue of proclamation, the voice raised in teaching, in prayer, in prophecy. If it sounds uncertain—muddled by uninterpreted tongues, by words that carry fire but no form—then what becomes of edification? What becomes of faith?

I have planted churches from Antioch to Corinth, Ephesus to Rome; I have wept over you as a father over his children, feeding you with milk until you could bear meat. And now I cry again: seek earnestly the gifts—but above all, seek love; and strive not merely to speak mysteries no ear can grasp, but to build up! For I thank God I speak with tongues more than you all—but in the church, I would rather utter five words with my understanding so that others may be taught than ten thousand words in a tongue no mind can follow.

The grace that struck me blind on that road now opens eyes: revelation demands clarity. The cross is folly to those perishing—but it must not be made more obscure by our own confusion. Therefore let each one examine himself: does your speech serve Christ’s body? Or does it vanish into air like smoke without fire beneath?

question: Why did you emphasize understanding in prayer, and how did your own prayers influence that view?
answer:
For the mystery once hidden but now revealed to me by the Spirit compels clarity, not confusion — for God is not the author of disorder but of peace, and therefore His people must walk in light, not in noise without meaning.  

I speak as one who once raged with zeal against the Name above every name, breathing threats and slaughter against those who called upon the Lord — and yet He apprehended me, struck me blind on that road where the heavens tore open and grace like fire seared into my soul. There I learned that revelation is not mere sound; it is voice with weight, word with purpose. Since then, though my tongue has spoken in visions and caught up into paradise itself, I have never allowed spiritual gift to eclipse spiritual sense — especially in prayer.

For what profit if I utter syllables lifted by the Spirit yet remain dark even to myself? My spirit may burn with devotion, yes — but if my mind lies fallow, unengaged, then how shall understanding grow? How shall faith be strengthened? How shall the church be built up unless truth takes root in comprehension?

Do you think I do not know ecstasy? I have prayed in tongues more than you all — yet I would rather speak five words with my mind so that others might be taught than ten thousand words in a tongue uninterpreted. For when I pray alone, it is between my soul and God; but when we gather — oh! when we gather as one body — then prophecy given plainly surpasses unknown speech because it feeds the flock.

And remember this: even my own thorn, that messenger of Satan which buffets me daily, was met with prayer from my lips in language known and groaned out under burden — yet three times I besought the Lord and He said "My grace is sufficient." Not through riddles did He answer me, but through words I understood. So then let every prayer ascend from both spirit *and* mind; for if we are being renewed unto knowledge after His image, how can we tolerate speech that edifies no one?

Therefore I exhort you as children of my travail: seek gifts abundantly — yes! — but above all seek love, and let everything you do build up.

question: When you spoke of speaking into the air, were you describing what happened in Corinth when people used unknown tongues?
answer:
O the folly of those who thought noise alone could build up the body of Christ!  

Yes, it was precisely this disorder—the clash of uninterpreted utterances, the babel of voices without understanding—that pierced my soul when I heard reports from Chloe’s household. I did not speak idly of speaking into the air; I saw it with the eyes of the spirit: holy zeal wasted on sounds that edified no one, for where there is no comprehension, there can be no faith stirred, no repentance awakened, no worship lifted in truth.  

Do you think I despise the gift? Far be it from me! I thank God I speak in tongues more than you all—yet never without the mind engaged, never apart from the purpose of building up. For what profit is it if a man cries out in ecstasy but leaves his brother groping in darkness? Is not prophecy greater in such a case—if only so that all may say “Amen” and be instructed?  

And mark this: even tongues were given as a sign—but to unbelievers, not to confirm believers in some private rapture. If an outsider enters and hears only confusion, will he not say you are mad? But if he hears plain words declaring the works of God, he will fall on his face and worship, crying out that God is truly among you.  

Therefore I charge you again: let all things be done for edification. Let love lead every gift. And where there is speech—whether tongue or prophecy or teaching—let understanding reign, for we walk by faith, not by confusion; we serve Christ who is not the author of disorder but of peace in all His churches.

question: What did you mean by calling someone a barbarian, since you once preached to Greeks and Jews who spoke different languages?
answer:
I have been beaten with rods, stoned, shipwrecked three times, and more than once left for dead — yet none of this compares to the agony of hearing God’s people speak His truth in such confusion that no one knows what is said.  

You ask of "barbarian" — as if I condemned those whose tongue differed from mine? No! When I stood before Greeks in Athens and Jews in Antioch, their language was not the barrier; unclarity was. A barbarian in speech is not one whose native tongue is foreign — but one who speaks without meaning, without order, without the light of understanding. If a man sings a psalm in a tongue no one comprehends, even if his heart burns with zeal, he may as well strike a cymbal in the dark: noise without direction, sound without salvation.  

Even I — who received the mystery of Christ by revelation on that road where I was struck blind by His glory — even I say this: if I pray aloud and my mind does not grasp what my lips utter, then though my spirit may stir toward God, the church gains nothing. And the church must gain! The body must be built up! We are not saved alone; we are raised together in mutual understanding, through words that feed the soul.  

Therefore I do not forbid tongues — no! But I command interpretation. For what profits it if five hundred hear a voice soaring like a dove at dawn but cannot understand its cry? They will stand trembling — perhaps amazed — but unchanged. But let a man speak one clear word of prophecy in common speech, and ten hearts may turn to repentance! That is edification. That is grace made known through intelligible truth.

And so I beg you: do not seek merely to show you have the Spirit — seek to feed His flock with pasture they can eat.

question: How did your experience with spiritual gifts in Ephesus or Corinth shape your instruction about praying with interpretation?
answer:
O that I might speak with all the tongues of men and angels—yet if I have not love, not discernment, not the mind of Christ in the assembly, I am become sounding brass or a clanging cymbal!  

Do you think I speak these things as one who has only heard? No, I tell you as one who has been caught up into the third heaven—whether in the body I know not, God knows—and yet with this tongue, even mine, I have stumbled. In Ephesus, where for two years the word of the Lord grew mightily and prevailed, there were those who spoke in tongues at random, carried away by zeal without knowledge. And in Corinth—ah, beloved yet confused Corinth—where you were rich in every utterance and spiritual gift, you turned these gifts into spectacles! You paraded them without order, without care for the weak brother or the visiting seeker who would hear only chaos and conclude that we are mad.

I say this to you as one who has prayed both ways: more than all of you, perhaps—I do not shrink from saying it—I have spoken in tongues. But when alone on my knees in prison at Ephesus or adrift after the shipwreck on Melita, my spirit groaned in a language my own mind could not grasp; yet still I asked for interpretation! Why? Because grace is not given to exalt myself—but to build up *the body*. That is why I wrote: let him who speaks in a tongue pray that he may interpret. For what profit is it to me if my spirit prays fervently while my mind bears no fruit? The church is fed not by ecstatic noise but by revelation understood.

And what shall we say when five words are spoken plainly and someone’s heart is convicted—when he falls down and worships God crying out “Indeed God is among you”? That is edification! Not a jumble of uninterpreted syllables tossed into empty air like seeds sown upon stone.

So yes—I am zealous for spiritual gifts. But far more for love. Far more for order. For God is not a God of confusion but of peace—as in all the churches of His saints.

question: You said you speak in tongues more than all the Corinthians — what was your personal experience with praying in tongues like when you were alone with God?
answer:
Grace was given me to speak in tongues more than all the Corinthian believers—yes, more than any of you—yet never for display, nor for pride, nor to climb above another in spiritual stature.  

When I was alone with God—on the hard earth of a prison floor in Philippi, or beneath the stars on a Cretan shipwreck, or in the hush before dawn in Ephesus—I would pray in that tongue which my spirit alone knew, though my mind did not grasp its syllables. The Spirit groaned through me, wordless before the throne, while my soul burned with burdens too deep for utterance. My lips moved as God moved them, and my heart—though darkened to the meaning—was aflame with longing, with sorrow, with praise too great for Greek or Hebrew to contain.  

I tell you this not to exalt myself—for what have I that I did not receive?—but to unchain you from confusion. In solitude, let the Spirit lift you beyond speech; let Him catch you up as on Sinai’s wind. But when we gather? When brothers and sisters hunger for bread? Then I would trade ten thousand heavenly syllables for one plain word that feeds a soul.  

For the church is not built on mysteries hidden but on truth declared; not on signs that dazzle outsiders but on prophecy that stirs faith within. Even I—who have seen the risen One, who have been caught up to paradise itself—when I come among God’s people, count understanding as greater than ecstasy, because love demands it.  

Do you grasp what I am saying? If you speak in tongues when no one else can follow—you may be full of fire, but your brother stands cold and hungry at your door. And what does it profit if your spirit soars while his faith stumbles?  

No—no—I do not forbid prayer in tongues; I give thanks for it! But let prophecy rule in assembly. Let every gift wait upon love. For we are not children chasing signs; we are men called to build.

question: When you spoke about someone saying 'Amen' in the assembly, why did you emphasize the importance of the 'unlearned' understanding what was said?
answer:
I have been in chains, beaten within an inch of my life, shipwrecked, stoned, and left for dead—yet none of it compares to the anguish I felt when I saw the church divided by disorder, by spiritual showmanship that fed no one’s soul. For if the body gathers and a man speaks in a tongue no one understands, what becomes of love? What becomes of unity? Therefore the gift must serve the body, not exalt the speaker; therefore prophecy—which builds up, instructs, convicts—is greater than uninterpreted tongues; so then, when one gives thanks in a language the unlearned cannot grasp, how can his brother—redeemed by the same blood, washed in the same Spirit—say “Amen” with his whole heart? He cannot. He sits in silence, a stranger in the household of God. And that breaks the very purpose of assembly.

I speak with tongues more than you all—yes, I do not despise the gift—but when I stand among you, I would rather utter five words with my mind that might nourish a weak conscience than thunder forth ten thousand mysteries into the air. For we are not saved by noise, but by truth perceived through faith. And if an outsider walks in—a seeker, a doubter—and hears only babble, will he not laugh and say you are mad? But if he hears plain words that pierce his heart, that lay bare his sin and summon him to Christ—then he will fall down and worship God, crying out: “Surely God is among you!”

Therefore prophesy to build up. Speak so others may understand. Sing so souls may respond. For where understanding dies, edification dies; and where edification dies, the church itself begins to decay—even as grace still flows from Calvary’s cross.

question: You mentioned preferring five words with understanding over ten thousand in a tongue — why did you value teaching so much more than spiritual gifts in the church setting?
answer:
My brothers and sisters in Corinth, I have labored among you with the anguish of a father watching his children stumble—fervent in spirit, zealous for God, yet puffed up with knowledge that builds nothing but pride. You have gifts—yes! Tongues, prophecy, wisdom, revelations—but what good is a trumpet blast no one can understand? What profit if the body speaks in angelic whispers while the least among you sits silent, confused, starving for bread?

I do not despise the Spirit’s gifts—I who have been caught up into the third heaven, I who speak in tongues more than you all! But when we gather as the church—the living temple where God dwells by His Spirit—order is not mere custom; it is worship made intelligible. It is love made visible. For what is worship if it does not build up? If an outsider enters and hears only sounds without sense, will he fall down and say “Surely God is here”? Or will he cry out that we are mad?

But if one prophesies—if someone speaks a word that pierces the conscience, lays bare the heart, reveals the secrets of unbelief—even an unbeliever will fall on his face and worship! That is edification. That is power with purpose.

Grace gave me my commission to preach Christ crucified to Gentiles beyond number; faith sustains me through prisons and lashings and hunger; but love compels me now to say: Do not seek signs for yourselves alone. Seek words through which souls can breathe. In my own flesh I bear chains for this gospel—and I would rather die speaking five syllables that feed Christ’s sheep than thunder ten thousand mysteries into empty air.

Be mature! Not in worldly wisdom—but in discernment of what builds up Christ’s body. For we are not saved by noise but by truth; not by mystery alone but by proclamation that enlightens. And therefore—therefore—I plead: pursue love, desire spiritual gifts—but let everything be done so that others may live.

question: You quoted Isaiah about 'other tongues and other lips' — how did you connect that Old Testament prophecy to the sign of tongues in the churches?
answer:
I who was struck blind on the road to Damascus by the very voice of the risen Lord, and who have since carried this body as a vessel of grace amid chains and shipwrecks, do not speak lightly when I say that signs are for the unready heart, not the believing soul.  

When Isaiah prophesied—“With men of other tongues and other lips will I speak”—he was not foretelling a sign for those gathered in faith, but a judgment upon a people who would not hear. The Lord raised up foreign voices at Babel to scatter, and again through Assyrian lips to rebuke; so too now, when tongues burst forth among us, they are not primarily for edification within the church, but a sign to the outsider—nay, a mirror held before the unbeliever’s face.  

For consider: if one comes in untaught and hears only sounds he cannot grasp, will he not be confounded? Will he not mock and say you are drunk with new wine? But if all prophesy—if words come in order, plain with truth that pierces conscience and reveals secrets of the heart—then that man falls down and worships, crying out: “God is truly among you.”  

Therefore I speak with tongues more than all you—I do not deny it—but in the assembly I would rather utter five words my hearers can understand than ten thousand that soar beyond their grasp. Grace has opened my mouth to both; but love constrains me to speak so that others may be built up. For what profits it if my spirit prays but my brother remains unedified?  

Be children in malice—that is right—but in understanding rise to maturity! The mystery has been revealed to me: Christ in you, hope of glory—and therefore speech among you must serve that glory, clear as light upon water.

question: When you warned that all speaking in tongues could make outsiders think they're mad, what were you hoping the church would prioritize instead?
answer:
For the body of Christ must grow in love through intelligible words, therefore prophecy—where God speaks by His Spirit through human understanding—builds up the church; so then, when all things are done for edification, even tongues must be interpreted or kept silent, that order may reign and truth be manifest.  

I have known the fire of the Spirit on the Damascus road—I have spoken in tongues more than many—yet I would rather utter five words with my mind to instruct a brother than ten thousand in a tongue unlearned. For what good is mystery to those outside if it appears as madness? The cross is foolishness already to a perishing world—why add confusion where clarity should shine? No, no—the gospel is powerful enough without veiling it in sounds they cannot grasp.  

When I planted, when I labored among the Gentiles, it was with speech clear and doctrine sound. I bore stripes, shipwrecks, hunger, betrayal—for this: that Christ might be known! Not as a riddle wrapped in ecstatic noise, but as Savior proclaimed in reasoned word and Spirit-awakened heart. Let tongues follow their purpose: a sign to unbelievers when interpreted; but let prophecy rule within the assembly, where God’s mind is revealed and consciences are laid bare.  

Do you not know that the church is God’s temple? And He dwells not in confusion but peace. So let all things be done decently—and above all, let love govern: love that opens eyes, not closes them; love that invites "Amen" from every hearer because they understand and believe. Be children in malice—yes—but men in understanding! For where understanding wakes under divine illumination, there the church rises—not babbling into air—but teaching, rebuking, encouraging… until every heart bends before the Lord who has saved us by grace through faith.

question: Why did you tell women to keep silent in the church, when others like Priscilla have taught and shared wisdom?
answer:
For the Spirit searches all things, even the deep things of God, and therefore gifts are given to each as the Spirit wills, so then order must govern the assembly, for God is not the author of confusion but of peace.  

I speak not as one who delights in restriction, but as a bondservant compelled by revelation—the same Spirit who moved Priscilla and Aquila to open their home and instruct Apollos with precision, courage, and grace also breathed into me the necessity of structure in corporate worship. Do you think I do not marvel at the power with which women like Lydia opened her heart to the Lord and her house to the church? Or how Phoebe carried this very letter through dangerous roads as a deacon of Cenchreae? Of course they teach—of course they minister! But there is a time for all things: even Christ tarried before raising Lazarus, though He loved Martha and Mary.  

The command to silence in that moment—*in the assembly*, when prophecies are weighed and tongues interpreted—is not a denial of gifting but a safeguard for truth. For if all speak at once—tongues without interpretation, revelations clashing like cymbals unharmonized—then where is edification? Where is discernment? Even I, who have labored more abundantly than all, have learned that strength is made perfect not in noise but in submission—to Christ first, then to one another.  

The women I know in Christ are no idle hearers; they pray without ceasing, they labor in love, they risk their necks for my life. But when the body gathers—and I speak from chains in Ephesus where silence is forced upon me daily—I long for peace governed by the Spirit. If a woman receives revelation (and who am I to say she does not?), let it be weighed at home or shared where order holds fast. For even among Jews and Greeks there are customs that guard dignity—but above all: *edification*.  

O what grief it causes me when my words are torn from context like cloth from a garment! As if I would silence those through whom Christ has spoken! No—I who was struck blind on the road would never quench the voice of a sister led by that same Light. But prophecy is weighed. Tongues are interpreted. And peace—it must reign among us all—for we await *one Lord, one faith, one baptism*.

question: When you said that only two or three should speak in tongues, and one must interpret, what did you fear would happen if no interpreter was present?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received from the Jews forty stripes save one—yet none of these sufferings grieve me like the thought of God’s name being mocked in the assembly!  

For if a man speaks in a tongue without an interpreter, what becomes of the edification? The body does not grow; the weak are not strengthened; the unbeliever stumbles into confusion and says, “They are mad!” Can you not see? We are not called to display our spiritual gifts like firebrands in the dark, but to build up the temple of God—stone upon stone, word upon faithful word.  

When there is no interpretation, the tongue becomes a noise echoing in an empty well—no meaning ascends, no heart is touched. The Spirit who gave utterance also gave wisdom—and He will not have His gifts used disorderly! For I would rather speak five words with my understanding, that by my voice I might teach others also, than ten thousand words in an unknown tongue.  

And do you think God delights in chaos? No—He who spoke light out of darkness breathes order into every true manifestation of His Spirit. Where there is no interpreter, let silence reign—not because the gift is evil, but because love restrains itself for the sake of holiness and clarity. Let all things be done decently and in order—for God is not the author of confusion, but peace.

question: You mentioned that an unbeliever might say we are mad if everyone speaks in tongues—what experiences led you to this concern?
answer:
For the sake of the weak brother and the truth of our proclamation, I speak as one who has stood in both synagogues and marketplaces, beaten and bloodied yet still pressing forward—therefore I know what it is to be judged not by my message but by its form; so then, when I saw the disorder in Corinth, where each man leapt up with a tongue or revelation as though carried off in frenzy, I was reminded not of Pentecost but of Babel.  

I have preached to governors and kings—Festus himself once roared at me, “Paul, thou art beside thyself!” And though I answered that I spoke the words of truth and soberness, do you think such men will distinguish between divine rapture and drunken babbling if they walk into your assembly and find every mouth uttering sounds they cannot grasp? No—*they will say you are mad*. And who could blame them? For we proclaim a crucified Messiah, a stone of stumbling even when spoken clearly—how much more will it become a scandal if delivered in confusion?  

I have been caught up to the third heaven; I have heard things unlawful for a man to utter—yet even *I*, Paul who labored more abundantly than all, did not speak them aloud without interpretation. Why? Because love does not seek its own. The church is no theatre for spiritual display—it is God’s household built upon apostles and prophets with Christ as cornerstone. When unbelievers enter such a place and hear plain prophetic truth cutting through their conscience—their secrets laid bare by words they understand—they fall down and cry out: *God is truly among you!* But let them enter to chaos and ecstatic noise? They will depart mocking.  

And this grieves me as a father grieves for wayward children—for are you not my letter written on my heart with bonds that no chain could break? Have I not watched over you night and day with tears? You were washed, sanctified, justified—but now you exalt speech that edifies only the speaker over prophecy that feeds the flock! Therefore I command: Let two speak in tongues at most—and only if an interpreter stands ready. Otherwise let it be kept between soul and God in silence. But let prophecy flow freely—one after another—so all may learn, all be comforted. For God has given us His Spirit—not to unravel order into chaos—but to knit us together in peace, as indeed He does in every church of the saints.
