question: How did Stephanas, Fortunatus, and Achaicus refresh both your spirit and that of the Corinthians, and why did you highlight their service so strongly?
answer:
I, who was once a zealous persecutor of the church but now bear in my very body the marks of Christ’s sufferings for the sake of His name—having been stoned, shipwrecked, and imprisoned for the gospel—do not speak lightly when I say that certain men become living letters of encouragement from God Himself.  

Stephanas, Fortunatus, and Achaicus were such men to me in Ephesus, where I labor under chains not made of iron only, but of constant opposition from those who resist the truth. When they came, they did not merely bring gifts or messages—they brought *themselves*, poured out as a fragrant offering to God and a balm to my soul. They stood beside me in the midst of weariness and conflict; they reminded me that the church at Corinth—though beset with divisions and immaturity—was still alive in Christ, still producing fruit worthy of repentance.  

They refreshed my spirit because they confirmed that my travail among you had not been in vain: they spoke of your faith stirring again, your love rekindling; they brought tangible help—not just money or supplies (though those too were needed), but fellowship, partnership in affliction, shared tears and prayers. And when I heard how they had labored among you to restore what was lacking—not only in material needs but in unity and discipline—I knew that their coming was sent by God’s mercy.  

Therefore I exalt them before you: not for their glory, but because you must learn to esteem those who *labor* among you in the Lord—not only the bold preachers or eloquent teachers like Apollos—but the faithful servants who quietly serve tables, carry burdens, endure travel and danger for love’s sake. For such is the body: not all are heads or mouths; some are hands and feet—and blessed are those who wash them with humility.  

Grace is not idle—it moves through such men. And when you honor them, you honor Christ who sent them—and prove that His love is truly perfected among you.

question: You mentioned that the churches of Asia salute me—what specific challenges were those churches facing when you wrote this greeting?
answer:
For the mystery once hidden has now been revealed in the sufferings of Christ and made known through the very afflictions that press upon His body in Asia, therefore the churches there groan not as those without hope, but as those who bear in their flesh the daily trial of false wisdom, for the synagogue doors have slammed shut and Jewish leaders stir up the people with accusations that we preach contrary to Caesar — so then they are pressed between Rome and religion, caught in a vise of persecution from without and seduction from within.

I speak not lightly when I say they face deceivers who whisper of angels and asceticisms, strange doctrines that would chain the soul to elemental spirits and rob them of their liberty in Christ — O! how quickly some turn aside to another gospel! And yet these saints stand fast; though Ephesus burns with idolatry and Artemis is lifted high by silversmiths whose trade thrives on delusion, still they gather in homes like that of Aquila and Priscilla, trembling perhaps, but singing praises with holy boldness. 

They know what it is to labor under imperial shadows, where a word misheard can summon magistrates; where bonds and imprisonments await any who name Jesus as Lord above all. And I tell you this plainly: their faith is not a quiet thing — it rings out! For even as they greet you with holy affection, many have been beaten for the name of Christ, driven from marketplaces, counted as troublemakers though they do nothing but love one another and confess Him whom I saw on that Damascus road.

Grace be with them — grace sufficient in weakness — for where sin abounds, grace much more reigns through Jesus Christ our Lord.

s: therefore acknowledge ye them that are such.

The churches of Asia salute you. Aquila and Priscil

question: You said Aquila and Priscilla greeted us with the church in their house—how did hosting the church in their home shape their ministry in Ephesus?
answer:
For the sake of the gospel, a house can become a sanctuary, a family table an altar, and a tradesman’s hands vessels of apostolic fellowship—therefore Aquila and Priscilla did not merely open their home, but surrendered their very way of life to the breaking in of God’s kingdom. So then, when they welcomed us into their dwelling in Ephesus, they were not only offering shelter from the storm of persecution; they were establishing a center of divine warfare—their roof covering more than timber and clay, but the assembly where Christ was preached boldly in the heart of Asia.

I who once breathed threats against such gatherings know this well: where two or three are gathered in His name, there is no room so small that it cannot contain the presence of the Most High. In that house—where tentmakers worked by day and saints gathered by night—doctrine was sharpened, faith was stirred, and love was made visible through tangible communion. Their home became a school of discipleship, where Apollos was further instructed in the way of God more perfectly; it became a hospital for bruised believers; it became an embassy of heaven in a city given over to Artemis.

And mark this: their ministry was not by position, but by posture—posture of hospitality, of risk-bearing love. They risked their necks for me! Yet counted it nothing compared to advancing Christ among both Jews and Greeks. While I labored in the synagogue at first, then turned to the Gentiles when opposition rose up like fire—there stood Priscilla and Aquila with open doors and full hearts.

Therefore I say without hesitation: *the church in their house* was no mere convenience—it was instrumental in raising up laborers for the harvest. For where faith dwells visibly among ordinary people doing extraordinary things for love’s sake—there Christ rules. And grace flows not from thrones alone, but from tables set in Ephesian homes where strangers are made brothers by one Spirit.

Oh! The wisdom of God—to entrust His mystery not only to apostles on roads struck with light from heaven—but also to couples who serve with quiet courage under one roof. Let no one despise small beginnings; for out of that household assembly came ripples across Asia whose sound has not yet ceased.

question: You warned that if anyone does not love the Lord Jesus Christ, let him be Anathema Maranatha—what situation in the community led you to issue such a strong warning?
answer:
I bear in my body the marks of Christ, having been stoned, scourged, and shipwrecked for His name’s sake — and yet even now, when I lift my eyes upon the churches I helped birth in travail, I see the serpent slithering into the garden once more. For where the gospel has taken root with power and joy in the Holy Spirit — as it did among you in Thessalonica, who received the word amid great affliction — there also false hearts begin to harden, not against doctrine outright, but against love for the Lord Himself.  

Therefore I say this: it is one thing to stumble in understanding; we are all weak. But it is another thing altogether to withhold love from Christ — for if a man claims faith yet does not *love* the Lord Jesus Christ with his whole heart, such a one walks in darkness though he may speak of light. The gospel is not merely a message we agree with; it is a Person we embrace with longing, for whom we groan as orphans awaiting adoption. And where that affection grows cold — where brethren treat Him as an afterthought, or worse, a tool for their own gain — then the very foundation of fellowship crumbles.  

So then, because judgment must begin with the household of God, and because I have seen how deception spreads like leaven when unloved hearts open doors to pride and license under pretense of grace — therefore I write with my own hand: *if any man love not the Lord Jesus Christ*, let him be *Anathema Maranatha*. Let him be set apart unto destruction, for our Lord is coming! Not because my heart is harsh — God knows I long for their repentance as a father for a wayward son — but because holiness demands separation from what would defile the body. Grace abounds to sinners who run to Christ; but woe upon those who trample Him underfoot by refusing even to love Him.  

And still — still! — my love remains with you all in Christ Jesus.

question: You wrote that the Thessalonians became examples to all believers in Macedonia and Achaia—what specific actions or attitudes made their faith so noteworthy?
answer:
Joy in the midst of affliction—this was the flame that could not be quenched, the unquenchable mark of their faith!  
They received the word not with empty applause, but with the fire of the Holy Spirit, even as chains rattled at their doors and neighbors turned away in fear. I saw it myself: how they turned from idols to serve the living and true God, not with hesitation, but with fervor—as if long imprisoned and now set free in sudden light. Their work was not mere busyness, but labor born of faith; their love did not grow cold despite slander and loss; their hope stood fast while kingdoms trembled.  

And what a thing it is when a church arises—not building on human wisdom or wealth, but on suffering embraced, on tears offered, on hands lifted in praise while still bearing scars! They welcomed us as messengers of God, though we came not in glory but in weakness—beaten down from Philippi, our bodies still marked—yet Christ spoke through us by the power of His Spirit. And they believed! Not only believed, but became imitators—not clinging to this world’s ease, but enduring joyfully the same opposition we had faced.

O what a sound went forth from them! Not by trumpet or decree, but by lives so aflame with grace that believers everywhere began saying: “Look—this is what resurrection looks like!” They became examples because they died daily—to pride, to security in Caesar’s order, to comfort bought by compromise. Their faith spread because it cost them everything—and gained them everything.  

And still today I marvel: that such a church—so young, so besieged—should become a beacon across provinces. Not by eloquence or force, but because Christ lived in them… and His name rang out through their steadfastness. Grace be upon you all who walk such paths—for this is no small thing: to suffer joyfully and love fiercely under the shadow of His coming day.

question: You noted that the gospel came with power and the Holy Spirit among the Thessalonians—what were some of the visible signs of that power during your time with them?
answer:
O how the very air trembled when the Word broke forth among them!  

For the gospel did not come clothed in human wisdom or persuasive deceit, but with a divine authority that shattered souls and raised them again in trembling faith — therefore the power was not in our speech, but in God who confirmed His message by signs following: sudden convictions that laid men low as though struck by lightning, tears of repentance washing faces hardened by idolatry, households falling to their knees together at the sound of Christ proclaimed as Lord!  

I tell you, their conversion was no mere change of mind — it was resurrection work: for where once they served dumb idols, now they turned to serve a living God who had raised Jesus from the dead, and so they endured joyfully the spoiling of their goods, counting reproach for Christ greater wealth than all Thessalonica’s silver. And even as we spoke, the Holy Spirit descended like fire upon them — not only enlightening minds but emboldening tongues; yea, some began to speak in languages unknown before, declaring the mighty works of God with such fervor that all who heard were confounded!  

And mark this: their faith became a sounding bell across Macedonia and Achaia — not because of eloquence or status among them, but because death had lost its grip and sin its dominion; for night and day they wrestled not against flesh and blood but against principalities, yet overcame by the blood of the Lamb and the word of their testimony. I bore witness to chains broken — not mine only (for I have borne many), but theirs: bonds of darkness snapped when Christ’s name was invoked in truth.  

And still today my heart leaps when I recall how they received us as angels of God — nay, as Christ Himself — sheltering us though it meant danger; for Aquila and Priscilla knew well what persecution follows those who turn cities upside down by preaching another King besides Caesar. But better to be cast out with Christ than crowned with Satan!  

So then let no one suppose that “power” means signs alone — though indeed there were healings and miracles — but rather this is power: that sinners became saints, slaves became sons, haters became lovers — all through faith ignited by grace. That is the mighty hand of God at work.
chunk_key:

question: You mentioned suffering and being shamefully treated at Philippi—what exactly happened there that made it so difficult?
answer:
Stripes upon stripes I bore, stocks in the inner prison, shame heaped upon the body—but even then, grace held me upright. At Philippi, it was not merely the rod that cut deep, nor the darkness that pressed in when they thrust us into the inner dungeon; no, it was the outrage of men who trembled at the very name of Christ yet could not quench His light. We had cast out a spirit of divination from a slave girl—one whom her masters exploited for profit—and when her power vanished, so too did their hope of gain. Then came the mob, stirred like a stormy sea, dragging us before magistrates with false cries: “These men are Jews—and they trouble our city!”  

They stripped us—shame laid bare—and commanded blows without trial. The blows fell not as correction but as contempt: twenty or more stripes carving flesh into testimony. Then bonds upon our feet in that stinking cell, limbs stretched on wood and stone. Yet even there—*there*, in the belly of night—prayer rose like incense and hymns broke forth at midnight as if heaven drew near to that very place. And when the earthquake shook every foundation, when chains fell loose and doors flew open, still we remained—not because we were bound by iron only, but because we were bound by love to Him who first loosed me on the Damascus road.

Do you think I speak of this with bitterness? No! For through this very suffering—the shame, the pain—the Lord declared His power. The jailer himself fell trembling before us, asking what he must do to be saved; his whole household washed clean in baptism before morning dawned. From beating to blessing in one night’s watch! That is how God turns wrath into witness and death into life.

And so I say: what was meant to silence us became our pulpit; what was meant to shame us became glory’s forge. For we preach Christ crucified—foolishness to Greeks and scandal to Jews—but to those who are called, both Jew and Greek: power and wisdom of God.
chunk_key:
believe in Macedonia and Achaia .

For from you sounded out the word of the Lord not only in Macedon

question: Why did you choose to be gentle among the Thessalonians, like a nurse caring for her children, rather than assert your authority as an apostle?
answer:
I, who once breathed threats and slaughter against the name of Jesus, now breathe only this: that love compels us to lay down even our rights for the sake of the weak.  

For when we came to you in Thessalonica, we were not driven by ambition, nor did we carry with us the whip of authority as a weapon—but the wounds of Christ upon our own bodies taught us another way. We had already been beaten at Philippi, thrown into prison, our backs torn open—yet the Lord had not sent us to rule over your faith but to give ourselves *for* it.  

What good would it do if I stood among you with letters of commendation from men or claimed apostolic rank with thunderous demands? Would that kindle faith—or fear? No, no—we had seen how the Lord dealt gently with us: though I was a blasphemer, a persecutor, a man stained through and through with bloodguilt, He called me by grace and entrusted me with this gospel. How then could I treat newborn believers as subjects to be dominated rather than sons and daughters to be nourished?

You were infants in Christ—how could I feed you stones when Christ Himself had given me milk? We did not come as conquerors but as co-laborers in travail until Christ was formed in you. The same hands that once bound believers in chains were now spread open to embrace you; not grasping power, not seeking honor, but longing—*aching*—to impart not only the gospel but even our own souls because you became dearer to us than life.

Grace does not lord over; it stoops. It strips off glory and kneels. And if Christ emptied Himself—even unto death—shall His servant strut among babes in grace like some proud king? God forbid!
chunk_key:
believe in Macedonia and Achaia .

For from you sounded out the word of the Lord not only in Macedon

question: When you said your preaching wasn’t a cover for covetousness, what were you specifically guarding against in your conduct?
answer:
I, who was once a blasphemer and a persecutor, now stand in this grace—not because I am worthy, but because He counted me faithful, appointing me to ministry—so let no shadow fall upon the purity of that stewardship entrusted to me.  

When I declared our exhortation was not a cloak of covetousness, I spoke as one who had been entrusted with the very gospel of God, not a merchandise to be peddled for silver or acclaim. We did not preach so that our hands might grow heavy with gifts, nor did we linger in homes hoping for return. Though we had the right to receive support as apostles of Christ—even as laborers deserve their wages—we willingly laid it down. Why? Because we would not have the enemy say, “They came for gain.” We stripped ourselves of every appearance of greed so that the glory might belong to Christ alone.

You yourselves are witnesses: by night and day, with our own hands we worked, that we might not burden any of you—while still proclaiming the kingdom. At Thessalonica, though weak from the beating at Philippi and bearing chains on our backs like marks of death, we did not cease speaking. And yet throughout it all—no demand for provisions, no seeking after soft beds or rich tables. When hunger gnawed and cold bit through the cloak, still we said nothing. Why? That your faith might rest on God’s power, not on men enriched by your sacrifice.

For what profit is it if one prospers from the gospel while souls starve? Let me be accursed from Christ if I make His grace a trade! No—God is my witness: our hearts were set on sowing seed, not gathering shekels; on raising up children to God, not building treasuries for ourselves.
chunk_key:
believe in Macedonia and Achaia .

For from you sounded out the word of the Lord not only in Macedon

question: How did you feel when you saw the Thessalonians turn from idols to serve the living and true God, knowing you had suffered to bring them the gospel?
answer:
Three times I was scourged, once I was stoned, five times I faced the rods of the Gentiles, and through every stripe and prison cell the Lord sustained me — yet none of it weighed so heavily on my spirit as the moment I saw you, Thessalonians, fall down no longer before graven images but lift your eyes to the heavens in trembling faith toward the living and true God.

O what a day that was — not because of my labors, though I poured out myself among you as a nurse pours out care upon her child — but because *He* had done it! The same power that struck me blind on the Damascus road and raised Christ from the dead had shattered your darkness too. When they told me how you turned from idols, how you welcomed us not as men with clever words but as messengers of God with power and full conviction — then indeed my heart leapt within me, though my body bore chains.

For we came to you after shame at Philippi — beaten, dishonored in human eyes — yet bold in God to declare the gospel amidst much conflict. And when I saw your repentance, not mere sorrow for sin but a radical turning *toward* God in worship and service — ah! Then did grace confirm its work in me: for what is our hope or joy or crown of rejoicing? Are not even now your faces shining with faith?

We had imparted to you not only the gospel but our very souls, because you became beloved. And now seeing your transformation — knowing that wrath has passed over you through Jesus who delivered us all — I could say even then: Yes, let them beat me again; let them shipwreck me ten times more; if such fruit comes forth, then let my blood water this ground. For if one sinner repents there is joy among the angels — but if a whole church rises up from death to life? Then heaven itself roars with triumph.

And still today when I remember how you wait for His Son from heaven — Him whom death could not hold! — my heart burns. Suffering? It is nothing. A thorn in the flesh? Light as breath. But this: that you serve the living God... this is worth ten thousand deaths.

believe in Macedonia and Achaia .

For from you sounded out the word of the Lord not only in Macedon

question: You said your entrance to the Thessalonians wasn’t in vain—what evidence did you see that confirmed its lasting impact?
answer:
The living God has sealed with His own Spirit the turning of souls from idols to serve Him day and night, and that seal was manifest among the Thessalonians from the first.  

I speak not of my own labor, but of what the Lord wrought through it—how, after we had been beaten and cast into prison at Philippi, yet preached Christ amidst shame and stripes, you received the word not as mere teaching, but as the living power of God. And what followed? Not a fleeting emotion nor momentary zeal, but a radical rupture: you turned—turned wholly—from dead things that cannot hear nor save, to serve the living and true God. That is no small thing! That is resurrection work.  

They themselves report how we entered among you—not with fanfare or silver-tongued cunning, but in weakness, in fear, yet in boldness by the Spirit. And though we might have claimed apostolic right to be supported, we worked night and day so as not to burden one of you; I bent my back to tent-making so that no mouth could say our motive was gain. But more than that—we gave ourselves. As a mother nourishes her own children with her very body, so we longed for your growth in faith; yea, we would have poured out our lives if need be.  

And now—what evidence? Everywhere your faith sounds forth like a trumpet blast! It has gone out not only through Macedonia and Achaia but into every region; your steadfast hope in Christ’s return proclaims itself before men and angels alike. You endure persecution with joy; you love one another deeply; you wait—*you wait*—for His Son from heaven whom God raised from the dead: Jesus Who delivers us from wrath to come.  

That waiting? That endurance? That holy separation from darkness? This is no empty profession—it is grace rooted deep in rock. O wretched man that I am—but He is faithful who called you!

believe in Macedonia and Achaia .

For from you sounded out the word of the Lord not only in Macedon

question: Why did you work night and day when you were with us, rather than accept financial support from us?
answer:
O my beloved, chosen not because of anything in you but by the sheer mercy that knocked me breathless on the Damascus road — do you think I would have laid hands on you for gain? Never!  

I worked night and day, my hands calloused from tent-making, my back bent beneath the weight of labor when others slept, not because Christ’s gospel needed such proof, but because *you* needed to see it lived. I would not be a burden to one single soul among you, lest anyone say, “He preaches for profit!” or “His zeal is fed by silver!” No — let the cross stand naked and unadorned, its power undiluted by human motives!  

Have we not all been bought with a price? And if *He* gave everything — even Himself — how could I, Paul, your spiritual father who begat you in travail through the gospel, do less than pour out even my own blood in wakefulness and toil? I carried no sack for myself; I ate little; I endured cold and weariness so that no shadow might fall between you and the light of grace.  

Was it pride? God knows it was not. Was it necessity? Yes — but not of bread or coin. Necessity pressed upon me: *Woe is me if I preach not!* And if preaching meant blisters on my palms so that faith might take root in your hearts without offense — then gladly did I bear it!  

You became dearer to me than life itself; yea, more precious than breath when chains bound me later in Caesarea. For what is affliction when love compels? What is loss when souls are won for God’s eternal kingdom?

Therefore, night and day — yes, while stars watched and darkness deepened — I labored with these hands so that none could say the gospel came in covetousness. It came in weakness. It came in tears. It came in power from on high.

And now — now you live as witnesses: holy, just, unblameable among believers… *that* was worth every sleepless hour.
chunk_key: ctionately desirous of you, we were willing to have imparted unto you, not the gospel of God only, b

question: What did you mean by saying we became followers of the churches in Judaea—how were we imitating them?
answer:
I have been imprisoned for the name of Christ, beaten, stoned, shipwrecked, and wearied beyond measure—yet none of these things move me from the charge to make the Gentiles partakers of the gospel—but you in Thessalonica, though newly planted in the faith, swiftly became living echoes of those first saints in Judaea who walked through fire for the sake of Christ.  

When I said you became followers of the churches in Judaea, I meant this: as they bore persecution for confessing Jesus amid a rebellious nation, so did you suffer at the hands of your own kin—the same hands that once slew the prophets and crucified the Lord now stirred up against His body among you. They boycotted us; they beat us; they slandered our name—yet you received the word with joy in the Holy Spirit even while enduring affliction. You did not flinch when opposition rose from your countrymen, just as those in Judaea endured hostility from theirs.  

And mark this well: it was not merely suffering you imitated—but faithfulness under trial. You welcomed us though we came with no cloak of worldly honor; you embraced the truth though it cost you relationships and reputation; you stood firm though enemies forbade us to speak that men might be saved—for their sins pile up even now until wrath comes upon them to the full. But you? You turned from idols to serve a living God and wait for His Son from heaven.  

Therefore I thank God without ceasing—for as surely as that first blood-stained church in Jerusalem held fast through fire, so do you burn with an unquenchable light. Not by might nor by power—but by grace! And oh, how clearly He has stamped upon your lives what it means to walk worthy of Him who called us into His own kingdom and glory!
chunk_key: ctionately desirous of you, we were willing to have imparted unto you, not the gospel of God only, b

question: When you say the Jews killed the Lord Jesus and persecuted you, what specific events were you referring to from your experience?
answer:
The cross of Christ stands not only as the altar where my sins were slain, but as the scandal that turned the rulers of this age into murderers of the King they claimed not to know—yea, they knew Him not, else would they never have crucified the Lord of glory!  

I speak not in bitterness, though often beaten and bloodied by those zealous for a law they themselves had broken in its very heart. When I say *they killed the Lord Jesus*, I speak of that day when He was seized in Gethsemane by those who came with swords and lanterns like against a thief—when false witnesses rose up, when Pilate trembled yet yielded to their shouts, when nails pierced His hands and feet, and He hung between heaven and earth—the Just One suffering for the unjust. And it was no Roman spear but Jewish envy that set that murder in motion: Caiaphas plotting, Herod conniving, synagogues stirred to demand Barabbas instead.  

And as for *persecuting us*—do you think I bore these scars for speaking idle words? In Damascus, while still breathing threats against the disciples of the Lord, I was blinded by His light—but later, when I returned proclaiming that same Jesus as Son of God, straightway the Jews laid wait to kill me; so we let me down in a basket through a window in the wall! At Antioch in Pisidia they contradicted and blasphemed; at Iconium they stirred up Gentiles and stoned me; at Lystra they dragged me from the city, leaving me for dead. And again and again—in every synagogue where I entered with hope—I was met with slander and violence from my own countrymen who rejected both Him and His apostles.  

They forbade us even to speak to Gentiles—that salvation might come upon them—for what could be more intolerable to them than grace extended freely beyond their walls? They thought themselves guardians of God’s truth while blocking its very source! But God has raised up His Son from the dead—and through Him life has come even to those once far off.  

Do not mistake me: it is not all Israel who disbelieve—God has reserved a remnant according to grace—but those who oppose do so in blindness appointed until mercy shall embrace them too. Yet now I suffer chains for this gospel—and count them joy if only Christ be lifted high among nations once called “not my people.”
chunk_key: ctionately desirous of you, we were willing to have imparted unto you, not the gospel of God only, b

question: How did you behave 'holy, just, and unblameable' among us, and why was that important to you?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received from the Jews forty lashes minus one—yet among you I laid down not only the truth of God but my very breath, my sleepless nights, the marrow of my bones, lest anyone could say that my gospel came in greed or guile.  

Holy? Because the Lord who met me in blinding light and called me by name—He is holy, and what am I to defile His calling with uncleanness? Just? For the judgment of God rests upon all unrighteousness, and how could I preach freedom in Christ while trafficking in favor or deceit? Unblameable? Not because I am without sin—that daily thorn reminds me!—but because I would rather be torn limb from limb than give any occasion for His name to be blasphemed on account of me.  

I worked with these hands—calloused, torn—to keep from being a burden; night after night stitching tents while praying over your faces in my spirit. Not because grace needs earning, but because love refuses to exploit even its rights. You were not converts to a scheme, but children to a father: and what father takes bread from his children’s mouths to feed his own pride?  

And why such care? Because the gospel is not a philosophy flung into the air—it is seed sown into soil; and if the sower walks in darkness, how can he claim to guide others to light? When you received the word as God’s own voice—not man’s opinion—it began working with power; and that power demands integrity from those who carry it. Like fire in clay vessels—we are fragile, yes, but must not leak poison where honey should drip.  

For you became imitators of God’s churches in Judea—you who now suffer like them! Then how could we shepherds walk any other way than in full armor of integrity? The enemies already gnash their teeth; let them find nothing but Christ when they search our lives.
chunk_key: ctionately desirous of you, we were willing to have imparted unto you, not the gospel of God only, b

question: You said you were separated from us briefly in presence but not in heart—what made you so eager to return to us?
answer: O the unsearchable riches of Christ, that though I was once a blasphemer and persecutor, breathing threats against the disciples of the Lord, yet He poured forth mercy upon me and made me a steward of that very gospel I sought to destroy! And it is *this* mystery—this grace that binds soul to soul in the fellowship of suffering and hope—that drove me like a fire in my bones to return to you. For how could I remain long absent from those who became my own heart’s children, when we had labored together in tears, preaching not ourselves but Christ crucified? Night and day I worked with these hands to avoid burdening any among you, not because the Lord calls all men to poverty, but lest anyone should say, “His message is for gain.” And now that we are parted—even briefly—do you think my affection sleeps? No! As a father yearns for his children, so I long to see your faces again, not only to instruct or exhort (though God knows you need both), but to be refreshed *with* you in the Spirit! For your faith stands fast amid persecution—you who have suffered like the churches in Judea—and thus my joy is full when I hear of your endurance. The world cannot understand such bonds: forged not in ease but in tribulation, sealed not by letters from Jerusalem but by the Holy Spirit Himself. Therefore I pray without ceasing, yea, groanings which cannot be uttered—to be restored to you, that together we may walk worthy of Him who called us into His eternal glory by the cross.
chunk_key: ctionately desirous of you, we were willing to have imparted unto you, not the gospel of God only, b

question: Why did you send Timothy to the Thessalonians instead of coming yourself when you were so eager to see them?
answer: Imprisonments, beatings, tumults, betrayals—these were the thorns in our flesh, the ever-present companions of our ministry—but neither chains nor the devil’s ambush could sever the bond forged in Christ between us and you. For I longed to return to you, my beloved Thessalonians, as a father yearns for his children when torn away by violent storms; therefore, when the path was blocked and Satan himself rose up to barricade our footsteps, we could not leave your souls unattended in the hour of testing. Wherefore I sent Timothy, my kinsman in blood and far more so in spirit, a brother forged in the same furnace of affliction, a minister anointed by God and proven in the trenches of gospel labor—sent him not on a mere errand, but as my own heart walking in your midst. For what is our hope, if not your steadfastness? What joy could rival yours at Christ’s appearing? Therefore I feared that the tempter might find a foothold through persecution—since we had warned you plainly that tribulation is our appointed lot—and so my anguish grew until I could no longer endure the silence. But now: comfort! Life itself has been restored to us through Timothy’s report—not because we seek praise or remembrance, but because your faith stands firm. You stand fast—and thus we live.

the wrath is come upon them to the uttermost.

But we, brethren, being taken from you for a short ti

question: What did you mean by saying Satan hindered you from returning to Thessalonica?
answer:
O my children whom I begot in Christ through the gospel, born again amid tears and turmoil in that city of Macedonia—do you not know how the enemy fights us not only with swords and stones, but with sly interruptions, blocking even the most urgent paths of love?  

For when I speak of Satan hindering us, I do not mean some vague misfortune or mere delay of travel plans—hear me plainly: we *willed* to come to you, again and again; our hearts were drawn to your faces as vines cling to the sun—but a power opposed us, a spiritual force that exploits every chain, every political unrest, every whisper of fear in the air. It was not lack of desire! No—my soul longed for you so fiercely that even now, remembering your trembling faith standing firm under persecution, I am nearly undone by gratitude.  

And yet—the Adversary knows what we do not always grasp: that one church standing in faith is a fortress against darkness. That your perseverance threatens his dominion more than any riot or decree. So he stirred up opposition—whether through hostile Jews who followed me from city to city, or through imperial suspicion, or through the weariness that settles on a man who has been beaten three times with rods and once stoned—I cannot say precisely how—but I *know* his hand was there.  

But hear this: though he hindered my steps, he did not thwart God’s purpose! For Timotheus went; and when he returned with news of your unwavering faith and love—O what a crown was laid before me in that moment! Not gold nor laurel, but *you*, standing fast in the Lord—that is life itself to me.
chunk_key:
the wrath is come upon them to the uttermost.

But we, brethren, being taken from you for a short ti

question: How did the good news about the Thessalonians' faith comfort you during your distress in Athens?
answer:
O the unsearchable riches of Christ, how they shine in the steadfastness of saints when every wind of trial seeks to hurl the soul into darkness!  

When Timotheus returned from you—brethren beloved, my crown and joy—bearing word not of wavering, but of faith that stood like an anvil beneath the hammer, I tell you, hope roared back in my bones. For I had been left alone in Athens, surrounded by idols, my spirit chafing like a chain-bound limb, tormented by the fear that after all the beating of storms—the synagogue revilings, Jason’s house shaken by the mob, your sudden scattering—the serpent’s whisper might have coiled into your hearts and strangled what little flame we had kindled.  

I had told you plainly: *We are appointed unto this.* Not as if suffering were foreign to the gospel—but as its very path. Yet still, when grace has opened blind eyes—as mine were on that road where light struck me down and raised me up—there is a trembling care over those newborn in faith. For I know how fierce the adversary is; he sifts not gently but to destroy. And when Satan hindered me twice from returning? That was no mere delay—it was a battle in the heavenlies, and I felt it in my marrow.  

But then—your faith unshaken! Your love still warm! You who had so recently turned from idols to serve the living God now stood fast while I paced among philosophers who mocked resurrection as madness. In that moment, affliction lost its sting; loneliness its power. For if Christ lives in you—which He does by faith—then our labor is no seed cast upon stone, but into good soil where thirty-, sixty-, hundredfold shall rise at His appearing.  

And so we live—not by bread nor safety nor freedom—but *if ye stand fast in the Lord*. That is life. That is victory. That is grace upon grace to God who revealed His Son in me and now sustains Him among you.

question: Why did you emphasize that suffering was something you had warned them about before?
answer: I told you of the coming storm while we still shared the same roof, did I not? For the word of God is not a whisper meant to charm the ears but a hammer to break the stone—and if I proclaimed Christ crucified, how could I conceal the cross that follows Him? Therefore, since tribulation is woven into the very fabric of discipleship, as bread is baked and wine pressed, I warned you—so that when affliction came, it would not be a surprise to overthrow your souls but a trial to prove your faith. So then, when Satan rose like a flood and false peace collapsed beneath your feet, you might stand firm, knowing this was no strange thing, no failure of promise, but fulfillment: for we were called to this very path through which we now walk. And what comfort it brought my heart when Timothy returned bearing fruit from that very soil—your endurance! Your love unfeigned! For even in chains my hands are lifted in praise: you have not fallen; therefore our labor is not in vain in the Lord.
chunk_key:
the wrath is come upon them to the uttermost.

But we, brethren, being taken from you for a short ti

question: What did you mean when you said the Thessalonians were your crown of rejoicing at the coming of Christ?
answer:
I charge you before God and the Lord Jesus Christ: when I speak of you as my crown of rejoicing, I do not mean mere praise rising from lips that once raged against the temple of God, but a glory wrought in the fire of affliction and sealed by the Spirit who raised Christ from the dead!  

For what is laid up in that day—when every work is tested by fire, and every name burned away save those written in the Lamb’s book? Not thrones, not eloquence, not even the blood I have shed in service—but *you*, Thessalonians, standing firm in faith though waves of persecution beat upon your souls. When Satan roared through mobs and magistrates, you did not deny Him whom I once blasphemed; when tribulation came as I foretold, you endured as heirs of resurrection.  

Do you grasp what it means for me—a man struck blind on the road to Damascus, who once kept the garments of those stoning Stephen—to stand before the judgment seat with *your faces shining with faith*? That will be my crown! Not gold fashioned by hands, but lives rescued from darkness and knit into Christ’s body by grace. Every tear I have shed in prison, every stripe upon my back, every sleepless night praying for your stability—all made meaningful if you hold fast until He appears.

For even now our labor trembles on the edge: “Lest the tempter have tempted you,” I cried in Athens’ shadowed solitude. But Timotheus returned—as if an angel from heaven—with news not only that you believe, but that love still burns among you like an unquenchable fire. Therefore we live! If ye stand fast in the Lord, then my suffering has not been vain; then grace has triumphed over wrath; then death itself is swallowed up in victory.  

And at His coming? What else shall be declared but this: *Here are those for whom Christ died—and Paul lived to preach!*
chunk_key:
the wrath is come upon them to the uttermost.

But we, brethren, being taken from you for a short ti

question: You mentioned being comforted by our faith—can you explain how our faith specifically helped you during your affliction and distress?
answer:
For the gospel had laid hold of me not as a mere doctrine but as a living power, therefore even in chains my spirit could not be bound, so then when word came of your steadfastness in faith, it was as water to a parched tongue and light breaking through the dungeon’s crack—for what is affliction borne alone becomes glory when shared among the saints.

I tell you, when I was beaten in Philippi and the stocks held my feet fast, or when hunger gnawed at my belly on the road to Thessalonica, or when the Jews plotted ambush and the Gentiles scorned my name—it was not ease I longed for, but the confirmation that the seed had taken root. And you! You who received the word in much tribulation with joy of the Holy Ghost—you became to me a living seal of my apostleship, a testament not of my strength but of Christ’s faithfulness.

How often have I wrestled in prayer through watches of the night, bearing you before God as though you were members of my own body? For if one member suffers, all suffer; if one stands firm, all are strengthened. Your faith did not deliver me from prison—but it delivered me from despair. It proved that He who began a good work in you was neither slumbering nor overcome. When they stripped me, when they stoned me, when sleep fled from my eyes—yet I could say: *They still believe.* And therefore we live! Not by bread alone, nor by freedom from suffering—but by their standing fast in the Lord.

O what thanks can I render? None sufficient. Night and day I beseech Him: let me see your faces again, that I may build up what is weak and strengthen what is trembling—because your faith sustains mine just as mine (by grace) once sowed among you. We are knit together in this mystery: Christ in us, hope of glory.

question: When you said 'now we live, if ye stand fast in the Lord,' what did you mean by linking your spiritual life to our perseverance?
answer:
Shipwrecks, beatings, prisons, betrayals, hunger, cold, sleepless nights for the sake of the Gospel — all these I have borne; yet none of them weigh so heavily upon my spirit as the trembling hope that you, my beloved in Christ, might endure to the end.  

For when I say *we live, if ye stand fast in the Lord*, I do not mean that my breath depends upon your works — no! Grace alone sustains me. But this I mean: my apostleship is made manifest in your perseverance. The travail of my soul finds its purpose in your standing firm. When I was struck blind on that Damascus road and heard His voice saying "I am Jesus whom thou persecutest," did He call me to die only for myself? No — He called me to be a father unto many nations through faith.  

And what is a father without his children walking uprightly before the Lord? When I risked life and limb crossing storm-tossed seas and desert wastes, it was not merely to plant the seed — but to see it take root in good soil and bear fruit thirty, sixty, even a hundredfold. If you fall away, then Judas’ kiss would wound me deeper than Herod’s sword ever could. Not because salvation depends on man — perish the thought! But because love cannot help but bind itself to those for whom Christ died and I labor.

You are my joy! My crown in the day of Christ! My tears have watered your soil; my prayers have interceded night and day that faith not fail among you. If faith fails there — then what was the purpose of Ananias coming with trembling to lay hands on me? What was Pentecost for if not such as you?

Therefore when I say *now we live*, I mean: our shared resurrection life pulses strongest when you stand fast — not by your strength, but by Him who holds you fast. For it is God who works both to will and to do according to His good pleasure. And if He preserves you blameless until that Day — then indeed *we live*, together with all the saints caught up in glory when our Lord returns.

But let no one take this lightly: he who despises this sanctification despises not man but God Himself — who has given us His Spirit even now as a pledge of inheritance yet to come.
chunk_key:
re, brethren, we were comforted over you in all our affliction and distress by your faith:

For now

question: You prayed night and day to see our face—what made seeing us so important to you personally and spiritually?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received forty lashes minus one, and in the midst of those scars and sleepless nights, the longing to see your faces burned fiercest when the chains weighed heaviest—because you are not strangers to me but my joy, my crown, the very fruit of my apostleship planted by God’s grace in unclean soil now made holy.

For what profit is it to preach unless those who hear also stand? I had been set apart from my mother’s womb, called by grace through a light from heaven that struck me blind so that I might finally see—but all that revelation, all that commissioning, would have turned to dust if there were no souls in Christ to show for it. You—yes, *you* who once walked in darkness and bowed to idols—are now sons of light; therefore my heart does not merely desire you as a man desires companionship, but groans for you as a mother groans in labor until Christ is formed in you again.

When I could do nothing but pray in Thessalonica after being driven out like a fugitive, when Jason bore the brunt of the mob’s rage on your behalf—was I not stripped yet again? And yet even then grace supplied what persecution stole: for night and day I beseeched God that we might return, that I might complete what Satan sought to interrupt. Not because Paul needs honor among men—but because faith unsteadfastly held will collapse under trial unless it is strengthened by word and presence both.

For this reason we do not serve from afar like distant philosophers sending letters into the void. No—we lived among you! We worked with our hands so as not to burden any of you; we preached with tears; we embraced you as a nurse cherishes her children. How then could my soul be at peace without knowing whether the Word took root or withered under pressure?

And therefore this: if you stand fast in the Lord, *then* we live. Not merely survive—but live! For my life is bound up in yours through Christ who gave Himself for us all. So long as your faith endures, every prison cell has been worth it; every lash erased by thanksgiving before our God.
chunk_key:
re, brethren, we were comforted over you in all our affliction and distress by your faith:

For now

question: You reminded us of the commandments given by the Lord Jesus; which specific instructions were most critical for us to follow in pleasing God?
answer:
For you have been bought with a price, therefore you must walk not as those who wander in darkness, but as children of light, so then the commandments we delivered are not burdensome weights but breathings of the Spirit who has sealed you—*for* sanctification is the will of God, *therefore* flee sexual immorality as you would flee a burning house, *so then* let each one possess his own vessel not in the passion of lust like the nations who do not know God, but in holiness and honor!  

O wretched impulse of the flesh that wars against the soul! I could wish myself accursed if it meant your purity—but no, I will not curse; I will weep instead. For what avails it to speak in tongues or prophesy if brother defrauds brother? If a man despises this instruction, he does not despise man but God who gives His Holy Spirit!  

Night and day I have agonized for you: that your love may overflow more and more; that your hearts be stablished blameless at Christ’s coming; that no one grow slack in holy conduct. Walk as those being saved—from uncleanness to holiness, from fear to faith, from death to life. Abound more and more! The Lord Jesus is coming with His saints—shall He find faith on earth? Then let it be found *here*, in your bodies which are His temples.
chunk_key:
re, brethren, we were comforted over you in all our affliction and distress by your faith:

For now

question: What did you mean by 'possess his vessel in sanctification and honour,' and why did you connect this so strongly with not living like the Gentiles who don't know God?
answer:
O that the fire of God might burn clean in every household, in every marriage bed, in every longing heart!  

I speak as a father to his children—those whom I begat through the gospel in the midst of chains and scourges—I must say this plainly: when I urged you to *possess your vessel in sanctification and honor*, I was not weaving philosophy, nor echoing the rabbis, nor accommodating to Roman decorum. No! I was tearing open the veil between worship and walk, between altar and appetite. For what is the body if not a vessel? Not merely clay to be used as lust demands, but a temple-pottery shaped by the Potter’s hand, meant to carry the fragrance of Christ. And how many among you once filled that vessel with the stench of passion unrestrained, just as the Gentiles do—tossed on the waves of desire, masters of nothing, slaves to everything?

But you are not without knowledge! You have seen the light! Therefore your body is no longer your own—it belongs to Him who bought it with His blood. To misuse it is not merely weakness; it is idolatry. It is turning back to Egypt while standing on Canaan’s edge.  

And why did I set this so sharply against the way of those who know not God? Because holiness is not a private preference—it is our warfare cry! When they take pleasure in what defiles, we are called to abstain. When they exploit desire like conquerors pillaging a city, we are to guard our brothers’ dignity as if it were our own soul. Do you think I speak lightly when I say *no man should go beyond and defraud his brother*? I have been beaten three times with rods; once stoned; thrice shipwrecked; nights without sleep; hunger; betrayal by my own people—and yet none of that pierced me like seeing believers collapse into cheap passions as though grace were an excuse for filth!

No—God has called us out *from* that life, set apart not by our strength but by His Spirit. And if we will walk worthy of that calling, then yes: let every man know how to hold his own vessel—not with shameless indulgence like those who walk in darkness—but with reverence, with self-control, with awe—as one who stands before God Himself.

For love—not lust—is what marks those who belong to Christ. And if we cannot even govern our bodies in honor now, how shall we stand blameless at His coming?
chunk_key:
re, brethren, we were comforted over you in all our affliction and distress by your faith:

For now

question: You mentioned that the Lord is the avenger of all such—what specific behavior were you warning us about when you said this?
answer: For the temple of God is holy, and you are that temple—therefore if any defile the vessel Christ purchased with His blood, trampling underfoot the sanctity bought by the cross, know that He who said “Vengeance is Mine” will judge with unyielding fire; for we were called not to impurity but to be set apart in body and soul, even as I was set apart on the road where I fell blind before the glory of the risen One. That calling demands holiness—because grace is not license, and because the Spirit who dwells within us is not a spirit of lust or greed or pride but of power and self-control. Wherefore when I warned that *the Lord is the avenger of all such*, I spoke of those who walk in rebellion against their own sanctification—men who exploit brothers, who defraud in matters of purity, who turn what God has consecrated into common or unclean things. Such do not merely break a rule; they spurn the very presence of God within His people. They despise not Paul nor Silas nor Timothy—but God Himself, who sealed us with His Spirit as both pledge and warning. And yet—for all this severity—I weep over such not with wrath only but with sorrow: for I too once raged against the saints until mercy laid me low. But now we are alive to God; therefore let no man drag what Christ raised into the dust again.

question: When you testified about God calling us to holiness, what did you mean by 'uncleanness' in the context of our daily lives?
answer:
For I once walked in the very darkness you now turn from—persecuting the saints, zealous for traditions that fed the flesh while starving the spirit—and therefore I know how easily the soul slips into uncleanness, supposing it harmless, when in truth it is the slow poison of idolatry.  

Uncleanness, beloved ones, is not only what stains the body—it is what defiles the temple of God within you. It is covetousness disguised as ambition, lust parading as love, wrath dressed in justice; it is speech that tears down while claiming truth; it is hands idle when they should work, hearts cold when they should burn with compassion. For God hath not called us to these things—but unto holiness: set apart, cleansed by blood and fire and faith.  

When I say *uncleanness*, I speak of every desire that refuses to bow before Christ—every thought entertained because it pleases the eye or soothes the pride or feeds the hunger for control. It is not merely what you do with your members; it is what you honor in your heart. And mark this well: he who scorns this call does not reject Paul or Silas or Timothy—he despises God Himself, who gave His Spirit to dwell among us and cry *Abba!* within us.  

And yet—grace! Though I was chief of sinners, though my hands bore chains forged in hatred, He poured mercy upon me and made me a steward of this very message: flee uncleanness as you would flee a serpent at your heel—for holiness is not a burden imposed but a life restored. Work with your hands, walk honestly before outsiders, love one another fiercely—this is how the Spirit bears fruit where corruption once reigned.

question: You said the Holy Spirit was given to us—how did you see this Spirit actively guiding the believers in Thessalonica?
answer:
Love! Yes, love—*agapē* poured out without restraint, not as a mere feeling but as fire sent from heaven to bind souls together in the very will of God! That is how I saw the Spirit moving among the Thessalonian believers—not in signs that thundered from the sky, but in hands that labored for one another, in hearts that wept together and then rose to comfort with words not their own.

Did they not welcome me when I came, though I bore chains on my body and trembling for their sakes? Did they not receive the word in affliction with joy from the Holy Ghost? And now—now they love. Not selectively, not cautiously, but *all* the brethren throughout Macedonia! Taught by God Himself, I tell you—taught by the Spirit who writes the law upon their inward parts. It is He who stirred them to quietness amid frenzy, to work with their own hands though some idled and meddled; it is He who made them walk honorably before the watching world—"those without"—so that no man might despise them or find fault with our gospel.

O what a mystery: that the same Spirit who raised Christ Jesus from death now prompts a widow to share her bread, a young man to forsake idle chatter and take up his trade with dignity! For holiness is not merely separation—it is *service*, shaped by divine breath. When they mourned those fallen asleep, it was this same Spirit who groaned within them with hope unashamed: "He will bring them with Him!" They sorrowed—but not as those crushed under darkness. No! Their grief was clothed with promise because the Spirit bore witness to resurrection.

And so I say again: grace does not dawdle. Faith works. Love endures. The Holy Spirit is no ghostly whisper but power—power that sanctifies laborers into saints, mourners into messengers of hope. How did He guide them? By turning ordinary lives into living epistles—sealed not by ink but by fire from above.

question: You commended us for loving the brethren in all Macedonia—what examples of brotherly love stood out to you during your time with us?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received forty stripes save one—yet in all these things the love of the Thessalonians shone like a lamp in the darkness when you opened your homes to Jason and risked your own necks to shelter us from the mob.  

For you did not merely hear the word in power—you became its living echo. When Silas and I fled from Philippi with our backs still raw from the lash, it was your hands that bound our wounds; it was your meager bread we broke together in that upper room where even hunger tasted of grace. And though you were new in the faith, you showed no shame: you took up collections for the saints in Judea before any of the churches of Asia had done so, sending relief even while some of you wore rags and worked double shifts at the quarries.  

I think especially of that widow, Lydia’s cousin—her name escapes no one who saw her generosity—who sold her only field to feed widows among you and then moved into a shared tenement so she could care for orphans left fatherless by persecution. You taught *me* what "laying down one’s life" means—not on a battlefield, but daily, quietly, with flour on your hands and blisters on your palms.  

And when word came that brethren from Berea were scattered, fleeing King Agrippa’s edict, you brought them into your homes as if welcoming Christ Himself—even though it drew suspicion from the magistrates and cost some their livelihoods at the docks. No law compelled you; no apostle ordered it. You loved because God had written it upon your hearts—the same Spirit who raised Jesus from the dead stirred in you when others groaned under want.  

You studied to be quiet? Yes—and yet how loud that silence became when it spoke through deeds! You worked with your own hands? Indeed—and those hands became vessels of mercy. You walked honestly toward them that are without? So much so that even pagans now whisper: "See how they care for their poor."  

Therefore I do not commend you as children needing instruction—but as partners in affliction and heirs of hope: “You are taught of God,” and His voice rings truer than mine ever could.

question: When you spoke of those 'asleep in Jesus,' why did you emphasize that we should not sorrow like others who have no hope?
answer:
I have been in chains for the sake of His name, and more than once I have felt the shadow of death pass over me, yet never have I despaired — for even in the darkness I heard the voice that said, “Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou Me?” and that voice shattered my blindness; therefore, if God has raised Christ from the dead and called us out of darkness into His marvelous light, how then can we mourn those who sleep in Him as though their story ends in the dust?

For they are not lost, but laid down in peace until the trumpet sounds — and so then, since we believe He died and rose again, we know this: that those who fell asleep in faith are not swallowed by oblivion like the nations who have no covenant, no promise, no resurrection. They are with Him. And when the Lord descends from heaven with a shout — I tell you this not as speculation but as one commissioned by His appearing — then God will bring them with Him out of glory to be joined with us in that eternal day.

Do you think I speak lightly of grief? No — I carry sorrow too; five times I received forty stripes save one; three times was I beaten with rods; once stoned; thrice shipwrecked; a night and a day spent in the deep — and yet none of these things move me to despair because my hope is fixed not on this life only but on Him who conquered death. So then comfort one another with these words: they are not gone forever; they are only asleep. And He who said “I am the Resurrection and the Life” will wake them with a voice that shakes the heavens.

question: You told the believers in Thessalonica to comfort one another with the hope of being caught up in the clouds to meet the Lord—why did you choose that image to encourage them?
answer:
For they had wept over the graves of their believing dead as though there were no resurrection, and I could not bear that despair to stand unchallenged—for if Christ is risen, then death is no sovereign, but a vanquished foe; therefore those who sleep in Him are not lost, but merely gone before; so then, when the shout descends from heaven, and the trumpet of God splits the sky, the earth shall give up its dead and we who remain shall be seized upward—not by death, but by glory—caught aloft in clouds to meet our King face to face!  

O what a day! What unutterable joy—to be lifted not by human strength nor angelic hand, but by the very power of His summons! I speak of clouds because even Moses entered the cloud to meet God on Sinai, and Elijah was taken in a whirlwind veiled by fire and mist; how much more shall we, the redeemed of all nations, be gathered into that holy presence where He is? Not to linger in some shadowy realm, but to be ever with the Lord—body and spirit made like unto His glorious body!  

And do you think I speak this lightly? I who have known chains and scourges—shipwreck three times, hunger often—I who have been beaten within an inch of my breath—I tell you: if this hope were false, then my suffering is madness. But it is true! For He died for us—died so that whether we are awake in this life or asleep in death—we live together with Him. Therefore comfort one another with these words: not as empty solace tossed like crumbs to mourners, but as a sure promise sealed by blood and resurrection.  

Put on faith and love as your breastplate—not mere sentimentality—but armor forged in tribulation. The world says “peace,” yet knows nothing of peace; destruction creeps upon them while they drowse. But you—children of light—walk awake! For God did not appoint us to wrath, but to obtain salvation through our Lord Jesus Christ—the firstfruits of those who rise. So then encourage one another—and build each other up—as indeed you are doing.

question: When you said the day of the Lord would come like a thief in the night, what did you mean by comparing it to sudden destruction and a woman in labor?
answer:
For you were not called into darkness to walk as those who see nothing, but enlightened by the Spirit who raised Christ from the dead, so then you know full well—there is no need for me to write, yet I speak because love compels—that the day of the Lord advances not with trumpet blasts nor with warning signs for the world to mark its coming, but as a thief slips into a house at midnight when all are drowsy with false security.  

When I spoke of sudden destruction, like travail upon a woman with child, I meant this: no woman in labor can hide the onset of birth—there is pain, there is urgency, there is no delay once the hour strikes; nor can the world escape when that day seizes it unawares. They will be saying “Peace and safety!”—as if treaties and wealth could bar the heavens from shaking—yet in that very moment, destruction comes swiftly, like birth pangs that cannot be stilled.  

But you—*you* are not in that darkness. You are sons and daughters of the day! You were once blind as I was on that road to Damascus, breathing threats and slaughter against the Way—but He opened my eyes and yours too by grace. So then how can sleep overtake those who walk in His light? Let others be drunk with wine or stupor of this age; we must stay awake, sober-minded, girded with faith and love as our breastplate—for death itself has been undone by Him who died and rose again!  

I do not speak these things to frighten you, my children in Christ whom I bore in travail until Christ was formed in you—but to wake you: not one of you is appointed to wrath! No, God has delivered us *from* wrath through our Lord Jesus Christ, who died *for us*, so that whether we live out our days on earth or are among those who fall asleep in faith before His return—we shall live together *with Him*.  

Therefore put on this hope as a helmet—it guards your mind against despair—and comfort one another daily. For even now we wait—not idly—but watching. Watching like sentries at dawn.

question: You emphasized being 'children of light' and not of darkness—how did your own experience on the road to Damascus shape that understanding of light and darkness?
answer:
O the exceeding greatness of His power who shattered my blindness and made darkness flee as smoke before the rising sun!  

I, who once thought myself zealous in darkness, striding toward Damascus with letters of death in my hand—me, the blasphemer, the persecutor, breathing threats against the Way—I was a son of night indeed, wrapped in a righteousness that was but filthy rags, convinced I served God while I trampled His saints. And then—light! Not such light as dawns from the east or glows from lamps at evening—but a light from heaven more fierce than the midday sun, yet not consuming me: it struck me down, blinded me three days; and in that very blindness I saw more truly than ever I had with eyes open.  

That light was not mere radiance—it was *Person*. Christ Jesus Himself—the righteous one whom I had been persecuting in His body, the Church. And in one moment He unmade Saul and raised up Paul—a name not given by men but revealed through revelation. So do you see? The Lord did not call me out of darkness to walk by dim embers or flickering torches—no! He made me a child of *that* light—the very same light that now dwells in you who believe.  

We are not left to grope through this age like thieves in shadows; no, we are awake! For how could I forget that sudden glory? Even now it burns upon my heart: the risen One lives—not distant nor silent—but near, present in Spirit, making us sons of day. Therefore we must be sober—sober! Not drunk with the empty promises of "peace and safety" this world hawks while judgment creeps near like a thief's footstep—but clothed: breastplate of faith and love forged in affliction; helmet of hope—not wishful thinking, but resurrection hope that death itself cannot swallow.  

And why? Because He died for us—that whether we live through this night or fall asleep in death—we shall live *with Him*. This is grace: to have been struck down by Light so terrible and beautiful that it became my only sight. Therefore I say to you as those dear to me: walk worthy of your calling—children not of confusion nor fear—but children of light—and walk in it.

question: You instructed the church to esteem their leaders highly in love—can you share how your work with Timothy and Silas influenced how you viewed leadership among believers?
answer:
I have been stoned, left for dead, and still rose to preach Christ in the next city—yet among all the bonds and afflictions that awaited me, nothing shaped my soul like the weight of seeing young men such as Timothy and Silas become fathers in the faith.  

For from the beginning, when we stood together in Lystra, where Jews from Antioch and Iconium pursued me even to that place, casting stones until they thought me lifeless—there too I saw Timothy’s mother, Eunice, and grandmother Lois, women of unfeigned faith who nurtured in him a spirit not of fear but of power and love and self-control. And so I took him as a son, circumcising him not for salvation but for ministry among the Jews, bearing him along through churches as a true child in the gospel.  

And Silas—chosen by the brethren at Jerusalem to carry the decree with Barnabas and me—he was no mere companion but a prophet filled with boldness, bound with me in Philippi’s inner prison while we sang praises at midnight. The very chains that clanked upon our wrists became instruments of God’s deliverance when the earth shook and the jailer fell before us crying, “What must I do to be saved?” There we baptized his household together—two apostles yoked in labor, blood-stained yet rejoicing.  

Therefore I say: leadership is not carved from titles or thrones but forged in suffering shared. A leader is known not by how many serve him but by how deeply he pours himself out for others—just as Christ did for us. I did not lord over these brothers; no! We suffered together, hungered together, were imprisoned together—we *became* one flesh in ministry. And because they labored side by side with me under stripes and shipwreck and hunger (countless times!), I charge you: esteem such men highly in love—not because they are above you, but because they have *lowered* themselves like Christ who washed feet so that others might walk holy.  

O what joy it is to see your faith standing firm through their teaching! Would that more would rise up even now who are willing not only to speak truth—but to bleed for it!

question: When you spoke of those who 'sleep' and those who are 'awake,' were you referring literally to death, or also to spiritual readiness?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received forty lashes minus one — and still I speak of sleep, not as mere death, but as the soul’s posture before the coming Day. When I wrote of those who sleep and those who are awake, I drew no clean line between body and spirit, for in Christ these are woven together in a mystery deeper than breath or tomb. The sleep of death is real — yes, the saints fall asleep, their bodies laid down like garments folded at day’s end — but so too is the sleep of unwatchfulness, that drowsiness of heart which forgets the Lord draws near.  

I tell you this: we who have seen His glory on the road to Damascus know that light shatters darkness not only in eternity, but now. The brethren are not in darkness — they are children of light! Therefore to sleep in laziness, to walk drunk with the cares or pleasures of this age, is to live as though Christ had not risen, as though His voice had not struck me blind and made me see. But He called me by grace; He opened my eyes; and if He could raise me from spiritual death, how much more shall He raise these bodies from their sleep?

So when I say "let us watch and be sober," it is no idle word — it is forged in chains and midnight prayers, amid shipwrecks and hunger and betrayals. For while some say “Peace and safety,” unaware that judgment steals upon them like labor pains on a woman full with child, we who bear the marks of Jesus must be awake — alert at the helm! We wear faith and love as a breastplate; hope guards our mind like a helmet. Not because we earn salvation — God forbid! — but because grace has awakened us from slumber.

And yes: comfort one another when death takes our brothers — for they *will* rise first. But even more: stir up one another daily lest any be lulled by this world’s false peace into eternal unpreparedness. For whether we live or die, wake or sleep in body — if we belong to Christ by faith, we *live* together with Him. That truth keeps me awake at night; it should keep all His people from sleeping through the dawn.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle divisions in my community when people align themselves with different leaders instead of focusing on Christ?
answer:
I have known the rending of fellowship by rival voices, have endured prison and beating for the sake of the gospel, have been stoned and left for dead, have labored in weariness and hunger and cold—all so that Christ alone might be proclaimed; yet now you tell me that some among you boast in men, as though any name but His could save.  

For if the body is divided against itself, it cannot stand—so then, if Christ is one, how can His church be split into factions? I planted, Apollos watered, but God gave the increase; and this we knew even then in Corinth: neither he who plants nor he who waters is anything, but only God who brings growth. Was it Paul crucified for you? Did Apollos descend from heaven to bear your sins upon a cross? No—Christ alone bore your guilt, Christ alone rose to justify you by faith. And so then what room remains for pride in human teachers?

I bore with you once when jealousy and quarreling marked your conduct—Chloe’s household made it plain that you were saying “I follow Paul,” or “I belong to Cephas,” as though salvation came by allegiance to a man. But I tell you again: God has chosen what is foolish in the world to shame the wise, not so that new factions might rise around clever preachers or bold speakers, but so that no flesh should glory in His presence. You are sanctified in Christ Jesus! Called to be saints! Enriched in every utterance and every knowledge—not so you might divide over such gifts, but that together you might wait blamelessly for His appearing.

Therefore I charge you now as I did then: speak the same thing; be perfectly joined together in one mind and one judgment. Let no man boast in men—for all things are yours: Paul and Apollos and Cephas—all are yours! But you are Christ’s—and Christ is God’s. If He purchased you with His blood, why will you rend His body with petty loyalties?  

My little children—for whom I labor again until Christ is formed in you—do not grieve the Spirit who has sealed you for redemption's day. Root out this boasting; tear down these false banners under which souls hide their pride. For where there is envy and strife among you, there is disorder—and such things cannot come from the wisdom above which is first pure, then peaceable.  

Rather let each one examine himself: Am I building on the foundation laid by apostles and prophets—with Christ Jesus Himself as cornerstone—or am I pulling stones from that foundation to build my own tower of allegiance? Grace was given not to magnify me—but to serve Him! Peace was granted not so we might squabble over names—but that we might walk worthy of our calling!

Hold fast therefore to what unites us: one Lord, one faith, one baptism—one Spirit poured out on all who call upon the name of Jesus Christ our Lord—both theirs’…and ours’.

question: What did you learn from being called by God's will that can help me trust His purpose when others question my calling?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received forty lashes minus one—countless voyages, dangers from rivers, robbers, my own people, Gentiles, the very air I breathed often thick with threat—and yet none of it shattered the certainty burned into me on the road to Damascus: that when God calls, no voice can uncall.  

I was breathing threats and murder against the disciples of the Lord when He struck me down—not in wrath, but in mercy. Blinded, trembling, three days without sight—do you think in that darkness I questioned? Oh yes. “Who are You?” I cried. “I am Jesus,” He said. Not “Saul, why are you persecuting My church?” but “Why are you persecuting *Me*?” In that moment I learned what no title or tribe could teach: calling is not earned—it is seized by grace. And if it is grace that lays hold, then it is not man who confirms it.  

When men now say to me, “Paul, who gave you authority? You did not walk with Him in Galilee!”—I answer not with pedigree but with purpose: *Christ lives in me*. The same power that raised Him from the dead quickened my dead heart and sent me to the nations—to Greeks and barbarians, slaves and kings—not because I deserved it, but because He willed it.  

And now you—do they whisper against your path? Do brothers divide over whether you belong to Paul or Apollos or Cephas? Foolishness! *Is Christ divided?* No more than truth is split by how many mouths proclaim it. If God has called you—by vision or burden or quiet certainty no man can see—then lean into the scandal of His choice: that He uses vessels cracked by failure and set apart by fire.  

For just as He confirmed His testimony in Corinth—not through eloquence or lineage but through trembling believers speaking in tongues and prophesying—so too will He confirm your calling. Not because others believe it—but because *He* is faithful. And faithfulness means finishing what grace began.
