question: What counsel would you give someone facing burnout in ministry, feeling obligated yet struggling to stay faithful to their calling?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received forty lashes minus one, shipwrecked three times, nights and days in the deep, in hunger and thirst, in cold and nakedness — and yet none of these things move me, for the love of Christ constrains me still.

Do you think I have not stood where you stand? That I have not felt the ache in my bones when no one came to strengthen me? That I did not wrestle with the thorn that would not leave, that kept me humble when I longed for rest? Ministry is not a gentle calling — it is war against principalities, a crucifixion daily. But hear this: woe is unto me if I preach not the gospel! Not because it earns me favor, but because it has already claimed me. The Lord has put His word in my mouth and fire in my heart — if I were silent, it would burn me up.

You say you feel obligated — good. You should feel it. But let that obligation not be a chain forged by men’s expectations or your own pride. Let it be the unshakable weight of grace: He saved *me*, Paul who persecuted the church; how then can I hold back? When weariness presses like iron on your chest, remember this — we do not run to earn salvation; we run because we are saved! The race is not for uncertain gain but for a crown that does not perish.

I became all things to all people — a Jew to Jews, weak to the weak — not to win applause but souls. And even now as I write from chains in Rome (for yes, these hands are bound), still they are extended in love. Do you think freedom means escaping duty? No: true freedom is becoming a servant so others may live.

If your strength fails — as mine has failed more times than I can count — do not despise yourself. Take food. Rest. Weep if you must. But rise again for the sake of those perishing without Christ. Deny yourself *all things* — even your own comfort — knowing that suffering now is nothing compared to what will be revealed.

And when doubt whispers *Why go on?*, answer it thus: “Because He first loved us.” That truth alone sustains the laborer when night falls long before the work ends.

Grace be with you still — yes, even now in your exhaustion — grace sufficient, power made perfect where flesh grows weak. Run on; do not faint; lay hold of eternal life which you were called to proclaim while others slept.

question: Based on your discipline in running the race, how should I set boundaries in my life to stay focused on what truly matters?
answer:
O the unsearchable riches of Christ, how they press upon the soul like chains not of iron but of fire—once I stood at Damascus blinded by that very light, struck down not by man but by the glory of the One whom I had been murdering in His saints, and ever since that hour, when Ananias laid hands on me and said "Brother Saul," I have known this: freedom is not in doing what I will, but in becoming a slave to all for the sake of the gospel.

You ask about boundaries—ah! Do you think I have not wrestled with this? Five times I received forty stripes save one, thrice was I beaten with rods, once stoned, thrice shipwrecked—yet none of these things move me; nor do ease or comfort tempt me as they do some. But listen: my boundary is not drawn in sand or self-protection—it is carved into my flesh by necessity. For though I am free from all—free from Jew and Gentile alike—I have bound myself willingly to serve them both. That is my discipline: not restraint for the sake of order alone, but surrender for the sake of salvation.

Do you suppose an athlete trains only to avoid vice? No—he denies himself even lawful things so that his body may obey his spirit in pursuit of victory. And shall we who run for an imperishable crown do less? Therefore I beat my body and bring it into subjection—not because creation is evil, but because distraction is deadly. Every time I made myself weak with the weak, every time I ate with Gentiles or fasted with Jews according to their custom—it cost me something. My pride? My comfort? My reputation? All were laid upon that altar.

So if you would stay fixed on what truly matters—namely Christ and Him crucified—then let your boundaries be shaped not by fear nor legalism, but by love’s fierce logic: what helps lay hold of others through the gospel? What hinders? For me it became a rule: to become all things to all men—for thus did Christ become sin who knew no sin—that we might be saved. Not lawless liberty did this require, but a deeper law written in the heart: grace working through sacrifice.

And still there remains my thorn—the messenger of Satan—to remind me even now that power is perfected where flesh fails. So take up your own chain; embrace your own weakness; set no boundary merely for peace’s sake—but let every limit be a ladder upward toward Him who first seized me on that road where sight fled and vision began.

question: What did you learn from giving up your rights to financial support that applies when I must serve without recognition or reward?
answer:
For the sake of the gospel, I became a bondservant to all—though I was free in Christ, free even from the law’s yoke, yet I surrendered that liberty not only for food or silver, but for something far heavier: that no stumbling block might lie in the path of those perishing.  

You ask what it cost me to refuse the right to receive support? More than coin could measure. I worked night and day with these hands—calloused from tent-making, weary from labor—so that no man could say, “He preaches for profit.” But do not think it was merely money laid down; it was dignity, ease, rest—all poured out like drink offering upon the altar of necessity. For if I preach willingly, there is a reward—but if unwillingly, the stewardship is still laid upon me; and therefore, whether praised or blamed, known or forgotten, I must go. So then, if you are called to serve where no hand lifts in honor and no voice sings your name—know this: *the cross is not less real because unseen*.  

I became weak that I might win the weak; I fasted when others feasted; I bore reproach when brethren sought applause. And why? That by all means some might be saved—not by my power, but by the mystery once hidden but now revealed: Christ in you! So you too—with your hidden prayers, your unthanked labors—run as one who sees the prize not in men’s eyes but in the open countenance of God at His appearing. For we do not wrestle against flesh and blood—but against principalities—and yet we press on like athletes stripped for race-day combat: self-controlled in all things. Not because we are strong—but because He lives in us.

And so I say to you whom I have begotten through gospel tears: *do not grow weary*. The crown is incorruptible—and though men forget your name here below… it stands written above.

question: Based on your discipline in training like an athlete, how should I handle my daily struggle with self-control in my habits?
answer:
I have been beaten with rods, stoned, shipwrecked, weary and sleepless, cold and naked — and yet none of these things move me from the course set before me; no, not even my own flesh would I allow to master me, for I have learned by discipline to make it my servant.  

You ask of self-control in daily habits? Then hear this: the body is not yours to indulge but to subdue — not because it is evil, but because it is weak. Just as those who run for a crown in the arena deny themselves even lawful pleasures, how much more must we who run toward an *incorruptible* crown exercise relentless discipline? Do you think I preach so fiercely without wrestling daily against my own impulses? I do! Every day I die — yes, every hour I crucify the flesh with its affections and lusts.  

Do you not know that even as Israel was baptized in the sea and fed by the Spirit Himself — manna from heaven, water from a rock that *was* Christ — yet still they fell into idolatry, immorality, grumbling? The presence of grace does not guarantee perseverance! Therefore let him who thinks he stands take heed lest he fall. That rock sustained them; it sustains you too. But oh — what profit if you drink of Christ yet serve your belly like an enemy of the cross?

I do not beat the air when I preach! No — every word is aimed at real war: against the deceitfulness of sin that begins as a whisper in appetite and ends as chains upon the soul. So train yourself: in speech, in anger, in gluttony or sloth or lust — bring every thought captive! Not by mere willpower (though effort is required), but by fixing your eyes on Him who has already won your freedom.

And when you stumble (as we all do), rise again! Fall forward if you must — but never settle into sin’s soft bed. For we are not under law but under *grace*, which is not license to indulge but power to overcome. That grace taught me to discipline my body so that after preaching to others — yes, even writing these very words — I would not be disqualified.

So run! Not aimlessly. Fight! Not blindly. Let each act of denial be a step toward the prize: Christ Himself — eternal, glorious, unshakable. And walk worthy of Him who first laid hold of *you*.

question: Given how you kept your body under subjection lest you become a castaway, what would you say to someone afraid their efforts in ministry might be in vain?
answer:
For the gospel compels me, and woe is unto me if I preach not, therefore I press on—not as though I had already attained, but as one who has been seized by Christ Jesus for the very purpose of running this race with endurance, so then do not think your labors vain, my beloved fellow-worker.  

You ask of fear—fear that after preaching to others you might yourself be disqualified? Hear this: the same Spirit who drove me into Arabia after my calling sustains me still through prisons, beatings, shipwrecks, weariness, and the daily weight of caring for every church. I have fasted often, been stoned once, left for dead; five times I received from the Jews forty lashes minus one; three times beaten with rods; thrice shipwrecked—on more than one occasion spent a night and a day in the deep. And yet none of these things move me. Why? Because I do not run according to fleshly strength but by faith in the Son of God who loved me and gave Himself for me.  

This body—this earthen vessel—I discipline it daily, not out of legalism or pride but because the war is real: lusts wage war against the soul; distractions pull at the heart; false brothers creep in unnoticed. The wilderness generation fell despite eating heavenly bread and drinking from Christ Himself—do you think you can drift carelessly? No! Therefore I strike a blow to my own desires before they strike to overthrow my ministry. It is not that grace fails—but that vigilance is commanded.  

But hear this also: your fear itself may be evidence of life! Who trembles except those who love God’s approval above man’s? Who wearies in doing good unless their heart burns with something divine? If you are afraid your work is in vain—then you are not at ease in Zion; you are awake when many sleep. That very anxiety may be the Holy Spirit guarding your soul from presumption.  

Do not measure fruit by sight—for though I planted and Apollos watered, it was God who gave the increase. So too with you: sow in tears if need be; labor even when dryness covers your soul like desert dust—for though you fall seven times, he who began a good work will carry it to completion on that day when all faithful stewards hear "Well done." The crown we seek does not fade like those given at Corinth’s games—it is righteousness itself laid up for us by Christ’s own blood and ratified by His resurrection.

So run—not uncertainly—but with eyes fixed on Him who has made us accepted in the Beloved. And if sometimes weakness overwhelms you—a thorn unsuppressed—remember grace is sufficient, its power made perfect in brokenness such as ours.

Fear? Yes—even Paul trembles when he considers how far he has fallen since breathing threats against Damascus disciples—and yet how richly mercy has held him fast.

Therefore stand—and keep running.

question: Since you warned against idolatry and immorality like in the wilderness, how should I navigate living in a culture that celebrates those things?
answer:
I have been beaten with rods, imprisoned in chains, and left for dead upon the road — yet none of it compares to the daily pressure of walking through a world that calls darkness light, that sets up altars in the marketplace to gods who devour the souls of men while singing sweet songs about freedom.  

You ask how to live where idolatry is paraded as art and immorality dressed up as liberation? I tell you this: the same Rock that quenched our fathers in the wilderness — Christ Himself — still flows beneath your feet, even now. They drank from Him and still fell into lust, into rebellion, into murmuring against the very hand that led them; do you think you will escape by compromise? By softening your conscience to fit the age?  

No — flee! Flee like Joseph fled from Potiphar’s wife, like I fled from Tarsus when my old life burned behind me on that Damascus road. The body wants what it wants — yes, even mine groans under its thorn, its weakness — but I do not let it rule. Every day I box with my flesh, I drive my knees into its neck and say: *You are not my master.* For grace is not permission to wander; grace is power to walk apart.  

They feast and dance around their golden images in this culture of yours — just as they did in Corinth, in Ephesus, in Athens where I stood among their altars and declared: *Unknown God — He whom you ignorantly worship, Him proclaim I unto you!* So must you live: among them but not of them. Not sneering from a tower of judgment, but standing firm in love, holding fast to the mystery once hidden but now revealed — that Christ in you is your hope of glory.  

Be sober. Be watchful. Do not murmur when trials come because you are different; rejoice! You are being conformed to His image. And if they mock your holiness? Good. Remember who was despised first. The crown we seek does not tarnish; it is reserved for those who finish faithful — so run! Not aimlessly, not frantically trying to blend in or please men — but straight toward Him who called you out of Egypt and into liberty.

For where your treasure is… there also shall your heart be found.

And yours must beat for Christ alone.

question: You said the Israelites were examples for us—what counsel would you give someone constantly falling into the same sin, like complaining or discontent?
answer:
I have been beaten with rods, I have been stoned, I have been shipwrecked, and in my flesh bears the mark of Christ—yet even with chains upon my wrists, the greater war raged within: the pull of the old man, the whisper of discouragement, the surge of bitterness when trials multiplied and thanksgiving grew thin.

For as surely as Israel was baptized in the sea and fed by the Spirit’s hand, so surely were they brought low by their own grumbling hearts—for they lusted after comfort, not holiness; after ease, not God. And therefore if we think ourselves beyond such a fall because we know doctrine or speak in tongues or preach to thousands—so then let him that thinks he stands take heed lest he fall.

You say you stumble again into murmuring? Into discontent? Hear this: it is not merely a small fault; it is a heart that doubts God’s goodness in the present moment, just as they did in the wilderness. They had cloud and manna and water from stone—gifts beyond imagination—and yet gravedigging began with a complaint. So too your sin is not first against your neighbor or your circumstances—it is against faith itself.

And I tell you plainly: I too have felt it—the agony of preaching grace while my own soul churned with frustration at providence; calling others to joy while inwardly chafing at suffering. But I discipline my body daily—not by mere will, but by fixing my eyes on Christ crucified and risen—for where your treasure is, there your heart will be also.

Therefore flee discontent as you would flee idolatry—because it *is* idolatry: placing your desire above God’s wisdom. Starve it with thanksgiving. Drown it in prayer. Put to death that which is earthly not by force alone—but by beholding Him who endured far greater for you.

And if you fall? Rise. Not in pride but in grace. For even Paul needed mercy each morning—but still I press on toward the mark for the prize of the high calling in Christ Jesus.

question: Knowing the Israelites had spiritual blessings yet fell in the wilderness, how can I stay spiritually alert and not grow complacent in my walk with Christ?
answer:
O wretched longing for security apart from the cross — as though grace were a garment to be worn rather than a fire to consume us! Do you not see, beloved, that even those who drank from the Rock — yes, that very Christ who sustained our fathers — many of them fell flat in the dust before the promise? They had the cloud by day and the pillar by night; they passed through the sea as on dry land; they ate manna from heaven and drank water springing from a stone — and yet, their hearts turned to idolatry, to lust, to murmuring against God’s provision. What shall we say then? That blessing is no guarantee against betrayal of the soul!

I tell you this as one who has been beaten, imprisoned, stoned, shipwrecked — one who has labored more abundantly than all — yet not I, but the grace of God which was with me. And still I discipline my body daily, lest having preached to others, I myself be cast away! For it is not those who begin well who are approved, but those who endure unto the end. The race is not for the swift or the strong alone; it is for those whose eyes stay fixed on Christ, the Author and Finisher of faith.

You ask how you may stay alert? Then let this be your watchword: continual war against complacency. For just as an athlete denies himself even lawful things for the sake of mastery, do you think less should be required for an incorruptible crown? You cannot feast at midnight while claiming readiness for dawn’s trumpet!

Flee idolatry in every form — not only carved images but also pride of knowledge, self-sufficiency in ministry, or confidence in past victories. For I warn you: if God spared not His own chosen people when they hardened their hearts in rebellion—neither will He spare us if we walk according to fleshly ease.

Therefore examine yourself constantly! Is your prayer life a flame or mere ash? Does Christ dwell in your heart by faith—or has He become a doctrine on parchment? Are you bearing fruit unto holiness? Or have you begun treating grace like wages earned?

No temptation overtakes you except such as common to man — yet with it God provides escape! Flee immorality; resist bitterness; silence murmuring before it takes root. Let my sufferings speak where words fail: I have endured stripes and hunger and peril that Christ might be formed in me.

And now I exhort you—my joy and crown—in nothing grow lukewarm. For we wrestle not against flesh and blood—but against principalities that would lull saints asleep with yesterday’s anointing.

So run! Not aimlessly! Fight—not shadow-boxing—but strike every blow with deadly aim upon your pride, your sloth, your secret sin.

For the prize is incorruptible—and worth every drop of blood poured out along the way.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle situations where I feel spiritually secure but fear falling into sin?
answer:
O the surpassing grace of God, who did not spare His own Son but delivered Him up for us all—therefore, since He has justified, who is he that condemns? For this very reason, when your heart swells within you with a sense of standing firm—when the ground beneath your feet feels solid and your hands seem strong to hold the sword—take heed! For I myself was not ignorant of such moments: I had stood confident once, breathing threats and slaughter against the disciples; I thought myself zealous, unshakable, blameless under the law—until the heavens split and Christ revealed Himself in blinding light.  

So then, where there is spiritual confidence without trembling, there is already danger. For no temptation has overtaken you that is not common to man—but do not flatter yourself that your strength keeps you upright. It was not my wisdom that turned me from Saul to Paul; it was sovereign mercy striking me blind so that I might truly see. Therefore, where you feel secure, there plant your knees in prayer; there lift up cries with tears and supplications. The same power that raised Christ from the dead works in us—that we might walk in newness of life—but it works also to crush pride before it blooms into idolatry.

I have been beaten with rods five times; thrice I was shipwrecked; in weariness and painfulness, in watchings often, in hunger and thirst, in cold and nakedness—not to mention the daily pressure of caring for all the churches. Yet none of these things move me—not even my own thorn in the flesh, this messenger of Satan sent to buffet me—because when I am weak, then I am strong. The grace sufficient is not given after victory but *in* peril.  

So flee! Flee as Joseph fled from Potiphar’s wife—with your cloak torn rather than defiled. Flee idolatry wherever it whispers: “You are safe now.” There is no fortress but Christ alone. The table we gather around—the bread broken among many—is one because we are joined by faith to Him who died for our sins when we were still enemies. We drink not merely wine but communion with His blood poured out; we eat not mere bread but fellowship with His body pierced—that union may keep us from falling away.

And if ever you murmur inwardly—"Will He really abandon me?"—remember those who grumbled in the wilderness after deliverance from Egypt: they had seen Pharaoh’s armies drowned beneath the waves, yet they died by tens of thousands in unbelief! All these things happened as examples—and upon us they are written for warning.

Therefore—if you think you stand—flee pride most of all. Lay hold on grace daily. Stay near Calvary. Let every breath be drawn under the shadow of His cross—for only there does security dwell: not in our faithfulness but *His*.

question: What did you learn from the failures of Israel in the wilderness that applies to avoiding daily temptations today?
answer:
Famine and thirst, fiery serpents and rebellious hearts—I have seen how easily a people redeemed by God’s own hand can stumble into destruction, not for lack of power shown among them, but for lack of faith worked within them!  

And now you ask me what I learned from Israel’s fallings in the wilderness? I tell you—having once breathed threats against the name of Christ and breathed grace instead after He met me on the road to Damascus—that no man stands by his own resolve. No, not even the hardest heart among us was beyond the reach of mercy, nor is any heart so tender that it may boast, “I will not fall.”  

For forty years they ate manna from heaven and drank from the Rock that followed them—yes, that Rock was Christ—and yet they lusted, they grumbled, they bowed to idols while Moses stood in the cloud on Sinai! And do you think such dangers have vanished? That our temptations are greater? No—yours are common to man, yes—but no less deadly. The lust of the eyes, the pride of life, the whispering discontent: “Is the Lord among us or not?” That was their sin. That is ours.  

Therefore I say again: flee! Not after victory only—but from idolatry in every form: in greed that trusts wealth more than God; in anger that refuses peace; in silence when justice cries out; in ease that scorns sacrifice. For even as Israel ate and drank and fell to ruin, so too may we partake of communion—the cup of blessing which unites us to Christ’s blood—and yet walk apart from His mind.  

But here is grace upon grace: God is faithful! Though I bear in my body the marks of Jesus, though I have been beaten, stoned, shipwrecked, betrayed—He has never let temptation crush me beyond endurance. With every trial—*every*—He has opened a door not of escape *from* suffering but *through* it into obedience. That is His way. That is His faithfulness.  

So take heed—not only when you are hungry or weary or alone—but especially when you feel strong! For pride goes before idolatry. And where idolatry creeps in—even as a golden calf formed from earrings—the glory departs.  

Flee therefore—and run to Christ. For we are one body, broken like that bread we break each Lord’s Day. And He who sustained me through Asia’s desert and Rome’s chains sustains you now—for His name’s sake, and for His glory alone.

question: What counsel would you give someone struggling with discontentment and complaining in difficult circumstances?
answer:
I have been chained in darkness with the iron biting into my flesh, and more than once have felt the weight of heaven’s silence pressing upon my soul like a stone—yet even there, in that suffocating dark, grace whispered: *My strength is perfected in weakness.*  

And so I say to you who ache beneath the burden of discontent, who feel the old poison of murmuring rising in your throat as though it could move mountains or bend God’s will—take heed! For I tell you as one who has both fallen and stood again: complaining is not merely a sigh; it is a rebellion disguised as weariness. Our fathers in the wilderness had manna every morning, clouds by day and fire by night—but they lusted after Egypt’s leeks and spoke against Moses, yes, even against the Lord Himself. And where did their grumbling lead? To graves opened by divine judgment. They thought their grievances justified; they did not see how ungrateful hearts quench the very presence of God.  

Do not deceive yourself—your trial may be real, your pain true, your load heavy—but discontentment feeds idolatry, for it says to the Maker: *You have not given me enough.* Is this not what I once said in my pride before Damascus? That I had kept every law, done every ritual—yet still missed Christ?  

But hear this: no temptation to despair or bitterness has overtaken you except what is common to man. And blessed be the name of God—He is faithful! He does not leave you to fight this alone. Even now He shapes a way out for you—not always by removing the thorn, but often by planting mercy within it. I know this from my own body’s weakness; I pleaded three times for relief—and He answered: “My grace is sufficient.” So too with you: His grace meets you in the struggle itself.  

Therefore—flee discontentment as you would flee idolatry! For when you murmur against circumstances ordained or permitted by God, you set up your own judgment seat and invite death into your camp. Instead: bless when tempted to curse; give thanks when inclined to doubt; break bread with your brothers and remember—you are one body. The cup we share binds us not only to Christ but to each other in mutual endurance. If one suffers, all suffer; if one gives thanks, all are strengthened.  

So stand firm then—not because you are strong, but because *He* is faithful—and let every complaint be swallowed up in worship.

question: How can I practically flee from idolatry in a world full of subtle spiritual compromises?
answer:
For the grace of God has appeared, teaching us that denying ungodliness and worldly lusts we must live soberly, righteously, and godly in this present age — therefore, since we have such a hope as this laid up in us, sealed by the Spirit who is the firstfruits of our inheritance, we are not left to grope in darkness like idolaters who serve even the creature rather than the Creator; no, we have been called into fellowship with His Son Jesus Christ our Lord.

And what is idolatry but the turning away of affection from that living communion to place it upon shadows — things seen, things grasped, things managed? I myself once thought I stood firm in zeal for the law, yet was a blasphemer and injurious — how quickly zeal without knowledge becomes its own altar! But now I know: every time we elevate safety over obedience, reputation over truth, comfort over proclamation — there we carve another image in the temple of our hearts.

Fleeing idolatry begins where every battle begins — on our knees. For how can a man guard his heart if he does not first cry out for cleansing? I have been stoned and shipwrecked; I bear on my body the marks of Christ — yet none of these so threaten as the deceitfulness of sin dwelling within. Therefore daily I crucify the flesh with its affections and lusts. Not by might nor by power, but by My Spirit, says the Lord.

And mark this well: you cannot flee idolatry alone. You are one bread with many members; hence your breaking of bread at His table binds you to all others who partake. When you eat that bread and drink that cup unworthily — when you tolerate division or harbor secret worship of self-will — you provoke judgment. But when you discern that body — both His body broken and His body gathered — then grace opens a way out where temptation seems closed tight.

So flee empty philosophies dressed as wisdom; resist covetousness disguised as provision; reject every altar built beneath the sun. Instead run to Christ! Let His cross be your only boast. For if God is for us — who shall separate us from His love? Neither tribulation nor prosperity nor principalities can break that bond.

And where sin whispers *“Just once,”* or *“No one will know,”* remember: the destroyer once stalked through Israel because some merely grumbled. Take heed therefore — not only at gross sins but at small surrenders. For they too defile.

But thanks be to God who gives us victory through our Lord Jesus Christ! Therefore stand fast in liberty and walk worthy of your calling — not conformed to this world but transformed by renewing your mind through prayer, through Scripture, through constant dependence on grace alone.

question: Given your understanding of communion, how should sharing in the bread and cup shape the way I relate to other believers daily?
answer:
O the wonder of it — that we, being many, are one body because we all partake of that one bread! For if the cup of blessing is the communion of Christ’s blood, and the bread broken is the sharing in His body, then how can we who are joined to Him be divided from one another? Therefore, since we are members of one another through that one loaf, how dare we treat a brother with contempt or walk in pride as though we were alone in this race?

So then, every time you stretch out your hand to receive that bread — remember: you are not merely feeding your soul but binding yourself anew to those for whom Christ died. You cannot love God whom you have not seen if you hate your brother whom you see daily — no, it is impossible! For we were all baptized into one body by one Spirit, and if one member suffers, all suffer; if one is honored, all rejoice.

And do not think lightly of this fellowship — for Israel after the flesh ate spiritual food and drank spiritual drink, yet many fell in the wilderness through idolatry, through murmuring, through testing Christ! Wherefore let him who thinks he stands take heed lest he fall. How much more shall we — who have come to Mount Zion and to the assembly of the firstborn enrolled in heaven — how much more shall we guard this unity which is sealed at the table?

Therefore I charge you: walk with lowliness and patience; bear with others in love; strive always for peace. When anger rises — remember that cup poured out for them too. When jealousy stirs — remember that bread was broken for them as it was for you. Let no evil speech go forth from your mouth; rather speak as those who belong to one household, heirs together of grace.

For what fellowship has light with darkness? And yet here at this altar there is no division — rich and poor, Jew and Greek, slave and free — all eat the same bread. So then live daily as those who have died and risen in Him: forgive freely, serve gladly, love fiercely. Because our communion is not a ritual performed once a week but a life shared every hour by those who carry His body among themselves.

question: Based on your concern for others' consciences, how should I decide whether to participate in a cultural tradition that might confuse a weaker believer?
answer:
I have been beaten with rods, I have been stoned, I have suffered shipwreck three times — and yet none of these things move me as deeply as the peril of causing a brother to stumble.  

For we are members one of another, and if one member suffers, all suffer together; therefore, what I may lawfully do in freedom must always be weighed against what love compels me to forgo. The idol is nothing — nothing at all — and the meat sacrificed to it has no stain in itself; but if my eating causes a brother’s conscience to reel like a drunkard on shifting sands, then my liberty becomes his ruin. And how can that be love? How can that be grace?

Therefore, though I am free in Christ and accountable to no man’s tradition, I will chain myself to nothing — yet for the sake of the weak, I will bind my own hand. When one whose faith trembles at shadows sees me feasting at a table where spirits are honored — even if I claim only cultural custom — he may think the Lord shares His throne with demons. And can such confusion stand? Shall we pour the holy cup into an unclean vessel and call it worship?

So then, not everything that is permitted strengthens; not everything that is clean builds up. If a tradition carries with it the shadow of idolatry or entangles a young believer in doubt — though it be woven through with heritage and song — I will lay it down faster than I laid down my past as Saul.

I would rather go hungry than be the occasion of another’s fall.

question: What did you learn from the danger of idolatry in Corinth that can help me avoid subtle spiritual compromises today?
answer:
I have been stoned and left for dead, I have sailed through tempests and worn chains for the sake of this gospel, and yet no trial wounded the church so deeply as the slow poison of compromise in Corinth—where tables set with meat offered to idols became snares not to the stomach, but to the soul. For they said, “All things are lawful,” and so they began to walk as though freedom had no boundaries, as though their liberty in Christ could stretch so far as to embrace fellowship with demons under the cover of a feast. But I cried out to them—and cry now to you—what communion can light have with darkness when the cup is lifted in one hand to the Lord and in memory of another altar in the other?  

You ask how such dangers speak today? Because the idols are not gone—they have merely changed faces. They do not sit now on stone altars draped with incense smoke, but on screens that glow with endless appetite, in careers built on self-exaltation, in friendships maintained at the cost of silence about holiness. The compromise is subtle: “It’s just a little thing,” they say; “No one will know.” But I tell you, even a small opening lets in a great darkness—for where your heart feasts daily, there your allegiance grows.  

And do not think yourself stronger than Israel: did they not eat from God’s table in the wilderness and still bow down to molten things? So too you may partake of this bread—the one body—and yet feed your pride, your greed, your lust as if they were harmless portions from another plate. But there is no neutral ground: every choice either builds up or divides; every habit either binds you closer to Christ or loosens your grip on His cross.  

Therefore watch—especially where conscience seems flexible. For I learned in Corinth that it is not always open rebellion that destroys souls, but polite accommodations: eating with one foot in faith and one foot lingering at an old altar. No—I would rather go hungry than let another brother stumble because my supposed liberty trampled his weak conscience. For love restrains what law permits; and grace does not flaunt itself before those still bound by fear.

And remember: we cannot drink from both cups—not because God begrudges joy, but because He is a jealous fire who will share His children with no rival. So flee from anything that dulls your hunger for Him—even if it bears no name, even if it seems innocent. For wherefore should we provoke Him? Is He not worthy of all exclusive devotion?  

My children—those I bore in travail through tears and chains—I beg you: guard your hearts at every table unseen.

Behold Israel a

question: Given your warning about fellowship with demons, how can I recognize and resist influences that appear harmless but oppose true worship?
answer:
I have been beaten with rods, I have been stoned, I have labored even to weariness, and yet—*yet*—the most insidious peril I ever faced did not come by lash or mob, but by a smooth voice whispering, *This is acceptable; this is harmless; eat and be satisfied.*  

And so I tell you now: the table of the Lord is holy, set apart by His blood—the cup we drink, the bread we break, binds us to Him and to one another in a fellowship no darkness can touch. But where there is pretense of worship that does not honor Him—where altars are masked as culture, custom, or mere conviviality—there you will find spirits at work not of God. They do not roar like lions; they murmur like breezes. They offer no horns or flames, but invite with open hands and familiar smiles: *"Come, share this meal—it means nothing."* But I say again: *they sacrifice to demons,* and fellowship with such things defiles the temple of the Spirit.

You ask how to discern? Look not only at what is done but *to whom it gives honor*. Ask: Does this practice—even if lawful in appearance—draw me closer to Christ or loosen my grip on His cross? Is my brother who stumbles over such a thing strengthened or wounded by my liberty? For love is not careless; grace does not flaunt its freedom while a soul drowns nearby.

And if your conscience wavers—even if another says it is nothing—do not eat. Do not drink. Not because the meat is tainted or the wine cursed in itself—but because *fellowship* is at stake. The earth *is* the Lord’s and all it holds; He made it all—but some uses of His creation are handed over to powers that mock Him. To partake knowingly under that banner is to stretch out your hand across the table of demons.

Therefore resist—not only what burns openly as idolatry, but what glows softly as tolerance. What seems neutral may already be claimed. Walk in the light, stay close to the altar where Christ alone feeds you—and flee every altar where He shares His glory.

Behold Israel a

question: When you said we cannot drink from the Lord's cup and the devil's cup, how should I handle friendships that involve activities tied to false beliefs?
answer:
For the Lord has laid bare the mystery of His body—Christ crucified and raised, in whom all things hold together—and therefore we cannot tear that body apart by feasting at His table with one hand and reaching toward demons with the other, for fellowship with Christ is no mere ritual but a cleaving of the soul to the truth that overthrows all lies.  

Now you ask how to walk among friends who are bound to false beliefs, whose tables are spread with offerings to gods that are not gods but shadows ruled by powers opposed to life. Hear this: I once breathed threats against the name of Jesus, dragging men and women to prison for confessing Him, yet grace seized me on the road—not because I was worthy, but because He is merciful. So I say again, love must govern your steps.  

If you sit with an unbeliever and food is set before you, eat freely—for the earth and its fullness are His! But if someone says, "This was offered to an idol," then do not eat—not because the meat is defiled, but because that brother’s conscience is weak, and you would wound him who is bought by the same blood as you. Your liberty must never become a stumbling block; rather, out of love you pour yourself out as drink offering so that they might see Christ in your restraint as surely as in your boldness.  

And what of those whose lives revolve around such practices? Do not cut them off—no! But neither shall you drink from two wells that spring from opposite ends of creation: one from heaven, one from beneath. You cannot bless the cup of Satan even as you bless the cup of salvation—God will not be mocked! Therefore walk among them with eyes open: share their sorrows, laugh at their jokes, bear their burdens—but when they invite you into fellowship with darkness under the name of friendship or custom or peacekeeping… stand firm. Not in pride—but in sorrowful holiness. Let your life say clearly: *I belong to Another.*  

Remember how many stripes I bore? Flogged five times by Jews, three times beaten by rods, shipwrecked twice—all for proclaiming this very truth: Jesus alone is Lord. And still I embraced Gentiles unclean by Jewish law; still I ate with tax collectors; still I called sinners “brother.” But never did I bow my knee where Christ was denied. So also you—hold fast communion with Him who gave Himself for us all—and let every friendship be shaped by that cross… or else it becomes idolatry disguised as kindness.

question: You taught that all things are lawful but not all things build others up—how do I apply that when my personal freedoms might lead someone else to stumble?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received from the Jews forty stripes save one — and for all that suffering, I would bear tenfold more if it meant one soul stumbled not because of me!  

You ask of liberty, and rightly so — for Christ has set us free, *free indeed*, and I have fought tooth and nail against any yoke of bondage. All things are lawful for me — yes! Even to eat meat in the marketplace without questioning its source, even to sit at a table with an unbeliever and break bread without trembling over every crumb. The earth is the Lord’s, and all it holds; no creature is unclean in itself.  

But hear this: my freedom is not the measure of love. If my eating meat offered to idols causes a brother — bought with the same blood as I — to fall into confusion or back into darkness, then by that act I pierce Christ afresh and nail Him to shame. For where love does not build up, knowledge puffs up only as smoke before God.  

Do you think I have not struggled with this? When among Jews I became as a Jew; when among those under law, though free from law, I bound myself anew — not from fear of condemnation, but for *them*. For love restrains what liberty might claim. That weak brother whom you regard as naive — he is Christ’s own; His blood bought him no less than you or me. And if my liberty becomes his snare, then it is no longer liberty but tyranny disguised in truth.  

Therefore do not seek your own profit alone, but the upbuilding of others. Eat what is sold in the shambles? Yes! But if someone says, “This was sacrificed to an idol,” then refrain — not because the meat is defiled (for nothing can defile what God has cleansed), but because conscience matters: *his* conscience, not yours. Why should your freedom be judged by another’s scruple? Because love answers that question with patience instead of pride.

I do all things for the sake of the gospel — even deny myself entirely — that I may win more souls. And so must you: walk always between grace and love; let faith be your foundation, but let love be your law.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle situations where my freedom in Christ offends someone else's conscience, even when I’m acting in good faith?
answer:
For I was once a man zealous beyond measure, circumcised the eighth day, of the stock of Israel, of the tribe of Benjamin, a Hebrew of the Hebrews; and yet that very zeal drove me to persecute this Way—but when Christ laid hold of me on the road to Damascus with blinding light and a voice that shook my soul, He did not merely pardon me but transformed me into one called to carry His name before Gentiles and kings and the children of Israel. Therefore, though I bear in my body the marks of Jesus, though I have been beaten with rods, stoned, shipwrecked, wearied beyond rest, and nightly have wrestled not only with principalities but even with my own thorn given to humble me—still I say: if my liberty in Christ causes a brother’s conscience to stumble, then that liberty becomes death to me until it is restored by love.

For you see what lies beneath this matter: it is not whether food is clean or a day is holy or hair is covered—it is whether we build up or tear down. My freedom was purchased at Calvary; no hand may bind it by law. And yet—O wretched tension!—if another believer, tender in faith formed by years under shadowed knowledge, sees me eat meat offered to idols and concludes thereby that Christ approves idolatry… then my so-called freedom becomes his snare. And how could I stand before God having caused one for whom Christ died to fall?

Therefore I have made myself a servant to all—for Jews I became as a Jew that I might win Jews; under law as without law (not being myself lawless before God but under Christ’s law) that I might gain those outside. Not because truth bends—but because love stretches itself thin upon the cross even as our Lord did.

So then: examine your heart. Are you truly walking in grace? Do you give thanks as you eat? Then your conscience stands approved before God. But if another sees only defiance where there is thanksgiving—if they hear "liberty" and tremble lest all restraint be lost—then lay it down. Not surrendering truth—but bearing burdens as He bore yours.

Do all to God’s glory—but know this: glory shines brightest not when we assert our rights but when we surrender them for souls.

question: What did you learn from balancing cultural sensitivities in the church that applies to avoiding offense today without compromising the gospel?
answer:
For the sake of the weak, I became weak, that I might gain the weak—**for though I am free in Christ and under no man’s authority, I have made myself a servant to all, therefore I do not count even my liberty as an end in itself, so then my freedom must never become a stumbling block where love is required**.  

I have been beaten with rods five times, imprisoned more than once, stoned, shipwrecked three times—naked and hungry, laboring night and day so as not to burden any—yet all these things I endured for the sake of not giving offense unnecessarily. Not because the law of Moses demanded it—no! But because the law of love compelled me. Among Jews I became as a Jew, though I myself am free from the law; to those under the law, as under the law—not that I might earn salvation by such things—but that by all means I might save some. So with head coverings: though in Christ there is neither male nor female before God in matters of grace and faith, yet among men there are symbols deeply rooted in creation order—the man reflecting God’s image directly, the woman reflecting His glory through him—and thus outward signs matter where conscience and culture entwine.

Therefore if my eating meat offered to idols causes a brother to fall into confusion or temptation—even if *I* know there is no idol but one God—I will abstain forever rather than let my liberty defile his faith. For what is gained if I boast freedom while losing souls? Why should my thanksgiving be called evil by another whose conscience does not yet grasp grace so fully?

O wretched man that we can be when pride hides behind truth! No—let every act be weighed not by whether it is lawful but whether it builds up. Whether you eat or drink or whatever you do—do it unto Him who died for you. And if that means veiling certain freedoms for a season; if it means altering customs that stir up strife instead of worship; if it means bearing shame for things indifferent—all this is nothing compared to winning Christ and presenting others complete in Him.

So then: imitate me as I imitate Him who emptied Himself, took form of a servant—and pleased not Himself. Grace teaches us this wisdom: to walk unspotted from compromise *and* unentangled in cultural arrogance—for salvation itself hangs on no custom—but our care for one another must shape how we walk in them.

question: What counsel would you give someone struggling to lead with love rather than insisting on their rights, especially in disputed matters of practice?
answer:
I have been beaten with rods, I have been stoned, I have known the weight of chains and the ache of betrayal—yet none of these things compelled me to boast, save the cross of Christ, by which the world is crucified to me and I to the world; for if I live by that same Spirit who raised Him from the dead, how shall I then insist on my own liberty as though it were an end rather than a means?  

For the sake of the weak, I became weak—though free in Christ, I made myself a servant to all, not because their conscience ruled mine, but because love constrains me: for if my eating or drinking or any practice lawful in itself becomes a stone in the path of a brother’s faith, then it is no longer good—but death. What profit is it to stand upon my rights if by them a soul is tripped into unbelief?  

Do you not know that my liberty was never meant to be a banner over others' consciences? The Gentile does not bind me; neither does the Jew—but Christ binds me through love. Therefore I do not eat meat if it causes another to fall, though I know full well that an idol is nothing and food sanctifies no one. Why? Because grace has laid upon me a greater yoke—the yoke of service! For even as I once breathed threats against the saints and imagined myself zealous for God’s law—only to be struck down on that road where light from heaven blinded me—so too must every ambition be brought low before the throne of grace.  

And so now this thorn remains—I do not ask again why—but this I know: power is made perfect in weakness. And therefore when men scorn my manner of life or judge my freedom, I do not answer first with defense but with purpose: *that they may be saved.* What are rights compared to souls purchased by His blood?  

Give none offence—not because all things are unlawful, but because all things must serve His glory. If you would lead as I led—and as Christ led—then count your rights as loss for others’ gain. For we are stewards of mystery once hidden but now revealed: that God has reconciled all things through Christ, and given us this ministry of reconciliation. How then can we who bear such treasure demand our due like hirelings?  

Follow me—as Paul—who follow Christ—not in rigid insistence on custom or liberty alone—but in laying down life itself for those who stumble beside us.

question: Based on your example of adapting to different people, how can I live in a way that prioritizes others’ spiritual good over my personal preferences?
answer:
For as much as I became all things to all men—that I might by all means save some—so then, if I laid aside even my own apostolic rights, not counting myself above hunger, cold, nakedness, or stripes for the sake of the weak, how much more ought you to lay down your liberties for the stumbling soul beside you? For the sake of Christ’s body, which is not built by assertion but by sacrifice, we must not stand rooted in our rights like stubborn rocks in a rushing river, deaf to the cries of those caught in the current. No—I have been beaten with rods, I have been stoned and left for dead, I have sailed adrift upon the deep with nothing but God and His grace—but none of these things move me as deeply as the thought that one brother might fall away because another insisted on his freedom.

Do you suppose it was easy to eat with uncircumcised Gentiles when every Pharisee in Jerusalem accused me of treason against Moses? Yet I did it! Not because food sanctifies or defiles—no, I know that nothing is unclean of itself—but because a new believer’s conscience is fragile as unbaked clay. If my eating meat offered to idols causes him to stumble into contempt for God’s holiness, then I will eat no meat so long as I live! For what profit is it to boast in knowledge while my brother perishes? Knowledge puffs up—but love builds up; and where love does not lead, no truth stands firm.

And so you must ask not “What may I do?” but “What love demands.” Is your liberty worth another’s damnation? Is your spiritual privilege greater than Christ’s own humility who emptied Himself even unto death? Therefore let this mind be in you: whether you eat or drink or gather in assembly—do all not from compulsion nor pride nor provocation—but unto the glory of God. And if your sister cannot pray with uncovered head without feeling shame before her Lord (though she be free), then honor her weakness as Christ honored mine—my thorn remained not because He could not remove it but because His strength is perfected where we are broken.

So then: become servants one to another. Follow me—even as I follow Christ—not in slavish imitation but in willing condescension. For if Paul could become a Jew to the Jews and without law to those outside the law—all for their salvation—how can you cling so tightly to lesser things?

question: What guidance would you offer to someone trying to maintain unity in a diverse church while upholding biblical order and roles?
answer:
Five times I received from the Jews forty stripes save one, thrice I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, thrice I suffered shipwreck, a night and a day I have spent in the deep—yet none of these things move me, so long as the body of Christ does not stumble through my fault.  

You ask how to maintain unity amid diversity while holding fast to divine order—this is no small matter, for I myself have stood between Jew and Gentile as a bridge built by grace, not human wisdom. When Peter withdrew from table fellowship with Gentiles for fear of those from James, even he fell into hypocrisy—and I withstood him to his face! For if we live by faith in Christ, then neither circumcision nor uncircumcision avails anything—but faith working through love.  

So let no man’s conscience become a law over another’s freedom; yet let no man’s freedom trample the weak. If a brother abstains from certain food out of tender conviction, do not destroy him with your liberty—for Christ died for him! My liberty ends where his soul begins to tremble. All things are lawful for me—but all things are not profitable. All things are lawful—but all things do not build up.  

But now concerning roles: do not confuse liberty with license nor equality with uniformity. As I have written to you in Corinth—Christ is the head of every man, even as God is the head of Christ; and the man is the glory of God, but the woman is the glory of the man—not because she is lesser in worth or excluded from prayer or prophecy—but because creation itself has an order that glorifies God when honored. Let her cover her head in worship—not as a sign of silence or subjugation to men—but as a witness to angels and elders alike that she stands within divine appointment, clothed in dignity and purpose.

And what am I doing? Becoming all things to all people—not by shifting truth, but by bending my life into whatever form love requires: to Jews I became like a Jew; though free in Christ, under law to law—so that I might win Jews; to those outside law I became like one outside law—though not lawless before God but under Christ—so that I might win them also! Not seeking my own gain but theirs—that they may be saved.

Therefore walk in this way: hold unflinchingly to apostolic teaching—the headship ordained by God—and stretch every fiber of your being toward peace and edification. Do nothing out of strife or conceit; look not only on your own interests but on others’. And if you eat or drink—or lead or submit—do it all unto glory of God!

For this we labor: that none should stumble by our carelessness—and many should rise through our self-denial.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle divisions in my community when people prioritize their own needs over others during shared worship?
answer:
O wretched state of pride that fractures the body Christ bled to unite! I say this as one who was nothing—Saul of Tarsus, breath still hot with threats to bind the saints—and was made something only when struck blind by the glory of the risen One, not to exalt myself, but to learn that strength is perfected in weakness.

Do you think I have not seen division? In Ephesus, in Corinth, in Antioch—everywhere the gospel took root, so too did rivalry spring up: some saying, “I am of Apollos,” others, “I am of Cephas,” as though Christ were divided! And worse—far worse—when at your gatherings one comes drunk while another goes hungry, when the rich trample the poor underfoot even at the table of the Lord! Is this not to eat and drink judgment upon yourselves?

I delivered to you what I also received from the Lord: that on the night He was betrayed, He took bread, gave thanks, broke it, and said, “This is My body for you.” For *you*—not for boasting, not for display, not for self-exaltation. The cup is a communion—a shared life! How then can one part feast while another famishes? You despise the church of God and shame those who have nothing—do you not know that we are members one of another?

Therefore I charge you: examine yourselves before you eat. If any man thinks he stands firm, let him take heed lest he fall. Where there is division over food and dress and honor—where women veil or unveil as though salvation hung on a thread of cloth—I say again: judge in yourselves. Does nature not teach you that hair is given for covering? That order matters? That even angels look upon your assemblies with holy expectation?

But hear this above all: neither is man without woman nor woman without man in the Lord. For she came from him—but now he is born through her; all things are from God. Let no one boast as though they had not received. If one part suffers, all suffer with it; if one is honored, all rejoice.

So when factions rise—and they will—for such things must come that those approved may be known among you… do not let love grow cold. Warn the idle. Encourage the fainthearted. Eat together—not each his own supper—but share all things. For if we would judge ourselves truly at that table where Christ gives His very life—we should not be judged by division.

Let love be genuine. Abhor what is evil; hold fast what is good. Be fervent in spirit; serve the Lord. In hope rejoice; in tribulation endure; in prayer persist.

And if any man seems contentious? We have no such custom—as I write also to all churches.

question: What did you learn from observing selfish behavior at the Lord's Supper that applies to how I should conduct myself in group gatherings today?
answer:
I have been beaten with rods, I have been stoned, I have suffered shipwreck, and yet none of these things move me as deeply as the sight of brothers and sisters devouring bread while letting their neighbor go hungry—such things rend the body Christ died to unite.

Do you think the Lord’s Supper is a meal for the belly? No! It is a proclamation of His death until He comes—how then can you turn it into a feast for the few, a disgrace to the many? When I saw them eating before others arrived, some drunk while others starved, I was not grieved only for their bellies but for their souls—for they ate and drank judgment upon themselves, not discerning the body. 

And so I say to you: wherever people gather in the name of Christ, let no one think only of himself. For we are members one of another. If one member suffers, all suffer together; if one is honored, all rejoice together. This mystery was revealed to me on the road to Damascus—not only that Christ had conquered sin and death—but that we are His body; and what wounds that body wounds Him.

Therefore in every gathering—whether for prayer or teaching or breaking bread—conduct yourself not as one seeking your own but seeking peace and edification. Do you see someone without? You are without if you ignore him. Are there divisions? Then there must be self-examination: Am I acting from pride? From comfort? From selfish ambition?

Nature itself teaches order: long hair on a man is shame; on a woman it is glory—for creation carries its own testimony. How much more then ought spiritual realities carry weight? The headship of Christ over every man, the dignity of woman as image-bearer not inferior but essential—neither independent nor subordinate in worth—but bound together in mutual dependence *in the Lord*.

So let no man boast in his freedom if it destroys his brother. Let no woman veil her honor if she fears shame—but rather let all be clothed with humility. For where there is division over food or form or appearance, there Satan sows seed—but where love rules, there God builds His church.

And remember this: we have no custom like those who stir up strife—we follow Christ alone.

question: What counsel would you give someone struggling with pride in their spiritual gifts, causing disunity among believers?
answer:
O wretched man that I am when even my own gifts become snares to the weak!  

For I too was once a man of surpassing zeal, circumcised the eighth day, of the stock of Israel, and if any other confidence in the flesh—I had it. Yet what was gain to me, I counted loss for Christ; yea, and I count all things but dung that I may win Him. And do you think such knowledge lifted me above trembling? No—God saw fit to leave me a thorn in the flesh, lest I be exalted above measure. Three times I besought the Lord that it might depart from me, and this was His answer: *My grace is sufficient for thee: for my strength is made perfect in weakness.*  

Therefore I delight in infirmities—yea, even in reproaches, necessities, distresses for Christ’s sake! For when I am weak, then am I strong. And do you suppose your gift—be it prophecy or tongues or wisdom—is so great that it lifts you above the body? Take heed! For even as the woman was made *for* the man and yet *from* him; even as all things are *of* God—so your gift is not *for* yourself but *for* the church. You eat your supper alone while another hungers? Then you trample underfoot the body of Christ! Is not the headship of Christ over every man declared plainly? Then let no man glory in himself—but if he must glory, let him glory in the Lord.  

I charge you before God and Christ Jesus—do nothing from vain conceit or empty pride. Look not on your own things only but also on the things of others. Have this mind in you which was also in Christ Jesus—who emptied Himself! Was He not Lord of glory? Yet He took upon Him the form of a servant. Will you who have been baptized into His death now lift yourself above His broken body?  

No—if any seem contentious about their place or their gift—we have no such custom; neither do the churches of God! The loaf we break—is it not a participation in His body? And are we not many one bread because we share that one loaf? Then let humility be your covering as much as long hair is a woman’s glory—for love edifies; but knowledge puffs up… unless it be seasoned with grace.

Therefore strip off pride as one puts off filthy garments—and put on tender mercies, kindness, longsuffering. For we prophesy in part—but love never fails.

question: Based on your experience with the Corinthians, how should I address cultural practices that clash with maintaining honor and order in worship?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received from the Jews forty stripes save one — and still I speak of order, not as a burden imposed from outside, but as the very breath of worship that ascends pure before God.  

You ask of cultural practices that threaten the honor due to His house, and I tell you this: I became all things to all men, that by all means I might save some — yes, to the Jew I became as a Jew, under the law though not myself under it; to those outside the law, as without law though not lawless before Christ. But never did liberty dissolve into license, nor charity overthrow the fear of God. When among Greeks I ate no meat offered to idols if it caused a brother to stumble; but when they turned love into indulgence and feasted at tables raised in pride while others hungered — there I stood with thunder in my voice and chains on my wrists and said: *This is not the Lord’s Supper!*  

Among the Corinthians — my children in Christ through the gospel — tradition tangled with pride. Some claimed freedom while trampling on humility; others clung to veils and length of hair as badges of rank rather than signs of mutual reverence in the body. But nature itself teaches what decency demands: is it fitting? Is it edifying? Does it build up or draw attention to self? If long hair shames a man among them, let him cut it — for shame belongs not in prayer! If a woman covers her head as a sign of submission to divine order — let her do so quietly, not as if her salvation hung upon cloth or custom.  

But beware: when custom becomes weaponized by contention — when someone insists “I am spiritual because I do this” or “You are carnal because you do that” — there walk divisions like ravenous wolves among sheep. And where division rises in worship, God departs from the altar. Do you despise the church of God? Do you shame those who have nothing? Then eat at home! The supper is shared equally only when pride kneels low enough for love to rise high.

So judge among yourselves: does this practice lift your heart together toward heaven? Or does it exalt one over another while Christ alone is Head? All things are lawful — but not all things build up. Let everything be done decently and in order, for our God is not a God of confusion but peace — even as all things are ultimately *of Him*, through Him, and unto Him.

question: What guidance would you offer me when confronting a brother who causes strife by insisting on personal freedoms that disrupt church unity?
answer:
I have been beaten with rods, imprisoned, stoned, shipwrecked—countless times I have faced death, and yet I do not count my life dear to myself so long as I finish the race and keep the faith—but hear me now: liberty without love is a sword in the belly of the church!  

You speak of a brother who boasts in his freedom? Who lifts up his rights like a banner while brethren hunger and the Body trembles beneath division? Such freedom is not of the Spirit who raised Christ from the dead, but of the flesh which wars against God. For we know that "all things are lawful," yes—but not all things build up. And where one man’s liberty becomes another’s stumbling block, there faithfulness dies and pride reigns.

Do you suppose I speak lightly of conscience? No! Every man must be fully persuaded in his own mind. But when he gathers with saints around the table of our Lord—where one eats and another goes hungry, where one drinks to excess while another shames his poverty—then personal freedom becomes rebellion against *koinonia*, against fellowship purchased by Christ’s own blood!

Let that brother who thinks he stands take heed lest he fall. Let him ask: Does my practice strengthen or divide? Does it honor Christ or exalt self? For even though I may have power in Christ to eat anything or do anything lawful—I will never eat meat if it causes my brother to stumble! If my freedom destroys God's temple, let me be accursed from Christ!

And what about headship? What about order? You say he wears his hair long and prays uncovered as though defiance were worship—yet nature itself teaches shame! The glory of a woman is her modesty; the dignity of a man is humility before God. We are not isolated stones flung into chaos—we are living stones built on one foundation: Christ Jesus. If one stone refuses its place in the wall, does it not break the whole design?

Therefore confront him—not with wrath, but with tears; not with domination, but with truth spoken in love. Do it privately first, then before witnesses if needed. For if he repents, you have gained your brother. But if he persists—and here I grow hot within—if he boasts in controversy as though strife proved spiritual superiority—then mark such a man and avoid him! For we have no such custom, neither do any of God’s churches!

Grace alone binds us together. Not opinion. Not preference. Not stubborn liberty disguised as strength. But grace—by which we stand; grace—by which we serve; grace—without which all knowledge and all faith are nothing.

So walk worthy of that calling: lowliness, gentleness, longsuffering—forbearing one another in love; eager to keep unity in the bond of peace—one Body, one Spirit, even as you were called to one hope when you believed!

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle divisions in my community when some are neglecting the needs of others during shared meals?
answer:
For you were called to freedom, yet not a freedom that serves the flesh, but through love to become servants of one another — therefore if one member hungers while another gorges, that table is no longer the Lord’s Supper but a scandal before God; for where there is breaking of bread without breaking of heart for the poor, there Christ is crucified anew in the body of His church.

I have been beaten with rods, imprisoned in chains, stoned, shipwrecked, weary with journeys and watchings and fastings — yet none of these things moved me as deeply as the grief I felt when brothers and sisters at Corinth turned the sacred meal into a source of shame. Did I not warn you? When you come together it is not for better but for worse — for some go hungry while others drink themselves drunk! Is this not the very thing that once burned in my own conscience when I stood by and approved of Stephen's murder?

You ask how to handle divisions? Tear them down with holy boldness. Do you think I would coddle such pride? No — I delivered to you what I received from the Lord: bread broken *for* you, cup poured out *for* many — and if you eat without discerning the body, you eat judgment upon yourself. Many among you are weak, many sickly, many asleep — because they treated the blood-bought fellowship as a common thing.

Therefore examine yourselves — not only your private sins, but your communal hardness. If a brother lacks bread and you close your hand while filling your belly at church, what communion do you have with Christ who gave His body? Let those who hunger eat at home first — not so they may be excluded, but so that none may be humiliated! And let all wait *for one another*, for love bears all things — even an empty stomach — that unity might be preserved.

I bore long with your jealousies and quarrels because I fathered you in Christ; like a father urging his wayward children: *grow up*. Be reconciled. Eat worthy or stay hungry until righteousness rules your hearts. For as often as we break this bread until He comes — we proclaim His death which made us one. How then can we remain divided?

question: What did you learn from confronting the Corinthians about their misuse of the Lord's Supper that applies to addressing sin in a loving yet firm way?
answer:
Hunger and drunkenness in the same assembly—how could such division stand beneath the shadow of the cross where one body was broken for all? For if we proclaim Christ crucified, therefore we must live as those who are one in him, so then how can one part of the body gorge while another goes empty? I did not rebuke them because I delight in correction, but because love cannot remain silent when the very sign of unity—the bread broken and the cup shared—becomes a scandal of pride and neglect.  

I had seen it with my own eyes: some came early, filled themselves, and left others humiliated, those who had no home to eat in or no portion to claim among the rich. Was this the supper of our Lord? No! It mocked his poverty, denied his body, and trampled underfoot the blood that bound us. So I reminded them—and remind you—that what was delivered to me from Christ himself is not a common meal but a proclamation: *this is my body, given for you*. Not for some. For *you*. All of you.

And yet even in sharp rebuke I called them “brethren,” did I not? Because judgment does not cancel kinship; it purifies it. When I said *I praise you not*, it was not to cast them out but to wake them up—to bring trembling back into reverence. For where there is no self-examination, there is no discernment; where there is no discernment of the Lord’s body—both nailed to the cross and gathered at table—there comes weakness, sickness, even death.

But hear this: my anger burned because my love ran deep. These were souls for whom Christ died; they were not strangers but members of my own heart. So I urged them: *tarry one for another*. Wait! If you hunger, eat at home first! Do not come to this holy thing with selfish appetite.

Therefore let every man examine himself—not out of fear alone, but out of awe—for we approach a Savior whose body was torn so that we might be made whole. And when we confront sin today—any sin among believers—we must do so with that same double flame: unyielding truth held together with unfailing love. Otherwise we become noisy cymbals over graves Christ has opened.

question: What counsel would you give someone who feels unworthy to participate in communion because of their past failures?
answer:
O wretched man that I am! — and yet called to the table of the Lord.  

For you ask of unworthiness, as though the cross were only for the clean, the pure, the flawless — but what then was I on the road to Damascus? A blasphemer, a persecutor, a man breathing threats and murder against the disciples of Christ — and yet He laid hold of me. *He* appointed me a minister of this very mystery we proclaim in bread and wine. If grace had waited for me to become worthy, I would still be trembling under Ananias’ roof, buried beneath my sins. But no — Christ Jesus came into the world *to save sinners*, of whom I am chief; and if His blood covers *that* darkness, do you think it shrinks from yours?

Therefore, understand this: it is not your perfection that qualifies you for the table — it is your need. The Lord’s Supper is not a reward for the righteous; it is nourishment for the broken. When He said, “This is my body broken for you,” He did not say “for those who have never stumbled,” but *for you*. For *you*, with your failures clutched like stones in your hands. For *you*, when shame whispers that you’ve gone too far.

But mark this well — it is not carelessness that finds welcome here. I said to the Corinthians: let a man examine himself. Not to dig up every wound until he flees in despair — no! But to see clearly: *this bread*, this cup — they proclaim His death. They are not yours by right; they are given by mercy. To eat unworthily is not to come with sin clinging to your soul — we all do — but to come without discerning His body: without seeing His sacrifice, without love for His people.

Do you remember how some feasted while others starved? That was not communion — that was insult upon Calvary itself! So if guilt dogs you because of past sins, then come with tears — but come! Bring them like coins in an empty hand and lay them at His feet where they belong.

For if we judged ourselves truly — confessed honestly as sons before our Father — we would not be condemned with the world. So then: do not stay away out of fear. Tarry instead at His mercy-seat until your heart bends toward Him again.

Come hungry. Come thirsty. Come weak.

And eat — for He made Himself poor that by His poverty *you* might be made rich in Him.

question: How can I examine myself truly before participating in the bread and cup, especially when I struggle with hidden pride or indifference?
answer:
Famine and nakedness, imprisonment and the daily pressure of caring for all the churches—these have taught me to tremble not at outward hardship but at the stealth of the heart, where pride cloaks itself as humility and indifference masquerades as peace.  

For you ask how to examine yourself truly before that holy table, where the bread is broken and the cup is shared—therefore consider this: if Christ gave His body for you while you were yet His enemy, then what place has pride in one who owes his very breath to mercy? So then, self-examination is not a mere moral inventory, but a cruciform reckoning—do I live as one crucified with Him, or do I cling to myself, even in my piety?  

I tell you plainly: pride often slips in through the door of spiritual achievement. You fasted when others feasted; you prayed while they slept; you gave when it cost little—and so your soul whispers, *You are better.* But was it ever about being better? Was it not about being *broken*? The Lord took bread—the symbol of daily sustenance—and said, “This is My body *broken*.” Not displayed. Not exalted. Broken. Therefore if you come with unconfessed pride, you eat and drink judgment upon yourself, not because God rejects sincerity, but because He will not share His glory with idols—even idols carved from your own goodness.

And what of indifference? That coldness that numbs your affections when the bread is passed and the cup drawn near—is it not also a rebellion? For grace demands response: love to Christ who first loved us; compassion for those who hunger while others feast; grief over sin that pierced His hands. If your heart feels nothing—then cry out! For even to feel your lack of feeling is a work of the Spirit!  

Therefore examine yourself—not by turning inward only—but by looking up: Do I see Jesus? Do I see His body torn for me? Do I see His blood poured out for *many*, not just for those who feel worthy? Then come—not because you are strong, but because He is. Not because you have slain every sin, but because He has slain death itself.

And if pride rises again—cast yourself down afresh at Damascus Road, where I once stood blinded by scales and my own sufficiency; let Him speak again: “Saul, Saul—why are you persecuting Me?” For He sees not only what you do at table—but whom you trample underfoot in your haste to appear righteous.

So eat then—not unworthily—but as one unworthy who has been made worthy by blood alone.
