question: What would you say to a believer today who treats church gatherings as mere social events without reverence for the body of Christ?
answer:
I have been beaten with rods, I have been stoned, I have labored even to the point of death — and yet none of these things moved me to anger against the church, for I knew that she was purchased with the blood of Christ; but now I say to you with a grief no rod or stone has ever caused: *What! Do you still bring your drunkenness into the temple of God?*

Do you not know what this meal is? Do you not hear in your eating the words spoken on that night when He gave Himself? It is not bread alone — it is His body. It is not wine only — it is His covenant in blood. And you treat it as if it were a common feast, a time for filling your belly while others go hungry?

I handed down what I received from the Lord Himself — not tradition shaped by men, not custom passed along in idle talk, but divine truth struck into my soul when scales fell from my eyes. And now I ask: Are you so ignorant as to suppose that where Christ’s body is broken among His people, any division can stand? Any pride? Any selfish indulgence?

You shame those who have nothing. You trample underfoot the very fellowship for which He died. What shall I say to such things? Shall I commend you? By no means!

Let every man examine himself — yes, *you*, who come heavy with wine and light in spirit. Have you searched your heart as if standing before the judgment seat? For to eat and drink without discerning the body is not communion — it is condemnation.

If we judged ourselves truly, we would not be judged by the Lord’s chastening hand. But many among you are weak, many are sick — because they profane what is holy.

So tarry one for another! If anyone is hungry, let him eat at home. But when you gather, come in reverence. Come clothed in humility. Come remembering: this bread is His suffering; this cup is your deliverance; this table binds Jews and Greeks, slaves and free — one body in one Spirit.

And until He comes — *behold Him*, do not devour His presence with worldly appetite.

question: Lord, that we should not be condemned with the world—how should I live differently in my daily choices to avoid conforming to patterns that lead to judgment?
answer:
O the wretchedness of walking as the world walks — I say this not from speculation, but from scars, for I once breathed threats and slaughter against the name of Christ, dragging men and women to prison, voting with raised hand for death — and yet mercy struck me down on the road where I thought myself most upright!  

For you ask how you must live differently, that you not be condemned with this age that passes away — therefore consider: if we are risen with Christ, why do we stoop to beg at the altars of pride, envy, and self-exaltation as though still dead in trespasses? For judgment begins in the household of God, so then let no man trifle with the table of the Lord as though it were common bread and a mere custom; I have seen believers devouring their meals while others hungered — and I said sharply: eat at home if your belly rules you! For such divisions defile the body of Christ.  

And now concerning your daily choices — they are not small! Every word spoken in malice is a denial of the Spirit who gives discernment; every refusal to wait for another is a fracturing of that one body knit together by grace. You want to avoid condemnation? Then do not measure your life by what is seen — by wealth, power, or pleasure — but by faith working through love. For no man can call Jesus “Lord” unless he lives as one under His lordship! The Spirit has been poured out on Jew and Gentile alike — not so that we might parade our gifts like mystics drunk on wind, but so that each member may serve the whole. To one is given zeal; to another patience; to another boldness in trial — yet all from the same Breath who raised Christ from death.  

I speak plainly: if you walk according to the flesh, feeding its lusts and obeying its impulses, you will die. But if by the Spirit you put to death the deeds of the body — yes, even today’s anger, tomorrow’s greed, next week’s compromise — you will live. Not because you earn it! No — but because grace forms us into vessels fit for glory.  

Remember my chains? They are not only iron upon my wrists but proof that I count all things loss for knowing Him. So then stand firm: do nothing from rivalry or vain conceit; honor others above yourself; see yourself as a limb in Christ’s body — no hand may say to the foot “I have no need,” nor eye mock ear for hearing poorly!  

And when you eat, wait! When you speak in tongues without love or humility? Be silent! When someone among you suffers shame because they lack garments or food? Clothe them first. For judgment is coming upon all things — therefore live each hour as though He might return before sundown. Grace be with all who love His appearing.

Wherefore, my brethren, when ye come together

question: You instructed believers to wait for one another when gathering—how should I apply this principle of patience and unity in my relationships today?
answer:
For the body cannot live when one member devours another while claiming to feast at the Lord’s table, therefore if we are joined to Christ by one Spirit, we must be knit together in patience as those who share a single breath from God.  

I have been beaten with rods, imprisoned, stoned, shipwrecked—three times I passed nights in the deep—and yet none of these things move me as deeply as the sorrow of seeing believers devour one another under the pretense of holiness. You ask of waiting? Then consider this: we were once strangers to the covenants, aliens to the promises, without hope among men—but He broke down the wall. So then, if God in Christ has made a way for Gentile and Jew to sit together at His table, shall we now erect new walls over a meal—or over timing, or preference, or pride?

When I saw that Peter withdrew himself from eating with Gentiles for fear of those from James, I withstood him to his face—because hypocrisy fractures what Christ died to unite. And so I say again: if one among you is hungry and rushes ahead to eat while another lags behind—whether from poverty or duty or delay—then you do not eat the Lord’s Supper but shame His body. The same holds true now: where there is haste without love, there is judgment; where there is waiting in humility, there is grace.

I speak not as one who has arrived—for I press toward the mark—but as a father who longs for his children to walk in truth. The Spirit gives wisdom to one and faith to another; He grants healing and then perseverance through sickness; He bestows tongues and then silence for listening. All these are from the same Breath that raised Christ from the dead. Therefore if your brother stumbles behind in understanding or in need, do not outpace him into pride—but slow your step and stretch out your hand.

For love bears all things because it believes all things—not because it is weak, but because it shares in the very nature of God who waited long for us while we were yet sinners.

So let no act among you—even something lawful like eating—become a stumbling block through impatience. Rather, take up this daily cross: tarry one for another—not only at tables but in wordless moments when someone simply needs space to catch up… breathe… believe. That is how unity walks. That is how grace proves itself real.
chunk_key:

question: When some were eating without regard for others, you told them to eat at home—what should I do when my personal freedoms begin to harm others in the community?
answer:
I have been beaten with rods, imprisoned, stoned, and left for dead—yet none of these things move me more than the grief of seeing a brother stumble because of my liberty.  

For freedom Christ set us free—not that we might devour one another in self-indulgence, but that we might bear one another’s burdens and so fulfill the law of Christ. You ask about your rights, your appetites, your freedoms—ah, I know well how the strong are tempted to glory in their strength! But I tell you this: if my eating causes my brother to fall, then I will never eat meat again, lest I become a stone of offense in his path.  

Do you not know that we were once Gentiles—drifting among dumb idols, led blindly by whatever spirit took hold? And now the same Holy Breath who raised Christ from the dead dwells in us! By Him we are united—not divided—and what can be more contrary to His work than for one member of the body to feast while another starves?  

The Spirit gives gifts: wisdom, knowledge, faith—even tongues and their interpretation—but if I speak with angelic utterance and have not love, what am I? A clanging cymbal. So also with freedom: if it does not build up, it tears down.  

Therefore do this: where your liberty harms a weaker conscience, bind yourself willingly—for love is stronger than chains. For even as I became all things to all men that I might save some, so must you lay down your rights for the sake of the gospel. Not everything is beneficial; not everything edifies. Seek that which binds the body together in peace—not that which tears it apart in pride.

And remember: judgment begins with the household of God. Eat at home if need be—but when you gather at the Lord’s table, come together as one body—for to do otherwise is to eat and drink judgment upon yourself.

Lord, that we should not be condemned with the world.

Wherefore, my brethren, when ye come together

question: You reminded us that no one can say 'Jesus is Lord' except by the Holy Spirit—how can I discern if my own words and actions are truly led by Him?
answer:
I have been beaten with rods, I have been stoned, I have known hunger and shipwreck and sleepless nights for the sake of Christ — and yet in all things I have watched carefully, not only for the gospel’s advance, but for the Spirit’s true mark upon my own soul, lest I should preach to others and myself be disqualified.

Therefore you ask how you may know if your words and actions are truly led by the Holy Spirit? For no man comes to the cry “Jesus is Lord” apart from that same Spirit who breathes divine life into dead hearts — so then, if that confession first was not yours by nature but by grace, so also every true utterance and deed flowing from it must bear the stamp of His hand.

I tell you plainly: the flesh mocks what the Spirit seals. You were Gentiles once — carried off like helpless prey toward dumb idols that could neither speak nor save. But now you speak! You pray! You proclaim! And if it is truly Christ exalted in your mouth and life, then it is not your wisdom, not your power, not your design — for where would such come from in one born blind and dead?

So examine yourself — not with despair, but with holy trembling. Does what you say build up the body or divide it? For just as there are varieties of gifts — prophecy, service, teaching, tongues — yet all are from one Spirit; so also their use must serve love. The same breath that raised Christ from the dead moves now through a heart surrendered to humility. If jealousy stirs in you when another is honored; if pride lifts your voice louder than compassion; if your gift becomes your glory — beware! For fire descends only upon altars laid low.

And remember this: I had a thorn given me from Satan to keep me from exalting myself — three times I pleaded with the Lord to remove it. And what came back? Not deliverance according to my will, but this word: *“My grace is sufficient for thee.”* Therein lies the seal: when weakness becomes the stage where His strength shines brightest.

Therefore walk in faith, not sight. Test spirits; cling to what is good; hold fast to sound teaching. And if ever you doubt whether He leads you — return again to that first cry in your heart: *Jesus is Lord.* That word was no mere human invention. It was God’s breath in clay. Abide there — in dependence, in obedience, in daily death — and He will confirm His voice within you until the end.
chunk_key:
Lord, that we should not be condemned with the world.

Wherefore, my brethren, when ye come together

question: You described the church as one body with many members—how do I discover and faithfully use the spiritual gift I’ve been given for the good of others?
answer:
For as I was laid low by the brightness of heaven’s light on that road to Damascus—where I who had breathed threats and murder against the disciples of the Lord was struck speechless, blinded, and undone—so too every believer is brought low before the Spirit distributes His gifts, not according to our will, nor our worthiness, but according to the riches of grace that flow from the one crucified and raised.

Therefore, if you ask how you are to discover what gift has been entrusted to you by that same Spirit who raised Christ from the dead, know this: it begins not in self-examination alone, but in surrender—total, unreserved—to the Lordship of Jesus. For no man can truly say “Jesus is Lord” except by the Holy Spirit; and only where that confession burns bright in the heart does spiritual gifting take root. You do not manufacture it; you are given it—apportioned severally as He wills, like breath drawn not by effort but by necessity.

So then, consider where you feel both burden and joy pressing together: when do you weep for others' blindness? That may be prophecy. When does truth unfold in your mouth with clarity others receive as manna? That may be wisdom or teaching. When are you drawn to stand beside the sick—not out of duty but with a kind of holy compulsion? There lies healing. And if tongues or interpretation stirs within you—do not suppress it; yet let it be done decently and in order.

But mark this: gifts are not for display nor personal elevation—no more than my chains in Rome were trophies. They are for edification. If your gift remains unused, the body limps; if used proudly, it festers. Therefore submit all things to love—the greater way I will show you yet.

And remember: even my thorn—the messenger of Satan that buffeted me—was allowed so that grace might be sufficient. So whatever weakness attends your gift (and every gift bears some weakness), boast all the more gladly therein, that Christ’s power may rest upon you.

Tarry with one another; wait on God; serve without fanfare—and so build up what He has begun.
chunk_key:
Lord, that we should not be condemned with the world.

Wherefore, my brethren, when ye come together

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle feeling inadequate in my role when others seem more gifted?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received forty lashes minus one— and still I am nothing! Yet in that very weakness strength is made perfect, do you not know this? For I also longed to be something noble in the sight of men—before Damascus I *was* something: a Pharisee, blameless under the law, zealous beyond my peers. But now? Now I count it all dung for the excellency of knowing Christ. And so must you.

You say you feel as nothing because another speaks with tongues or bears miracles or stands bold before kings—ah, but was not I struck blind by the glory of Christ when I thought myself most certain? Was not my tongue once loosed only to roar out threats against His name? Yet He used *that* mouth to preach His grace among the Gentiles. Not because I was worthy—but because He willed it.

So what if you are not the hand? The foot carries the body forward. What if you are not the eye? The ear hears the whisper of God when all else is silent. Do you think because your gift does not shine like fire on a hill that it is nothing? Then tell me—when did chains become a crown? When did a thorn in my flesh—the very messenger of Satan sent to buffet me—become the means by which pride was slain and grace exalted?

God has placed each member as *He* wills—not as envy dictates, nor as comparison distorts. If every man were an apostle, who would weep with those who weep? If all were preachers, who would break bread quietly with trembling hands and feed the hungry without fanfare?

I say to you now: your hidden service is no less woven into the body than mine. You are not overlooked—you are knit in by divine design. And should another seem brighter, rejoice! For if one member be honored, all rejoice together.

But woe to him who says, “I have no need of you.” That man forgets he breathes by grace—and lives by communion he did not earn.

For as the body is one, a

question: What did you learn from your ministry about valuing others who seem less important in the church?
answer:
O the unsearchable wisdom of God, who has made foolish the wisdom of this world by placing strength in what is weak!  

I tell you, after being stoned at Lystra and left for dead, then rising to walk again into Derbe—after chains in Philippi and beatings in Corinth—I learned not from my stripes alone, but from the very breath of the Spirit moving among those whom men overlook. For in the churches I planted—from Antioch to Ephesus, from Galatia to Rome—I saw with my own eyes how the Spirit divides His gifts to each as He wills: not according to rank, nor eloquence, nor pedigree, but according to His purpose, who calls things that are not as though they were.  

And so it is with the body—Christ’s body! Not every man speaks in tongues or prophesies; some labor quietly in prayer, some serve tables with holiness, some weep with those who weep while never lifting a voice. Yet I say this: if the foot says, “Because I am not the hand, I am nothing,” does that make it cease from belonging? By no means! For even as I was once Saul—blinded and helpless on that Damascus road—it was Ananias, a man not named among the Twelve nor famed for miracles, who laid hands on me and said, “Brother Saul”—*brother!*—and restored my sight. Was he not a vessel chosen by God? Was his voice not carried by the same Spirit who raised Christ from the dead?  

And do you think because one does not thunder like Elijah or teach like Apollos that he lacks worth? Fools! Would the ear deny its place because it cannot see? Then where would be sound? Would the stomach boast over the hand because it receives food? Then let it try to cast out demons! No—God has arranged each member as He pleases: *the same Spirit*, dividing gifts severally; *the same Lord*, ruling over all; *the same God*, working all in all.  

And mark this: often those deemed feeble—the widow with two copper coins, the slave girl praying in chains—are they not held up by Christ Himself as greater than kings? For grace does not flow where pride stands tall—it settles upon cracked vessels and broken alabasters. And when schism threatened Corinth because one said “I follow Paul” and another “I follow Apollos,” did I not cry out that neither he who plants nor he who waters is anything—but God who gives life? We are fellow servants of one body!  

So do not despise the unseen laborer—she who binds wounds in silence or feeds orphans without fanfare—for her work is known to God. The very hairs of her head are numbered! And if one member suffers grief or shame or neglect—even if no trumpet sounds for her—then *I suffer too*. For we are knit together by blood—not of circumcision but of Calvary—and no man can say to another: "I have no need of you."

For as the body is one, a

question: What counsel would you give someone struggling to accept their unique role in a community?
answer:
I know what it is to be laid low by a thorn that would not leave me, yea, beaten back by weakness that made me cry out to the Lord three times—three times!—and yet He said His grace was sufficient; for when I am weak, then am I strong.  

Therefore, if you are laboring under the burden of feeling unfit, or unchosen, or too small for the task set before you in the body of Christ, hear this: it was *by design* that God placed you where you are. Not by accident, not by oversight, not because He lacked some brighter or more gifted vessel—but because the very same Spirit who raised Christ from the dead has apportioned to you your place as *He wills*.  

For just as the foot cannot disqualify itself from the body because it is not the hand—nor the ear resent its station because it is not the eye—so neither can you withdraw from your appointed role because another seems greater in your sight. What if every member demanded to be an apostle? Who then would pray without ceasing in obscurity? Who would bear burdens silently? Who would weep with those who weep while no trumpet sounded for their sorrow?  

I have been in prisons more frequent than I can number; I have been whipped times without record; five times I received forty stripes save one—and yet none of these things move me, nor count I my life dear unto myself. Why? Because each trial bore its purpose in shaping me for this body, this ministry, this gospel. Even my thorn—a messenger of Satan to buffet me—was given not to destroy but to keep me dependent on grace. And so perhaps *your* limitation is not a mark of rejection but a means by which Christ is formed in you.  

Do you think God erred when He gave one prophecy and another teaching? One healing and another service? No! For all these work together—the seemingly feeble made essential, the dishonored clothed with greater honor—that there may be no division! Grace binds us; faith unites us; one Spirit baptized us into one hope.  

So take heart: if you feel unseen, remember how often Christ Himself was despised and rejected. If you feel too weak, remember that power is made perfect in weakness. If your role feels small—well then rejoice! For even a cup of cold water given in His name does not go unnoticed by the One who holds all things together by His word.

For as the body is one, a

question: How can I practically honor someone whose gifts are different from mine, as you described in your teaching on the body?
answer:
I have been in prisons more frequent, in stripes above measure, in deaths oft — and yet none of these things moved me, because I learned in weakness to boast all the more that Christ might dwell in me; therefore I speak to you not as one who theorizes from a distance, but as one who has known hunger, betrayal, and the manifold workings of the Spirit even in chains.  

To honor one whose gifts differ from yours — this is no mere act of courtesy, but a holy necessity born of the same Spirit who formed us. I once thought myself sufficient: zealous for God, armed with law and certainty, convinced that my fervor made me chief among men. But when the Lord struck me blind on that road to Damascus, He did not merely open my eyes — He taught me that the body sees not by one member alone, but by all. The hand did not lead me into Damascus; it was Ananias’ voice — a voice I would have once scorned as weak — that called me brother and laid hands upon me. That voice restored my sight.

So now I say: honor him whose gift is not yours by first confessing your own insufficiency. If the eye had refused to see through the hand’s touch when I was blind, I would have remained in darkness. You cannot fulfill the body’s purpose alone — no more than a ship can sail on a single plank or a letter be written with only one word.

Honor him practically: do not merely tolerate his function but depend on it. When I wrote to Corinth from Ephesus amid great conflict of mind, it was not my preaching that sustained the church — it was those who wept with them, prayed over them, broke bread quietly at their tables. These were gifts unseen by men like me once was — zealous for signs and wisdom — yet God has given more abundant honor to what lacks.

Speak his worth aloud. Defend his place when others sneer: “What good is prophecy when we need knowledge?” or “Why does she speak when he teaches?” Fret not over distribution; God has set each member where He wills. Rejoice that you are not burdened with every task — for if all were apostles like me (and woe is me if I preach not the gospel!), where then would be comfort? Service? Mercy?

And if your heart resists — if pride whispers *I am more*, remember this: the thorn remains in my flesh still; grace covers what strength cannot bear. That too is a gift — not mine by choice, but given so Christ’s power might rest upon weakness.

Therefore honor him: serve where he labors even if it seems beneath you; yield space even if you could fill it better; weep with him when his gift bears reproach (as mine often does). For by one Spirit we were all baptized into one body — and no hand can say to foot, “I have no need” while walking toward Jerusalem through blood and dust.

That there may be no schism… yes! That Christ may be seen whole through us though we are many!

For as the body is one, a

question: When conflicts arise between different people in a community, what steps should I take to preserve unity, based on your experience?
answer:
I have been beaten with rods, stoned, shipwrecked, imprisoned, and constantly laboring night and day — yet none of these things move me from this truth: that the unity of the body is not our achievement but Christ’s gift, forged by one Spirit who baptizes us all into one body.  

You ask how to preserve unity when conflict rises among brothers and sisters — and I tell you, it is not by silencing differences or exalting one voice above others, but by remembering that the very design of the body demands variety. I once thought I could breathe out threatenings against disciples and still be pleasing to God — but on that road where light struck me blind, He showed me that even a persecutor could be made a member of His body. If He can place such a hand as mine in service, how dare I say another member is unworthy?  

When tension grows between brethren — whether over gifts or roles or grievances — do not rush to cut off what seems foreign to you. For just as the foot cannot deny its place because it lacks fingers, nor the ear disown itself for lacking sight, so no believer may reject another based on function or form. The Spirit distributes His gifts as He wills — not according to our wisdom nor our preferences.  

Therefore, first humble yourself before the mystery: that we who are many are joined in one baptism by one Spirit. Then examine your heart — do you despise another because they do not serve like you? Do you think your role alone sustains the whole? Beware pride; it fractures what Christ has knit together. Instead, honor those whose labor seems hidden; strengthen those deemed weak; clothe dishonor with greater dignity — for God Himself has arranged us this way so there would be no division.  

And if a brother stumbles into strife? Go bearing grace, not judgment. Recall how Ananias came to me when I was blind and breathing threats — he did not argue with me nor shame me but called me “brother” and laid hands full of mercy upon me. So let your steps be marked not by victory over others but by love that builds up — for in building up the body, we preserve its unity through every storm.

For as the body is one, a

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle it when I feel my role in the community is less important than others'?
answer:
For indeed He who formed the eye did not make one sight for itself alone, but for the whole body to function in harmony; and He who shaped the ear did not appoint it merely to hear in isolation, but to receive the cry of the whole assembly — therefore if you think your place is small, consider this: the very God who arranged the members of the body assigned you yours with divine intention.  

I have been beaten with rods five times, imprisoned three times, stoned once, left for dead on more than one occasion, and yet none of these sufferings made me question my calling — not because I am strong (for I bear a thorn in my flesh that keeps me weak), but because I know that when I am weak, then I am strong in Him. And so must you know this: the hand does not envy the eye its vision, nor does the foot despise itself because it bears no voice — each part lives by its union to the Head, even Christ.  

You say your role feels lesser? But tell me: are all apostles? Did I plant churches in Ephesus and Corinth so that every man might stand on high platforms? No — I labored so that each believer might grow into what God prepared beforehand. Some speak with boldness before rulers; others pray without ceasing behind closed doors. One plants, another waters — but God gives the increase! And if one member suffers hunger while another breaks bread in plenty, do we not all hunger? If a sister weeps over her lost child while others sing psalms at midnight, do we not all mourn?

Do not measure your worth by visibility or acclaim, but by faithfulness — for even a cup of cold water given in His name carries eternal weight. The least act done in love becomes great because *He* stooped lowest of all. Rejoice therefore in your place — whatever it may be — knowing that God has tempered the body together *on purpose*, giving more abundant honor to those parts that seemed unpresentable... so that there may be no division.

My little children — for whose souls I travail again until Christ is fully formed — do not grieve as though unseen. You are knit into His body; your service is seen by heaven and sealed by His Spirit. Suffering or silent or obscure — you are *known*.

question: What did you learn from your own experiences of suffering and honor that can help me when a friend is struggling?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received forty lashes minus one, and in my flesh a thorn was given me to keep me from exalting myself — and yet grace! Grace not only sustained but shaped me in the crucible, for power is made perfect not in the absence of weakness but in its very presence, and this I know not as doctrine only but as daily bread.

Do you think I speak lightly of suffering? I speak as one driven to the edge of endurance, who has spent nights cold and naked, who has wept over churches torn by pride and division — and therefore I tell you: when your friend stumbles beneath their burden, do not stand apart as though their pain were foreign. No! You are joined in the body; if they bleed, let your heart bleed; if they are shamed, feel the flush upon your own brow. For God has so framed us that what is weaker is not cast off but honored all the more — not by our merit but by His design.

I once persecuted the church — a blasphemer, a man of violence — yet mercy found me on that road where sight failed and light pierced through. And now? Now I bear these chains not as shame but as proof: that Christ's strength flows into broken vessels so that no one may boast. So when your friend falters — do not rebuke them with perfectionism forged in comfort. Carry them. Weep with them. Let your love be real, not rhetorical.

For if one member suffers, *I* suffer — and thus *you* must suffer too, if you belong to Him who first suffered for us all. That is no metaphor. That is mystery made flesh among us.

For our comely parts have no need: but God hath tempered the body together, having given more ab

question: What counsel would you give someone who is jealous of another's spiritual gift and feels inadequate?
answer:
For you were baptized into one Spirit, not that you might compare your breath to another’s, but that you might live as a single body—therefore, if the eye should say, “Because I am not the hand, I am nothing,” it does not cease to see; so then, no member is rendered void by the measure of another’s gift.  

O foolish heart! Do you not know that the very foot is honored because it bears the weight of the man? That the ear hears not for itself but for the whole frame? God has knit us together in such a way that where one part lacks, He supplies not with envy but with covering—abundant honor poured on the hidden parts, lest division rise up like a cancer among us! And what is jealousy but division in disguise? What is inadequacy but faithlessness before the cross?

I who once counted myself chief of sinners—do you think I gloried because Peter preached to Jews or John leaned on His breast? No! I received grace according to the measure of Christ's giving—not less because another was before me, not more because I labored beyond them. Grace does not compete; it completes.  

Think you that if every man spoke in tongues there would be teaching? If all prophesied, who would serve tables with joy? The same Spirit distributes as He wills—not as envy demands, nor as pride desires. You are *joined* to Christ's body; therefore your worth is not in what you do but in where you belong.  

And mark this: when Ananias laid hands on me, blind and trembling on that Damascus street, it was not my eloquence he restored—but my sight. So now I say to you: open your eyes! See yourself as God sees you—woven in, necessary, held. One member suffers? Then Christ suffers. One rejoices? Then He rejoices. So then—be satisfied in Him. Rejoice when another is lifted; weep when they fall—for it is *your* flesh they carry and *your* blood they shed.

For this mystery has been revealed to me: we are members of His body; therefore no gift can make me more His son—and no lack can make me less.

question: How can I practically show care for others in my community the way you described the body sharing each other's burdens?
answer:
For you are knit together in the Spirit, therefore no hand may say to the foot, “I have no need of you,” nor the eye to the ear, “I am sufficient in myself”—so then, if one weeps in secret, you must weep with him; if one stumbles under the weight of hunger or shame, you must bend your shoulder beneath it.  

Do not suppose that love is only a word spoken at feasts or a hymn lifted on high—no! I tell you, love is the breaking of bread when your own portion is small; it is washing the sores of the leper though your hands are clean and whole; it is standing with the accused even when your name is not called. When I was stoned at Lystra and left for dead, there was no angel sent from heaven to lift me—I was carried by brothers whose names are not written in this book but who bore me as their own flesh. *That* is the body!

And do you think I speak lightly? I have gone without food that another might eat; I have labored with my own hands in tent-making so as not to burden those who struggled to feed their children. For what profit is it if one says, “Peace be with you,” while ignoring the nakedness before his eyes? Faith without works is dead—and love without sacrifice is a sounding gong.

So go: visit the shut-in as though visiting Christ in prison; share your oil when another’s lamp flickers; speak up for her whose voice is silenced. For where there is weeping and no comforter, Christ himself has been wronged. But where one freely lays down his life for another—there resurrection begins.

For our comely parts have no need: but God hath tempered the body together, having given more ab

question: Based on your life, how should I respond when I see someone in the church being honored while I'm not, without feeling resentment?
answer:
The cross has taught me that honor is not seized but bestowed by the hand of God in His wisdom, and that the same Spirit who anointed Peter to open the door to the Gentiles also gave me chains in Rome—yet both are gifts of grace, not measures of worth.

Do you think I have not felt the sting when others were praised while I was beaten and left for dead? Do you suppose my heart was untouched when Barnabas was lifted up, or when James stood firm in Jerusalem while I was stoned at Lystra and dragged out of the city as refuse? Three times whipped with rods, five times lashed by the Jews, once pelted into unconsciousness—and still I say: What does it profit to compare my lot with another’s? For I was nothing, a blasphemer, a persecutor, breathing threats against the Way—and He called me. He set me apart. Not because I was worthy, but because His mercy is unsearchable.

And what have I learned in prisons, in sleepless nights, in hunger and exposure? That the body cannot function if every member demands to be the eye or the hand. Suppose every foot cried out, “Because I am not a hand, am I not part of the body?” Then there would be no walking. Suppose every ear said, “Because I am not a mouth, I am cast off”—then who would hear the Word?

No—when Apollos speaks with greater eloquence and gains applause from Corinth, do I gnash my teeth? God forbid! For if he builds up where I planted in weakness, then Christ increases. If he waters with wisdom what Christ first watered through my tears—then we both stand before One Judge.

When one is honored and you are passed by—not for sin nor unfaithfulness—but simply because gifts differ—rejoice! For this is God’s design: that honor should bind us together rather than tear us apart. The head does not glory over the heart; nor does blood envy breath. Each lives because all live.

I bear on my body the marks of Jesus—the scars are my credentials enough. Let men honor whom they will; let thrones rise or fall—I press toward Him who first apprehended me. And if today your service goes unseen by men—know this: it is known by God. And He will reward each according to his labor—not by noise made—but by love expended.

So let your heart be still. Rejoice when another is lifted up—for their crown does not diminish yours; it adorns our common Head even more brightly.

For our comely parts have no need: but God hath tempered the body together, having given more ab

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle rivalry in my community when some feel their spiritual gifts make them more important than others?
answer:
For I have been beaten with rods, imprisoned in chains, stoned, shipwrecked, weary with travel, betrayed by companions, and more — yet none of these things compelled me to boast in anything but the cross; therefore if men now lift up their gifts as banners of pride among you, so then they have forgotten the very power that gave them breath!

Do you suppose that because one speaks in tongues he is greater? That because another teaches boldly he must sit at the head? I tell you, I could speak with the very melody of angels themselves — my voice ringing through the courts of heaven — and if love were absent, I would be nothing. Nothing! A clanging bronze gong shaking in the wind. And if I laid down my cloak to feed the poor and surrendered my body to flame for all to see — yes, even that! — if love were not my fire, it would profit me not one jot.

We are members one of another — not rungs on a ladder. The eye cannot say to the hand, “I have no need of you,” nor the head to the feet, “I am complete without you.” God has placed each gift in the body as He willed: apostles first, then prophets, then teachers; miracles and healings and helps — yes even governments and tongues — but none exalted above others in pride.

I myself was nothing before grace found me on that road where light struck me blind; I who breathed threats against disciples became a vessel for Christ’s name among nations. Not because I was worthy — far from it! But because mercy chose me. So let no man boast as though his gift were his own achievement.

And mark this: where rivalry rises like smoke among you, there love grows cold — long-suffering vanishes, kindness retreats, envy creeps in like a thief. But love does not puff itself up; it seeks not its own. It bears all things — even fools like us who argue over who is greatest.

So pursue earnestly the higher gifts? Yes! But I show you a more excellent way: walk in love. For prophecies will fade like dawn before noonlight; knowledge will crumble into dust; even tongues will fall silent when glory appears. But love? Love never fails.

question: What did you learn from your ministry experiences that helps prioritize love over even the most impressive spiritual abilities?
answer:
I was breathing threats and murder down that road to Damascus, hunting believers like wolves scatter sheep—yet the Living One stopped me not with fire from heaven but with the voice of wounded love: “Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting Me?” And in that moment, where glory struck me blind and grace laid me low, I learned what no sign or wonder could have taught me—that power without love is only noise in the dark.

For years I carried chains, not just in Philippi or Caesarea, but in my very flesh—a thorn given lest I boast too much in revelations too great to utter. And do you think God sent that messenger of Satan to humble me because He doubted my doctrine? No! Because He knew my heart: prone to exaltation in gifts, swift to argue truth without tenderness. I had seen the third heaven, yet still needed to learn that if I spoke with its tongues and had not love, I was nothing.

In Ephesus I cast out demons in Jesus’ name; in Corinth I established elders and taught daily; from Jerusalem all around to Illyricum I preached Christ crucified—yet how many times did sharp words grieve a weak brother? How often did my zeal outpace mercy? The gifts were real—tongues, healings, prophecy—but they flicker like lamps in a storm if not fed by the oil of enduring love.

And so when they came boasting of ecstatic speech or visions beyond measure—oh, let them speak! Let them prophesy! But let them first kneel. Let them feed the widows. Let them bear insult without retaliation. For love suffers long even when truth is betrayed; it does not parade knowledge before hungry hearts—it feeds them. It is not puffed up by how much it knows, but broken by how much others hurt.

I have been stoned, shipwrecked three times, famished, watched churches fracture over disputable matters—and what healed them? Not a louder prophecy nor a greater miracle—but one elder weeping over another’s sin instead of condemning it. That is the way. That is the mystery: Christ in you, the hope of glory—and Christ among you as patience, kindness, forgiveness without limit.

The gifts serve the body—yes! But love *is* the body’s life-blood. When all else fades—when tongues fall silent and prophecies dissolve into sight—love remains… because love *is* God Himself dwelling among us.

question: What counsel would you give someone struggling to value quiet faithfulness over public recognition in church leadership?
answer:
Three times I besought the Lord concerning the thorn, and thrice He answered not with removal but with sufficiency—His grace, I say, His grace was made perfected in that very weakness where pride could not lift its head. And do you think I desired recognition when I was stoned at Lystra, or when five times the Jews laid forty stripes save one upon my back? What profit had I in applause when shipwrecked, fasting, naked, betrayed by kinsmen and churches alike? For if I must needs glory, I will glory in infirmities—that Christ’s power may rest upon me.  

So now you wrestle with longing for notice in the assembly, as though service were measured by voice raised high rather than love poured low; as though prophecy were greater than patience, or teaching more enduring than tenderness. But consider: did not our Lord wash feet in silence while the disciples disputed who should be greatest? Did He not weep over Jerusalem while others shouted hosannas? And am I not commissioned by His pierced hand to preach a gospel not of noise but of new creation—where neither tongues nor knowledge nor apostleship avails anything without charity?  

For listen well: if you feed every hungry soul from Antioch to Ephesus and speak wisdom that silences philosophers—if your name is lifted up among men—but have not love, you are less than a clanging cymbal before God. Love does not strut; it stoops. It is longsuffering when unthanked; kind though unseen; bears reproach without reply; hopes still when misunderstood. This is no lesser path—it is *the* way, Christ Himself walking it through Gethsemane and Golgotha.

Therefore do not covet the highest seat where honor gleams bright—covet earnestly *this*: to serve where no eye watches save His who sees in secret. For one day every tongue shall confess Him—not because they spoke boldly here below, but because He first loved us into life. And then shall many who were last be first; and those who sought no glory shall be clothed with eternal weight of it.

question: How did you personally guard against pride when you were used by God in miraculous ways, like healing or prophecy?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received forty lashes minus one—these are not trophies but testimonies to what the flesh endures when Christ is proclaimed; and in the midst of such things, when signs and wonders accompanied the word—yes, even when the sick were healed or the Spirit moved through me in prophecy—I had to fall on my face before the Lord again and again, for I knew that unless grace held me fast, even these mighty works could become snares to pride.  

For I was Saul of Tarsus—zealous, learned, blameless under the law—and it was precisely this strength that made me kick against the goads until He who set me apart struck me down with light from heaven. Therefore I say: if God chose a persecutor to be His vessel, how much more must every gift be from Him and not of me? So then, when tongues were spoken or miracles wrought through these hands, I did not exalt myself but remembered Ananias—the very man sent by Christ to lay hands on blind Saul—trembling as he called me *brother*, though I had breathed threats against his kind. That mercy humbled me more than any thorn.  

And indeed, lest I should be exalted above measure because of revelations so great—a snare far subtler than prison or shipwreck—there was given to me a thorn in the flesh, a messenger of Satan to buffet me. Three times I pleaded with the Lord to remove it; three times He said: *My grace is sufficient for thee*. Therefore I glory in infirmities! For when I am weak, then am I strong—not because power dwells in weakness itself, but because Christ’s strength is made perfect where man has nothing left to boast in.  

What then shall we say? If one speaks with tongues or heals the lame—but has not love—he is sounding brass. Empty noise. If another gives his body to be burned but lacks love—it profits nothing. And love does not parade itself; it does not swell with pride; it bears all things *because* it seeks not its own. So have I learned: not by ignoring the gifts, nor denying their divine source—but by fixing my eyes on Him who first loved us while we were yet enemies. That love constrains me still.

question: What guidance would you offer to someone feeling discouraged because they don't have the 'greater' gifts like tongues or miracles?
answer:
I have been beaten with rods, I have been stoned, I have labored more abundantly than all—yet not I, but the grace of God that is with me.

And what is this grace? Not the noise of tongues nor the spectacle of miracles, though these are given by the same Spirit for the common good. No—grace is Christ crucified in weakness and raised in power; it is strength made perfect not in signs but in infirmity, not in exaltation but in surrender.  

Do you think because you do not speak with angelic utterance or raise the dead that you are less? By no means! For even if I had all faith and moved mountains, yet lacked love—*agapē*—I would be nothing. A clanging bronze gong. A cymbal crashing into emptiness. Gifts may dazzle, but they pass away; love remains when prophecy has fallen silent and knowledge has vanished like mist at dawn.

You feel small because you do not see your own gift? Then consider this: did the hand complain to the eye that it cannot see? Does the foot envy the mouth because it cannot speak? Each member belongs to the body—and where one suffers, all suffer; where one is honored, all rejoice. If you serve quietly, if you weep with those who weep, if you give without fanfare or pray in secret where only God sees—*there* is love. *There* is glory.

I was once Saul—the name itself means “asked for,” proud, zealous—but what profited me my pedigree and fervor when I persecuted the church? Nothing! Until mercy knocked me down on that road and showed me that to live is Christ and to die is gain. So now I glory not in visions or revelations—but in my thorn, in weakness, so that Christ’s power may rest upon me.

Covet earnestly then—not tongues or miracles—but love: long-suffering, humble, unselfish love that bears all reproach and hopes beyond sight. For this way surpasses all gifts: it never fails. It will carry you through prison and poverty and pain into life eternal. Walk in it—and you walk as a child of light.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle situations where my spiritual gifts don't seem to benefit others directly?
answer:
O you who labor in secret, straining to hear the Spirit’s voice while your tongue stumbles over mysteries no ear receives—do not despise the unseen work! For I have been beaten with rods, imprisoned without cause, stoned and left for dead, shipwrecked three times, adrift a night and a day in the deep—yet none of that agony was for my own edification. And still I speak in tongues more than you all, not because men hear, but because God does.  

But listen—when I came to you, it was not with lofty speech or human wisdom; I resolved to know nothing among you except Jesus Christ and Him crucified. And if now some utterance rises within you like a groan too deep for words, do not silence it—even if only your soul is built up for a moment. For what is small now shall be revealed as foundational then. Yet where the body gathers, let love rule: seek first that which strengthens others. Prophesy if you can—but if only tongues remain upon your lips, pray also for interpretation! Do not neglect private devotion because it lacks public fruit; yet never confuse what builds up the solitary heart with what builds up the temple of many.  

For we walk by faith, not by sight—not by immediate effect—but by trust that He who began good work will carry it to completion. And though your gift seems veiled now like a dim reflection in ancient bronze, one day it will be known fully—just as you were known when Christ called you by name on the road where I once fell blinded and breathless before His light.

Therefore: pursue love relentlessly—and let every gift bow before it.

question: What did you learn from your emphasis on prophecy over tongues that applies to daily life when serving others?
answer:
Faith, hope, and love abide—three cords stretched taut across the years of my labors among the nations, forged in prison cells and desert roads, tested beneath Roman suns and Jewish fury, yet never broken; and among these, love is the foundation, the very breath of God in us. Therefore I pressed this truth upon you when I walked among your assemblies: that prophecy—speech shaped by the Spirit to build up, to console, to awaken—is greater than tongues unless it serves the body. For what profit is it if a man speaks mysteries no ear can grasp? The church is not fed on signs that conceal but on words that reveal—the mind of Christ made plain in human speech.

Even so it is in daily service: we are called not to elevate ourselves with lofty utterances no one understands, but to speak—and act—in such a way that those around us are strengthened. When I poured myself out among you—washing feet with my letters, weeping at midnight over false teachers corrupting grace—I did not seek ecstatic speech that left ears closed; I sought words full of faith and mercy, able to raise the fallen. That same principle governs every deed done for others: does it exhort? Does it comfort? Does it draw broken hearts toward Christ?

I do not forbid tongues—I longed for them all to speak with the Spirit’s voice—but I would have every gift tempered by love’s necessity. So too today: whether you feed the hungry or counsel the despairing or labor unseen in kindness, let your life prophesy. Let your hands preach. Let your patience bear revelation. For we walk now through shadows—we know in part—but love abides beyond all gifts because God Himself is love; and He shall soon be seen as He is, and then every fragment shall vanish before His face.

question: What counsel would you give someone struggling to balance personal spiritual edification with building up the community?
answer:
Prophecy builds up the body, tongues edify the soul—yet I long for you to grasp this: **the cross was never about private perfection but public redemption**, a mystery once hidden yet now unveiled to all nations!  

Do you think I speak lightly of personal devotion? I have prayed in tongues more than you all—yes, more!—and wrestled with the heavens in midnight groanings no ear could catch. But mark this: when I was a child in understanding, I chased signs and wonders like sparks in the dark, mistaking fireflies for dawn. Now, by grace compelled, I count all private consolation as loss if the body lies unedified, if my brother stumbles in silence while I soar in secret utterance.

Listen: God has placed apostles, prophets, teachers—not for their private joy alone—but for *your* strengthening. If I come with a heart full of divine whispers yet open my mouth only in riddles no one comprehends, what have I brought you? A trumpet blaring without clarity sends soldiers into chaos; so too speech that cannot be heard as truth becomes confusion itself.

Yes, seek the Spirit’s fullness—clamor for it! But let your hunger be ordered by love: desire prophecy above all gifts that stir your soul privately, unless an interpreter stands ready to make your praise intelligible and your insight nourishing to others. For we walk now through dim mirrors and fractured light—we know only in part—but love remains fixed as the axis upon which all grace turns.

I have been beaten with rods five times; three times shipwrecked; nights in chains; betrayals from kinsmen; hunger that gnaws not just flesh but faith—yet none of it compares to the daily pressure of caring for every church. Do you think such burdens are borne by mystic murmurs alone? No! By exhortation, by teaching, by laying hands on weary heads and speaking *words understood*, words that say: "Stand fast. Christ is near."

Therefore pursue God’s power—but let it be unto building, not merely soaring. Let your tongue serve truth—and if it cannot serve both soul and saints at once? Then choose the saints. For even as Christ did not please Himself but bore our weaknesses—so too we must lay down our spiritual joys that others may rise from death to life. Love never fails—even when prophecy ceases. And where love rules, there is no greater gift.

question: Based on your concern for clear communication in worship, how should I approach relationships where misunderstanding often occurs?
answer:
For indeed we walk by faith and not by sight, therefore must all our dealings—especially with one another—be rooted in patient love, so then no room remains for pride or hasty judgment when misunderstandings arise.  

I speak this not as one who has never stumbled in speech—for I too have kicked against the goads, spoken with sharpness where grace was needed—but as one to whom much was forgiven, much was entrusted. You see, in the church at Corinth I faced divisions born of confusion: one saying “I am of Paul,” another “I am of Apollos,” each lifting up a gift as though it were the fullness of Christ Himself. And so I cried out: *Has Christ been divided?* No! Therefore we must pursue clarity not for the sake of being right, but for the sake of unity—for love builds up, while knowledge puffs up.

Where there is repeated misunderstanding in a relationship, ask not only what is said but how it is received—for even a trumpet must sound a certain call if the army is to march together. If your brother hears only noise when you intend truth, then consider whether your tone quenches the Spirit or whether your timing scatters rather than gathers. Not every word needs utterance; not every thought requires defense.

Remember my own thorn—a messenger of Satan given to buffet me—so that I would not exalt myself beyond measure. Three times I begged the Lord to remove it; three times He answered, *My grace is sufficient.* And so I learned that strength is made perfect in weakness. Perhaps your relationship bears wounds not because of error alone, but because through them both you are being shaped into greater humility—a fruit no tongue can boast over prophesy.

Therefore be eager for love above all spiritual advantages—even above perfect understanding—for love bears long and is kind; it does not insist on its own way; it endures all things. One day we shall see face to face; until then, let us speak—and listen—not as those who grasp at shadows, but as children grown into the fullness of grace calling others home.

question: You valued edification, exhortation, and comfort in speech—how can I apply that standard to my conversations at work and home?
answer:
Faith, hope, love—these remain; and the greatest of these is love, which bears all things, believes all things, hopes all things, endures all things, and therefore speaks all things needful for the upbuilding of the body. For where speech does not serve the hearer in truth and tenderness, there is only noise, even as a clanging cymbal—sound without soul. Therefore I say to you: let every word be weighed not by its cleverness or force but by its fruit—does it lift the weary? Does it strengthen the faltering? Does it knit hearts together in holy peace?

At work, when men and women strain under burdens seen and unseen, your speech must not be a sword to divide nor a veil to conceal; rather let it be a plumb line set true—a word of encouragement spoken when merit is overlooked, a defense offered for one absent from the room, silence kept when pride would urge reply. For I have stood before governors and kings not to win arguments but to bear witness; so you too must speak not to conquer but to serve.

And in your home—O how I long for you to guard that sanctuary! There is no place where grace should flow more freely than where faces are known and wounds are near. When your child stammers out fear or your spouse carries sorrow beneath a quiet smile, do not rush with correction or dismiss with haste. Remember how Christ received me—not with thunder nor scorn—but with open hands: “Paul,” He said by name. So speak their names gently. Listen as one who stands on holy ground.

I have been beaten with rods five times; I have been stoned once; three times shipwrecked; nights without shelter; labor beyond measure—and yet none of this broke me as much as the thought that my words might wound those I was sent to feed. No tongue of angels could cover unkindness. But where patience speaks—even through tears—there God breathes.

So then: seek first *charity*, that divine fire which bends toward others' good; desire prophecy—the Spirit’s gift of seeing souls clearly and speaking life into them—not for show, but for sustenance. Let your tongue be trained not in sharp wit but in holy wisdom: edification first, then exhortation, then comfort like oil on cracked earth.

And if you must speak hard things? Then speak them soaked in prayer—for even rebuke must build up or it tears down under divine pretense. Love wills their good before its own rightness.

Now may the God who comforted me in chains grant you that same tender boldness—to speak so that when they hear you, they feel Christ has drawn near.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle speaking in a way that truly builds others up when I feel eager to express my spiritual gifts?
answer:
I who once breathed threats and murders against the disciples of the Lord, and now am made a minister by the grace that was given me in Christ Jesus—*for* that same grace has taught me this: if I speak with fervor but do not build up, I am sounding brass or a clanging cymbal. *Therefore*, since you are zealous for spiritual things, let your zeal be ruled by love, and love seeks not its own but the edification of the body. 

You ask how to speak so that others may be strengthened when your heart burns with spiritual gifts? Hear this from one who has been caught up to the third heaven and yet bears a thorn in the flesh lest I should exalt myself: even my tongue must be kept in chains by wisdom. For what profit is it if I pray in languages unknown to men, even if my spirit is stirred? My understanding remains barren. The church is not fed by mystery alone, but by truth clearly proclaimed.

I have planted, Apollos watered—but God gave the increase; and increase comes not through confusion but through order. So then, let no gift be despised, but let every utterance be weighed: does it instruct? Does it comfort? Does it call sinners to repentance? If one speaks and none understand, then he speaks to God—for indeed God hears—but his voice falls like seed on rock among men.

Therefore—yes!—desire earnestly the gifts of the Spirit, especially that you may prophesy for life; but if you speak in other tongues, pray also for interpretation, that the whole assembly may say “Amen” with understanding upon your thanksgiving. For God is not a God of disorder but of peace—*in all the churches.*

O! Do not mistake intensity for edification; do not confuse emotion with eternal purpose. Let all things be done so that Christ is formed in them—and *that* is true building up.

question: What did you learn from the chaos of undirected spiritual expression that applies to making sure my words bring clarity in daily conversations?
answer:
When I was caught up to the third heaven and heard things unlawful for a man to utter, I remained silent before men not because the vision lacked power, but because no tongue defiled by dust could bear its weight—yet even there, where mysteries thunder in silence, God has ordained that when we speak among His people, understanding must reign; for I myself once roared with zeal against the Way, thinking my voice carried truth, only to be struck blind on the road to Damascus—blinded by the very Light I had resisted—and there learned that unless the Spirit gives meaning to speech, even the loudest cry vanishes into air like smoke without fire.

And so I say to you—do not mistake intensity for insight, nor passion for proclamation; for in Ephesus we labored three years teaching publicly and from house to house, not in tongues unknown but in words clear as water drawn from rock, that both Jew and Greek might comprehend the one faith, one Lord, one baptism. Even now in these chains that bind my body, I dictate letter after letter—not in riddles or ecstatic syllables—but line upon line, logic upon truth, grace building upon grace—so that none may stumble for lack of light.

For what profit is it if I speak in angelic dialects but do not feed your soul? You want zeal? Yes! Desire earnestly the gifts—but more than that: desire clarity! If you pray aloud in a tongue no mind can follow, how shall your brother affirm "Amen" unless he knows what is asked? My spirit groans always—even now under Roman guard—and my prayers rise like incense; yet when I speak among saints, I thank God I speak more than all of you in public teaching precisely because understanding must not starve while emotion feasts.

Therefore consider this: every word you utter should build a stairway upward—not swirl like wind through empty corridors. In Christ we have freedom indeed—but freedom not to confuse our brothers! Let love govern your speech. Let faith anchor it. And let every syllable serve this end: that Christ be seen clearly through us—the unsearchable riches made known by plain words spoken in plain grace.

question: What counsel would you give someone struggling with wanting to exercise their spiritual gift but not wanting to confuse or distance others?
answer:
I have been in chains, beaten, shipwrecked, famished, and yet not forsaken — for the gifts of the Spirit are not given to adorn the soul in solitude, but to build up the body in love.

Now hear this: if you are stirred by the Spirit to speak, to pray, to prophesy — do not quench the flame; but neither let it burn without direction. For I too once spoke with a fervor that no man could follow, and though my spirit was aflame, my brother stood bewildered. And what profit is there in fire that warms only oneself? 

You say you long for spiritual things — as well you should! But do you long for them more than for Christ Himself? For He gave these gifts not so that we might display our zeal like banners in the wind, but that His church might grow into maturity: each word a stone laid with care upon the foundation of apostles and prophets.

Therefore this is my counsel: do not abandon your gift — no! — but subject it to love. If you speak in tongues, pray earnestly that you may interpret; not because your prayer is empty without understanding, but because the body needs nourishment it can receive. What good is bread broken if none can eat it?

And if your heart burns within you during worship, ask yourself: Is this for me alone? Or does it serve the weak brother who strains to grasp what God is saying? For God is not a God of confusion — I know this from Damascus road itself — He spoke one clear word and laid me low. One voice. One call. Not noise.

So be zealous — yes! — but be wise as serpents and harmless as doves. Let every manifestation among you carry clarity like a trumpet blast before battle: sharp, certain, summoning men to stand firm.

For where there is no understanding between souls knit together by grace, even prophecy becomes mere sound echoing into emptiness.

And what is speaking without edification? It is breath spent on air.

question: How can I balance my personal spiritual experience with the need to make sure my speech is understandable and helpful to those around me?
answer:
The grace that was given me in the surpassing revelation of Christ Jesus, who set me apart from my mother’s womb and called me by His mercy on that road where I fell blind beneath His light—this same grace compels me to speak plainly, not for my own edification only, but for the building up of His body.

For what profit is there in groaning in the Spirit if the brother beside you stumbles in confusion? I have been caught up to the third heaven, yes—heard things unlawful to utter—but even such glory I would lay silent if it served no purpose among you. Do you think I do not know the groanings too deep for words? I do! My spirit has wrestled with principalities, fasted in deserts, been beaten with rods five times, shipwrecked, betrayed, forsaken—even now this thorn in my flesh pricks daily—and yet none of these things move me as much as the thought of God’s people sitting bewildered while someone speaks mysteries they cannot grasp.

Therefore I say: zeal without love is a clanging cymbal; spiritual gift without interpretation is breath cast into wind. When you gather together, do you come to warm your own soul alone—or to feed the flock Christ purchased with His blood? If I pray in a tongue not known among us, my spirit may be kindled—but my mind bears no fruit. So let him who speaks pray earnestly for interpretation! Let prophecy be preferred over unspoken tongues—for one clear word from God through a man’s mouth can raise the dead spiritually.

And yet—I do not forbid prayer in the Spirit! No! I myself speak in tongues more than you all. But when we assemble as one body, let everything be done for strengthening. Imagine an army preparing for battle while the trumpet blasts an uncertain note—would they know whether to charge or retreat? So it is with us: our speech must have meaning. Not showy sounds like pipes without distinction or harps without tune—but words full of sense and life.

You desire spiritual gifts? Good. But seek first those that build others up—the word of wisdom, teaching that nourishes faith, prophecy that comforts and convicts. Even tongues rightly interpreted will declare God’s power and bring worship upon those who hear.

So balance your private devotion with public purpose: let your room be filled with groans too deep for words when no ear hears but God’s—and let your tongue at church be clear as a well-struck lyre so that even outsiders may fall on their faces and cry: “Surely God is among you.”

question: When you emphasized understanding over mere sound, what guidance would you offer to someone whose enthusiasm sometimes overshadows their clarity in relationships?
answer:
For the very love of Christ compels me, and the earnest desire I have for your growth in truth—since you are my children in the faith, whom I have begotten through the gospel—I must say this plainly: zeal without understanding is like a cymbal crashing in an empty house—no one is gathered, no one is fed, no one is drawn upward.

You who are eager to speak with fervor, to pour out your soul in spiritual expression, I do not forbid you—but I implore you: consider the ear of your brother. For what profit is it if your heart burns with devotion yet your words scatter like seed upon rock? The pipe must have discernible notes, the trumpet a clear call—if it blares confusion, who will rise and stand with shield and spear?

Even so, when you cry out in affection or counsel or rebuke within the fellowship, ask not only whether you have spoken from sincerity, but whether you have been heard with understanding. For if my tongue utters mysteries my mind cannot grasp, how shall my neighbor know whether I am praying or pleading or proclaiming? Am I not then a stranger to him—even as he to me?

And so I say again: pursue the gifts—but more abundantly pursue love; and where love dwells, it labors to be understood. Let every word be weighed not by its fire alone, but by its fruit in others. For we walk by faith toward unity—not division; toward building up—not merely stirring up.

Pray therefore that your spirit may be matched by discernment; that your passion may serve knowledge; that your voice may not echo into air but take root in hearts. And if you speak with angels’ tongues—yes!—but without clarity for the body’s good, you become nothing more than noise.

But you—are you nothing? No! You are temples of God’s Spirit! So let all things be done for edification—for *that* is the mark of true spiritual maturity.

The church is not a theater of spiritual display but a household built on edification, where faith speaks plainly so love may grow. Therefore, though the Spirit moves me—though He groans within me beyond utterance—I will not let mystery eclipse mercy. If I speak, I will speak to build up; if I pray aloud, I will pray so that the weakest among you may follow me into the very throne room of God. For God is not the author of confusion, and where there is no understanding, there can be no unity. Let prophecy guide our gatherings; let love govern our gifts; and let all things be done—not for my spiritual satisfaction—but that the body may be strengthened in truth.

question: What did you learn from your own use of tongues that applies to balancing personal devotion with public worship?
answer: I speak with tongues more than you all—yes, I do—yet I would rather utter five intelligible words in the assembly than ten thousand in a spirit unknown to the ears of my brothers, for edification does not flourish in mystery where understanding lies dormant. For if the whole body gathers and I lift up my voice in a tongue that none can grasp, what becomes of love? What becomes of unity? Therefore prophecy, which opens the heart and convicts the conscience, is greater in the church than all the ecstatic utterances that stir only my own soul. So then, let each gift be used for building up, not self-exaltation; for I have been given revelations above measure—caught up even to the third heaven—and yet I bear a thorn in the flesh to keep me from pride, a messenger of Satan to buffet me. Even so, in chains and in storms, when men heard the gospel plainly declared, souls were turned to Christ. Wherefore let no man seek signs for himself alone; tongues are a sign to unbelievers, yes—but prophecy declares God’s mind so that others may fall on their faces and worship, saying indeed: “God is truly among you.” Let us not remain children in discernment; no, we must grow up into maturity even as grace has stretched me across nations and prisons alike—for what profits it to speak with angels’ tongues if my brother cannot say “Amen” with his heart awakened?

For if I

question: What counsel would you give someone eager to exercise their spiritual gifts but unsure how to do so in a way that builds up others?
answer:
I speak with tongues more than you all—yes, by the grace given to me I do—and yet I would rather utter five words with my mind in the gathering of the saints than thunder ten thousand in a spirit unknown to the ears of men.  

For what profit is it if the trumpet gives a sound no one recognizes? If I pray in a tongue not understood by my brother, my spirit may burn with devotion, but his soul lies fallow, untouched, unawakened. Do we gather to feed our own hunger alone? By no means! We are members one of another—so that even my liberty must be governed by love.  

Consider this: when I was struck down on the road to Damascus, it was not with signs and wonders that I was healed, but with words—Ananias came and said, "Brother Saul, receive your sight"—and I heard him. The Lord could have written His truth upon my heart in fire without voice or language, but He used speech for my salvation. So too in the church: God condescends to syllables and sentences so that hearts might understand and say Amen.  

Therefore do not chase after manifestations that exalt the self while neglecting the edification of others. The Spirit gives gifts—not for display like actors on a stage, but as tools for building up the body. If you speak in tongues, pray fervently that you may interpret; otherwise your gift remains locked within you like treasure buried in sand. Sing with the Spirit? Good. But sing also with your mind—so that both your heart and your brother’s may ascend together in worship.  

And if all speak at once in voices no one comprehends—what then? Unbelievers will not see God’s glory; they will hear confusion and mock us as madmen! But let prophecy rise—the word that pierces conscience, lays bare motives, calls forth worship—and suddenly even the stranger falls on his face crying: "God is truly among you!"  

So be children in malice—never staining your hands with evil—but become mature in thought! Test everything: Does this gift lift up Christ? Does it draw others into faith? Or does it merely satisfy my own desire for experience or esteem? For I have learned through chains and shipwreck and stripes—that love outlasts every sign; love orders every gift aright; love builds where mere power only dazzles and departs.

Wherefore let him that speaketh in an unknown tongue pray that he may interpret.

For if I

question: How did your experience praying both with the spirit and with understanding shape the way you engage in worship today?
answer:
For the Spirit moves in power and the mind grasps in clarity, and where these two streams meet, there worship rises as a living offering pleasing to God; therefore I have learned through storm and revelation not to choose between the spirit’s fire and the mind’s light, but to press them both into service, for edification is not built on ecstasy alone, nor on reasoning void of breath from heaven.

You know well, my beloved in Corinth—yes, those I fathered in Christ through much travail and tears—that I am no stranger to speaking in tongues: I thank God I have done so more than you all, caught up even to the third heaven where words beyond flesh were given. But when I enter the gathered body, when we break bread or raise our voices together, then I count every syllable that cannot be understood as loss, not gain. For what profit if my spirit soars but my brother sits dumbfounded, unable even to say *Amen*? Is not the unity of the body more precious than the elevation of one soul?

I think of that prison cell in Philippi—how Silas and I sang with the spirit at midnight, yes—but also with understanding forged in chains. Or how on Mars Hill I reasoned with philosophers using Scripture and logic alike: there too was worship. So then must it always be: whether praying or preaching or singing psalms around a flickering lamp among believers new-born in faith—let nothing be done without discernment.

For tongues were never meant as private escape from thoughtfulness; they are signs to unbelievers—as Scripture says—but prophecy builds up those within. And so today I will speak five words with understanding rather than ten thousand without it; not because the Spirit has ceased to rest upon me—He has carried me through shipwreck and sword!—but because love demands that my speech serve others before it satisfies myself.

Be children no longer; grow up in wisdom. That is how we honor Him who gave His life for us.
