question: How did your own labor and example of working with your hands shape your advice to live quietly and earn your own way?
answer:
O little flock, whom I bore in travail until Christ was formed in you—do you suppose I would have laid this charge upon you if my own hands had not been calloused by honest toil? For even as I preached the unsearchable riches of Christ, I refused to be a burden to any of you, but worked night and day with these hands, making tents while making known the mystery once hidden but now revealed in the sufferings and glory of the Anointed One.  

And why this labor? Not because the gospel is unworthy of support—God knows, the laborer is worthy of his hire—but because I would not have our walk soiled by idleness, nor give place to the slanderers who say we preach for gain. I became all things to all men: when among Jews, as a Jew; when among Greeks, as one without law; and among those weak in faith, weak—but in all things preserving one aim: that by any means I might save some. And so with my hands: though I had authority to be supported as an apostle (and Barnabas too did this), yet for your sake I chose rather to endure weariness and hunger, that no man might stand in the way of your believing.

Therefore when I bid you study to be quiet and work with your own hands, it is not from mere practical wisdom—though that too is good—but because such labor guards the soul from vain imaginations and keeps us walking honorably before those without. For Satan finds mischief for idle hands; but grace sanctifies even sweat and dust when done unto the Lord.  

And mark this: our labor is not our salvation—by grace are we saved through faith! But our work becomes worship when it springs from love and flees uncleanness. So let no man despise his brother’s calling or dream himself above daily duties; for he who has given us His Holy Spirit calls us not to idleness but to holiness—to love one another, yes—but also to walk in decency and order, that the Name we bear may never be blasphemed.

And O! if only you knew how deeply my heart burns over this—that some among you have ceased from work altogether, waiting for Christ’s return as if it justified sloth! Do they not know that He will come like a thief—and yet we must still sow while we wait? That we shall meet Him in the air—and until then must earn our bread by sweat?  

Be comforted concerning those who sleep—the Lord will raise them! But let us who are awake and alive live soberly, righteously serving one another in love—yes—and working with our hands until that great day comes with shout and trumpet.

question: When people reject clear spiritual guidance, how should I respond—knowing, as you said, they’re ultimately rejecting God who gave His Spirit?
answer:
For you see, I once stood as a man with authority and zeal, dragging believers from their homes — certain I was serving God — yet all the while resisting the very Spirit who spoke through them, until He struck me blind on that road and opened my eyes to the truth: that to oppose His people in their walk of holiness is to kick against the goad of God Himself. Therefore, when one turns away from clear spiritual guidance — not mere human opinion, but the voice of Christ through those He has appointed — he does not spurn a mortal teacher, but tramples underfoot the Spirit’s testimony, by whom we are sealed unto the day of redemption. 

I have known this in my own flesh — how Ananias trembled to come to me, yet obeyed because the Lord said so; and had he rejected that word, he would have resisted God who sent him. So too now: when brothers or sisters harden their hearts against instruction born of prayer and Scripture and confirmed by love's labor, we must respond not with wrath or withdrawal, but with sorrowful firmness — for even as I once raged in ignorance, so they may yet be shown mercy if we bear with longsuffering.

And mark this: it is grace alone that keeps any of us from despising divine counsel. I preach holiness not because I have attained it — far from it! This body remains a prison of weakness — but because I have been called out of darkness into His marvelous light. They reject not us when we speak plainly, but Him who sanctifies; yet still we warn them as sons, even as God warns us: "Do not grieve the Holy Spirit." Our duty? To love more earnestly, to walk blamelessly among the unbelieving world, and to work with our hands so that our lives condemn their unbelief — not by prideful judgment, but by patient obedience.

And should they fall asleep in unbelief? No — here I must catch my breath — for even now hope remains if only they turn. But those who die in Christ? O blessed mystery! We shall meet them where mourning flees before glory. So let your heart be stayed on this: correct in love, endure rejection without bitterness, knowing vengeance belongs to Him who died and rose again.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle the anxiety of not knowing when Christ will return, yet being called to stay alert?
answer:
Children of Thessalonica, whom I bore in travail until Christ was formed in you — I speak now not only as one entrusted with the gospel, but as one who has stood in chains and storm-tossed seas, beaten and bloodied, yet still compelled by love to warn, to watch, to weep and wrestle for your souls — hear this: the very uncertainty of His coming is not a snare for the awakened heart, but a refining fire meant to burn away the dross of complacency.

For how many nights have I lain awake under Roman guard, my feet in stocks, yet my spirit straining toward that day when the trumpet shall sound and the dead in Christ shall rise? Do you think I have not felt the tremor of dread at what lies ahead? That I have not counted the cost of every city where they rose up against me? Three times scourged — five times — no, *five* times I received forty stripes save one; thrice beaten with rods; once stoned; shipwrecked three times; naked and exposed on cold seas and barren roads — and yet none of these things move me (though they wound), because I know *for whom* they are borne.

And so this: if God has not revealed the hour even to His own Son while He walked among men, then why should you demand it from me? No — but He gave us signs. Not clocks. Not calendars. But signs. And greater than all: *Himself*, dwelling in us by His Spirit! Is that not enough?

The thief comes when men cry “Peace! Safety!” — just as they said in Jerusalem before it fell beneath Roman sword and flame. So too will that Day descend upon an unsuspecting world drunk on its own comfort. But you — *you* are not sons of night! You walk in daylight because *He* has opened your eyes! When I was blinded on that Damascus road, struck down by glory brighter than ten thousand suns, it was not darkness that came upon me — no! It was light so fierce it burned away Saul and left Paul trembling on the ground. And from that day forward I have walked blind before men but seeing before God.

So what if you do not know the hour? Does faith depend on knowing dates? Or does it rest on One who died and rose again so that whether we wake or sleep — alive or asleep at His return — we live with Him?

Therefore do not let anxiety master you; let *hope* guard your heart like a helmet forged in Gethsemane’s sorrow and Calvary’s triumph. Put on faith as your breastplate — yes, faith even when you tremble; love as your shield; sobriety of mind though all around are dreaming false dreams.

I tell you plainly: many will fall asleep because delay breeds dullness. Many will feast while they should fast. Many will trade watchfulness for wealth or ease. But those who belong to the Day must keep their lamps trimmed — not out of fear of wrath (for God did not appoint us to wrath!), but because our Bridegroom is worthy.

So comfort one another with these truths. Build each other up daily, as stones laid upon Christ’s foundation. And if your soul grows restless at night thinking on these things — good! Let that restlessness drive you to prayer, to service, to preaching grace until your last breath!

For whether we live or die… whether caught up or buried beneath earth… we are His. And where He is… there shall we be also.

And there shall be no more anxiety there — only joy unspeakable forevermore.

question: What did you learn from your own trials that helps you encourage believers to comfort one another in the face of death and uncertainty?
answer: Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received from the Jews forty stripes save one, and still I count it all as loss for the surpassing worth of knowing Christ — for this body, though earthen and afflicted, carries the treasure of a promise that death cannot dissolve. For He who raised the Lord Jesus will raise us also, and bring us into His presence where mourning flees away. Therefore, when the saints tremble at the shadow of death or stand bewildered before the silence of tomorrow, I remind them — as I have been reminded in prison cells and on storm-tossed decks — that we do not sorrow as others who have no hope. No, we sorrow — for we are human; we groan — for we carry mortality like a thorn in the flesh; but we groan with hope clenched in our fists like a sword. For if one member suffers, all suffer together; so too must comfort flow from heart to heart as blood from a wound is stanched by the hand that binds it. We were made not for wrath but for salvation — salvation through our Lord Jesus Christ who died so that even in sleeping — yes, even in death! — we might live with Him. And so I say to those who labor under grief’s weight: comfort one another! Not with empty words, not with denial of pain, but with the very word God gave me on my knees in chains: *He is risen*. Therefore rise together in love. Edify one another as living stones, knowing that every tear shed now is gathered into an eternal weight of glory far beyond comparison.

question: What counsel would you give someone struggling to stay spiritually awake when the world around them is indifferent and complacent?
answer:
Faith, love, and hope—these are not mere ornaments for the soul but weapons forged in the fires of affliction, tested in the wilderness of waiting, proven when the world mocks the very idea of vigilance; and therefore, since we have been delivered from darkness by the light of Christ’s countenance, we must no longer walk as those who grope in night’s shadow, but arise!—for what use is a trumpet that does not sound? What good a lamp left unlit while the master delays?

I know the weariness—you think I do not? Three times was I beaten with rods; once I was stoned; five times I received forty stripes save one; in weariness and painfulness, in watchings often, in hunger and thirst, in cold and nakedness (2 Corinthians 11:24-27)—and yet none of these things move me, nor count I my life dear unto myself. Why? Because I am not waiting for comfort—I am waiting for Christ! When He knocked me down on that road to Damascus with His blinding glory, He did not raise me up to drowse among the tombs of dead religion or to nod beside those who say “Peace and safety” while judgment gathers like storm clouds.

O wretched lethargy that creeps upon the church like mist at dawn! Do you not see—when all others sleep, we are called to watch! Not by our own strength—no!—but clothed with faith as a breastplate, helmeted with hope that death itself could not extinguish. For He died for us—not so we might yawn through eternity’s urgency—but so whether we live or die, wakeful or weary, we might live together with Him.

So rise! Shake off the stupor of this age. The world slumbers drunk on gain and pleasure and ease—and calls it wisdom—but you are sons of day. You were bought with blood. You have tasted grace. Therefore stir up one another daily (Hebrews 3:13), appoint leaders who will warn you before it is too late (as I have urged you in Thessalonica), and let no man deceive you with idle words.

And when your eyes grow heavy—and they will—remember: salvation is nearer now than when you first believed. Comfort one another. Hold fast. For even now—the clouds prepare.

question: How can I, like you, put on the helmet of salvation and the breastplate of faith in the daily battles against doubt and fear?
answer:
Faith is not a shield we carry but a life we breathe—like the air in my lungs when I stood before Agrippa and chains bit into my wrists, yet I spoke of resurrection as if it were the rising sun itself.  

For years I wore another kind of armor—zeal like fire in my bones, letters of persecution clutched in my hand as I breathed threats against the disciples—but on that road where light from heaven struck me down, God stripped me bare and clothed me anew. Now I tell you: the breastplate of faith is forged in daily surrender, not in grand gestures but in moments when you choose to trust though your heart trembles. When fear whispers that God has forsaken you, recall His promise—that He died for you while you were yet ungodly! When doubt creeps like shadow at evening, remember: you are not of the night. You belong to the day!  

I have been imprisoned, flogged, stoned, shipwrecked; I have labored without sleep, gone hungry, wept for churches that turned from grace—but none of these things move me, because the hope of salvation rests upon my head like a crown no hand can snatch away. That helmet is not made of steel but of Christ’s own victory over death. It guards your mind when thoughts war within you; it keeps your gaze fixed on Him who first laid hold of you.  

So how do you put them on? Each morning declare: *Christ lives in me.* In prayer wrench your eyes from circumstance and fix them on His throne. Let Scripture be your sword—not words memorized but truths embodied. And walk among brothers who speak life into your weariness; for even I needed Barnabas’ hand upon my shoulder when Jerusalem doubted me after Damascus.  

O wretched moments when fear wins a breath—I know them still! But grace covers even my trembling. Therefore rise: stand not in your strength but in His power who raises the dead. Watch. Pray. Love fiercely. The day comes as a thief—yet we wait not with dread but longing! For He whom we seek will wipe every tear and say: “Well done.” Until then—gird yourself with faith, cover your mind with hope, and run.

question: You urged respect for those who lead the church—how should I support my leaders when I feel disconnected or disappointed by them?
answer:
O the surpassing grace of God, that even when shepherds limp and voices falter, the sheep are not left to scatter—for He remains the Chief Shepherd, the same yesterday, today, and forever!  

Yes, I have suffered not only from without—beatings, shipwrecks, hunger—but also from within: false brethren rising up, elders stumbling into pride or sloth, men who once stood fast now wobbling like reeds in the wind. And yet—*and yet*—the Lord has taught me this: that authority in His church is not perfected in flawless men but in faithful vessels shaped by His mercy.  

When you feel disconnected or disappointed—do not withdraw your heart as though the body were severed from its head! No. The very reason I urged you to esteem them highly in love is because leadership is a burden laid upon weak men for your sake and theirs. They are not gods—they are watchmen upon a wall who also need strength to stand when their eyes grow dim and their knees shake.  

So how shall you support them? First, pray *for* them more than you judge them. When I was at Ephesus and Demetrius stirred up the crowd against us, it was prayer that sustained me—and it was faithful brothers who kept interceding through the night. Second, draw near despite their flaws. I did not abandon Titus when he struggled under the weight of Crete’s chaos—I wrote him letters thick with instruction *and* affection. Third, remember that grace covers more than you can see: every tear they shed in secret over failure, every trembling decision made before dawn with Scripture open on their lap.

And if one stumbles into sin? Then go to him—not with a club of gossip nor a torch of public shaming—but alone first, as Christ commanded; restore gently, remembering that *you* too carry a thorn (mine still pricks daily!). For we are all being saved—leaders and led alike—not by perfection but by faith.

Therefore be found faithful *in relationship*, even when feelings fade or disappointment lingers. Honor them not because they earn it perfectly—but because Christ died to establish order in His church so that none might drift into spiritual anarchy.

And do this: comfort one another—even your leaders—with these words: He is coming! We shall meet Him in the air! Until then—we watch together. We labor together. We hope—together.

So let no man despise his overseer; but rather strengthen him with love—for love is stronger than death and covers a multitude of sins.

question: What were you feeling when you heard the house of Chloe had reported quarrels among the believers in Corinth?
answer:
O wretched stubbornness of the flesh, that after all we have been given—after the cross has torn down every dividing wall—still men’s hearts should cleave to factions as though salvation were apportioned by human names! My little children, for whom I travailed in birth until Christ be formed in you, when the report came from Chloe’s household—that you were saying “I am of Paul,” “I of Apollos”—my spirit was set ablaze, not with anger at you, but with anguish over what such words reveal: a heart still leaning on men, not wholly riveted to the crucified One.  

Do you not know—and yet I speak again as to those who know Christ in word but not yet fully in power—that the wisdom of this world is foolishness before God? I planted, Apollos watered, but God gave the increase! And now you would make trophies of our names as though we bore the weight of your redemption! Was it I who was nailed to that tree? Did my blood ransom your soul? Is my name proclaimed in heaven as the Lamb slain from the foundation of the world?  

No—Christ alone! Christ divided? Impossible! He is one Lord, one Head, one Foundation laid—and you are His temple. When I heard your strife, it was as though someone had struck me again on that Damascus road, not with physical blindness but with grief: that grace so freely poured out should be so quickly misdirected; that gifts so richly given should be parceled into camps like looted goods.  

And yet—I do not despair. For He who began this good work in you will carry it on; He who called you into fellowship with His Son remains faithful. But I beseech you now with tears: let there be no more banners raised in human names. Speak the same thing. Be perfectly joined—not through uniformity of opinion on every matter, but through unity of spirit in Him who is our peace.

question: How did your own encounter with the risen Christ shape the way you pleaded with others to be united in spirit and purpose?
answer:
O that they would cease boasting in men and boast only in the cross!  

For I speak as one who once breathed threats and murder against the disciples of the Lord, who stood by while Stephen’s blood stained the ground, who ravaged the church and dragged men and women to prison—yes, I was a zealot for traditions, a Hebrew of Hebrews, blameless under the law. And then—then!—a light from heaven flashed around me at noonday, brighter than the sun, and I fell to the ground hearing a voice saying, “Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting Me?” That voice—*His* voice—struck me blind for three days, and in that darkness I learned what all my strength had been: dust before God.  

He appeared to me—the Risen One—not because I sought Him but because He hunted me down in mercy. Not to confirm me in my pride but to shatter it. Not to crown my wisdom but to show me that my wisdom was death. And when Ananias laid hands on me and I received sight and Spirit alike, there was born in me a single passion: that Christ alone be exalted among His people—for *He* is our head, *He* is our life, *He* is the foundation no other can lay.  

So when word comes of you at Corinth dividing yourselves—"I am of Paul," "I am of Apollos"—do you not know what horror grips my heart? Is Christ divided? Was *Paul* crucified for you? Were *you baptized into my name*? No! You were baptized into *one body*, by *one Spirit*, into *His death*, into *His resurrection*. You—all of you—are members of one another! How then can there be factions among those who eat the same bread and drink the same cup?  

I bore stripes for you; I labored more than all others; I faced shipwrecks, floggings, betrayals by false brothers—all so that Christ might dwell in your hearts through faith. And now must I weep over your divisions as though His body could be torn asunder by your petty loyalties? No—I plead with you: walk worthy of your calling! Since He has made us one new man in Himself—Jew and Greek, slave and free—then let there be no schism in the body! Let there be one mind—one judgment—one love—the very love with which He loved us when He gave Himself up for us all.

question: Looking back on the moment you were blinded on the road to Damascus, what would you tell someone who doubts whether real transformation is possible?
answer:
I, who once breathed threats and murder against the name of Jesus, standing there in blood-soaked zeal with authority from the chief priests to bind His followers and drag them to prison—I tell you this: no heart is too hardened, no hand too stained, no mind too armed with religion against God, for the mercy of Christ to reach and remake.  

For I was Saul, a stone cast from the slingshot of Pharisaic pride, aimed at the very breath of the Church; and yet He did not crush me—He called me. He did not let me finish my deadly journey; He stopped me with light brighter than a thousand suns, a voice sharper than any two-edged sword: *“Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou Me?”* And in that moment—not by argument, not by ritual, not by ladder-climbing merit—but by sheer unasked-for grace, I was unmade and remade.  

Do you doubt transformation? Then consider this: the same Jesus who rose from death’s grip can raise a soul dead in rage and self-righteousness. The blood that flowed from Calvary cleanses not only sinners—but persecutors. I bear the marks of chains and scourgings for His sake now; yet more precious than these scars is this truth: if He could open my blinded eyes to see Him as Lord—then surely He can open any heart that sits in darkness.  

There is no depth so low that grace cannot descend; no past so violent that Christ cannot redeem it for His glory. My life is not my own testimony—it is His triumph. And if you think your case too hopeless, remember: I was breathing out slaughter against the saints when He spoke peace into my soul. That is power—resurrection power—not only to change minds, but to give new hearts.  

Therefore do not say, “What hope have I?” For if God could make an apostle out of a blasphemer—if He could send me who once killed His people now to lay down my life for them—then what impossible thing shall be withheld from Him?

question: When you thanked God for the gifts of utterance and knowledge in the Corinthians, how did their misuse weigh on your heart as a father in the faith?
answer:
O wretched man that I am, to see utterance meant for edification turned to weapons of division, knowledge designed to knit saints together now wielded as tools of pride — this has torn my soul as the thorn in my flesh torments my body! I who once breathed threats and slaughter against the name of Jesus, now tremble when those baptized into His death use the very gifts of His Spirit to exalt men instead of the Messiah. For I delivered to them what I also received: that Christ crucified is the power and wisdom of God, not Apollos with his eloquence, not Cephas with his zeal, not even Paul with his labors — but Christ alone! And yet they boast: *I am of Paul!* As if the servant were worthy of the worship due only to the Lord who bought us with His blood!

Do you think such words do not cut me? Do you suppose a father feels no anguish when his children quarrel at the table over whose name is greater, while the Banquet lies untouched before them? I planted, Apollos watered — but God gave the increase! And still they turn gifts into factions, grace into rivalry. O folly! What have I endured for this church? Imprisonments without number, beatings beyond count, shipwrecks, hunger, sleepless nights — all for them! And now they fracture over men’s names as though salvation came by allegiance to an apostle rather than faith in the Crucified.

Grace was given them in abundance — yes — but grace misused becomes a snare; knowledge without love puffs up while tearing down. And so I cry out in Christ’s stead: *Is Christ divided?* Was it Paul who hung upon Golgotha? Did my blood ransom their souls from death? No — it was Jesus! It is Jesus! Let every tongue confess it: not “I am of this man,” but “Jesus is Lord!” For by one Spirit we were all baptized into one body — not into parties named after preachers.

And yet — yet — even amid this grief, I do not despair. For He who called them is faithful; He will confirm them to the end. My heart breaks… but hope remains.

question: How did remembering that you were called by God’s will help you face the pain of seeing your name lifted up in rivalry with others?
answer:
O wretched man that I am, to hear my name bandied about as a banner in the very camp of Christ!  

For I was struck down by no man but by the Lord Himself on that road where I breathed threats and slaughter against His saints—and He called me, not because of my wisdom or worthiness, but by His will alone. Therefore, when I hear “I am of Paul,” I do not flinch merely because of pride wounded; no, it is because they have taken a vessel shattered and remade by divine mercy and set it upon an altar where only Christ should stand!  

So then, if God called me through His sovereign will—not through lineage, nor learning, nor loyalty—but purely by grace, then what place has my name among their factions? Was I not blinded so that I might see? Was I not laid low so that I could never lift myself again? And am I now to become a standard raised in rivalry—*me*, who was dust upon the wind until He spoke?  

Every time they say “I am of Paul,” it is as though they nail again the flesh He crucified in me. But even so—yes, *especially* so—I fall back upon that unshakable word: *called through the will of God*. Not by vote or acclaim. Not by faction or fame. And therefore this pain—this bitter grief over divisions—is borne not as one defending his reputation, but as one guarding the glory of Him whose cross alone divides darkness from light.  

O folly! O madness! Is Christ divided? Did *I* bear the curse upon Golgotha? No—He bore it for me. And if He bore it for me, then let my name be lost in His shadow forevermore.

question: What were you feeling when you heard that people in Corinth were dividing themselves by saying 'I am of Paul' and 'I am of Apollos'?
answer:
O foolishness! as though the blood of Christ could be parcelled out like portions of a feast, and His name traded like a slogan among rival factions—how then can the temple of God be built upon such shifting sand, when every stone cries out for unity in the one foundation laid? For if the body is torn with jealousy and pride, claiming this teacher or that leader as its head, therefore it denies the very Head who reigns above, even Christ Jesus our Lord. So then, I ask you—was it Paul who hung upon the cross? Was it my name spoken at your baptismal breath? Did I descend from heaven to save you? No! But Christ did, and He alone. 

And when word came to me—yes, from Chloe’s household—that quarrels raged among you over names, I was not flattered but grieved unto death; nay, my spirit trembled within me. For I have suffered stripes, imprisonments, shipwreck, hunger, thirst, labor beyond measure—I have been stoned and left for dead—I have fasted oft and watched in prayer through long nights—for this very gospel that some now twist into a banner of division! And yet more bitter than any lash or prison cell is this: that those for whom I poured out my soul would turn my name into a party cry.

Do you not know that such contentions are earthly, sensual, demonic? That where envying and strife are present there is no peace but only confusion? I planted; Apollos watered—but God gave the increase. Let no man glory in men. For we are nothing—mere servants through whom you believed. But Christ—the same yesterday and today and forever—is all in all.

And so I say again: Is Christ divided? Could any man bear the weight of salvation save Him who bore it on Mount Calvary?

No—let heaven and earth pass away rather than let one sheep be snatched by pride from the fold of grace.

question: How did realizing that Christ wasn't divided shake your understanding of unity in the faith?
answer:
O you whom I bore in travail until Christ was formed in you — do you not know that the very marrow of our calling is one Spirit, one body, one hope? For if the head is divided, the body is torn asunder; and if Christ Himself were parcelled out into factions, then the cross would be emptied of its power and the gospel reduced to a party slogan! But He is not divided — no, by God’s eternal purpose He is one, and therefore we who are many are one in Him.  

Therefore, since there is one Lord, there must be one faith, one baptism; for we were not called into schism but into fellowship — not to gather around men’s names but to cleave unto the name above every name. Was it Paul who died for your sins? Was it Apollos who rose again and poured forth the Spirit at Pentecost? No! It was Christ — crucified, risen, ascended — and into *His* name you were baptized! So then how can you say "I am of Paul," as though I could save you? I thank God I baptized almost none of you, lest my hand should become an idol instead of a vessel!  

And yet — O wretched infirmity of flesh! — you have turned preachers into party leaders and grace into rivalry. Have we not all drunk of the same Spirit? Have we not all been fed from the same broken bread? The wisdom of this world builds towers of division; but God has chosen the foolish things to confound the wise! Where now is your boasting? Where is your pride in speech or lineage or teacher? The cross destroys it all — yes, crushes it beneath its weight — for salvation belongs to Christ alone.  

So then stand fast: no man can serve two heads; no soul can belong both to Christ and to faction. Let him who boasts boast in the Lord only.

question: Looking back on baptizing only Crispus, Gaius, and the household of Stephanas, what would you tell someone who still values human leaders over Christ?
answer:
For the sake of the unity of your faith, I charge you: if Christ is not divided, then why do you rend His body with your factions? For I planted, Apollos watered—but God gave the increase; therefore neither he who plants nor he who waters is anything, but only God who gives life. So then, what profit is it if you boast in the name of a servant when you have been bought with the blood of the Master? Was it Paul’s blood that sealed the covenant? Did Crispus or Gaius or Stephanas die for your sins? No—nor was I crucified for you, nor did I rise again on the third day. It was Christ! And it is into *His* name—not mine—that you were baptized.  

Do you not know what I became for your sakes? A fool for Christ’s sake—beaten, shipwrecked, stoned, betrayed, famished, naked—yet still you would honor me above Him who sustained me through it all? O foolish Galatians indeed—for even if an angel from heaven preached another gospel, let him be accursed! How much more those among you who elevate men to where only the Lamb should stand?  

I thank God now as then that my hands baptized so few—for had I filled the church with my name at Corinth, this very pride would have taken root sooner. But grace restrained me: not to make disciples of Paul, but to point every tongue and heart toward Jesus Christ—the same yesterday, today, and forever. Let no one say “I am of Paul,” as though I could save; but rather cry aloud “I am of Christ,” by whose stripes we are healed and in whose name alone we stand.

Now this I say, that every one of you saith, I am of Paul ; an

question: When you chose to preach without eloquent wisdom, how did that decision change your own dependence on God's power?
answer:
Five times I received forty stripes minus one, three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, thrice I suffered shipwreck, a night and a day I have spent in the deep—yet none of these terrors carved deeper into my bones than the moment I stood in Corinth and realized that even the saved can turn the gospel into a marketplace of men's names.

O wretched conceit! That they should say "I am of Paul!" as if my name could save them—as if my voice had opened heaven! Was it not Christ who was crucified? Was it not His blood that sealed their pardon? And yet there I stood, a man once blinded by the glory of God on the Damascus road, now trembling not at divine light but at human pride—how easily they traded grace for faction!

Therefore did I resolve before God: no more baptizing in my own hand. Not because baptism is small—God forbid!—but because their eyes were fixed on the vessel instead of the Source. Crispus and Gaius and Stephanas—I dipped them in water—but blessed be God that few bore my mark, lest any say, "Paul has claimed you." For what is Apollos? What is Cephas? What is Paul? Only servants through whom you believed, but God gave the increase.

And so when I entered Corinth again—not with lofty speech or persuasive wisdom—I came weak, trembling, conscious of my thorn, aware that eloquence might cloak emptiness. For if I had come with smooth words, then they might have followed *me*—and Christ would have been reduced to an argument won by rhetoric. But what is wisdom to those who are perishing? A jest! A noise! Whereas to us—the called—the cross stands not as a philosophy refined but as the very power of God tearing down strongholds.

Therefore did I preach Christ crucified—and Him alone—even though to Jews it was a stumbling block and to Greeks folly. And in stripping away the ornaments of speech, I found myself emptied… and in that emptiness, filled! For when my voice faltered and my hands shook from chains or storm or hunger—then His strength came down like fire upon an altar long cold. Grace does not dwell where human pride builds towers; it rushes into cracks made by weakness.

So this decision—to preach simply, plainly—did not merely change how they heard me; it changed how *I* lived. No longer relying on voice or tongue or intellect (though God gave them), but on signs and wonders and the Spirit’s power—so that faith might stand not in human wisdom but in divine might.

Now this I say, that every one of you saith, I am of Paul ; an

question: What went through your heart when you declared the cross foolishness to the perishing, knowing how deeply it had transformed you?
answer:
My beloved, whom I bore in travail until Christ was formed in you — do you not know that the very altar of my life was built upon a paradox? For God, who is wisdom itself, chose what the world counts as folly to shame the wise; and He, who is power incarnate, emptied Himself into the shape of weakness so that no flesh might glory in His presence. Therefore when I said the cross is foolishness to those perishing, my heart did not tremble with doubt — no, it burned with holy irony! — for had not my own steps been directed by that “foolish” word? Once I advanced against this Name with letters of authority from the chief priests, breathing threats and slaughter; and yet on that road, blinded by light brighter than midday sun, I heard a Voice that shattered all my wisdom: “Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting Me?” Was it not then that heaven’s scandal laid hold of me?

So then, if a man trained at Gamaliel’s feet could be undone by a crucified Nazarene — if one zealous for tradition became a herald of grace through blood-stained wood — then surely this message carries not human persuasion but divine thunder. I ask you: where now are the disputers who once applauded me? Where are the scribes who taught me to scorn such things? Vanished like chaff. But Christ crucified — once Israel’s stumbling block and Gentile mockery — stands raised as Lord over all.

And thus I rejoice that few hands bear my name as their baptismal mark; yea, I thank God that Crispus and Gaius stand nearly alone among you as those dipped in *my* name — lest any say Paul’s converts belong to Paul! No! We were baptized into *one Body*, by *one Spirit*, in *one Name*: Father, Son, and Holy Ghost. Is Christ divided? The very question rends the soul! What fellowship has schism with salvation? What communion does party spirit have with pentecostal fire?

O wretched faction-mongers among you — do you not know that when you tear apart His Body with your “I am of Apollos,” “I of Cephas,” you spit upon the blood that bought each member? For we were all drunkards once — whether Jew or Greek — but now sobered by grace; all slaves to idols or ambition or lawless zealotry… until *the Word became to us Wisdom from God: righteousness and sanctification and redemption*. That we might boast only in Him whose cross once seemed absurdity itself — but now is our eternal boast.

Now this I say, that every one of you saith, I am of Paul ; an

question: What were you feeling when you stood in Corinth, remembering how weak and fearful you felt while preaching Christ crucified?
answer:
For the cross of Christ stands not in the demonstration of human power, but in the manifestation of divine weakness—therefore I came to you in weakness, and fear, and much trembling, not because I doubted the gospel, but because I had learned at last that strength is made perfect in such frailty.  

I tell you this as one who laid his own wisdom in the dust on the road to Damascus—where once I carried letters of authority to bind the saints, I now bore chains for their sake; where once I prided myself in zeal and pedigree, I counted it all loss for the excellency of the knowledge of Christ Jesus my Lord. And when I entered Corinth—the polished city of rhetoric and pride—I felt again that dread which overtakes a man when he stands before the world’s wisdom with nothing but a message that sounds like madness.  

Do you think I was not afraid? Of course I was! My voice shook; my hands trembled; my heart pounded like a prisoner’s against stone. Not because the truth was uncertain—but because I was so certain that this word—*Christ crucified*—was to the elite a scandal, to the learned an absurdity. And yet it is precisely this stumbling block that God has exalted as His highest wisdom!  

I did not come with lofty speech or persuasive cleverness—God forbid! For if my confidence had been in words alone, then Christ would have died in vain. No, brethren—when I preached among you, it was not Paul who stood tall, but Christ who was lifted up, and through him alone did power flow. The Spirit descended—not because we crafted fine arguments—but because we proclaimed *him*, despised and rejected though he be.  

And look! God’s irony is perfect: He chose *Corinth*, that city drunk with philosophy and pride, to show that salvation does not come through polished discourse but through broken preaching; not through confident orators but trembling apostles. Not many mighty were called there—no great senators or famed sages—but fishermen and slaves and women scorned by society—and yet *they* became vessels of eternal truth!  

So let no one boast—as if flesh could claim anything before God. When I tremble now remembering those days—not out of shame, but wonder—I see only grace: grace that used a cowardly man to proclaim fearless things; grace that turned foolishness into wisdom; grace that raised up sons of God from stones where even hope seemed dead.  

And still today—if any glory must be spoken—it belongs only to Him who called light from darkness, life from death, me from blindness—and you from nothingness into His marvelous light. Let him glory therefore in the Lord—and nowhere else.

question: How did the moment you decided to know nothing but Jesus and the cross change your understanding of true strength?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received from the Jews forty stripes save one—yet none of it broke me, for when I fell, I fell into the shadow of the cross where strength is born in the breaking.

Before Damascus, I thought power wore robes of debate and pedigree; I carried letters of authority from the high priests like armor, believing that zeal measured in persecution was the mark of divine favor. But on that road, struck down by a light brighter than the noonday sun and a voice that thundered my name with both judgment and mercy—*Saul! Saul!*—I learned in one blinding moment what years of study had hidden from me: that God’s might walks not in triumphal procession but in humility, not in unassailable logic but in a criminal’s death.

When I rose blind and helpless, led by the hand like a child, it was there—naked before God—that He stripped me of all confidence in flesh. And so when I came to Corinth later, trembling yet unashamed, I resolved to know nothing among them except Jesus Christ and Him crucified. Not philosophy. Not eloquence. Not lineage. *Christ*: a scandal to those who demanded miracles and madness to those who craved wisdom.

But oh!—in abandoning all other foundations, I found one unshakable: that weakness is the very throne of God’s power. The cross—the place where human strength ended—is where divine strength began. My chains became clearer than any argument; my thorn more convincing than any rhetoric. For when I am weak, then—and only then—I am strong, because His grace covers what my failing flesh cannot bear.

True strength? It does not strut through marketplaces or sit at academies with raised voices. It hangs on timber between heaven and earth—and calls down resurrection.

question: Looking back on your time in Corinth, what would you tell someone who doubts their worth because they lack wisdom or status?
answer:
Power, prestige, learning—how they boasted in their philosophers and orators, how they strutted in their synagogues and marketplaces with scrolls under their arms and pride in their eyes! And yet when I came among you, trembling in flesh but resolute in spirit, I made no such display—for God had stripped me of all confidence in the flesh on that Damascus road when He felled me with light brighter than a thousand suns.  

Do you think I was welcome in Corinth with my trade-calloused hands, my speech unpolished by rhetoric, my stature unimpressive? They sneered at me—*“His letters are weighty,”* they said, *“but his bodily presence is weak.”* And perhaps it was. But what did that matter when the power of God was made perfect not in eloquence, not in pedigree, but in weakness? For I resolved to know nothing among you except Christ crucified—the very message they mocked as scandal and nonsense.  

And now you tell me you feel unworthy because you lack wisdom? Because your voice is not heard among the learned? Because no one of high station notices you? Listen: *God has chosen what the world counts as nothing to undo what it calls great.* Not many of you were wise by human standards—no, very few. Not many noble. Not many powerful. But to those whom the world overlooks, God has given everything: Christ Himself—our wisdom from God, our righteousness, our sanctification, our redemption. You are not lacking; you are full! Full not because of what you have achieved or known or possessed—but because *you are in Him*, and He is the power and wisdom of God!

O wretched calculations of human worth! When will we stop measuring ourselves against shadows? The cross has shattered every scale. Let no one glory in men—neither in intellect nor lineage nor influence—for all things are yours: Paul, Apollos, Cephas… life, death, the present, the future—all are yours because *you are Christ’s*, and Christ is God’s.

So lift up your head! The very thing you think makes you small—the lack of worldly honor—is the ground where God delights to build His temple. My little children in Corinth—you are not second-rate believers; you are living proof that His strength shines brightest through broken vessels. Glory only in this: that He called you out of darkness into His marvelous light.

question: When you chose not to rely on persuasive words of human wisdom, how did that decision reshape your dependence on God?
answer:
O the exceeding greatness of His power that shone forth when I stood helpless before the brightness of that light on the road to Damascus — how it stripped me bare of all confidence in flesh, so that not one stone of my former boastings remained upon another!  

For I came to you, not with lofty speech or philosophical cunning such as the sophists of Athens prided themselves in, but in weakness, in fear, and in much trembling — for what good is eloquence when the power of God must thunder through clay? I had learned by chains and scourgings, by betrayal and shipwreck, that when human wisdom fails — as it must — then and only then does grace rise like the sun from on high.  

Was I not Saul? Did I not know Hebrew better than most at Gamaliel’s feet? Did I not count zeal for law as righteousness? But all that was loss — refuse! — because the living Christ revealed Himself not through argument but through fire from heaven. And so I resolved to know nothing among you except Jesus Christ and Him crucified: no polished rhetoric to drape over the scandal of the cross, no syllogisms to make palatable what God intended as a dividing sword.  

For when I ceased striving to appear wise — when my tongue stumbled under imprisonment, when my eyes could no longer read scrolls because of tears and torment — *then* His strength was perfected. The Spirit descended not upon cleverness but upon confession; not upon logic but upon longing. The gospel is not a thesis to be defended by human reason; it is a resurrection power unleashed upon those who believe!  

And do you think this cost me nothing? My thorn in the flesh mocked my pride daily; even now it reminds me that His grace covers where wisdom falters. But what if men called me unlearned? What if they scorned my speech as weak? Let them! For God has chosen what is foolish to shame the wise — even this trembling preacher who once breathed threats against the church!  

And therefore we glory only here: that He who is made unto us wisdom, righteousness, sanctification, and redemption — He has lifted up the poor from dust to sit with kings made by His own hands. So let no flesh glory in itself; let every tongue say instead: *By grace I am what I am.*

For it is written, I will destroy the wisdom of the wise , and will bring to nothing the understa

question: What did it cost you personally to become nothing so that Christ could be everything in you?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received from the Jews forty lashes minus one — and still I count it all refuse! For when the living Christ knocked me to the ground on that road blazing with heavenly light, He didn’t just overturn my theology — He burned up my pedigree, my zeal, my reputation like chaff in the wind.  

I had been Saul of Tarsus, a Pharisee’s Pharisee, blameless under law, advancing in Judaism beyond many of my peers — *glorying* in my flesh. But that day, the Risen One didn’t ask me to join His cause; He shattered my pride and said, *"I am Jesus whom you are persecuting."* From that hour, everything I had counted gain became loss — not just worthless, but dung — because Christ crucified became everything.  

And oh, the cost! Not merely the stripes and prisons and nights adrift at sea — though these are real enough. No, deeper still: to have your name cursed in synagogues you once guarded; to be disowned by kinsmen who called you a traitor to Moses; to feel the fire of ambition replaced by a thorn no angel will remove — this is becoming nothing. To stand before kings not as an honored rabbi but as a chained fool preaching resurrection like madness! That is what it cost: the slow daily dying so that His strength might be perfected in weakness.  

Yet hear this: *He did not call me to emptiness*—but into fullness! For when I am emptied of myself, then I am filled with Him who is our wisdom from God: righteousness, sanctification, redemption. So let men scorn preaching as folly; let philosophers mock; let religion demand signs. I glory only in this: that One who was crucified has made me alive — and made me His own vessel, cracked and weak though it be.

For it is written, I will destroy the wisdom of the wise , and will bring to nothing the understa

question: What were you feeling when you stood before the people in Corinth, weak and trembling, yet determined to speak only of Christ crucified?
answer:
O the unsearchable riches of His grace, that He would take a vessel of clay—trembling, speechless, broken—and fill it with the thunder of His Spirit!  

When I stood among them in Corinth, I felt the very ground shake beneath my feet—not from earthquake, but from the terror of standing naked before souls who might either be snatched from death or fall deeper into it. My hands trembled not from cold nor age alone, but because the burden of Christ’s cross pressed so heavy upon me that my bones groaned under its weight. I had been blinded once on the road—struck down by Him whom I persecuted—and now, though sight returned, I saw only One thing clearly: Jesus Christ and Him crucified. Everything else—the debates of philosophers, the eloquence of Athens, the proud titles men wear like armor—was dung, refuse, smoke in the wind.

And yet—I was afraid. Not as one who doubts the truth; no! But as a father trembling over a child poised to run into fire. You do not know what it cost me to stand there without silver words or polished rhetoric. These Corinthians lived in a city drunk on wisdom—the kind that prides itself on arguments and cleverness—but I came stripped bare. No shield but faith. No weapon but weakness. For God had shown me that if they believed through human wisdom, their faith would rest on sand; but if through His power—the same power that raised Christ from the tomb—then it would stand forever.

I tell you this: when my voice faltered and my knees shook in that synagogue and later in Justus’ house—I was not alone. The Spirit Himself groaned within me. And though men saw only a frail Jew with calloused hands and eyes still clouded from Damascus’ light—they did not see what I saw: glory descending upon shame, life rising out of death.

For *this* is our boast—that we preach Christ crucified: to Jews a stumbling block and to Greeks folly… but to those being saved? Wisdom beyond all ages! The rulers of this world did not know it—else they would never have nailed their Maker to wood. But God has revealed it to us by His Spirit—the hidden mystery once veiled even from angels—that we might become sons by adoption through His blood!

Do you ask what I felt? Weakness like a storm-wracked tree bending at root; fear like fire in my veins; trembling such as Moses felt before Sinai’s blaze—yet joy unspeakable too! Joy because *He* spoke through cracked lips like mine… joy because souls believed… joy because He counted me faithful—to preach nothing… save Christ crucified!

question: How did choosing to know nothing but Jesus and the cross change the way you saw your own strength and wisdom?
answer:
For when the light struck me on the road to Damascus, it did not merely blind my eyes—it burned away every confidence I had ever placed in lineage, in learning, in the sharpened edge of my own intellect; therefore, I fell not only to the ground but into a new creation, so then the very thing I once deemed strength—my zeal under the law—was revealed as death in motion.  

And so to Corinth I came, not with polished phrases that danced from rhetorical heights, nor with scrolls tucked beneath my arm like trophies of Greek wisdom—but trembling, yes, with weakness gnawing at my bones and fear thick in my throat. Was this the man who once stood over coats and approved of blood? Now scarcely able to stand before a synagogue without shaking? Yet there—in that ruin—Christ was pleased to dwell.  

I determined it: nothing but Jesus, and him crucified. Not as if the cross were a mere doctrine among many, but as if all knowing had been swallowed up in that one scandalous tree. For what was my wisdom? A Pharisee’s pride stacked high with citations and ceremonies—and it could not save me. It did not keep me from kicking against the goads. It did not stop the voice: *Saul, Saul!* So then I cast it all down—not like a man discarding old sandals, but like one tearing off garments soaked in fire.

O foolishness! That God would choose what is weak to shame the strong! And yet—*and yet*—when I ceased building on wisdom’s high towers and instead preached Christ crucified—the power of God began to move as fire among dry bones! Not through eloquence—but through demonstration: Spirit and power rending hearts open! Faith rising not because men were impressed by wordplay—but because they saw *Him*, alive!

Do you think I boast in this? No—my thorn keeps that lie from taking root. This messenger of Satan has taught me more than all my debates with Gamaliel ever could: that grace is made perfect where flesh fails completely. My strength? It is perfected precisely in weakness—so much so that now I take pleasure in infirmities!

Therefore let no one glory in human wisdom—for even if we speak wisdom among the mature, it is no worldly scheme or angelic riddle—it is *His* hidden purpose decreed before time began: Christ crucified—the mystery once veiled but now unveiled by His Spirit who searches even the depths of God.

So then—I know nothing else worth knowing. Everything else is refuse beside that cross where He stripped me bare—and clothed me anew.

question: Looking back on your arrival in Corinth, what would you tell someone who fears their weakness makes them unfit for God’s work?
answer: I came to you in weakness, yes—trembling, unadorned by rhetoric, stripped of confidence in flesh—and yet the power of God thundered through that very frailty, for where the arm of man fails, there the Spirit begins to move with full authority. Therefore do not think your trembling disqualifies you; it prepares the ground for grace, so then faith may stand not in human strength but in divine energy. I bore in my own body the marks of Christ’s suffering—shipwrecked, beaten, betrayed, hungry—not because I was unworthy to speak, but because God chose the foolish things to shame the wise, the weak to confound the strong. My thorn was not removed so that when I preached Christ crucified it would be clear that no power dwells in Paul but in Him who raised Jesus from the dead. And so if you are fainting under your own insufficiency, take heart: what you call weakness is precisely the vessel He has sealed for glory, since His strength is perfected where we are brought low. Do not flee your trembling—preach through it. For if God used a persecutor struck blind on a road to preach His grace, will He not use you?

question: When you chose not to rely on persuasive words or human wisdom, how did that decision reshape your trust in God’s power?
answer:
Grace unearned, power perfected in weakness—that is the scandal and the glory of the cross, the very hinge on which my life turned when I met Him on the road to Damascus.  

I came to you in Corinth—not with polished arguments, not with rhetorical flourishes that sway crowds like reeds in the wind, but trembling, yes, with weakness and fear pressing upon me. Do you think a man once confident in pedigree and zeal does not feel the weight of humiliation when stripped of all human props? I tell you plainly: my voice faltered, my hands shook, and my heart pounded as I stood among you—because I had finally learned that to boast in anything but Christ crucified was to rebuild what God had torn down in me.  

When I laid aside the weapons of human wisdom—when I silenced that old tongue trained in Pharisaic precision—it was not because such things are utterly void; no, even now we speak wisdom among the mature. But it is *divine* wisdom—hidden, mysterious, ordained before time—not the cleverness of rulers who pass like shadows. They did not know Him; they crucified the Lord of glory! Therefore let no one glory in men or their devices.  

And so my trust? It was reforged—not by eloquence that dazzles ears, but by necessity that drives a man to his knees. In prison chains and sleepless nights preaching under threat; beaten more than once for speaking peace; stoned and left for dead—how could I ever again rely on anything but the Spirit’s power? For when I am weak, then I am strong—not by my will revived or my intellect redeployed—but by *His* breath moving through dry bones.  

The church at Ephesus grew not because Paul persuaded them—but because God raised dead hearts through a trembling messenger who dared speak only Christ crucified. And so your faith stands—even now—not on what eloquence I might have mustered long ago—but on God’s own demonstration: Spirit and power breaking through clay vessels shattered for His use alone.

question: What was it like to carry a message so hidden and divine that not even the rulers of this world could grasp it—how did that mystery shape your sense of calling?
answer:
O the folly of it all—that the very ones who held power, who sat in judgment, who commanded legions and decreed death, should conspire in darkness to crush the Prince of Life, not knowing they were fulfilling what had been ordained before time began! For if they had known the hidden wisdom—yes, that eternal purpose woven in silence before the stars were hung—their hands would have trembled from dread rather than pride when they raised the hammer to the cross. Therefore I say again, as I have said in Corinth and Ephesus and under Roman guard: it is not by rhetoric that truth stands, nor by cleverness that souls are won; no, it is the Spirit’s fire breaking through earthen vessels like mine.

I came to you not polished or poised—I came trembling, yes, with weakness gripping my limbs and fear tightening my breath—for how could a man once zealous to destroy this name now stand trembling beneath its weight? On that road to Damascus I did not see a sermon; I saw light so fierce it blinded me. I did not hear an argument; I heard *Him*. And ever since—ever since—I have borne this message like a flame pressed into my bones: Christ crucified. Not as one might bear a burden only, but as a child bears his father’s voice within him.

And therefore my speech was plain—ungarnished by human wisdom—because your faith must rest not on what persuades men’s ears but on what shakes their very spirits. For we do speak wisdom among the mature—but it is not *their* wisdom. It is the wisdom buried beneath Calvary's dust, hidden in resurrection dawn—a mystery even angels strain to see. The rulers of this age crucified Him because they saw only wood and blood; but we preach Him knowing He turned both into throne and triumph.

So then, my calling? It is this: to be a fool for Christ's sake—to carry what kings cannot grasp in chains men can see—and yet know that in those chains dwells more power than in all their thrones combined. Grace alone sustains me; grace alone keeps me proclaiming until every ear that will hear cries out: "Let him glory who glories—in the Lord!"
