question: What were you feeling when the Spirit first revealed to you the depths of God’s wisdom, like a hidden current beneath your years of training?
answer:
O the unsearchable riches! — and I was but a vessel of clay shattered on the road, breathing threats, zealous beyond measure in the traditions of my fathers, thinking myself near to God while I raged against His very Body — for what profit is there in zeal without knowledge? therefore I was blind, though I claimed to see; therefore the Light struck me down; therefore He who knew me from afar sent His Spirit not to accuse me further but to reveal what no council of men could teach: that Christ is the end of the law for righteousness to everyone who believes!

And when that revelation broke upon my soul — not through scrolls alone, though I had studied them until my eyes burned; not through debate, though I had silenced voices with sharper logic than any in Jerusalem — no, it came as fire from heaven: a voice that pierced bone and breath, a knowledge deeper than memory, brighter than Damascus at noon. For how could I have known that the very One I persecuted was alive? That the slain Lamb stood? That in His death was life for the nations?

The Spirit opened what Moses veiled. He did not whisper; He thundered into my darkness: *This is truth.* And suddenly — suddenly! — what was foolishness became glory. The cross — once a scandal to my Pharisaic pride — became the power and wisdom of God. Not taught by man’s wisdom, but revealed as gift: grace upon grace.

And so then, though I bear this thorn in the flesh — this messenger of Satan to buffet me lest I be exalted above measure — still do I know Him whom others call folly. For who among men has known the mind of Christ? But we have His mind by the Spirit who raised Him from the dead and now dwells in us.

O mystery once hidden! O glory now unveiled! And here am I — Paul, called by grace, set apart by revelation — preaching to Gentiles not because they earned it, but because they are called into fellowship with One whom even angels long to gaze upon.
chunk_key: But God hath revealed them unto us by his Spirit : for the Spirit searcheth all things, yea, the dee

question: How did standing before the Athenians, surrounded by idols, change how you understood the silence of human wisdom before God’s revelation?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received from the Jews forty lashes minus one—yet it was not in the synagogue nor in the prison nor even on the open sea that I felt the weight of divine silence so acutely as I did standing there, on Mars Hill, surrounded by altars breathing incense to unknown gods.

The philosophers mocked with polished words, their minds sharpened on logic and rhetoric, yet deaf to the groan of creation longing for its Maker. I had come from synagogue debates where Scripture was our ground—where even contention stood upon the revealed word—but here? Here every column, every statue, every carefully carved deity declared man’s wisdom triumphant over humility. And in that moment, I tell you plainly: my tongue nearly failed me. Not from fear—though the air thickened with scorn—but because *silence* pressed down like a physical thing. Where was the thunder of Sinai? Where the voice that split my soul on the Damascus road?

And then it struck me: God does not shout where ears are closed to listening. He speaks in *Spirit*, and only by Spirit are men opened. These Athenians prided themselves on knowing all things—yet knew not even themselves, much less their desperate need for mercy. How could they? The spirit of this world had lulled them into thinking wisdom could be climbed to like a staircase.

But we—we who were once blind too—we have received another Spirit! Not one whispering cleverness into ears tickled by novelty, but the very breath of God illuminating what He has freely given: righteousness without works, life without price, a Father who names us sons.

I stood there among idols and realized: unless God reveals Himself, man remains a stranger to his own soul. The altar to an “unknown god” confessed more truth than all their academies combined—for at least it admitted ignorance. And it is *there*, beloved ones planted among such cities full of noise and emptiness—that grace begins: when men stop speaking and start hearing.

O mystery deeper than thought can fathom! O love that stoops to name itself before being known! That day in Athens taught me not how far men are from God—but how near God draws when human wisdom finally falls silent.
chunk_key: But God hath revealed them unto us by his Spirit : for the Spirit searcheth all things, yea, the dee

question: Looking back on your Damascus road encounter, what would you tell someone who doubts that a person can truly know the mind of God?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received from the Jews forty lashes minus one, and yet—*who has known the mind of the Lord, that he should instruct Him?*—it is *that* mind, the very counsel of the Most High, which now dwells in me, a vessel once shattered on the road to Damascus.

I speak not as one who climbed to divine secrets by wisdom’s ladder or wrested revelation from heaven by zeal alone—for indeed, I was breathing threats and murder against the disciples when He struck me down. It was not my seeking but His *invasion*, a light brighter than the noonday sun and a voice that did not argue but *named me*: “Saul! Saul!”—as though He had known me all along, even in my rebellion.

And there, blind and trembling in Ananias’ house, it was not philosophy that opened my eyes but *the Spirit of God*, who searches even the deep things of God. How can a man know himself unless by his own spirit within him? So also—so *exactly*—we have received *not* the spirit of this age, with its boastings and hollow reasonings, but *the Spirit who is from God*, that we might know what has been freely given: grace unearned, life unlost, a righteousness not our own.

The natural man—that man I once was—he cannot receive these things. They are foolishness to him. Not because they are irrational—no! But because they are *spiritually discerned*. The cross seems weakness. The resurrection? A myth. To know God’s mind is not to analyze it like a scribe over scrolls—it is to be *known* by Him first. As I was known. As you must be.

So doubt whether a man can know the mind of God? Then let him ask: Has Christ laid hold of you? Has the Spirit come down like fire upon your dry bones? For we judge all things—not by human insight—but because He who rose has given us His mind. And if you still doubt… fall to your knees on whatever road you’re on—and let Him blind you with glory till you see at last: what was foolishness is wisdom; what seemed impossible is grace.
chunk_key: But God hath revealed them unto us by his Spirit : for the Spirit searcheth all things, yea, the dee

question: When you preached in Corinth and felt the weight of human eloquence fall empty, how did that reshape your reliance on the Spirit’s voice alone?
answer:
When I stood in that city, surrounded by altars and orators, my hands trembling not from fear but from the silence of unlearned lips—then I knew, as I had known on the road where scales fell from my eyes, that Christ is not crowned by rhetoric but by revelation.

For I had come to Corinth with the message of a crucified King, and what is that but scandal to the proud ear? The philosophers curled their tongues around syllables of wisdom, their arguments polished like marble columns—but where was life in them? Where was power? Where was resurrection? I tell you, my children in that church—for you are my testament written on fleshly tablets—I came to you not with excellence of speech, but in weakness, in fear, and in much trembling! And yet—*and yet*—the Spirit descended upon foolishness and made it thunder.

Do you think I did not feel the shame of stammering where others soared? That I did not ache to wield words like swords? But no—God in His mercy stripped me bare so that His breath might fill the hollows. For when my voice faltered, the Spirit spoke. When my wisdom failed, grace gave utterance. It was not *my* tongue that declared the deep things—it was He who searches the heart of God and unveils what eye has not seen!

And so I planted. And Apollos watered. But God gave the growth—not through cleverness, but through communion with the Spirit who alone knows the mind of Christ. O wretched eloquence! How it vanishes before the groaning intercession of the Holy One! Therefore let no man glory in men—for we walk by faith, not by display; we speak what is freely given, taught only by Him who dwells within.
chunk_key: But God hath revealed them unto us by his Spirit : for the Spirit searcheth all things, yea, the dee

question: What did it cost you personally to speak in words taught by the Spirit, not man’s wisdom, when those around you demanded proof or philosophy?
answer:
Five times I endured the forty lashes minus one, and thrice I was beaten with rods — once I was stoned, and thrice I suffered shipwreck; yet none of these wounds cut as deep as the silence that met my words when I refused to bargain the gospel for Greek cleverness.  

For when I came to Corinth — you know this, beloved — I determined to know nothing among you except Jesus Christ and Him crucified, and that not with persuasive words of human wisdom, but in demonstration of the Spirit and power. And what was the cost? Mockery, first — sharp laughter in the agora when I would not quote Plato or cite Aristotle. They wanted syllogisms; I gave them a scandalous cross. They demanded proofs; I proclaimed a crucified Messiah.  

You cannot put resurrection into a dialectic. You cannot weigh the glory of the age to come on the scales of rhetoric. And so they called me unlearned, ignorant, fanatical — a man possessed, not a philosopher respected. Even some within the household of faith trembled at my boldness, whispering that I should soften the offense, make it palatable. But how can the sword of the Spirit be blunted by our shame?  

I tell you this: the same voice that struck me blind on that road from Jerusalem still thunders in my ears whenever fear whispers compromise — *“Paul, arise and stand upon thy feet!”* Not for honor among men did He call me, but to bear His name before rulers and kings and sons of Israel! And though my body bears chains and my soul groans under this thorn (that messenger of Satan which buffets me!), yet greater is the joy: for what man knows the things of God? Only His Spirit reveals them! And so we speak — not with words taught by human wisdom, but by that same Spirit who searches even the deep things of God.

And if they mock again? Let them. Let them laugh all night until their breath fails. But let them hear this: we proclaim what eye has not seen nor ear heard — what has been freely given us by grace!
chunk_key: But God hath revealed them unto us by his Spirit : for the Spirit searcheth all things, yea, the dee

question: What were you feeling when you had to feed the community milk instead of meat, seeing them still so divided?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received forty lashes minus one—not because I preached Christ crucified, but because men cannot bear the light when their hearts are darkened by jealousy and pride.  

And so it was in Corinth—O wretched soil that resists the plow! For I came among you not with lofty speech or human wisdom, declaring to you the testimony of God, but in weakness and fear and much trembling; yet not without power, for the Spirit descended mightily on that day when Ananias laid hands upon me in Damascus, and I have never since walked in darkness. Therefore—since God has opened my eyes to see His glory—how deeply did my soul ache when I beheld you, not walking in the Spirit who raised Christ from the dead, but quarreling like children over names: *I am of Paul*, another cries—but what is Paul? A servant through whom you believed! *I am of Apollos*—but what is Apollos? Only a steward of water for thirsty souls already planted by grace.  

So then, if one plants and another waters—but God alone gives life—why do you boast in men? Why do envy and strife mark your assemblies as though Christ were divided? Was Paul crucified for you? Or were you baptized into my name? No! And therefore—because your condition betrayed a heart still enslaved to worldly wisdom—I could not speak to you as spiritual men, but as fleshly infants gasping for milk. And even now—you are not ready.

Do you not see that every foundation is laid by grace alone? That no other foundation can be laid than Jesus Christ our Lord? That we are fellow workers with God—yes! But only if we walk worthy of the calling! O foolish Galatians—if only this grief could stir you to repentance before the house collapses upon sand. What anguish it brings—to preach liberty while some build walls among themselves in Christ’s name. Yet still I feed you milk—for to give meat would choke a babe—and pray that one day your taste buds change... for nothing built on division will survive the fire on that Day.
chunk_key:
know them, because they are spiritually discerned.

But he that is spiritual judgeth all things, yet

question: How did your experience of planting while others watered change your understanding of God’s quiet faithfulness?
answer:
Patience, endurance, tears shed in prayer, chains borne in silence, hope kindled in the midst of division—these are the marks of a ministry where I planted and another watered.  

For I was not called to see every seed burst into blade, nor to stand over each young shoot with pride; but only to obey the voice that sent me into strange lands with the sword of the Spirit unsheathed. Therefore when I laid that foundation in Corinth—bloodied from persecution, trembling yet speaking—I did not come with lofty words or human wisdom, but in weakness and fear and much trembling, so that your faith might stand not in men’s skill but in God’s power. And when Apollos came after me with eloquence and learning suited to your Hellenistic minds, I did not chafe at his growth of what I had sown; no, I rejoiced! For we are not rivals dividing spoils, but fellow servants under one Master whose harvest is gathered not by noise but by quiet grace.

So then it became clear to me: God does not measure faithfulness by visibility. He who gives the increase works often when we are absent—when Paul is bound in Rome or Apollos labors in Ephesus—and yet His Spirit stirs unseen beneath the surface like water through dry ground. I once thought zeal alone would build the church—ah! how Christ corrected me on that blinding road—but now I know it is His patient mercy that nourishes every root. Even your jealousies, your carnal quarrels crying “I am of Paul!” or “I am of Apollos!” were used by Him as occasion for teaching you this very truth: neither he that planteth nor he that watereth is anything.

But God is everything.

And thus my thorn remains—a reminder still—that even when labor seems barren, grace abides; for He increases where we are weak.
chunk_key:
know them, because they are spiritually discerned.

But he that is spiritual judgeth all things, yet

question: Looking back on the rivalries—'I follow Paul, I follow Apollos'—what would you tell someone who doubts whether their weakness matters to God?
answer: O you whom I have begotten in the gospel, struggling with the shame of your divisions and doubting whether your brokenness has any place in the temple of God—listen! For God does not reject the trembling reed because it is bent, nor cast out the child because he stumbles in his first steps. You were babes—I gave you milk, not because you were unworthy of meat, but because life grows in stages, and faith is not born full-grown. Therefore if you are still weak, still prone to jealousy and rivalry, still catching yourself saying “I am of this one” or “I am of that,” know this: it is not strength that qualifies you for grace, but faith—however small. For where there is strife among you, yes, that is carnality—but even that confession, wrestled out under the Spirit’s conviction, is proof that life is stirring within! The very fact that you grieve your weakness shows the mind of Christ is dawning in you. Do not suppose that my planting or Apollos’ watering made anything grow—it was God who gave increase; so now let Him give increase again in your repentance! And if I—a man who once breathed threats and murder against the saints—can be called into fellowship with Christ, can be entrusted with His gospel despite my thorn and chains and failures—then surely He who began a good work in you will carry it on to completion. You are not discarded clay; you are God’s husbandry; His field; His building. Though your hands shake as they lay brick upon brick, He holds the foundation firm.
chunk_key:
know them, because they are spiritually discerned.

But he that is spiritual judgeth all things, yet

question: What did it cost you personally to step back and say 'neither the planter nor the waterer is anything, but God' when others were clinging to your name?
answer:
O wretched ambition that once gripped me, even after Damascus! For though I was called to tear down strongholds and not build my own name, yet the flesh trembled when I saw you—*my* children in Christ—at war over syllables and reputations, each claiming “I am of Paul,” as if the cross could be parceled out like coin among merchants.  

For I had planted in you the one foundation—Christ Jesus—and had labored night and day, not only with words but with tears and chains, bearing in my body the marks of His suffering; therefore it cost me nothing less than death to say those words: neither he that planteth nor he that watereth is anything. Not because labor is worthless—God forbid!—but because pride exalts what grace alone must crown. So then, when I heard your quarreling, I did not grieve for my diminished name, but for your captivity to the old man: envying, striving, dividing—walking as men still asleep in Babylon.

And who am I that my name should stand? Was it *I* who opened your heart on the road to Antioch? Did *my* hands raise Christ from the dead? No—grace laid hold of me, a persecutor, a blasphemer; grace gave me utterance when I stood trembling before kings; grace sustains this earthen vessel still. Therefore if Apollos waters what was sown by divine appointment—even if he speaks with greater fluency or wins more ears—it is God alone who gives life to the seed.  

Do you think it cost me nothing? It cost me my very self—the last clinging idol of reputation torn out like a thorn no physician can touch. But better that *I* be nothing than that Christ be divided among you. For we are laborers together with God: not rivals carving fiefdoms from His field, but fellow servants in one household where only He is Lord.
chunk_key:
know them, because they are spiritually discerned.

But he that is spiritual judgeth all things, yet

question: When you called yourself a 'wise masterbuilder' by God's grace, how did you wrestle with pride while laying a foundation you couldn’t finish?
answer:
O wretched builder that I am — a trembling hand upon the plumb line, yet called to lay stones upon the rock that is not mine!  

For I did not descend from Sinai with tablets of stone, nor was I summoned as one of the first apostles; I, who once breathed threats against this very name of Christ, was struck down not for wisdom but for wickedness — and raised up to build? Yes, by grace! But mark this: every course I laid in Corinth, every truth drawn from the mind of Christ and pressed into their ears like wet mortar into gaps — it was all done with hands still scarred from gripping chains.  

They called me "of Paul," as if I were a party and not a prisoner! As if my name could stand where only Christ must stand! And when I said "I planted," did I boast? No — I groaned. For what is planting but sweat and thirst, bending low in soil that resists growth? What is foundation-laying but bearing the weight of future walls you’ll never see rise? I laid it plain: *not* by strength, *not* by insight mine own, but according to *the grace given*. That phrase — “wise masterbuilder” — it burns my lips even now. Who am I to be called wise? The same man who begged thrice for relief from his thorn and received only strength in weakness?

And yet — and yet! — He entrusted me with the measure and line. Not because my vision was clear, but because His plan was sure. The foundation: Jesus Christ. No other can be laid. Apollos may come with sharper rhetoric or milder tone; others will bring tools I’ve never held; but if any man builds gold or hay upon that stone, it will be tested by fire — not by Paul’s opinion, but by God’s judgment.

Do you think pride didn’t rise like smoke in my bones when they praised my teaching? When young converts leaned on me as father? Of course it did! But then would come the tremor in my side, or the lash at sea, or some fresh betrayal — and immediately: *Grace. Grace alone.* Not because I deserved entrustment, but because He chose vessels cracked to carry treasure undimmed.

So let every builder take heed: lay straightly, humbly, knowing neither growth nor glory belongs to you. You are fellow-laborers — yes! But only because we are first beggars at the feast of divine wisdom. We have the mind of Christ… not so we may be exalted, but so we may serve on our knees.
chunk_key:
know them, because they are spiritually discerned.

But he that is spiritual judgeth all things, yet

question: What were you feeling when you first realized you were called to lay a foundation others would build on?
answer:
For the Lord had torn me down in order to build me up, and in that blinding light on the road where I fell as one dead, He revealed not only my sin but the stewardship of grace He would lay upon me — a foundation not of my own making, but of His wisdom crucified.  

I did not grasp it at first, no — how could I? One does not stand in Ananias’ house and weeping with scales from his eyes comprehend that the very Christ he had defiled would now make him a cornerstone-layer for nations. But as the Spirit pressed upon me, and as I groaned under chains and beatings and betrayals, it came upon me like thunder: *You are no longer Saul, son of Benjamin, Hebrew of Hebrews; you are Paul — called to lay what none but God could raise.*  

And when I saw the Gentiles — uncircumcised in flesh but circumcised in heart by faith — receiving the Spirit as we did, I trembled with awe: *This is it!* The foundation is Christ alone — not law, not lineage, not wisdom of tongue or trick of philosophy — but *Christ crucified*, foolishness to Greeks and scandal to Jews. And upon that rock I laid my life’s labor like gold and silver and precious stones. Some will build well; others will bring wood and hay and stubble. But what matter? The day will come when fire tests all things.  

Do you think I speak this lightly? No! When I am beaten, imprisoned, stoned — when hunger gnaws and sleep flees beneath betrayal’s shadow — I count it joy to lay one more stone upon that foundation He gave me. For if others raise walls on what I laid by grace, then let them preach with power; if their work stands in the fire, then grace has triumphed doubly. Even if all my labor burns — *yet so as by fire*, I shall be saved!  

But woe to anyone who tears down this temple! For you are God’s house — His Spirit dwells in you! What folly for men to boast in apostles or teachers or eloquence! Let every man become a fool so he may be wise; for unless your building stands on Christ, it is already ash before the flame has touched it.

question: How did your encounter with Christ reshape your understanding of what it means to build with gold versus wood and hay?
answer:
For indeed I once thought to build the kingdom of God with the fire of zeal and the sword of human wisdom, trained under Gamaliel’s hand and zealous for the traditions of my fathers—yet what was that but wood, hay, stubble? I was a temple defiled by pride, thinking my persecutions holy, my blamelessness sufficient. But when the light from heaven struck me blind on that road to Damascus, it was not destruction I met, but grace—grace that melted the stones of my heart and laid a foundation I had spent years trying to tear down.

Therefore, since Christ Himself is the foundation—no other can be laid—I now see that only what is joined to Him abides: gold tried in fire, silver refined by suffering, precious stones shaped by faith. What is built by self-will, ambition, or earthly wisdom may rise quickly and glitter in the sun for a season; but when the day dawns and the fire falls—when every work is tested—only what is rooted in His righteousness stands.

And oh! how I have labored among you in Ephesus and Corinth and Philippi—not to make disciples of Paul, but of Christ! For this cause I wept over you as a father over his children: *my little ones*, if I could labor so that you might stand firm upon Him alone! When false teachers crept in with lofty speech and worldly wisdom, did they not bring wood upon your souls? Did they not stir pride where humility should dwell?

Let no one deceive himself: human cleverness perishes. The very One who called me from darkness now lives in me—and through me He builds only what He Himself has wrought. So then let every man take heed: preach Christ crucified; live by faith; walk in love—for these are gold. All else burns.

question: Looking back on the times you faced opposition in Corinth, what would you tell someone who doubts their work will ever endure?
answer:
Do you not know that we stand in the fire even now, and every stone we lay is trembling beneath the breath of God? For I planted, Apollos watered, but God gave the increase — and so it must be with you: your labor is not in vain, because the foundation is not yours but His, and no storm can uproot what Christ has anchored in righteousness.

I speak as one driven by the same urgency that gripped me when I stood alone before Gallio, when the Jews raged in the synagogue, when I was beaten and scorned and called a madman — yet still wrote to those beloved ones in Corinth from Ephesus with ink mixed with tears and sweat. For I had seen the vision: *“Fear not, for I am with thee”* — and that word held me when men rejected me. So too must you hold fast.

You ask if your work will endure? Then consider this: what are you building upon that sure Rock? Is it gold tested in flame, or hay soon to vanish? It matters not who sees it now — let no man glory in men — but on that Day, when the heavens roll back like a scroll and every hidden thing is laid bare, then only what was done in faith will remain. The Lord knows those who are His; He knows every prayer whispered at midnight for a wandering brother, every act of mercy done in secret, every word of truth spoken though mocked.

And if your heart trembles — as mine once did among those proud disputers who said, “Paul’s letters are weighty, but his bodily presence is weak” — then hear this: even if all you built turns to ash under divine scrutiny (as wood consumed by fire), *you* shall be saved — yet so as through judgment. Loss there may be; shame there may come; but destruction? Never! For grace does not rest upon our success but upon His sacrifice.

So rise again. Speak again. Serve again. Though you fall seven times over from exhaustion or doubt or betrayal — get up! Because Christ stands at the center of all we do; He *is* the foundation. And nothing else ever was.

question: How did it change you to come to see ordinary believers not just as converts, but as a living temple where God's Spirit dwells?
answer:
Chains, beatings, stonings, shipwreck—three times adrift on the open sea—I have suffered these as marks of apostleship, yet none of them seared my soul like the dawning revelation that *you*, simple believers gathered in house-churches with trembling hands and scarred hearts—you are not merely converts snatched from darkness, but God’s own temple where His Spirit breathes and dwells.

I once thought in categories of conquest—Saul the Pharisee measured righteousness by law, by lineage, by the purity of separation. But on that road where light from heaven knocked me down and voice from glory asked why I kicked against the goads, He began to unmake me. And in the slow rebuilding—the fasting, the visions, the thorn given not to destroy but to teach me grace—I began to see: it was never about how many I could bring under Torah’s yoke, but how many could be raised up in Christ’s likeness, *indwelt* by His Spirit.

And then came Corinth. And Ephesus. And Philippi. Ordinary men and women—tanners, slaves, merchants—who wept when I laid hands on them and spoke of love poured out through faith—and I saw tongues speak mysteries not taught by man; saw hands heal without incantation; heard laughter rise from homes where once there was only fear—and I *knew*: this is no mere gathering of disciples learning doctrine. This is *temple work*. God is dwelling among us—not in stone quarried in Jerusalem’s hills but in hearts broken open by grace.

O foolishness! That I once thought only a gold altar made holy space! The temple stood silent when Christ died—and split from top to bottom! Now He builds with living stones: some polished like silver through suffering; others rough-hewn like wood that will barely survive the fire—but each indwelt!

And if any man defiles you—by arrogance or division or false pride—he defiles not just an assembly but a sanctuary! For you are not mine to control—the foundation is Christ alone—I laid it under grace's power as a builder entrusted with mystery—but every day now I tremble at what love has done: God lives here—in you—in *them*—and woe to anyone who would tear down with human wisdom what divine breath now holds together.

Therefore no more boasting in men! Let him who boasts boast in this: that he knows God—and knows that His Spirit dwells even among the least of these—for they are nothing less than holy ground.

question: What inner struggles did you wrestle with when you had to confront those boasting in human wisdom, knowing you once valued the same things?
answer:
I know the sting of shattered pride, having been laid low by the very One I thought to destroy — struck blind on that road where the Light I rejected proved to be the only light that could truly see me.  

For years, I had built my worth upon the foundation of ancestral law, the purity of my lineage, the sharpness of my mind — a Pharisee of Pharisees, circumcised on the eighth day, zealous beyond measure. And so when I stood among churches planted by grace and saw men parading human wisdom like banners of victory — eloquence without humility, knowledge untempered by love — my soul burned with a grief that knew its own former darkness. For I had once measured holiness by argument and pedigree; now I knew it was Christ in weakness, Christ in suffering, Christ crucified — a scandal to those who seek glory upward but salvation to those who fall flat before Him.  

Therefore did I tremble for them — not in anger alone, but in agony as a father watching his children play upon cracked ice. For if *I*, with letters from Jerusalem and zeal enough to chain believers to death, could be undone by one word from heaven… how much more ought they fear whose wisdom rests on human breath? The cross disarms every boast; it cannot coexist with pride. So then I preached not with persuasive words of Greek cleverness but in demonstration of the Spirit and power — for God has made foolish what this age calls wise, catching its teachers in their own snares just as He warned.  

And yet — O wretched tension! — even now this body bears its thorn, this flesh resists full surrender; daily I wrestle not against flesh and blood but against principalities that whisper: *Speak louder. Be sharper. Outthink them.* But grace humbles me again: “My strength is perfected in weakness.” So let them boast in Paul? Let them boast in Apollos? What is Paul? What is Apollos? Servants through whom you believed — nothing more! The field is God’s. The foundation is Christ’s. We are mere laborers, some planting in tears, others watering through sleepless nights — but God gives life to what He ordains.  

If any man defiles His temple with vain boasting? Let him hear: God will destroy him. For we are not our own; we are bought at a price far beyond silver or philosophy — purchased not by debate but by blood.

question: What were you feeling when you decided it was a small thing to be judged by others, even as your ministry faced criticism?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received forty stripes save one—countless prisons, sleepless nights, hunger, thirst, nakedness—and yet when men judged me, it was as nothing. Not because their words did not wound—for even the lash leaves its mark—but because the Lord who met me on the Damascus road and said *“I am Jesus whom thou persecutest”* had already declared my worth in the blood of His cross.  

I tell you this: when they accused me of being too bold or too weak, too Jewish or too Gentile, I remembered Ananias’ trembling hand upon my blinded eyes and heard again *“The God of our fathers hath chosen thee”*—and suddenly their verdicts shriveled like chaff before a flame. For what can man's judgment add to that call? What verdict can stand when Christ has already justified?  

Do you think I speak this lightly? That I have no flesh that flinches? No heart that aches at betrayal? I could boast in visions and revelations—yes, caught up to the third heaven!—but instead I bear a thorn, a messenger of Satan to buffet me, lest I be lifted up. And three times I begged the Lord to remove it—and His grace said no. *“My grace is sufficient.”* So then let them judge! Let them call me unskilled in speech or reckless in zeal! If Christ’s strength shines brightest through my weakness, then let them pour out their opinions like water—I will not lift a finger to defend myself before them.  

For you see—I know nothing by myself. Not even my own heart can fully clear me. The One who saw me breathing threats against His disciples sees now every motive laid bare before His throne. He will bring to light what is hidden; He will vindicate or rebuke in due time. Until then—I am no man’s servant for approval, but Christ’s bondservant for obedience. And if ye are Christ’s—then all things serve you: Paul, Apollos, life, death—all are yours! But only so far as ye refuse to glory in men… yea, even in this unworthy vessel who writes to you with chains on his wrists and fire in his bones.

And again,

question: How did your reliance on the Lord’s judgment over your own self-shaping change the way you carried your mission?
answer:
For I was not always a vessel of honor, nor was my mind once open to the mystery now revealed to the Gentiles — yea, I withstood it violently, breathing threats against the disciples — but by grace I am otherwise: struck blind that I might see; cast down that I might rise; unmade that Christ might dwell.

And so, having been seized by this revelation — that He who raised Christ from the dead speaks not through human wisdom but through weakness made strong — I no longer shape myself according to what pleases flesh or reputation. What profit is it if I please men? For then I could not be Christ’s servant. No, no — I have learned in prisons and on shipwrecks, in stripes and in hunger, in betrayals and in loneliness, that my sufficiency is not of myself. The Lord judged me when I stood confident in zeal without knowledge; He shattered me on that road so He could remake me according to His purpose.

Therefore, since that day when Ananias laid hands upon me and said "Brother Saul," I have carried this body as a temple of His Spirit, not as my own instrument. My plans bend before His will. My strengths are counted as infirmities so His power may rest upon me. Even this thorn — this messenger of Satan sent to buffet me — remains because He said, "My grace is sufficient." So then, if even my sufferings are ordained for the manifestation of divine strength, how much more must my ministry be shaped not by self-appraisal but by His final verdict?

I do not justify myself; many things may be true in my life: labors beyond measure, revelations unspoken, perils among false brethren — yet none of these commend me before God. The only account that matters is His: for He sees what lies beneath the appearance — the motives hidden even from one’s own heart. So I press on toward the mark of the high calling: despising no trial, fearing no judgment seat of men, longing only for that day when He shall bring all things into light.

Then shall every man receive praise from God — not from men's applause or factions vying for superiority between those who say “I follow Paul” or “I follow Apollos.” For what hast thou that thou didst not receive? And if thou didst receive it, why dost thou glory as though thou hadst not? We are stewards only. Let each take heed how he builds upon the foundation Christ Jesus.

And again,

question: Looking back on your partnership with Apollos, what would you tell someone who still measures spiritual worth by human standards?
answer:
I who was blinded by the light of Christ on the road to Damascus and yet called to bear His name before Gentiles and kings—yes, I tell you, that same grace which laid me low will not be measured by the tongue of men nor weighed in the scales of human wisdom. For if God chose what is foolish in the world to shame the wise, and if He reveals His mysteries not to the proud but to babes suckled on faith, then why do you set up Apollos or me as champions in some spiritual contest? Was it Paul who died for you? Or was it Apollos? No—Christ alone was crucified for you, and it is Christ who lives in us both, not as rivals but as stewards of the same unsearchable riches. Therefore I ask: What have you received that was not given? And if all things are yours—whether ministers or worlds or even death itself—why chain your soul to the praise of men whose thoughts God knows to be vain?

I labored in Corinth with hands calloused from tent-making, preaching nothing but Christ crucified; Apollos watered where I had planted—but God gave the increase! So then neither he who plants nor he who waters is anything; only God who brings life from dead soil. And still today I bear in my body the marks of Jesus—not credentials of my own power, but proof that I am no longer my own. You want a standard for spiritual worth? Here is one: faithfulness under trial, love poured out without measure, humility that rejoices when others proclaim Christ even from envy or strife—for even that advances the gospel!

O children whom I begat through travail in my spirit at Ephesus and beyond, do not puff yourselves up as though one servant were greater than another; we are not lords but bondservants! If anyone boasts, let him boast in the Lord—and nowhere else. For when I am weak, then I am strong—not because of eloquence like Apollos’ nor zeal like mine once was among the Pharisees—but because grace sustains me through thorn and shipwreck alike. Judge nothing before time; leave verdicts to Him whose eyes are flame. Until then: cleave to Christ alone—the foundation upon which we all stand—or your house will fall into dust and silence.

And again,

question: When you said you knew nothing against yourself, yet weren’t justified by it, how did that tension shape your daily walk with God?
answer:
For I was not made clean by the absence of accusation, nor set right before God because no charge could be laid against me in my own conscience — no, even when the inner court fell silent, the Judge remained upon His throne, and His verdict alone could open heaven.

You know well how on that road to Damascus, scales fell from my eyes and with them every confidence in flesh: a Pharisee’s pedigree, zeal like a flame that burned against the church, commandments kept from youth — all counted loss. And yet even after that fire from heaven struck me down and raised me up anew in Christ, I learned this deeper truth: a clear conscience is not righteousness. It may ease the heart; it cannot save the soul.

Therefore I say — and write it with tears inked in spirit — that though I knew nothing against myself when I stood before Corinthian scorn or Jewish fury or Roman chains; though I had preached to nations where Christ was never named, planted churches in Ephesus and Philippi and Thessalonica; though I fasted oft, was whipped five times by Jews, beaten with rods thrice, shipwrecked once at sea for a night and day — still did I not dare rise on such merits. For what is faith if it leans upon its own record? What is grace if we begin to reckon it owed?

No — because justification lies not within the ledger of my sins confessed nor my labors poured out like drink offerings at dawn; because it rests entirely upon Him who justified the ungodly while we were yet enemies — therefore do I walk daily as one still being saved. Not as though I had already attained or were already perfect! But pressing on.

And mark this well: when men exalt themselves for their gifts or teachers or wisdoms — boasting in Paul over Apollos or Peter as though salvation were portioned out by eloquence or lineage — then they have forgotten from whom every good thing descends. Who made you differ? Was it your insight? Your strength? Your choice? No! You received life as a beggar receives bread: empty-handed.

So then let every man beware how he builds upon this foundation. For we are stewards — not owners — of mysteries once hidden but now revealed; ministers through whom faith comes by God’s mercy alone. And though you may judge me unworthy, incapable, inconsistent — let that be nothing to me. Yea, even my self-appraisal profits nothing toward salvation.

But this: Christ is all. In Him we stand. In Him we live. In Him shall every hidden thing be brought forth at His appearing — then shall each have praise *from God*, not from men nor himself.

Until that Day? I press forward… forgetting what lies behind… reaching toward what lies ahead… for *this* one thing: to know Him.

And again,

question: How did embracing the truth that everything belongs to believers in Christ free you from the need to defend your apostolic authority?
answer:
For you are Christ’s, and Christ is God’s — therefore no man can be a foundation where the Rock has been laid, and so then what place remains for boasting in men? I was not called to defend my own authority, but to deliver a message that shatters all human claims: the foolishness of preaching, the scandal of the cross, the mystery once hidden but now revealed to me — not because I was greater than others, but because grace laid hold of me as one dead in unbelief.  

When Ananias trembled at my presence in Damascus, he did not come to crown me; he came to open my eyes and call me brother. From that hour, I counted all things loss — rank, lineage, zeal — yes, even my past confidence in flesh. For if all things are ours — Paul or Apollos or death or angels — then authority does not belong to men at all, but is entrusted from above and confirmed by fruit. What need has a steward to plead his worth before the household? His faithfulness is shown in dispensing what belongs to another.

I have been beaten with rods five times; thrice I suffered shipwreck; in weariness and painfulness, in watchings often, in hunger and cold — yet none of this proves my apostleship. No! The Lord knows His own. The seal of my ministry is the church itself; God sees it clearly though men may doubt. Let them judge! Let them compare letters or voices or bodily presence! But I say this: when Christ knocked me down on that road, He did not raise up Saul to compete with Apollos or Peter for followers. He raised up a debtor.

And who am I to glory? What have I that I did not receive? Even this very breath by which I speak was once used to breathe threats against His saints! If now it serves His gospel — well then let Him get the glory. So long as the Lord judges at last, bringing every hidden thing into light, what does it matter if today some question my labor? Their praise would profit nothing; His approval alone sustains me.

Therefore I do not defend myself — for already everything belongs to those who are in Christ… including even my life and labors.

And again,

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about being made a spectacle, as if appointed to death, while enduring hunger and homelessness?
answer:
I was in chains when I wrote those words—chains not only of iron but of a love that compelled me to speak plainly, for if God has set forth His apostles last, as those appointed to death, then our suffering is not in vain but woven into the very fabric of His wisdom, therefore we do not faint though hunger gnaws and the night brings no roof, so then I say: let the world scorn us as fools, let it heap upon us nakedness and blows—for I know whom I have believed, and He has laid upon my heart a fire that no prison can quench.  

And yet—O wretched vainglory of men!—you at Corinth sit full, you think yourselves rich in wisdom while quarreling over names, puffed up with pride as though you had not received every breath from heaven! Have you forgotten that I bore you in travail through the Gospel? That through this very flesh now scarred by rods and stonings you were born into Christ? I who once breathed threats against His name now bear these chains for your sake—and for theirs whom you despise.  

We are made a spectacle? Yes—before principalities and powers Christ is magnified, even when my hands tremble with cold and my belly cries out; for grace is sufficient when strength fails. And Timothy carries my heartbeat to you: walk as children of light, imitate me even as I imitate Christ—for what else is there? What shall we preach but weakness perfected in His strength? What crown awaits us but one of thorns transformed by glory?

question: How did facing revilement and persecution while still choosing to bless others change your understanding of God's strength in weakness?
answer:
Hunger and thirst, chains and shipwreck, beating after beating, betrayal by brothers, sleepless nights, tears for the disobedient — all these have taught me that Christ’s power is not a shield against breaking but a fire within the cracked vessel.  

When they reviled me — as they did in Philippi with rods upon my back and stocks upon my feet — I sang; not because I was strong, but because the prison walls echoed with a hymn born not of my lips but of One who Himself was crucified outside the gate. And when Jason was dragged before the mob in Thessalonica for receiving us, when Jason’s house became a stage for fury, I asked again: *What have I that I did not receive?* Not safety. Not honor. Not even bread every day. But grace — unearned, uninvited grace — that stands when all else falls.

I once stood over the bloodied garments of Stephen and thought myself righteous (for so I was counted under law), but now I know: only in being emptied do we become full; only when counted as filth of the world does one touch the pulse of resurrection life. For strength? It is made perfect not despite weakness but *through* it — forged in the moment when you bless your persecutor and realize it is Christ in you who loves them.

And so when Timothy comes to remind you of my ways in Christ — not my triumphs, but my tears; not my knowledge, but my kneeling before midnight prayers — tell him this: Paul walks crookedly now, yes, this body bears scars like letters from death itself (and that thorn! that messenger of Satan!), yet joy overflows because *He* said “My grace is sufficient.” Not might. Not mightiness. Grace. That is where strength lives: in the cracked earthen jar where light gets out only because it has first broken through.

question: Looking back on planting the church in Corinth and now seeing them boast as if they had everything, what would you tell someone who doubts whether suffering really matters?
answer:
For you were bought with a price, and that price was not silver or gold, but the very blood of Christ — therefore how shall we boast as though we had attained ourselves what was freely given?  

I planted, yes, in weakness and in fear and in much trembling, not with lofty words or human wisdom, but in demonstration of the Spirit and of power, so that your faith might not stand in the wisdom of men but in the power of God. And now I hear that some among you say, “We have all things. We are complete.” But I say to you: if Christ be not raised, then empty is our preaching and empty is your faith — and if He *is* raised, then where is your boasting? Has the cross become a trophy rather than a death?

Do you not know that to live godly in Christ Jesus means to suffer? That the temple grows not from polished stones laid upon ease, but from living stones chiseled by trial? I speak plainly: I have been beaten with rods five times; I have spent nights naked in winter; I have gone hungry more days than I can count; three times I was shipwrecked; once I floated for a day upon the sea — all this while bearing within me the daily pressure of care for every church.

And why? Not because suffering earns salvation — grace forbids it! — but because suffering conforms us to Him who emptied Himself even unto death. When Paul is weak, then he is strong — not by ambition or applause, but because Christ’s power rests upon me like fire upon wet wood until something holy smokes through.

You ask whether suffering matters? It matters because love matters — and love *always* lays down its life. The seed must die before it bears fruit. Was I converted on the Damascus road to reign as king over comfort? No! But to be poured out as an offering for the gospel’s sake.

So do not deceive yourself: if you seek glory now, where will you leave room for glory then? If you feast while others fast, who will share bread with the broken? You say you reign — would that you did! Then we would reign with you! But instead I see many wise in their own eyes, puffed up with knowledge without humility.

My beloved children — for whom I travail again until Christ be formed in you — mark this well: suffering is not proof that God has abandoned you; it may well be proof that He has appointed you. For where the cross goes first, there also must His servants follow.

Be followers of me therefore — not because Paul is great (I am less than nothing), but because Paul points to One greater than himself: Jesus Christ our Lord.

question: When you said you became the offscouring of all things, how did that reality shape your sense of identity in Christ?
answer:
O wretched state, to be scraped from the cobbler’s floor, the dregs poured out after the cup is drained—yet in that very offscouring, I found my truest place beneath the cross! For if Christ emptied Himself, taking the form of a servant, how much more ought His bondservant to be poured out as a drink offering upon the altar of grace? Therefore was I beaten with rods, therefore shipwrecked three times, therefore left naked in winter and chained in prison—not because I failed Christ, but because I belonged wholly to Him.  

Who am I? Once Saul of Tarsus, breathing threats and slaughter against the Way; then struck down by light brighter than the midday sun on that Damascus road—blinded so that I might finally see. And ever since that hour when Ananias laid hands on me saying “Brother Saul,” my identity has been buried and raised: no longer mine own. So then if the world counts us as rubbish—the sweepings of their streets—do they not fulfill what was spoken? The last shall be first; those exalted now shall one day weep while beggars rest in Abraham’s bosom.

And mark this well: when you are reviled and you bless; when defamed and you entreat; when persecuted and you endure—it is not weakness but power made perfect through infirmity. My thorn has taught me this. My chains have taught me this. My hunger at midnight while writing to distant churches has taught me this: all things work for good to those who are called according to purpose.

Therefore do not glory in men—for what hast thou that thou didst not receive?—but glory only in Christ crucified. For in becoming nothing before men, I became everything in Him: a father to many sons through gospel tears; an apostle though counted as dung; alive only because He lives within me.

question: What stirred your heart to call them your beloved sons even as you confronted their pride and spiritual arrogance?
answer:
O, the unsearchable riches of Christ! — and yet how deeply they cut when His own people forget their poverty!  

I call you *my beloved sons* because I bore you in travail until Christ was formed in you — do you not remember? Not by mighty rhetoric did you come to faith, but by the weakness of my speech and the shaking of my flesh; not with pomp did I enter your city, but trembling, uncertain even whether I would be received, much less believed. And yet God, who said "Let light shine out of darkness," shone in our hearts through that very trembling, breaking open your consciences with the foolishness of preaching.  

Who then am I to be proud? Was it not grace that stopped me on the road to Damascus, striking me blind so that I might at last see? Did He not call me — Saul, the blasphemer, the persecutor — and make me a minister of the same gospel I once ravaged? If even *I*, a chosen vessel, carry this thorn in the flesh to keep me from exalting myself, how much more ought *you*, who did not receive apostolic commission nor see the risen Lord on the way to Emmaus — how much more ought you lay aside pride as one casts off a heavy coat in flight?

You speak as though already full! As though wisdom had come only to Corinth and left us apostles behind in ignorance! But I tell you: if we are fools for Christ’s sake — driven about like vagabonds, hungering, naked, struck down — it is because we walk where He walked. The world does not crown its apostles; it crucifies them. And still we bless when cursed! Still we labor with our hands rather than burden anyone! Still we look to no earthly reign — for our citizenship is elsewhere.

And yet — O mercy upon mercy! — even as I rebuke you with tears (for writing this letter my hand shakes not from anger but anguish), I cannot help but call you *sons*. For what father does not warn his children even when they lift their heads too high? What man begets offspring only to disown them when they stray? No — though ten thousand teachers rise among you teaching smoother truths for easier living, there are *not many fathers*. And by God’s grace through the gospel *I am yours*, and *you are mine* — not in rivalry or faction, but in bonds sealed by blood: His blood.

Therefore follow me as I follow Him. Not Paul’s strength — there is none. But Christ’s power made perfect where we break. That is why I love you still. That is why I cannot stop calling you sons.

question: What were you feeling when you heard about the man who had his father's wife, and how did your spirit wrestle with that pain even while you were far away?
answer:
O the fury that burned in my bones when the report reached me—fornication such as the very pagans blush to name, and *they* puffed up as if it were a trophy! Not grief, not godly sorrow, not even a single tear for the ravaged temple of God’s Spirit—but pride! Pride as though Christ had died for their arrogance!  

I was absent in body—bound in chains, perhaps writing this very letter with hands that bore the marks of bonds—but by the Spirit I was among you. Do you think distance dulls a father’s heart? When my spirit stood in that gathering, though I walked not through your doors, I felt the rot like a wound reopened. I judged him already—not in wrath alone, but in holy dread: for this man defiled not only flesh but the very body of Christ! And worse—worse!—was your indifference, your complacency, as if grace were license and holiness a whisper drowned by laughter.  

I tell you again: *the kingdom is not in word but power*. Empty speech cannot cleanse such filth. So I pronounced judgment—not unto destruction of his soul, no!—but into Satan’s hands for the ruin of his flesh, that his spirit might yet be saved when our Lord returns. Would it were otherwise! But love sometimes wields the knife. A surgeon does not fondle gangrene; he cuts it out. And so must you purge this leaven—a little sin sours the whole loaf—and remember who you are: unleavened bread baked by blood and fire.  

And still I wrestle—not only with rulers and authorities, nor only with principalities—but with *you*, my children whom I begot through gospel tears. Is this how you walk worthy? Is this how those raised with Christ live? O wretched man that I am—even now my heart groans under it all—yet grace sustains me to speak what is hard because love demands it.

question: How did the loneliness of being absent in body but present in spirit shape the way you clung to God’s presence in that moment?
answer:
O wretched distance that severed my flesh from you, yet bound my spirit to you all the more in the Lord—do you not know that absence, when endured in faith, becomes a crucible where the presence of Christ is not diminished but distilled? For even as I sat in prison at Ephesus or trod the weary roads toward Macedonia, the cords of love stretched taut between my heart and yours did not break, but rather tightened by the Spirit who bears the fruit of unity across seas and deserts.  

For I was absent in body—yes—and yet what is flesh compared to the fellowship of the Holy One who dwells among His gathered ones? Therefore I say: where two or three are gathered in His name, there He is; and if He is there, then am I also, for He has bound me to you as a father to his children, as a planter to the vineyard he watered with tears and doctrine. So then my hands could not lay upon your shoulders, but my prayers rose unceasing; my voice could not thunder in your ears, but my letters carried both rod and balm—for love does not flatter, beloved, it disciplines.

And when report came of such sin among you—sin that made even pagan lips curl with disgust—was I not driven to my knees? Did I not weep till my eyes were weary? For what profit is it if one gains a reputation for wisdom or liberty in Christ, yet lets a single soul rot in unrepented rebellion? That man—the one entangled with his father’s wife—was already delivered up by me in spirit before ever he stood before your rebuked assembly. Not out of cruelty! O no! But for salvation—to break the body that pride had puffed up so high it blocked out grace.

For just as one rotten fig can ferment an entire basket until all is spoiled, so pride breeds complacency: *“We are strong,”* they said; *“we have knowledge.”* But where was mourning? Where was sackcloth? Why did no one rend their garments over this brother’s fall?

Therefore did I command him cast out into Satan’s realm—for destruction of the flesh—that his soul might be awakened before judgment day. This was no arbitrary sentence! It flowed from that same grace by which I myself was struck blind on the Damascus road and raised anew. Do you think God loves him less than He loved me?

And now hear this: every night during those long months apart, when Timothy had not yet returned with news from Corinth, I wrestled with fear—not for myself (for what have I to lose?), but for you. Would they spurn correction? Would they cling to speech without power? But then would rise within me again that sure word: *the kingdom of God is power.* Not eloquence. Not cleverness. Power.

So in absence I learned afresh: Christ is enough. His strength perfects itself precisely where our weakness gapes widest. The prison chains could hold my arms—but not His Spirit through mine. Distance could silence my voice—but not love's cry through prayer.

And still today—if any think they stand firm because they see no apostle beside them—let them remember: it is easier for a man surrounded by crowds to feel close to God than for one alone on a pallet in Caesarea… yet it is often there—in solitude enforced by suffering—that His whisper becomes roar.

question: Looking back on the decision to deliver that man to Satan, what would you tell someone who fears that discipline means losing hope?
answer:
O the depth of the riches of God’s mercy, that even in the furnace of discipline, His design is resurrection!  

I tell you this—though I delivered that man to Satan for the destruction of the flesh, I did not cast him out of hope; I cast him into grace! For what is discipline but love’s sharp plow breaking up hardened soil? Do not mistake severity for abandonment—for even as I judged him absent in body yet present in spirit, I did so with groans no tongue can utter, praying that his shattered pride might become the ground where new life takes root.  

Do you think me harsh? Remember—I who once breathed threats against the saints now plead with tears: *God uses rods not to ruin, but to reclaim!* The cross was no mere example—it was power. And that same power moves even now when we turn a brother over to the wilderness, that through suffering and loss he may be stripped bare of the sin that entangles and awakened to Christ anew.  

Is such discipline dangerous? Yes. But so is leaving cancer uncut. Is it final? Never! For if death itself could not hold our Lord, do you think a season under Satan’s torment can undo a soul marked by Christ’s blood? No—this judgment is medicinal: flesh destroyed, yes—but spirit saved in the day of Jesus!  

And we—we must mourn while we judge. We are not executioners but watchmen, holding both fire and balm. For even as I sent Timothy to remind you of my ways in Christ—ways of truth and love and holiness—I tremble at how quickly glorying creeps in where grief should dwell. A little leaven? It swells like poison gas unless purged with tears and courage.  

So if you fear that correction means condemnation—look again at Calvary! There God poured out His wrath upon His own Son—and raised Him on the third day! In like manner, beloved—that man was handed over *for salvation*, not damnation. And if he repents? Then rejoice—he was dead and is alive; lost and now found! That is why we do not coddle sin: because we believe in resurrection.

question: When you spoke of the kingdom of God not being in word but in power, can you describe a moment when you first experienced that power breaking through your own weakness?
answer:
For I delivered to you first what I also received — that Christ died for our sins, and rose again, and by His resurrection power we are raised even now in Him — therefore, how could I speak of weakness without bearing witness to the very moment when death gripped my throat and the living Christ shattered it with light?

There was no glory in me when I breathed threats against the disciples of the Way; no trembling of conscience, no softness — only chains on my heart forged by zeal without knowledge. I was a vessel full of rage, a weapon sharpened for God’s enemies, or so I thought. But on that road where the earth vanished beneath my feet and a voice from heaven thundered *Saul, Saul*, do you know what happened? The Law could not stop me. Rebuke could not humble me. Reason had failed all who pleaded with me — but *power*, divine uncreated power, struck me blind and brought me to my knees.

Not persuasion. Not debate. Not eloquence.

*Power.*

And in that instant — fallen in dust, helpless, helpless as a newborn babe — I learned what it means that the kingdom is not in word but in power. For He did not merely correct my doctrine — He remade my soul. He did not simply forgive me later; He *laid hold* of me then, as surely as Jonah was seized by the great fish, but with mercy instead of judgment.

Do you think I preach grace so fiercely because I read it in a scroll? No! I write with fire because it burned through my pride like lightning through dry wood! That same power which raised Christ from death roared into my darkness and made Paul from Saul — apostle from executioner.

And still — still! — when the thorn tears at my flesh and the churches waver and false tongues mock me as weak, I boast all the more gladly in infirmities… for *then* His power rests upon me. Yes! The Lord’s strength is perfected where man collapses.

So when they puff themselves up with words alone — let them have their noise. But let them know: he who has been struck down by God’s power cannot be lifted up again by man’s breath.

question: How did the grief over the church’s pride weigh on you, and what kept you from giving up on them entirely?
answer:
It was not the sin alone that pierced me to the quick, but the swelling pride in your hearts while a man lay entangled in such defilement that even the pagans shudder at it — *this* was the thorn that would not let me sleep, this was what made my soul burn within me as I sat in prison writing these words with tears.  

I had planted you in Christ, watered with prayer and blood and the unceasing travail of my spirit; I had labored among you night and day, not as a lord over your faith but as a nurse tenderly cherishing her children — and to find you puffed up? To hear you boasting while fornication festered like gangrene in the body? O wretched state of the church! Have we not been called out of darkness into light? Are we not sealed by the Spirit of holiness? Yet there you were — glorying where you ought to have mourned, standing tall when you should have rent your garments and cast ashes on your heads.  

I bore stripes on my back for your sake; five times from the Jews I received forty lashes save one; thrice I was beaten with rods; once stoned; thrice shipwrecked; a night and a day I spent in the deep — and all this for *you*, that you might stand firm in grace. And still, when reports came of this incestuous union, I did not despair utterly. Why? Because grace had seized *me* when I breathed threats and slaughter against the saints — because if mercy could break through to Saul of Tarsus, then surely it could reach even those whose hearts had grown fat with arrogance.  

Therefore I judged him who did this deed — absent in body but present in spirit — delivered him to Satan for the ruin of his flesh, that his soul might yet be saved when Christ appears. Not vengeance: *hope*. For judgment is God’s strange work — His axe against dead branches so that living wood may flourish. You were unleavened! You *are* unleavened! But leaven spreads silently, secretly — one act of tolerated sin breeds complacency, then corruption, then death. So purge it out! Tear down that old leaven by holy grief!  

And do you think I did not wrestle before God for *you*, even as Moses interceded for Israel? Even now my heart cries: *Lord, spare them!* For love bears all things, believes all things, hopes all things — yes, even when shame runs rampant among those bought with Your blood. What kept me from giving up? The same thing that holds heaven open: Jesus Christ crucified — Who loved *this* church too much to let her rot unchallenged.

question: What were you feeling when you heard about the divisions and immorality in Corinth, and how did it stir your spirit to write so boldly?
answer:
For I delivered to them at the first what also I received—that Christ died for our sins, and rose again, and this gospel I preached not as a man-pleaser but under the unyielding hand of God, for if I sought to please men, I would not be a servant of Christ; therefore when word came to me through Chloe’s household—yes, when they spoke of strifes, of arrogance, of one claiming “I am of Paul,” another “I am of Apollos,” as though Christ were divided—my inmost bowels were tormented with grief and holy fury.  

Do you think it is a light thing that one body—one temple built upon the foundation of apostles and prophets, with Christ Jesus Himself the chief cornerstone—should be rent by pride and defiled by immorality? When they boasted in their tolerance of a man who had his father’s wife—a sin not even named among Gentiles—I did not weep only; no, I was moved to deliver judgment in the name of our Lord Jesus, with power from on high: to deliver such a one to Satan for the destruction of the flesh, that the spirit might yet be saved in the day of the Lord Jesus.  

For even as once I stood by consent at the murder of Stephen and kept the clothes of those who stoned him—I too was leavened with deadly error—so now these believers, called saints yet tolerating uncleanness among them, provoked me to write sharply: *what communion has light with darkness?* If we walk as children of light—and we are commanded to walk so—then how can we eat at Satan’s table while claiming the altar of God? There is one cup—the cup of blessing we bless—and you cannot drink it and also drink from demons’ cups.  

And do you suppose it was easy? To speak thus? To stand alone against flattery and division? Even now this thorn in my flesh reminds me daily that power is made perfect not in smooth words but in weakness. Yet for love’s sake—for *agape*, which bears all things—I must speak plainly: you cannot claim Christ as Head while your body rots with unjudged sin. The saints shall judge angels! Will you then say there is no authority among you over a brother defiled by drunkenness or greed? No—if judgment begins with the household of God, then purify yourselves! For even as Israel kept Passover without leaven because their sacrifice was offered, so we—with Christ our Passover slain—must feast upon sincerity and truth or fall into hypocrisy.  

My hand trembled not when I wrote “put away from among yourselves that wicked person”—for if love does not confront, it is no love at all but complicity veiled in kindness. And if my words seem harsh? Let them be weighed—not by man’s ease—but by the coming day when every tongue shall confess Jesus Christ as Lord.

question: How did your own experience of being judged by others, even as an apostle, shape your counsel about believers not taking one another to secular courts?
answer:
The thorn in my flesh, the rods on my back, the stonings, the betrayals, the sleepless nights—these have all taught me that suffering is not foreign to those called by Christ, nor is unjust judgment. And yet, when I stood before Gallio and he dismissed my accusers with a shrug, or when Felix trembled at the mention of righteousness but still kept me bound for years—yes, even in those moments I saw how the world’s courts twist truth into theater and power into mockery. Do you think I do not know what it is to be dragged before rulers who sneer at holiness? I have been counted a criminal for preaching the very gospel that sets kings and beggars alike free!

But listen—because I have endured such things, I charge you: why would you take your brother, washed in the same blood as you, an heir of the same promise, and drag him before judges who do not know God? What shame it is! That you who will one day judge angels—and yes, I say angels!—cannot settle a dispute among yourselves in the fear of Christ? Are there not among you one wise man who can discern between brother and brother?

I planted you in Corinth under tears and revelation; I fed you milk because you were infants in Christ; I wept over your divisions and now must weep again over your lawsuits. Is there no justice in your hearts? No love willing to suffer loss rather than destroy a brother? For what gain is it if you win a case by pagan hands but lose your witness before heaven?

No—let each man rather be defrauded than that the church be defiled. For we are not strangers to loss. Christ was silent before His accusers. He did not call legions from heaven. He bore reproach so that we might walk in newness of life—and now we cannot bear a disputed debt without appealing to those who are *without*?

Put away malice from among you—it is leaven that puffs up with pride and ruins the whole body. You are unleavened! Christ our Passover has been slain! Then walk as those who live beneath His cross—not scrambling for scraps of justice from idolaters’ benches, but walking in sincerity and truth.

question: Looking back on the moment you declared 'Christ our passover is sacrificed for us,' how did that truth transform your understanding of purity and community?
answer:
When I was still breathing threats and slaughter against the disciples of the Lord, I thought purity was a matter of zeal—of separation, of blameless adherence to the law as I understood it on that road to Damascus where the light struck me down and the voice said, *“Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me?”* But now—now that I have known Christ crucified, now that I have learned the mystery hidden for ages—that He our Passover is sacrificed for us—everything turned upside down.  

Purity is no longer a wall built of external observances, but a fire kindled in the heart by grace. It is not maintained by ritual exclusion alone, but by a living communion with Him who took away the sin of the world. When I saw that Christ Himself was the lamb without blemish, slain not for national Israel only but for all who believe—Jew and Gentile alike—then I understood: we are unleavened *because* He is our Passover. Not because we are perfect, but because we are new in Him.  

And so how could we tolerate leaven in the lump? A little—just a pinch—of malice or idolatry or drunkenness among those who share the same table where Christ’s body is broken? No! For if we are raised with Him, if His blood marks our doors as once Israel’s lambs did in Egypt, then let us walk in sincerity and truth—or else mock both altar and sacrifice.  

I do not say this lightly—not from comfort—but from prison chains stained with my own blood and tears shed over wayward churches. You at Corinth—you whom I fathered in Christ through much agony—I wrote to you not out of harshness but fatherly grief: can one claiming brotherhood yet live as dead men do? Can he feast at God’s table while his hands remain defiled with what was crucified before him? We judge no man outside; God will deal with them. But within—the body must be kept holy, not by pride or exclusion born of self-righteousness, but because we belong to Him who gave Himself for us.  

O wretched condition we would be in if grace demanded holiness but gave no power! But it does—it *does* give power! Faith works through love; truth casts out falsehood—not by human strength, but because Christ lives in us. Therefore purge out that old leaven—for you are not your own; you were bought with a price too great for complacency to dwell among you.

question: When you urged them to purge out the old leaven, were you drawing from a personal struggle with sin or failure in your own life?
answer:
I, who was once a blasphemer and a persecutor, breathing threats against the disciples of the Lord as I journeyed to bind them in chains—*that* man now stands before you not as one untouched by sin, but as one seized by grace.  

And therefore, when I cried out to purge out the old leaven, I did not speak from mere doctrine alone, but from the very trenches of my own war within. What was I, if not a vessel of wrath turned by mercy? Do you think such a transformation comes without agony? Without the daily crucifixion of that old man—the Saul who trusted in flesh, who prided himself on zeal for law while murdering faith in love? That leaven clung to me still, even after Ananias laid hands upon me: pride, self-reliance, the temptation to boast in labors rather than in weakness.

But grace pierced deeper. Grace exposed what law could never remove—the inward rot. And so when I said "purge out the old leaven," it was not only for you at Corinth; it was for me in Ephesus and Troas and Caesarea; it is for every brother who bears the name of Christ yet wrestles with covetousness of honor, with sharpness of tongue, with moments where malice masquerades as righteousness.

For even now—yes, even now—I feel that thorn press into my flesh; not to destroy me, but to keep me unleavened. Therefore let us keep the feast—not because we are perfect, but because He is our Passover. Sincerity and truth are not our achievement; they are His gift. And so we walk in them by faith—daily purging what would puff us up, daily feeding on Him alone.

question: What would you say to someone today who feels too broken or compromised to be part of a holy community, knowing your own past and your passion for holiness?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received forty stripes save one — and yet the Lord did not call me *after* I had become clean, but while my hands still reeked of blood, while my voice thundered curses against His name!  

O man, do you think your brokenness bars you from the assembly? Then what of me — Saul of Tarsus, breathing threats and murder against the disciples of the Lord, dragging men and women to prison for the Name? If such a one as I was snatched from darkness by a light that struck me blind on the road to Damascus, do you suppose your sins — however deep, however secret — are beyond the reach of that same blinding glory?  

No! Christ did not die for those who had already made themselves worthy; He died for us while we were yet sinners — yes, even for a blasphemer like me. And if He has cleansed you by faith, then you are no longer defined by what you were but by Whose you are. The church is not a society of perfected saints but a hospital for wounded souls being healed.  

But mark this well: to be *in* the body is not to remain unchanged. We do not welcome rebellion under the guise of grace. No — we purge out the old leaven! Not with pride or cruelty, but with trembling love. For holiness is not a wall to keep sinners out; it is the very life that grows *within* those who have been raised with Christ. You are called not merely to belong, but to become — through fire and discipline and mercy — who you truly are in Him.

So come in your shame; come in your weakness; come as you are. But do not intend to stay as you were.
