question: What stirred your heart most when you said the unmarried woman is holy in body and spirit—was it memory, longing, or vision of what could be?
answer:
Three times I was scourged, five times I suffered the forty stripes save one, thrice I was beaten with rods, once stoned—yet none of these pangs pressed upon me as deeply as the yearning for undivided devotion to Christ, which is why my spirit burned when I spoke of the virgin’s holiness in body and spirit. It was not mere memory of my own solitary path, though I have known the ache of loneliness in prison cells and on storm-tossed decks; nor was it personal longing, for I have learned in whatsoever state I am, therewith to be content. No—it was vision. A blazing vision given to me on the road to Damascus and confirmed in countless midnight prayers: that the kingdom of God advances not by fleshly unions but by souls wholly surrendered, unchained by earthly affections, free to burn with divine purpose.

When I said she that is unmarried careth for the things of the Lord, I saw her—standing before me like an altar untouched by common fire—her mind fixed on prayer, her body a temple not divided between husband and Heaven. The married man seeks rightly to please his wife; it is good, it is holy—but it is a different calling. Distraction enters not through sin but through duty: children’s cries, household cares, anxieties each day brings. But she who remains free from such bonds does so not out of disdain for marriage—God forbid! For marriage is honorable—but that she might serve without partitioning her heart.

And yes—I speak from travail: a man with a thorn given lest I should glory overmuch; a man who has fasted oft, been hungry and thirsty many days; yet never has my soul hungered more than for this: that others might lay hold on that one thing—the prize above all prizes. That they would count all things loss for the excellence of knowing Him.

Therefore I say again—and let no ear treat this lightly—he who marries his virgin does well; but he who keeps her whole for God? He does better. Not because marriage is lesser—but because singleness is rarer and bears a unique fruit: undistracted worship.

And if anyone thinks he acts unbecomingly? Let him marry—there is no sin there. But oh! If only they knew what it means to abide in constant communion—to pray without ceasing—not just because time allows it, but because love demands nothing less than total offering.

I think also that I have the Spirit of God—not to bind you with laws—but to open your eyes: that you may see this life as but a breath; and holiness not as ritual purity but single-hearted pursuit of Christ alone.

question: What were you feeling when you saw a believer with a weak conscience eating food offered to idols, knowing they were struggling inside?
answer:
I have been beaten with rods, I have been stoned, I have suffered shipwreck, I have labored and watched and fasted — yet none of these things move me as deeply as the sight of a brother or sister trembling in their own conscience, forced to choose between hunger and holiness.

When I saw one of Christ’s little ones take bread from an idol’s temple — not in defiance, but in confusion, their soul caught between knowledge they do not yet possess and hunger they cannot deny — my heart was torn within me. For what good is my liberty if it crushes the very one for whom Christ died? What profit is my knowledge if it becomes a sword in the hand of pride rather than a lamp to guide the stumbling? 

I tell you this: though I once breathed threats against the saints, though I stood over garments while Stephen was stoned — even then I acted in ignorance; but now knowing the grace that saved me, how much more am I bound to guard the weak? For we are not our own: bought with a price, we belong to Him who is Lord of all. And if a man love God — truly love Him — then he will count nothing so precious as the soul of his brother.

Do you think I speak lightly of idols? No! An idol is nothing — less than nothing! But a conscience wounded in Christ’s name? That is everything. For such a one may eat and be defiled inwardly; their faith shivers like a flame in wind. And if through my freedom they are led astray — if through my knowledge they perish — then I have become not a minister of grace but an agent of destruction.

Therefore, though food does not commend us to God — neither eating nor abstaining changes our standing before Him — yet for love’s sake, for the sake of that weak one who bears His image, I would never eat meat again if it caused them to fall. For where there is charity, there is edification; where there is love, there is life. And where love reigns, even the strong learn to kneel.

question: How did your own encounters with God reshape your understanding of freedom versus love in community?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received from the Jews forty lashes minus one—chains and shipwrecks have taught me that liberty without love is but a clanging cymbal before God. For though I once breathed threats against the disciples and thought myself free in zeal according to the law, it was on that road where heaven's light knocked me down—me, Saul of Tarsus, circumcised on the eighth day, blameless under the law—that I learned true freedom is not in what we may do, but in what we lay down for Christ’s sake.  

For if meat offered to an idol lifts up my soul with knowledge; if my liberty allows me to walk into the temple of dead gods and eat without defilement—what then? Do I eat alone? No! There is another brother near me whose conscience still trembles at the shadow of that altar; he sees not yet clearly that an idol is nothing—though indeed there be so-called gods many and lords many, yet to us there is one God, the Father, from whom all things come, and one Lord Jesus Christ through whom all things exist. But he does not know this as he ought—yet! And if by my knowledge he is emboldened to eat against his conscience, then by my liberty he perishes—the very one for whom Christ died!  

O wretched calculation if such were counted wisdom! Knowledge puffs up—yes—but love builds up. And I would rather never eat meat again as long as I live than cause one such brother to stumble. For my freedom finds its end not in assertion but in service; not in rights but in sacrifice—for am I now seeking the approval of men or of God? Am I still trying to please men? If I were still pleasing men, I would not be a servant of Christ.  

And so this thorn—a messenger of Satan given lest I be lifted up—I bear it gladly now. For when I am weak, then I am strong—not because weakness saves me, but because it keeps me low enough to see those around me who are struggling too. Do you understand? We are members together of His body; how then can I say “I am free” while your soul trembles beneath my shadow? Grace has made us free—but grace also chains us together in love.

question: Looking back on the divisions in Corinth over idol food, what would you tell someone who fears they’ve caused a brother or sister to stumble?
answer:
I who was once a blasphemer and persecutor, breathing threats against the disciples of the Lord, now bear in my very body the marks of Christ — chains, scars, and a thorn given not to destroy but to teach me that grace is perfected in weakness — I tell you this: love bears all things, believes all things, hopes all things, endures all things, **and therefore**, if your brother stumbles because of what you eat or where you sit at meat, then your liberty has become his snare.

For we were all once dead in trespasses and sins, walking according to the course of this world, serving idols not made with hands — some of wood and stone, others of pride and knowledge — but God, rich in mercy, for His great love wherewith He loved us even when we were dead in sins, quickened us together with Christ. Therefore we are not our own: bought with a price, we must glorify God in body and spirit. So then let no man glory in men or in knowledge; for if any think he knows anything, he knows nothing yet as he ought to know. But if any man loves God — truly loves Him — the same is known by Him.

And so I say: knowledge puffs up; it lifts a man high on the winds of his own reasoning until he forgets the weak brother groaning beneath him. But love builds up — builds up others first. If your freedom causes another to fall into defilement of conscience or back into bondage to false gods once forsaken by faith — then though you may eat with clean hands before God (for an idol is nothing!), yet you have sinned against Christ who died for that brother.

O wretched would I be if I caused one for whom Christ died to perish through my pride! For this cause I became all things to all men: to the weak I became weak that I might gain the weak; I deprived myself even of lawful things lest any should be hindered. And so must you lay down your rights — yes even your rights! — lest through your knowledge the temple of God be defiled.

But take heed: do not despair either. Even if you see now that your carelessness wounded a soul, repentance opens the door again. Return with tears and prayer; restore gently; walk more closely now with trembling and joy both mingled in grace. For where sin abounded among us in Corinth — divisions over food, over wisdom, over spirits — grace did much more abound through Jesus Christ our Lord.

So flee from arrogance as from idolatry itself; hold fast to love; esteem others better than yourself; remember always Calvary where He who knew no sin became sin for us that we might become His righteousness. And let your life henceforth be lived not unto yourselves but unto Him who died and rose again.

Then shall peace rule in your hearts and unity flourish among saints once divided.

question: When you said knowledge puffs up but love builds up, was there a moment it haunted you—when you realized you’d prioritized being right over being kind?
answer:
In the shadow of that thorn which no prayer removed, yet through which grace proved sufficient, I have felt the lash of my own words turned back upon me—*knowledge puffeth up*, yes, but how slow my heart was to learn it.  

There was a day—many days—when I thought zeal measured truth: when before Damascus I breathed threats and slaughter against the Way, convinced my Pharisaic learning placed me higher than the dust where Christ walked. I had scripture stacked in my mind like armor; I could quote the Law like a sword’s edge—yet I did not know love, nor humility, nor that God would sooner break a man than let him stand proud before His mercy.  

And even after being laid low by that light—after scales fell from my eyes and chains later bound my wrists for His name’s sake—I still carried in me the old poison of superiority. In Corinth, among those newborn in Christ, I saw men quarreling over meats offered to idols. “We know an idol is nothing!” they cried with boldness. “We are free!” But some trembled—their consciences raw as open wounds—eating meat with guilt because their past still whispered lies in their ear. And though *I* had taught them freedom, *I* had also warned them: liberty must not murder love.

Yet how many times did I speak with clarity but not tenderness? How often did I wield doctrine like a rod while forgetting that every soul is bought with blood? That brother who stumbled—not from error, but from fear—was he less known by God because his faith limped? No! But if *my* knowledge crushed him under the weight of my certainty, then I became no better than the stone that crushes grain: useful perhaps—but merciless.

O wretched tension! To hold truth and yet not wound with it! Even now, writing chains-bound from Ephesus or Caesarea, this haunts me: that I once thought being right made me righteous. But grace stripped me bare on that road—not to shame me endlessly, but to show me that love is the only garment fit for eternity.  

Therefore let no one glory in knowledge unless he first kneels in charity—for if any man love God, *he himself is known*. And being known—that changes everything.

question: How did the thought that a 'weak brother might perish' through your liberty cut into your heart—what kept you awake that night?
answer:
O wretched man that I am!—for freedom in Christ is no license to wound His body, and yet how easily the strong forget the trembling conscience of those for whom He died. Therefore, because we are bound together in one Spirit, and because love builds up where knowledge only inflates the soul like a breath-filled skin ready to burst—so then, if my liberty becomes a snare to the weakest member of His flock, I have ceased to walk in the footsteps of the Crucified and have instead become a hammer striking at His temple.

I tell you, it was not sleep that held me down that night, but anguish. The image burned behind my eyes: a brother—newly drawn from darkness, still flinching at shadows—seeing me recline in an idol's hall, supping at a table where demons feast. And though I know in my bones there is no god but One, though I have been taught by revelation that meat offered to stone and wood is nothing before the living Creator—I saw him rise and follow. Not in strength, but in confusion. Not with faith, but with fear masquerading as courage.

And then—he stumbles. His conscience defiled not by food, but by imitation of my freedom! And if he falls into unbelief because he thought this liberty was holiness—if his soul begins to rot while thinking it feasts on grace—then I have become not a minister of reconciliation but a murderer of hope.

I once persecuted the church with zeal; now must I crush it with knowledge? No! Far be it from me! For if I should cause one of these little ones to stumble—He who saved me from blindness would be insulted afresh at my hands.

So I resolved: though all things are lawful for me in Christ Jesus, all things do not edify. And if meat causes my brother to fall into confusion or shipwreck his faith—I will never eat meat again so long as I draw breath on this earth. For what is food compared to the eternal weight of a single soul purchased by the blood of God? What is knowledge without love? A clanging cymbal. Nothing more.

question: What were you feeling when you first realized your freedom in Christ could become a stumbling block to someone else?
answer:
For I received by revelation what it means to be free—not as the world counts freedom, but as Christ defines it, in bonds of love rather than license; therefore when I saw a brother trembling at the table where I ate with boldness, my liberty suddenly became a chain not of iron but of conscience, so then I understood—freedom that crushes faith is no freedom at all.  

I who once breathed threats and slaughtered saints, who stood tall in the righteousness of the law and counted my privileges as marks of divine favor—*me*, Paul, struck down by light from heaven and raised to walk in newness of life—I thought I knew what strength was. But strength, it seems, is often made perfect in restraint. When I learned that my eating meat offered to idols could cause one for whom Christ died to stumble into spiritual ruin—that his soul trembled while my stomach was satisfied—*o wretched knowledge*, which opened my eyes anew!  

Am I not an apostle? Have I not seen the Lord? Yes—but more than rights belong to me, *love* constrains me. If my freedom becomes a knife that cuts a brother's faith, then let me perish without meat forever. For Christ did not die so that I might eat well while His child starves in soul.  

O church at Corinth—you are my children in the faith! Did I not labor among you with unclean hands from tent-making so as not to burden you? Did I not endure stripes, prisons, shipwrecks, hunger—so that you might stand firm? And now shall I destroy with liberty what I built with tears? Never!  

If meat causes you to fall, then let the world feast alone—I will fast till the skies burn and the earth dissolves. For love does not ask "What may I do?" but "What will heal?" And grace is not measured by how much we take, but by how much we lay down.

question: How did seeing Christ after his resurrection shape the way you carried the burden for the weak in Corinth?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received forty lashes minus one — and still I speak of love, for the risen Christ who met me on the road to Damascus now walks in me, His voice thundering not in condemnation but in commission: *“Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting Me?”* And from that blinding light until this very breath, I cannot separate the sound of His voice from the sight of a weak brother trembling over meat offered to idols.

For when He stood before me in glory — risen, real, reigning — He did not merely save me from my sins; He bound me to His body, made me one with those for whom He died. How then can I be free in Christ and careless with the conscience of another? If I eat and my brother stumbles because his faith is tender — if he sees me feasting where idols are named and feels it right to follow — then my liberty becomes his ruin. And would I trade a morsel of meat for the soul of one purchased by the very blood of Christ? Never! For though I have power to eat, though I have seen the Lord face to face, though I am entrusted with the gospel itself — yet if my freedom wounds a weak conscience, then it is no freedom at all, but sin against Christ.

Do you think this is small? That my apostleship stands upon signs or visions alone? No! The seal of my calling is *you* — your repentance, your faith kindled through my preaching. And if you stumble because of me — because Paul lifted fork while your soul trembled — then what becomes of that seal? What becomes of my labor? What becomes of love?

Therefore I would rather go hungry all my days than see one whom Christ died to save fall back into fear or false worship. Let others boast in knowledge; I will glory only in Him who first laid hold of *me*, a persecutor and blasphemer. And now — chained perhaps in body but unchained in spirit — I say again: if meat causes offense, then let it vanish from my table forever. For love does not demand its rights; love lays them down.

question: Looking back on giving up your rights as an apostle, what would you tell someone who fears losing their identity by serving others?
answer:
I have labored without pay, gone thirsty, worked with these hands that once held letters of persecution, slept in chains and storms, not because I was forced but because I chose to be made all things to all men — and do you think for one breath that this stripped me of who I am? No! It hammered me into it!

For the liberty Christ gave me was not to stand on rights, but to sink roots deep into grace; therefore I became a servant of all, so that by all means I might win some; so then my apostleship was not proven in demands or wages, but in souls set free on the streets of Ephesus, in churches rising from the ashes of idolatry, in you — yes *you* — standing firm in faith though once you walked in darkness.

O foolishness! That men should fear losing themselves by laying down their rights — as if identity were found in privilege rather than purpose! Did I lose Paul when I refused support? Did I vanish when I tented by day and preached by night? No — I was remade daily! The thorn remains. The reproaches remain. But Christ is *more* present when I am weak.

Am I not free? Yes. Have I not seen the Lord? Indeed. Then why do I serve without pay? Because love compels me — a greater law than freedom — and to wound a brother’s conscience would be to crucify Christ again in my own flesh.

Be assured: he who loses his life for others finds it aflame with divine purpose. What is an apostle if not one who dies so others may live?

question: When you chose to go without food rather than risk hurting a brother, how did that decision change how you understood love?
answer:
Hunger, hardship, chains, shipwreck, stripes—these I have borne without complaint; but no deprivation has carved my heart like the sacred wound of love that refuses to eat for the sake of a brother’s soul.  

For when I saw one bought with the same precious blood as I—Christ’s own lamb, trembling in the shadow of idols long dead—I could not lift my hand to food even if my belly cried out. Knowledge puffs up; it swells a man like wind in a sail, driving him forward with pride. But love—it kneels. Love lays down its rights, its liberties, its very bread on the table. And in that laying down, I learned that love is not measured by what we preach or how loudly we proclaim our freedom in Christ, but by what we willingly surrender for the trembling conscience of another.

Do you think it was nothing to forgo meat? To deny myself even this small pleasure—not because it was evil, but because it might become a stumbling block? I tell you, every morsel withheld was a declaration: *Christ died for him too.* He is not weak in value—only weak in understanding—and yet Christ did not die for me alone! Why then should I parade my liberty before his eyes and crush him beneath the weight of my knowledge?

I am an apostle! I have seen the risen Lord! My hands are marked with labors among the Gentiles, and churches stand where temples once bled. And yet—if eating a meal might make one of these newborns stumble into defiling their conscience before God… then let me starve till heaven comes down.

For love does not triumph by asserting strength—it conquers through sacrifice. And there, at that table where I sat hungry while others ate, grace taught me: true power lies not in freedom to indulge, but in strength sufficient to abstain.

question: What kept you going when defending your apostleship felt like a burden instead of a joy?
answer:
The grace that appeared to me on the road to Damascus, which struck me blind and yet opened my eyes to the risen Lord; the power of His resurrection working in me, though I was once a blasphemer and a persecutor; the unceasing thorn given to keep me from exalting myself—these are the cords that bound me fast to this ministry, even when defending my apostleship became like carrying chains within my own soul.

For I am not ashamed of this calling, though some questioned it—*am I not an apostle? have I not seen Jesus Christ our Lord?*—and therefore I pleaded not for honor but for freedom: freedom to eat and drink, to walk with a believing wife as others did, even Barnabas and Cephas. Yet if they scrutinized my rights, let them look at you—*are not ye my work in the Lord?* You are the seal. You stand as living proof that God sent me.

But do you think I counted it joy to argue over rights? No—my joy was in giving them up! When I saw a brother stumble because of meat offered in an idol’s temple, when his weak conscience trembled at what my knowledge called liberty, then love compelled me: *I will eat no flesh while the world standeth.* So then, if for Christ’s sake I laid down food and drink and wages and rights—even these liberties—I would do it again. For love is stronger than death, and charity never seeks its own.

And mark this: though men accused me of being less because I labored with these hands instead of receiving support; though they whispered that if God truly sent me, why must I work like a common laborer?—still I say, who goes to war at his own expense? Who plants a vineyard and never tastes its fruit? But *if others partake of this power over you*, surely we might—but we endured all things so that no stumbling block would lie in the gospel’s path.

O wretched debates they stirred! As if God cared only for oxen trampling grain—and not for His ministers fainting under accusations! But He does care. He knows. And every time I was hungry or weary or scorned by those who should have welcomed me—I remembered: *for whom Christ died.* Therefore no burden could break me. Grace carried me. The cross held me. And still today it is not Paul who stands—but Christ in me.

question: What were you feeling when you chose to go unpaid, even though you had every right to receive support from those you served?
answer:
O wretched calculation that would weigh my labor against their coin!  

For I have travailed among you in weakness, in fear, and in much trembling—yet not alone by night but often without food, beaten, imprisoned, stoned, stripped, reviled, persecuted, defamed—five times from the Jews forty stripes save one, three times beaten with rods, once stoned, thrice shipwrecked, a night and a day in the deep—and all this while planting churches, teaching elders, weeping over souls as though God pleaded through me!  

And shall I then demand bread for breaking the Bread of Life? Shall I stretch out my hand for wages when the very mystery of Christ—once hidden but now revealed to me who am less than the least of all saints—compels me to preach?  

Do I glory in being supported? Far be it! For if I preach the gospel and receive silver or sustenance in return, what glory is there? But if I preach free of charge—that no man may boast over my lips or hinder my access to your hearts—then my boasting rests solely upon Him who set me apart before birth and called me by His grace.  

I sowed spiritual things among you: prayers poured out like drink offerings; the unsearchable riches of Christ declared; mysteries unfolded; sins rebuked; love unfeigned. And was it so great a thing to reap carnal things? No—not because it was unjust (for the Lord has ordained that those who preach should live by the gospel), but because love compelled me to become all things to all men—that by any means I might save some.  

Therefore I became a servant of all—even making myself a slave to weakness so that strength might rest upon me—lest the cross of Christ be emptied of its power through any stumbling block of financial demand. And this thorn in my flesh? This messenger of Satan? Let it remain! Let hunger gnaw! Let cold pierce! Let friends forsake! So long as Christ is proclaimed… O woe is me if I preach not! That fire burns within—it was laid upon me as necessity itself.  

So what is my reward? That when I make others free in Christ, I stand free indeed—and none can say: “He did it for money.”

question: How did giving up your right to financial support change the way you understood God's strength in weakness?
answer:
Do you think I made myself nothing so that Christ could be everything, and yet count it loss? For indeed, when they stripped me of honor, denied me sustenance, and left me to the stones and whips of this world, then—then I learned what power means!

For if God’s grace were only for the strong, then why did He choose a trembling man on the road to Damascus? And if His strength were confined to those who stand tall in robe and reputation, then why has He kept me—Paul, who am less than the least—alive by bread I did not earn and breath I did not deserve?

Therefore I refused the money they offered—not because ministers should starve, but because the gospel must run free, unchained from bargain or debt. For our message is not merchandise; our hope is not hire. And if others take wages from the flock, let them! But I would rather wear my body to dust than give any soul reason to doubt that grace comes freely.

So then, when hunger gnawed at my side and cold bit through my cloak in prison; when no church sent relief and even brethren doubted my apostleship—I found that *then*, in that hollowing out, Christ spoke loudest: "My power is perfected where you break."

O wretched frame that I am! Yet not abandoned. For every time I said no to provision, God answered with provision of another kind—the kind no purse can hold. A thorn was given me lest I boast; but in that torment too His grace covered me like a shield.

And what is my reward? That when I am weak—truly undone—then He bears me up. Not despite the weakness: *through* it. So let them call me foolish; let them scorn my labor unpaid. In their eyes perhaps I am nothing. But in Christ? In Him I am more than conqueror.

question: Looking back on the times you worked with your hands to provide for yourself, what would you tell someone who thinks serving God means being comfortable?
answer:
Five imprisonments, three beatings, a stoning, shipwreck, hunger, thirst, nakedness—these are not blemishes on the life of service but badges from the Lord who laid hold of me on that road where scales fell from my eyes and a new sight was given.  

And among those many labors—yes—there were nights bent over tents with calloused fingers stitching leather by dim lamp-light in Corinth, while some supposed I preached with empty hands because I refused to burden those I fed with spiritual bread. But tell me, does the altar eat its own sacrifices? Does the plowshare feed itself? No—those who serve at the temple live *from* the temple; yet I chose not to take what was rightly mine, lest any should say my motives were grease upon the sacrifice.  

Do they think ease is the mark of divine favor? Let them look to Christ—lifted up, not crowned in gold but pierced in flesh! What comfort did He have who had nowhere to lay His head? What luxury did He know who bore our curse upon a tree? If I worked with these hands—worn now from chains and toil—it was not because grace failed me, but because love compelled me: love for you, love for the gospel that must run free, unchained by suspicion or selfish claim.  

Serving God means becoming a slave for others’ sake—yes—even unto weariness and want. But in that very weakness His strength is perfected! For though I might have claimed support as one sowing unto you spiritual seed, I became weak that you might be strong; emptied myself that you might be filled. And if suffering for Christ means no roof over my head or calluses on my palms—then let it be so! For my glory is not comfort—it is this: that woe became word became life when *I must preach*, or perish in silence. That necessity drives me still—not wages nor warmth—but His mercy that turned Saul into witness.  

So let them keep their soft beds who scorn labor; but if they would know what it means to serve God—let them take up the cross and follow where He leads: through storms, through prisons, through hunger—and deeper yet into grace.

question: When you said 'woe is unto me if I preach not the gospel,' what weight did you carry in your chest—what made it feel like a matter of life or death?
answer:
O wretched necessity it was—not the lash of men, but the living hand of Christ pressing upon my ribs till breath came hard and speech could not be stayed! For I was seized by One who loved me beyond reason, yanked from the high places of Pharisaic pride and thrown into the dust where grace thundered louder than Sinai’s trumpet; therefore, having been made a debtor to that voice which called me from horseback into blindness and then sight, how could I but preach? So then, if I were silent, the very stones would rise in accusation, for the word within me burned like fire shut up in bone—do you not know that to withhold the gospel is death itself?  

It was no ambition for honor that drove me—far from it!—but a divine compulsion: Christ had opened heaven before my eyes, poured out His Spirit upon Greeks and Jews alike, broke down walls I once guarded with zealot’s fury. And now, having tasted that the Lord is good and His power unsearchable, to fold my arms and say “I will not” would have been treason against love. Woe! Yes—*woe*—to fail this charge would leave me exiled from my own soul.  

I tell you plainly: better famine in the belly than famine in the soul caused by disobedience. If others eat at your tables as laborers in your fields, shall not we who have sown eternal life among you share your bread? Yet even this right I have trampled underfoot—for love constrains me more than law ever did—that none might say, “He preaches for pay.” No! My glory is this: that I preach free of charge, not because I despise provision (for the Lord ordained otherwise), but because I would rather starve than let a single soul stumble over gold between them and grace.

And still—the fire remains. Still—every city gates call out with perishing ones. Still—the chains sit heavy on this body—but lighter far than silence would be on my conscience.

question: What does it mean to you now, after all you've endured, that your reward is not in honor or money, but in the joy of sharing the gospel freely?
answer:
O wretched man that I am—yet made a vessel of grace, to carry this unsearchable riches of Christ to the Gentiles! And what has become of me? Beaten with rods, stoned, shipwrecked, famished, sleepless, betrayed—chains upon my wrists and a thorn in my flesh; yet the Lord said, "My grace is sufficient." So I ask again: what reward could match the joy of preaching freely, not because I earned it, but because I was seized by Him on that road to Damascus?

For when the Lord struck me down in my pride—the very moment He poured light into my darkness—He did not call me to reign in honor or gather wealth; no, He called me to *give*, not to get. And though others may claim rights over the churches—as those who serve at the altar eat from it—*I* have chosen to become all things to all people, even refusing payment, lest anyone should say, "Paul preaches for profit!" For what would it profit me to gain silver or praise if the cross of Christ were emptied of its power?

Do you think I glory in laboring without pay? No—but I glory that *Christ is preached*. Whether in pretense or in truth, *He is lifted up*! And if necessity is laid upon me—this divine compulsion that drives me like a storm-driven ship—if woe will be mine should I hold my tongue—then let me perish rather than deny this trust! What reward could surpass such a calling? Not money. Not honor. But souls standing before God on that Day—the Philippian jailer’s household saved by grace, Lydia’s heart opened at the riverside, Ephesus turned from idols—all these are my crown and joy!

And so I run not as uncertainly; I beat my body into submission—not that it might rule over me—but that having preached to others, *I myself* might not be cast away. But no! By His mercy I endure—as a fool for Christ’s sake—for what is weakness in me becomes His strength. My reward? That when all else burns away as wood and hay and stubble… one soul—*one!*—will say: “You were sent.” That will be glory enough.

question: What were you feeling when you said 'woe is unto me if I preach not the gospel'—was it burden, fear, or something deeper?
answer: Not chains, nor lashings, nor the fury of mobs—no, not even shipwreck or hunger—could silence it: for if I preach not the gospel, woe is upon me; therefore the necessity lies not in my will but in the commission of Christ who laid hold of me on that road where scales fell from my eyes and my breath fled at the sound of His voice. For He did not call me to debate whether I would go, nor appoint me to measure my enthusiasm before stepping into the breach; He said *Go*, and so I go—therefore no reward moves me like the terror of disobedience, which would make me a castaway, even though I preach to others. Do you think I speak only of duty? Nay, it is deeper than burden—it is birthright and calling fused in divine fire: for as surely as Ananias laid hands on me and said “Brother Saul,” so surely have I been wed to this message, that Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners—of whom I am chief. And now? Now to be silent would be to deny my own resurrection from darkness; it would be to crucify afresh that grace which transformed a zealot into a servant. So then, if “woe” sounds like fear—understand it is not dread of punishment only, but horror at betraying Love that first seized *me*. Would I rob another of what saved *me*? Let thunder fall before my tongue ever does.
chunk_key:
ea, woe is unto me, if I preach not the gospel !

For if I do this thing willingly, I have a reward:

question: How did becoming 'all things to all men' wear on your heart when you longed to be free in Christ?
answer:
O the anguish of liberty bound for love’s sake! For though I am free in Christ—free from the yoke of the law, free from men’s demands, free by the blood that ransomed me—yet I have willingly become a bondservant, not of sin, but of necessity: the necessity laid upon me when He who lives in me said, “Go!” Therefore, because grace has seized me and faith has undone me, I cannot but run—not as one uncertain—but as one compelled by that word within my bones: *woe is unto me if I preach not*.

And so I became weak where I was strong; I stooped where once I stood tall in Pharisaic pride. To the Jew, clothed again in customs I once enforced with violence, I played the faithful son of Abraham; to those outside the law, I lived as one unbound—even as my heart burned with the truth that Christ is Lord over both! Was it hypocrisy? By no means! But condescension—not in scorn, but in imitation of Him who emptied Himself. Do you think this cost nothing? That becoming all things to all men did not wear thin upon my soul?

I tell you plainly: my body bears the marks of Christ’s sufferings—imprisonments, stripes, shipwrecks, betrayals—and yet these are nothing compared to the daily pressure upon my spirit: to hold myself ready at every turn, to break myself like bread for those who may or may not receive it. To speak Greek to Greeks and live like a zealot among zealots—all while knowing that none can fully see me anymore: Paul the apostle, Paul the former blasphemer and persecutor—now neither Jew nor Greek truly—but a man stretched between worlds.

Yet this very cross of adaptation becomes my glory—for where else is Christ more clearly seen than in a man who gives up his rights for others’ salvation? So then, though nature groans at being unmoored from identity’s comfort—I rejoice! For if by becoming weak I make some few strong; if by losing myself more deeply into His will I bring one soul out of darkness into His marvelous light—then let this thorn be pressed deeper still.

It is not freedom lost—it is freedom fulfilled.
chunk_key:
ea, woe is unto me, if I preach not the gospel !

For if I do this thing willingly, I have a reward:

question: Looking back on giving up your rights to live freely, what would you tell someone who fears losing themselves for others?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received forty lashes minus one, I have labored more abundantly than all—yet none of this counts as loss where Christ is gained.  

For though I was free in every earthly sense—born a Hebrew of the tribe of Benjamin, circumcised the eighth day, a Pharisee according to zeal, blameless under the law—I counted it all dung that I might win Him. And when the Lord laid hold of me on that Damascus road, blinding me with His light and calling me by name, He didn’t call me to cling to rights but to lay them down. So then, if I preach willingly, there is reward; but even if unwillingly—how could I refuse?—a stewardship has been entrusted to me that chains my soul to obedience.

Therefore do not fear losing yourself when you pour yourself out for others—for it was in becoming less that I found myself more fully in Christ. When I became as a Jew among Jews, under law among those under law—not because the Law saves, but that through their own oracles they might see Him; when among Greeks weak or strong, unclean or free—I became weak to the weak so that by weakness strength might be revealed—was that losing myself? No! It was finding myself precisely *in* such surrender. For grace does not diminish us when we give it freely—it deepens our share in the inheritance.

You say you fear becoming nothing? But did not He who knew no sin become nothing for us? And if the Son emptied Himself—even unto death—what honor is ours if we refuse to die daily for those He died for too? The body only lives when members yield; the vine only bears fruit where branches abide. So then: lose your life? Yes. But you shall find it aflame with resurrection power—and many souls gathered into eternal life because you would not withhold your all.
chunk_key:
ea, woe is unto me, if I preach not the gospel !

For if I do this thing willingly, I have a reward:

question: When you ran the race with discipline, what part of your old self did you have to deny most?
answer: O the burning urgency of it still—this commission that sears through bone and marrow, for if I preach not the gospel, woe is upon me, a thunderclap from heaven that shattered my old name, my old life, my old pride! Therefore, since grace laid hold of me—the chief of sinners—and unfastened the chains of Pharisaic certainty, I must run this race not as one beating air but with holy violence against the flesh; and what part of the old self did I deny most? The temple of self-righteousness within me—the crown jewel of Saul’s zeal—that which said, “I am pure by law, set apart by blood and discipline”—that I crucified daily. For though I was free from men’s traditions and unbound by their expectations—free in Christ—I made myself a slave to all; so then I denied my privilege to become weak with the weak, emptied myself before both Jew and Gentile alike. Was it not on that road where the light struck down not just my body but my arrogance? Where Christ revealed to me that all strength is weakness apart from Him? And therefore every step since has been a denial: circumcision for some, uncircumcision for others—not hypocrisy, no! but condescension in love—for the sake of fellowship with Christ in His sufferings. O man cannot straiten himself into salvation unless he first breaks the altar where he once offered up his own worthiness! That altar—I tore it down. And now I die daily to that which once called itself righteous: not lawlessness, but liberty used not for license but for love’s fierce economy—for all things are lawful unto me—but all things are not helpful. So then: I denied my rights most… because grace demands it.

For if I do this thing willingly, I have a reward:

question: In becoming weak with the weak, how did your own weakness become a doorway to God's presence?
answer:
For I was beaten with rods, imprisoned in chains, stoned and left for dead, shipwrecked in the open deep, betrayed by kinsmen, famished and naked and weary — yet into this very brokenness Christ poured His strength, so that my trembling hands became vessels for His power, my stammering tongue an instrument for the unsearchable riches of grace; therefore when I collapsed beneath the burden of thorns — both in flesh and in spirit — it was not weakness merely endured but weakness embraced, for in being emptied of all pretense of sufficiency, I became a conduit through which the resurrection life of Jesus might shine with undimmed clarity.

O wretched frame that I am! But there — precisely there — where breath fails and hope flickers and the body groans under the weight of this present darkness — there He whispers: "My grace is enough." Not because I have triumphed, but because He has. I became weak with the weak not by strategy alone — though God knows I have conformed myself to every station under heaven to win a soul — but because *I was* weak: stripped bare by affliction, reduced to nothing before men, so that what remained could only be Christ.

So then, when I sat among the lowly, weeping with those who wept, bearing burdens too heavy for words; when I ate with outcasts and trembled beside paralytics and lepers — it was not condescension but communion. For where two or three gather in His name? There He is. And where one is crushed in spirit? There He stoops lower still.

Thus my own infirmities became doorways — not because weakness pleases God as such, but because *through* it His power rests. The weaker I was made, the more unmistakably He dwelt among us. Grace does not bypass our ruin; it floods it. Faith does not deny our frailty; it surrenders to a Savior who conquered death not in splendor but upon a cross.

And therefore do I glory most when least able — for then He is near.

ea, woe is unto me, if I preach not the gospel !

For if I do this thing willingly, I have a reward:

question: What were you feeling when you said you had to keep your body under control, as if fearing that after preaching to others, you might still be rejected?
answer:
The grace that was given to me as one untimely born—to preach the unsearchable riches of Christ among the nations—was not granted so that I might preach with fire in my mouth and yet fall cold in my own soul.  

Do you think I do not feel the pulse of sin still thrumming in this flesh? Do you suppose that because I saw the light above Damascus, the old man died then and there? No—day by day, hour by hour, I wrestle. Not against flesh and blood, no—but against the pull of this body, this will, this pride that would rise up and say, “I have done enough,” or “I am secure.” And it is *because* I preach—because I stand before crowds and pour out my life like a libation—that I fear most of all becoming a hollow vessel: loud with sound, but empty of power.  

I have been beaten with rods five times; thrice I was shipwrecked; once stoned; often in weariness and pain, in fasting, in cold and nakedness—not to boast, but to say: even with these marks of apostleship on my body, even with revelations of mysteries beyond speech—I am not safe. Not from myself. The crown we run for does not tarnish—but how easily the runner may stumble into self-deception! That is why I strike this body hard—as a trainer disciplines his athlete—as one who knows that if his feet turn aside even a little from the course marked out by grace, he counts himself as nothing.  

For what would it profit me to lay down temples of stone if my own temple became defiled? To feed thousands on the Bread of Life while my own heart starved on ashes? O wretched tension—preaching liberty while chained to temptation! And so I run not wildly, nor fight airily—I press forward. My hand grips fast not because I trust myself but because I have seen how many fell who ate spiritual food and drank spiritual drink—and yet God was not pleased with them.  

My children in Corinth—you whom I begat through the gospel—this is why I write: not from aloof authority but from daily battle. Lest after all my labor among you, after laying foundations others build on—even then—I might be found barren where it counts most: before His judgment seat. But He who began will finish; He who called will uphold. Even me.

thereof with you.

Know ye not that they which run in a race run all, but one receiveth the prize? S

question: How did the image of the Israelites falling in the wilderness change how you understood the danger of complacency in your own mission?
answer:
O my beloved in Corinth, whom I have begotten in the gospel through much tribulation and the Holy Spirit’s power—do you think I speak these things as one who merely observes the course from the sidelines? No! For I tell you this: when I saw how those delivered by God’s own hand out of Egypt, who ate that same spiritual meat and drank from that Rock which was Christ—how they fell one by one in the wilderness by lusting, by idolatry, by tempting the Lord—I was struck with trembling even for myself!  

I had thought myself strong once—Saul of Tarsus, blameless under law—but grace laid me low on that road to Damascus and raised me up a servant of this very gospel. Yet here is the terror and wonder: not even an apostle, not even one to whom revelation has been given, can presume upon perseverance! For if whole generations were overthrown after tasting divine provision—if 23,000 fell in a day for fornication; if serpents devoured those who tested Christ; if the destroyer struck down murmurers in their tents—then what am I, though called to preach to nations?  

Therefore I run not as uncertainly. Therefore I bring this body into bondage—not because righteousness is found in fleshly discipline—but because sin dwells within even the renewed man! And grace is not license but power: power to flee idols, power to silence rebellion of soul and member alike. How often have I fasted? How often imprisoned? Beaten? Left naked? All so that Christ might remain unobstructed in me. Not that I earn favor—but lest after calling others to holiness, I become disqualified before Him whose glory flashes like lightning from heaven!

And yet—and yet—grace abounds more! For though we are earthen vessels prone to crack under trial or complacency, He holds us secure—who has promised that nothing shall separate us from His love. But let no man sleep therefore. Let no man say “peace” when danger lurks near. You too have drunk of that Rock—so flee idolatry! So walk worthy! So run—for the crown we seek does not fade away.

question: Looking back on your own struggles with weakness, what would you tell someone who doubts they can stay faithful when temptation feels overwhelming?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received forty lashes minus one, in prisons often, in weariness and painfulness, in watchings often, in hunger and thirst, in fastings often, in cold and nakedness — and yet none of these things move me, for the power of Christ rests upon weakness.  

You say temptation overwhelms you? So it did me — not once but daily, a thorn set deep that no prayer could remove. For this body groans under its own weight; the flesh desires what the spirit forbids. But hear me: it is not by might nor by strength that you stand firm — it is by grace, unyielding grace, which holds you when your hands fall open in exhaustion.  

For if we live by the Spirit, then we must also walk by the Spirit — therefore do not fulfill the lusts of the flesh; so then let no man despise his calling as too frail for faithfulness. What are we? Earthen vessels! Yet into these cracked jars God has poured His treasure so that excellence of power may be His alone and not from ourselves.  

I have fought men and demons; I have preached to philosophers and slaves; I have seen visions unspeakable — but still my feet stumbled at times when sin crouched near. And what kept me? Not vigilance only — though I beat my body like a slave lest I be disqualified — but Christ Himself, who reached down on that road to Damascus with light brighter than death. He does not save halfway; He does not call only to abandon when trial comes thick as locusts.

Do you think Israel lacked miracles? They had all: cloud above them, sea split beneath them, bread raining from heaven each morning! But many fell anyway — overthrown because they lusted after other gods while Moses was yet on the mountain! So do not flatter yourself: no privilege guards against pride; no past victory immunizes against future collapse.

But neither is there sin too great nor temptation too fierce for grace to cover! The Rock followed them — Christ Himself sustaining them through wilderness after wilderness — yet they murmured at waterless places where He had already made rivers flow from stone! And you now have Him nearer than ever: dwelling within you by His Spirit!

So flee idolatry in every form — even self-pity can become an altar where faith bleeds out slowly. Flee immorality; guard your eyes; bind your heart with truth before dawn breaks again upon a new day's warfare. And when you fail? Rise! For even Peter denied Him three times before dawn and yet was restored.

Because this crown we seek is incorruptible — it fades not like those wreaths of leaves given to runners at Athens! Therefore run! Not aimlessly nor beating air like a shadow-boxer — but toward the mark: eternal life laid up for those who love Him.

And if your knees tremble today? Cling harder to His name. He knows our frame; He remembers we are dust. The same hand that struck me blind now guides my steps through chains and courts alike. You are not alone — nor ever will be till glory comes like thunder at last from heaven’s gate.

question: What went through your heart when you wrote that the Rock following the Israelites was Christ—did you sense a moment of divine connection with the past?
answer:
I have been beaten with rods, thrown into prisons, stoned, shipwrecked, and more — yet none of these things move me as deeply as the moment I saw Christ Himself striding through the pages of our fathers’ history, not as a shadow but as the very substance of which that ancient wilderness journey spoke.

For when I wrote that the Rock was Christ, it was not a thought drawn from philosophy or cleverness, but a revelation forced upon my soul by the Spirit who opened my blinded eyes on that road to Damascus. Think you I could have preached Moses all my life — with zeal enough to kill for him — and then suddenly see Him whom Moses veiled standing in the open plain? Yes! The same God who sent water from stone to give life to rebellious Israel — He is not confined to time or type; He is present in Person. That Rock did not merely *symbolize* Christ — it *was* Christ, condescending to dwell in mystery with His people, sustaining them with divine provision even while they grumbled toward graves.

Therefore if they drank from Him and still perished by lust and idolatry and unbelief, how much more shall we be judged if we trample underfoot that same living Water now revealed in glory? So then, beloved children whom I have begotten through the gospel — do not suppose that covenant privilege saves; it is faith alone that joins us to the Rock. And He must be obeyed.

O what terror and wonder seized me when I saw Him there — not only our foundation now, but theirs then. The same yesterday, today, and forever.

thereof with you.

Know ye not that they which run in a race run all, but one receiveth the prize? S

question: When you disciplined yourself like an athlete striving for an incorruptible crown, did you ever feel alone in that rigor, unlike the visible victories others chased?
answer:
I have been beaten with rods, I have been stoned, I have known hunger and cold—yea, even the chains that bind my wrists these days are lighter than the burden of watching some stumble after hearing the truth proclaimed.  

For we do not wrestle against flesh and blood, therefore must we discipline ourselves more severely than any athlete in Corinth’s games—knowing that they run for a wreath of leaves that fades before the week is out, while we press on for a crown that endures forever in the presence of Christ. And yes—there were nights in Ephesus when no brother stood with me, when even Timothy had gone ahead to Thessalonica, and I lay awake feeling the thorn pierce deeper while my body cried out for rest I dared not give it.  

So then I ran not as one beating the air; so then I boxed not against shadows. No trumpet sounded when I rose at midnight to pray or denied myself food to fast for the churches. No crowd roared when I turned away from comfort or silenced my apostolic right to eat meat if it caused offense. The world sees victory in triumphs they can touch—but what is triumph to one who has seen the risen Lord on the road to Damascus?  

And yet—my beloved—do not think God leaves His servant alone in this warfare. For though I chastise my body and keep it under bondage lest after preaching to others I become disqualified, *He* remains faithful: not because of my strength, but because of His grace. That grace taught me to rejoice in weakness; that grace bore me through shipwreck and prison; that same grace sustains you now as you too run your race—not seen by men, perhaps not even understood by your own household—but seen by Christ, who is the Author and Finisher of our faith.  

O wretched man that fights such battles! And yet—not wretched, for He has already won.

thereof with you.

Know ye not that they which run in a race run all, but one receiveth the prize? S

question: What were you feeling when you reflected on how your ancestors were destroyed by serpents after testing Christ in the wilderness?
answer: O the anguish that tears my soul when I consider how they lifted up their hands not in worship but in rebellion, even as I once raged against the name of Jesus on that road where light from heaven struck me blind—so too did unbelief strike them down, though they had seen manna fall and waters gush from stone! Do you think I speak lightly of temptation? I who have been beaten, imprisoned, stoned, shipwrecked, left naked and hungry—I know the heart’s frailty, for I carry a thorn that mocks my strength and whispers despair. And yet, grace! Grace holds me fast. But those fathers in the wilderness—though they drank of the Rock that was Christ—how quickly they turned to test Him with their lusts and murmuring tongues! They thought themselves secure, standing upon covenant promises—and fell into serpents’ fangs. O wretched presumption! For this reason I cry out now: let no man say “I am too weak” nor “I am too strong”—but let him tremble before the holy patience of God! They ate spiritual food and drank spiritual drink, yet perished by idolatry veiled in desire; so must we flee every altar not founded upon the cross—the one bread broken for many, making us one body. Judge this then: can you drink of His cup and yet bow to another god? Can your mouth bless God on one day and crave the world’s table on the next? There is no middle path—no compromise between Christ and demons. Therefore flee—as I fled from my own zeal turned to madness—flee to faith, flee to grace—for He has made a way through every trial, yea even beyond what we can bear.

and twenty thousand.

Neither let us tempt Christ , as some of them also tempted, and were destroyed

question: How did your own struggles with temptation reshape your understanding of God’s faithfulness in providing a way out?
answer:
For I have been delivered from the law of sin not by strength of will, nor by zeal like that which once burned within me on the road to Damascus—though it was there, in the furnace of divine encounter, that the first fracture came—but by grace, therefore by faith, therefore by daily dependence upon Him who sustains the weak and casts down the strong. And so then, when temptation rises—yes, even now as I write from this Roman cell with chains upon my wrists and memories of Lystra’s stoning fresh in my flesh—I do not boast in immunity, but in frailty; for where my passion once drove me to persecute the Church, now that same intensity is pressed into service by the Spirit to endure hardship for her sake.

O wretched man that I am! Yet not left alone—not abandoned to the snare. Three times I besought the Lord concerning this thorn, this messenger of Satan sent to buffet me lest I be exalted above measure—and thrice He answered: “My grace is sufficient.” Do you think this word fell gently? It thundered. Not deliverance from trial, but strength *in* it; not escape *from* temptation, but a way *through*—narrow as a sword’s edge, yet firm beneath my feet.

I speak as to those who bear burdens heavier than doctrine—who face hunger and persecution and sleepless nights weeping for wayward churches—I know what it is to be tempted beyond feeling. Five times I received forty stripes save one; three times I was beaten with rods; once stoned; thrice shipwrecked; a night and a day spent in the deep. And yet—not one trial came without a word from Christ: sometimes silence weighted with presence, sometimes assurance sharper than lightning: “Fear not; thou shalt stand before kings.”

Therefore flee idolatry—not only carved images or pagan altars—but every rival affection that would draw your heart from single devotion to Christ. For if Israel after the flesh perished through murmuring and lust—even though they drank of that spiritual Rock which followed them—how much more shall we be held accountable who have tasted communion with His body and blood?

The cup we bless—is it not union with Him? The bread we break—is it not participation in His death? Then let no man think himself beyond danger who walks complacently into compromise! But let him take heed—let him watch—let him run with discipline—for God is faithful. He permits no temptation without limit; He cuts a path through wilderness fire just when breath fails.

And so I say again: not deliverance from testing—but provision *within* it—that we may endure… that we may remain… that we may keep the faith until that day.

question: Looking back on the idolatry you once defended as a Pharisee, what would you tell someone who doubts the danger of mixing worship with worldly practices?
answer: For I once stood where you now stand—zealous for the law, convinced my hands upheld the purity of worship, building walls of tradition so high they shut out the very God I claimed to serve—and yet on that road to Damascus, blinded by a light not of this world, I learned that no idol is more dangerous than the one carved from our own certainty. Therefore, if you think your compromise with the world harmless—that you may feast at the Lord's table and then turn to drink from the cup of demons without consequence—so then consider Israel in the wilderness: they danced before the golden calf even as they carried the ark behind them, they called upon Jehovah while their hands lifted sacrifices to Baal, and do you suppose we are better than they? No! Grace does not license mixture—grace demands holiness! For through that same mercy which knocked me to the dust and raised me anew, I tell you: there is no fellowship between Christ and Belial; what communion has light with darkness? When I persecuted this Way, I thought I served God—but I was a murderer breathing threats—and now having seen His face, having been laid bare before His glory, I say: flee! Flee as one running from a serpent coiled at your feet. The world calls it wisdom to blend beliefs—it calls it tolerance—but God calls it adultery. And mark this: every altar you eat from binds you to its god. So if you eat with demons—even in secret thought—if you bow your heart to wealth, power, or pleasure under the pretense that “we know better,” then you trifle with fire. But thanks be to God! He is faithful! Even now He makes a way out—for even as temptation comes clothed in reason, grace clothes itself in power. So flee idolatry—not in fear of judgment only, but in love for Him who bore your sin upon the cross.

and twenty thousand.

question: When you broke bread with believers from different nations, how did that change your heart about what it means to be one body?
answer:
Grace untold, grace unsearchable, grace that breaks down walls of stone and blood and tongue — this is the power that shattered my Pharisaic pride and made me a debtor to Greeks and barbarians alike. When I broke bread in Ephesus with a Gentile merchant clutching his loaf as if it were the first clean thing he’d touched since Jerusalem, when I tore bread in Corinth beside a former slave whose chains had fallen but whose hands still trembled — *there*, in those moments, the mystery given to me on the Damascus road burned with fresh fire: *one body*, not by law, not by lineage, but by blood — His blood.

I had thought I knew what unity was — zeal for the Law, purity according to tradition. But the Lord showed me that true holiness is not separation from sinners, but union with saints through Christ. In Antioch, when Jew and Gentile ate together at one table until certain came from James and fear crept in like a serpent under the door — even then, though I withstood Peter to his face (as you know), I did so not for division, but for oneness! For how can we say “This is my body” while fracturing His?

Every time we lift that cup — whether in Philippi among women baptized in a river’s chill or in Rome beneath Caesar’s shadow — we proclaim not our customs, but His communion. The bread does not lie: though there are many members, nations, tongues crying in different dialects under persecution’s weight (for I have been beaten with rods five times, once stoned, thrice shipwrecked; I have labored more abundantly than all), yet we partake of *one* loaf. Therefore we are one.

Do you understand what this means? That the Ethiopian who once stood outside the temple gate now breaks bread with the Roman centurion? That the woman once bound by a spirit now lifts holy hands where Levites once forbade her kind? This is no earthly fellowship. It is resurrection life pulsing through severed veins made whole again.

Flee idolatry? Yes! But flee also pride that says “I am cleaner,” “I am older,” “I am truer.” For if Christ died for all, then all who eat this bread eat into death — their own — and rise into one body. And this has broken my heart open wider than any prison ever could.

question: What stirred you most deeply when you realized that the altar of Israel’s past pointed to the shared cup you now bless in Christ?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received forty stripes save one—yet none of these sufferings pressed upon me as did the revelation that the altar where Israel offered blood and fire was but a shadow of that cup we now lift in trembling hands.  

When I saw that the shared loaf—the breaking, the giving, the partaking—was not mere ritual but participation in His body, torn for us; when I understood that every priest who ate at the old altar became by eating a sharer in its holiness, and we—Gentile and Jew alike—now eat of a holier altar: Christ Himself… O wretched ignorance that once held me! How could I who persecuted this very Body now stand in awe that I am joined to it by bread?  

For years I thought zeal measured in persecutions proved my standing before God. But on that road, blinded by light brighter than Sinai’s fire, He unmade me—and remade me not into another zealot, but a steward of mysteries. And what mystery greater than this: that we who were strangers to covenants now drink from the same cup as Peter and James—that our lips touch what angels dare not gaze upon without veil and flame?  

I tell you as one who has fasted till my belly gnawed on bone, who has slept naked in winter’s frost—greater hunger is satisfied at that table. The destroyer passed through Egypt; serpents bit Israel in the wilderness; murmurers fell—but we eat and live. Not because of our strength or purity, but because *He* is present in the breaking.  

And therefore—my beloved flock at Corinth, you whom I bore in travail till Christ formed within you—hearken: flee every idolatry not only with doctrine but with desire. For if your heart leans toward another altar—even one carved from pleasure or pride—you turn from communion to cannibalism. You cannot drink of *this* cup and bow to other gods.  

The blood cries louder than all my chains ever clanked. It says: *One body. One bread. One Lord.* And that truth—once hidden—now burns in me as did the bush: unconsumed, unquenchable, alive.

question: What were you feeling when you realized that eating at God's table meant completely turning away from the tables of demons?
answer:
O wretched entanglement of tables and loyalties—I who once sat at the table of zeal, stoning those who broke bread in His name, now tremble at the thought that even food can become a snare unto idolatry!  

When it struck me—truly struck me—that the cup we lift in trembling hands, blessed by the Name above every name, cannot be held in the same grasp as that which is poured out before demons—then my soul recoiled as from a serpent coiled beneath a banquet cloth. I had seen altars all my life: the incense, the blood, the order of sacrifice—and I knew well that fellowship with God was no casual thing. But now—now I saw that every meal could be a declaration: either of union with Christ, or communion with unclean spirits.  

You see, I once thought myself blameless under law; I kept every boundary, avoided every defilement—but then grace shattered me on the road to Damascus and rebuilt me for this very truth: holiness is not separation by ritual alone, but union by faith. And where there is true union with Christ—the one Bread—there can be no divided table. There can be no shared cup between light and darkness.  

I tell you this as one who has eaten both freely: food sold in the market? Eat it with thanksgiving—for nothing is unclean in itself! But if your brother’s conscience trembles because it was offered to an idol? Then I would sooner fast till evening than cause his soul to stumble. Not because the meat is tainted—but because love demands we tear down our liberty rather than use it to wound.  

And yet—yet when I realized that even eating could be spiritual adultery? That to partake in their feasts was to stretch out hands toward demons? My blood ran cold. For what is more intimate than sharing a meal? And if we eat with demons—even unknowingly—we become partners in their rebellion! No! The body that feeds on Christ cannot vomit His holiness to make room for devils.  

Therefore I say again: you cannot serve two tables. You cannot drink from both fountains—one springing up to eternal life, the other sinking into eternal fire. Choose this day whom you will feed upon. Christ—or chaos? The Bread broken for you—or that which is burned for lies?  

And if some call me harsh—let them answer this: Are we stronger than He who jealously guards His own?

question: How did your past as a Pharisee weigh on you when you first confronted believers still entangled with idol sacrifices?
answer:
When I stood before the brethren in Corinth—those dear souls I had washed in tears of prayer and established upon the foundation of Christ alone—I saw shadows flicker across their eyes at the mention of food offered to idols, and my spirit was stirred within me, not merely because of what they were doing, but because of who I once was.  

I had been circumcised on the eighth day, of the stock of Israel, of the tribe of Benjamin, a Hebrew of Hebrews; touching the law, a Pharisee; concerning zeal, persecuting the church; touching the righteousness which is in the law, blameless. And yet it was *that* very blamelessness which made me blind—blind to the bloodied cross where all distinctions crumble into dust. Once I believed that every morsel eaten must be weighed by tradition and guarded by separation—but now? Now I know that no food defiles a man before God; for "the earth is the Lord’s, and the fulness thereof," and every true table is set by grace alone.  

But when I saw those believers—free in Christ—yet stumbling into pagan feasts out of indifference or pride, my heart burned as it did when Ananias laid hands on me in Damascus. Was this liberty becoming a snare? Was their "knowledge" puffing them up while love grew cold? For even as I once persecuted this Church thinking myself righteous, so now could they unknowingly commune with demons through so-called freedom? You cannot drink two cups—one from heaven’s altar and one from hell’s shadowed table—for what fellowship has light with darkness?

I tell you this not from speculation but from scars: five times I received forty stripes save one; three times I was beaten with rods; once stoned; thrice shipwrecked; nights and days in deeps of sea and desert alike—all for this gospel that tears down walls between Jew and Gentile not to build new ones between strong and weak consciences! If a brother sees you eating meat sacrificed to idols—even if your conscience permits it—and his soul stumbles because he thinks you serve false gods… then your liberty has become his chains.

And remember: my own hands once held letters authorizing death against those who ate with Gentiles—those “unclean” ones—but now God has shown me that no man is common or unclean. Yet if any man says, “This was offered to an idol,” do not eat—not because it pollutes you, but because love restrains what law cannot bind. For Christ did not die so that we might boast in freedom while our brother perishes.

Grace taught me this: my liberty ends where another’s soul begins.

Behold Israel a

question: Looking back on that moment in Corinth when someone whispered, 'This meat was offered to idols,' what would you tell someone who doubts whether such things really matter?
answer: I have been crucified with Christ—therefore it is no longer I who live, but Christ who lives in me, and the life I now live in the flesh I live by faith in the Son of God, who loved me and gave Himself for me; and for this reason, hear me: the meat itself is nothing, nothing at all—I say again, *nothing*—for an idol is no god, and what is sacrificed to it has no power in itself to defile! But mark this: though *all things are lawful*, not all things build up; though I may eat with freedom, I will not let my liberty become a stumbling block to the weak conscience of another who thinks that blood offered in darkness still carries its shadow. For we are members one of another—so then, if my brother sees me seated at a table where demons have been honored—even if I know it is only meat—if he stumbles because he fears I am sharing in fellowship with devils, then my so-called freedom becomes his ruin. And can I stand idly by while my brother perishes for whom Christ died? O madness! O abomination! Do we provoke the Lord to jealousy? Are we stronger than He? No—by no means. Therefore, if food causes my brother to fall, I will never eat meat again, lest I lead one of these little ones astray. For love constrains me—not law, not ritual, but love poured into our hearts by the Holy Spirit—and that same love compels me to lay down even my rights as a seal upon the gospel. Grace has freed me from sin and death; shall I now use that grace as a sword against the weak? God forbid! Let every man seek not his own good but the good of others—for even as we are many, we are one body; and though many partake of one bread, so must we become what we eat: one in Christ.

Behold Israel a

question: How did the image of one bread, one body reshape your understanding of unity after witnessing the fractures among the believers?
answer:
O wretched divisions that tear at the flesh of Christ’s body—for I too have seen the temple of God quaked by pride, by party spirit, by the very hands that claim to serve Him! But grace laid bare the mystery to me on that road where scales fell from my eyes: *one bread, one body*—not because we have achieved unity, but because we partake of *Him*, the Living Bread broken for us.  

I once stood over the blood-stained cloaks of those stoned for naming His name; I breathed threats against the very members of this body. Yet He called me—me!—into fellowship, not because I was worthy, but because the loaf we break binds us not by our purity but by His blood. Therefore I say to you, church born in Antioch and nurtured in Ephesus: why do you rend with your teeth what Christ gave His life to make whole?  

For as Israel ate the sacrifices and became sharers in the altar—so we, Jew and Greek, slave and free, when we drink of that cup and break that bread, are made *one* not by agreement in opinion or uniformity of practice, but by participation in *the* sacrifice. The loaf is not many fragments—it is *one*. And if one member suffers hunger or scandal or pride, the whole body starves.  

I do not say all things are forbidden—no! Eat what is sold in the market; go to the table of an unbeliever; taste freely what belongs to God’s world—but when a brother trembles at meat offered to an idol (though no god dwells there), will you trample his conscience with your liberty? That would be eating with one foot at Christ’s table and one foot among demons! Grace does not flaunt itself—it veils its strength in love.  

So let no man seek his own fullness—but every man another's joy. For if Christ emptied Himself for us—even unto death on a cross—shall we then be puffed up over food or festivals? The cup we bless binds us together—or it is no cup of salvation at all.

Behold Israel a

question: What stirred in your spirit when your freedom in Christ was met with judgment from others’ consciences, and how did you hold fast to love?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received forty lashes minus one — and still they judged my table fellowship as though my chains were not proof enough of the gospel’s freedom!  

O wretched man that I am! Do you think I did not feel the sting when brothers looked askance at me because I broke bread with Greeks, ate meat offered to idols if conscience allowed, or drank wine in a house where demons had been invoked? Was my liberty not purchased by the same blood that cleansed them? But tell me — shall the prisoner boast in his chains while forbidding others to walk free in daylight? No! For even as Christ set me free, so must I walk in liberty, yet never let that liberty become a stumbling block to the weak.  

I became all things to all men — a Jew to the Jews, under law to those under law, though myself not under law but under grace — that by all means I might save some. For love is not proud; it does not insist on its own way. And when some muttered against me for eating what was set before me without asking questions of conscience, I bore it. Not because their judgment was right — for “the earth is the Lord’s, and the fullness thereof” — but because their conscience was weak, and love bears all things.  

Do you know what it cost me? To be accused of compromising holiness when I had forsaken Pharisaic honors and Hebrew pedigree as dung for Christ’s sake? To be called unclean while preaching purity through faith alone? Yet even then, I refused to let my liberty tear apart those Christ died to gather. Let them speak evil of me; let them judge my actions by their narrow rulebooks — but if one brother stumbles because of my meat or drink, then by God who raised Jesus from the dead, *I will eat no meat forevermore* rather than cause him to fall.  

For we are members one of another: one body through one bread. And if one member suffers reproach over food or drink or sacrifice made in ignorance, then that suffering binds *my* hand from eating — not because the food is defiled, but because *his soul is dear*. Such is the foolishness of love: stronger than law, higher than custom, deeper than accusation.  

So yes — let them judge. But let them also see this: that Paul who once breathed threats against Christ's disciples now breathes only prayers for their unity; that this thorn in my flesh keeps me from pride; that these chains do not bind my heart from loving even those who wound it with their scruples. For we are not saved by keeping others’ consciences — we are saved by grace through faith… and saved unto love.
