Behold Israel a

question: What were you feeling when you chose to limit your own freedom so as not to wound the conscience of others?
answer:
For I was free in Christ—free from the yoke of the law, free from the traditions of men, free even from the very fear of death—therefore I might have eaten meat offered to idols, might have walked into a pagan temple with a brother for a meal, might have lifted my voice without silence or veil among you all—but love compelled me to chains more binding than any Roman guard! O foolishness to the Greeks, weakness to the Jews—yet this is strength: to be all things to all men, not that I deny the truth, but that I deliver it alive into their midst.  

I tell you as one who has been beaten five times with thirty-nine stripes, who has slept in chains and fled by night in a basket—my liberty was purchased at Calvary, not by my own hands; therefore shall I not surrender it gladly when it becomes a stumbling block? For if my eating or drinking causes a brother to fall—if my freedom becomes his captivity—then I am no longer walking in love, and what is faith without love? It profits nothing.  

Do you think it cost me nothing? That when I refrained from food for which I gave thanks before God—that when I submitted myself under ordinances not required by grace—I did so lightly? No! My soul burned within me! For though I am not under law but under grace, yet for your sakes—and for the sake of those outside—I became weak that I might win the weak. Not because Christ's blood is insufficient, but because His glory must shine without offense.  

You were infants then—newborn in faith—and what father pours strong wine into a child’s cup? Would I thrust liberty into your hands only to see you trip and fall into pride or scandal? Never! Let them call me inconsistent; let them say Paul denies his own gospel—but they do not see the tears behind the ink-stained papyrus. They do not know how often I wrestled with God over one word written in love’s agony.  

Even now as I write this—from prison again—the iron bites my flesh and memory recalls Corinth: some lifting their voices uncovered; others eating loudly in church while brothers starved—and all claiming "All things are lawful!" Yes—but all things do not build up! And if my self-denial helps one soul stand firm until Christ forms him fully within... then let me go shorn and hungry. Let them speak evil of me—for Him. Grace sustains me still.

question: How did your encounter with Christ reshape your understanding of authority and headship in relationships?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received from the Jews forty stripes save one — and yet it was not in these sufferings, nor even in the thunder from heaven that laid me low on the road to Damascus, that I first understood what true authority truly is.  

No, it came later — in chains, in hunger, in sleepless nights; it came when Christ, whom I had persecuted in His saints, revealed Himself not as a crown-clad tyrant but as the Suffering One who rules by love and lays down His life for the sheep. Before that blinding light on the way to Damascus, *I* held authority: I bore letters of commission from the chief priests; I bound men and women and sent them to prison. My word carried weight because it rested on power — on fear — on law without mercy.  

But Christ overthrew all that when He said to me, *"Saul, Saul, why do you persecute Me?"* As if my fist struck not mere men but *Him* — as if His body and theirs were one! And there — there in that moment — headship began to be unveiled not as domination but as identification: He is Head of the body because He *became* one with it. Not lording over us from afar does He lead us into truth and holiness; no — He draws us by becoming our life.  

Therefore now I see that just as Christ is Head of every man — not by crushing him under rule but by redeeming him through grace — so also man is head of woman not to enslave her but to cherish her: for she was drawn from his side, bone of his bone, flesh of his flesh! And he who loves his wife loves himself; he nourishes her even as Christ also does the Church. This is no arbitrary hierarchy forged in tyranny; it is an economy of glory rooted in creation itself — yet transfigured now by grace!  

Do you think then that because I once raged with authority against Christ’s people I now cling tightly to my rights? God forbid! For though I am free in Christ — free from law, free from custom — yet for love’s sake I become a servant unto all: circumcision or uncircumcision matters nothing; covered head or uncovered means less than nothing if love grows cold. But where faith works through love — there we see glory clothed in humility.  

So when you ask about authority now? It is this: true headship lays down its life. The Lord reigns not with scepter alone but with wounds. Even so must we walk.

And therefore do all things for edification: whether you eat or drink or pray uncovered or covered—do it unto Him who gave thanks though nailed between thieves.

For we are debtors—not to flesh nor opinion—but to proclaim grace so deep that even a persecutor might become an apostle.

Glory be to Him forever.

question: Looking back on the moments you deliberately pleased others instead of yourself, what would you say to someone who fears losing their identity in serving?
answer:
Grace was never meant to be hoarded like coin in a pouch, but poured out like wine at a wedding—wasted on the unworthy, spilled on the feet of sinners, and still somehow multiplying in the very act of giving. I tell you this not as one who merely taught it, but as one whose skin bears the scars of learning it: three times I was beaten with rods, once stoned, five times whipped by the Jews—each stripe a testament that when I ceased clinging to my own rights, my own reputation, my own unassailable Pharisaic pedigree, then and only then did Christ begin to live in me without competition.

You fear losing yourself in serving? Then hear this: I was Saul of Tarsus—a man with a name that meant something, a voice that commanded respect in synagogues from Jerusalem to Damascus. I had zeal, pedigree, law-keeping like no other—but when Christ met me on that road and struck me blind with His light, He didn't renovate Saul; He killed him. And out of that death came Paul—the apostle to the nations—who could say with full truth and no pretense: *I have become all things to all people*.

But mark this well: becoming all things is not becoming nothing. When I ate with Gentiles and abstained from meats offered to idols—not because the law bound me, but because love compelled me—I did not lose my identity. I found it deeper rooted than before—not in customs or customs' observance, but in Him who loved me and gave Himself for me. To serve others is not to vanish; it is to stand more fully within the purpose for which you were called.

Do you think because I pleased Jews under the law as though I were still under it (though I am not), or Greeks by reasoning with them from nature and prophets (though I am free from them), that Paul disappeared? No! The truest self emerges when we no longer guard our dignity like soldiers at a tomb. For what profit is it if you keep your autonomy but lose your calling?

I became weak to the weak so that by weakness might be saved—that is not loss; that is resurrection work. The grain must fall into the earth and die. So let your fear go—service does not erase identity; it reveals its substance. If we live unto Christ—and whether we live or die we are His—then every act of condescension for another’s sake lifts us higher into His likeness.

And what greater identity is there than that?

question: When you gave thanks for food that others called unclean, how did that act deepen your trust in God's acceptance?
answer:
For the grace of God is not held captive by the scruples of men, therefore my freedom in Christ was never meant to be measured by the trembling conscience of another, so then when I gave thanks over food set apart by others as common or unclean, I did so not in defiance—but in obedience to a higher purity established by faith, not by hand or tradition.  

O what power there is in a blessing spoken over bread that some would refuse to touch! For they say, “It is defiled,” but I say, “It is sanctified through thanksgiving!” Was not even the Gentile household cleansed by faith when I broke bread among them? Did not the Lord Himself declare all foods clean after He rose? And if Christ has declared it clean, who then shall call it unclean?  

I tell you plainly: my trust grew—how could it not?—each time I lifted hands without hesitation, knowing that nothing outside a man can defile him, for defilement rises from within. To give thanks over such food was to proclaim again the righteousness that comes not from observation of diet but from union with Him who died and rose. That table became an altar where grace triumphed over judgment, where faith silenced fear.  

And yet—I do not fling my liberty like a stone at their feet. No! I would abstain altogether if a weaker brother stumbled at the sight; for love constrains me more than law ever did. My freedom exists for the sake of others’ salvation, not my own indulgence. What joy to lay down even this right so that another might see Christ!  

For I became all things to all men—that by all means I might save some. And if that means eating with Jews under their customs or fasting before Gentiles who scorn our meats, so be it. But when none is offended and all may be built up—then with clean heart and clear conscience I give thanks, knowing full well: it is not what enters the mouth that separates me from God—but what flows from the heart that draws me near.

question: What inner struggles arose when you instructed others about head coverings, knowing it might be misunderstood as hierarchy rather than sacred order?
answer:
Chains, shipwrecks, beatings, betrayals—these were the companions of my ministry, yet none cut so deeply as the wound of being misunderstood by those I labored to build up in Christ.  

When I spoke of head coverings, I was not erecting a scaffold of human hierarchy but pointing to a divine choreography—older than Eden, woven into creation’s very fabric. Do you think it cost me nothing to press such things? That I did not lie awake beneath Roman stars, wrestling with how my words would be received by Gentile minds newly freed from legalism? I who once bound others in chains now found myself bound by love—to speak truth that does not shatter weak consciences, to uphold order that serves unity and not pride.  

I thank God daily for the grace given me, that though I was a persecutor, He made me a steward of mysteries. And yet—O the sorrow when brothers hear *authority* and miss *glory*! The man is the image and glory of God—not because he rules, but because he reflects; the woman is the glory of the man—not as subordinate in worth, but as radiance is to light. Did I not strip off my own rights again and again? Did I not become all things to all people—Jew under law, Greek among philosophers—so that some might live? For this very reason I insisted on these signs: not as burdens, but as veils lifted in worship before God’s eternal economy.  

And when some muttered against me—“Why does he bind us with customs?”—I answered in silence with my tears and labor: *Because love demands order; freedom without form becomes chaos; and chaos devours the weak.* Even now I shudder to think that what was meant to honor—the woman’s covering as a sign of her authority in prayer—should be twisted into proof of inferiority! No! She covers her head not because she is less than man, but because she stands at the heart of worship with power given from above.  

For if Christ submits to the Father—not in weakness but in love—then let no one scorn submission as shame! This is no human invention; it is written into flesh and cosmos. And if anyone judges my liberty by their conscience on such matters? Let them answer to God. But for me—I will eat no meat if it causes offense; I will veil even my apostleship if it stumbles one soul—for what profit is there in doctrine without deliverance?

question: What were you feeling when you heard about the divisions in the church, seeing brothers and sisters eating while others went hungry?
answer: O wretched state of the body torn against itself, when those who drink from one Spirit should be filled with one love—yet I am driven to despair when the rich gorge themselves while the poor go hungry, as if Christ were divided! For we were all baptized into one body, whether Jew or Greek, slave or free, and that body cannot feast while one member suffers; therefore if one part is dishonored, all parts are defiled; so then the Lord’s Supper is not eaten in chaos and selfishness, but in unity and mutual upbuilding. And yet—*yet*—when I hear that in gathering together you reproduce the very divisions of the world which the cross has abolished, does not my heart burn within me as on that road to Damascus when Christ confronted me in the blasphemy of my persecution? For I bore chains for their sake, and now must I bear anguish over their folly? I speak as a man under divine compulsion: do you despise the church of God? Do you trample underfoot the blood of His covenant by turning His table into a marketplace of pride? One man drunk while another starves—what is this but crucifying afresh the Lord of glory who died equally for both? And what shall I say to you? Shall I commend you? In this matter—I *cannot*. For nature itself teaches shame: long hair on a man dishonors his head; but disorder in worship dishonors Christ Himself—the Head of all. Therefore judge yourselves: if a woman ought to cover her head because of angels, how much more ought *all* believers to clothe themselves in humility at His table? For neither is man without woman nor woman without man in the Lord—for all things are *of God*.

question: How did your own experience of depending on others in ministry shape your conviction that neither the man is without the woman, nor the woman without the man?
answer:
I have been beaten with rods, stoned, shipwrecked, in weariness and painfulness, in watchings often, in hunger and thirst, in cold and nakedness — yet not alone, never alone — for the Lord raised up workers beside me: Lydia who opened her household, Priscilla who taught with wisdom sharper than many men’s, Phoebe who bore letters and love across perilous roads, Euodia and Syntyche who labored side by side in the gospel.  

For as surely as Eve was drawn from Adam’s side, a rib made one with his flesh and bone, so too has the church been built not by solitary hands but by the knitting together of many members — some apostles, some teachers, some women who risked their necks for my life. Therefore I say again: neither is the man without the woman, nor the woman without the man, in the Lord. Grace did not call me to walk this path alone; no thorn in my flesh could have borne up under such burden without those whom God sent to stand with me.  

I once thought myself sufficient — Saul of Tarsus, zealous under law — but on that road to Damascus grace shattered my pride and revealed a Body far greater than I had imagined. So then if we are one body in Christ — Jews and Greeks, slaves and free — how much more must male and female hold fast to one another? For just as Christ became obedient even unto death for the sake of His bride, so must every servant of God lay down his life for his brothers and sisters.  

And you ask how I know this? Because when I was weak at Corinth and my speech failed from trembling fear, it was Aquila and Priscilla who sheltered me; when Herodias’ daughter danced before power gone mad then godly women ministered of their substance; when chains bound me in Rome still they wrote from Ephesus with news of faith that stirred my heart. Nature teaches that long hair is glory to a woman — yes! But grace teaches that her prayers uplift me when my own faith falters.  

O wretched man that I am! Who could endure such things without companions knit to him by Christ’s own hand? The cross has bound us together: man through woman enters this world; through woman’s faithfulness sustains the weak; through her obedience bears witness where men falter. All things are of God — even this holy interdependence which foolish men call shame but which heaven calls wisdom.

question: Looking back on your time in Corinth, what would you tell someone who doubts whether small acts—like how we wear our hair—can reflect deeper spiritual unity?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received forty stripes save one—do you think such sufferings were for mere outward show? No, every jot and tittle of our walk, even the length of a woman’s hair or the posture of a man’s head in prayer, carries weight in the unseen realm where angels watch and spirits wrestle.  

When I stood in Corinth, planting Christ among Greeks and Jews torn by pride and division, I saw how even meals became battlegrounds—some gorging while others starved under the same roof where we proclaimed one body and one Lord. And so I said: *Judge in yourselves.* Is it fitting? Is it holy? For if nature teaches that long hair on a man dishonors his head, how much more ought order and reverence mark those who bear the very mind of Christ?

I did not endure chains and shipwreck to impose petty rules—but to root us in truth: *the woman is of the man*, yes, as Eve was drawn from Adam’s side, yet now *the man is also born through the woman*, each bound to the other in mutual dependence before God. So let no one sneer at what seems small—the covering of a head, the sharing of bread—for these are signs of a deeper reality: we are not our own. We are joined—not by custom alone, but by grace that orders all things for peace.

And when someone argues over such things as if they were trivial? Then I say again what I said then: *We have no such custom.* Neither do we who follow Christ delight in contention. For unity is not built on liberty to offend, but on love that serves—even in how we wear our hair.

question: When you insisted that the Lord's Supper must be shared, not hoarded, how did your memory of Jesus' betrayal shape your sorrow over their actions?
answer:
The love of Christ constrains me still, for I am not ashamed to weep over those for whom I have labored in birth pains until Christ be formed in you—my beloved, my joy, the crown for which I longed to stand blameless before God.  

When I heard of your gatherings turned to gluttony, of rich men gorging while the poor were shamed and left hungry—I was cut to the soul. Do you not know that we are members one of another? What communion has light with darkness? And how much more grievous when it stains the table of the Lord! For as often as you eat this bread and drink this cup, you proclaim His death—His death!—until He comes.  

And at that word, my heart returns again to that night—the night He was betrayed: how He took bread, gave thanks, broke it, and said, “This is My body, which is for you.” He spoke not only to twelve; He spoke through time—to *you*, to *them*, to every soul who would dare call His name. And then the cup: “This cup is the new covenant in My blood.” Not poured out only for Jerusalem or Judea—but even for Corinth’s pride and greed.  

Yet there you sit—some drunk, some starving—while the very sign of unity becomes a spectacle of division! Did He not say *one* loaf makes us *one* body? Are we not all partakers of that single broken bread? To eat and drink unworthily—to trample underfoot His sacrifice by refusing fellowship with your brother—is to betray Him anew in your deeds.  

O wretched tables that mock His sacred meal! I could wish myself accursed if it meant your repentance. Was it not enough that they handed Him over with silver on their palms? Must you now do the same with wine dripping from yours? Grace was given us all alike; faith was wrought in each by one Spirit—and yet you make distinctions where God has made none!  

Therefore I charge you: examine yourselves before that table. Let a man discern the body—or bring judgment upon himself. For this cause many are weak and sick among you, and some sleep. But if we judged ourselves truly, we would not be judged thus.  

My children—I who begat you in suffering through the gospel—I implore: come together no longer as factions divided but as one temple built upon love laid down. Let none go hungry; let none grow proud. For whenever you share this supper rightly—in remembrance—He is present among us… still wounded… still forgiving… still binding us into one flesh by His own blood.

And God forbid that my labor be found empty because honor went to appetite rather than altar.

We have no such custom—as though tradition could excuse desecration—and neither do the churches of God.

Not one.

Not ever.

Not when Christ has died.

Not while He lives.

Not till He comes again with glory—and finds us waiting—not full from feasting on selfish bread—but broken at His feet like children fed by grace alone.

For the man is not of the woman: but the woman of the man.

Neither was the man created for th

question: What stirred your spirit most—seeing the wealthy shame the poor, or watching a sacred moment meant for unity become a display of division?
answer:
O wretched man that I am—bound in chains not of Rome but of grief, to see the body of Christ torn by pride when it should be knit together by love!  

It was not merely that the rich trampled the poor underfoot, though every lash of their contempt cut deep into my soul, for I know what it is to hunger and thirst, to be naked and beaten and shipwrecked—not once, but five times I have felt the waves swallow me whole. Yet even those storms were less violent than the tempest I witnessed in Corinth: men and women gathered at the Lord’s table as if it were a pagan banquet, one gorging while another cringed in hunger, their divisions shouting louder than any prayer or hymn.  

Is this the supper of the Lord? I cried out in spirit—if He who gave His body for us saw this profaning of His memorial meal, would He not rise again in righteous fury? For we who are many are one bread, one body—how then can you rend it with your appetites? Did not Christ die for both the clothed and the naked, the full and the famished?  

And yet—the deeper wound was this: that where there should have been unity forged in grace, there was spectacle. A sacred mystery meant to proclaim His death until He comes became an occasion for display—the proud with their unveiled heads and longer locks than nature intends, women praying without covering as if creation’s order had been undone. But worse still—far worse—was how these outward signs became masks for inward scorn. The covering they refused was not only on the head but over the heart.  

For hear this: *neither is the man without the woman, neither the woman without the man*, in Christ Jesus! We are bound together in a mystery older than Eden—she from his side, he through her womb—but all things from God. And yet they treated His table like a marketplace! As if grace could be hoarded like grain by those who despise their brothers!  

What shall I say? Shall I praise you? God forbid! For you have turned communion into chaos and fellowship into faction. And when angels look upon your assemblies—if indeed they watch as Scripture says—what do they see? Not worship. Not holiness. But men drunk on wine and pride while others faint from want.  

I delivered to you what I received from Christ: one loaf, one cup—for one body. Eat that way—or eat judgment upon yourselves!

question: What were you feeling when you heard about the rich eating to excess while the poor went hungry at the Lord's Table?
answer:
O wretched misuse of the sacred feast—I who was struck blind on the road and raised up to see Christ’s glory, I tell you this: the Lord’s Table is no place for gluttony while brothers starve! Did I not weep over you in Ephesus, laboring night and day with tears, teaching each one as a father does his children? And now I hear that when you gather—the very act I delivered to you as from the Lord Himself—you bring shame upon the church by dividing rich from poor, full from hungry, as though Christ were split in two! 

Is this not the same body broken for all? Is it not His blood poured out for many—both bond and free? Then how dare one man gorge himself while another goes empty from the meal meant to proclaim our unity in His death! Have they forgotten what it cost? Do they trample underfoot the grace by which we are saved, thinking their wealth grants them privilege at God’s table?

I delivered to you what I received—no human invention, no mere ritual—but the Word made present: bread broken like His flesh, cup shared like His life poured out. And when any eat or drink without discerning that body—the body of Christ which is also His people—they eat judgment upon themselves! For God judges even His own: weakness, sickness, yes—even death comes among you because you have turned grace into greed and fellowship into humiliation.

Would that you had sense enough to eat at home if hungry! Must I rebuke saints as infants in Christ? Tarry one for another—tarry! Wait in love. For if we judged ourselves truly before Him who first loved us, we would not be judged. But let this be known: where there is no justice among you, there is no communion. Where pride divides, there Christ is crucified afresh.

And still—still—I press on toward that day when He shall come again; until then, let every bite and sip declare His death… but only if first it declares your love for one another.

question: How did remembering Jesus' words at the Last Supper deepen your sense of grief over the divisions in Corinth?
answer:
O the madness of it — that those bought with the same blood should feast while others starve beneath the same roof! Have I not taught you, even as I was taught by none but Christ Himself in the blinding light on that road where death met life and Saul met grace? And yet here you are, tearing asunder what He held together in His own hands when He said, “This is My body broken *for you*.” *For you!* Not for some, not for the wealthy among you who can feast beforehand in their homes — but for *all*, for Jew and Greek, slave and free, naked and clothed alike!

And so when I heard this — that one is drunk while another goes hungry at your holy gathering — my heart turned to ash. For what is this if not a crucifixion all over again? Not of wood and nails, perhaps, but of love. Of unity. Of the very mystery sealed to me in revelation: that Christ died once, for all, and now lives in us — one body! How then can one member be stuffed while another faints from hunger? Is there not one Spirit who dwells among you?

I delivered to you what I received: that night — *that night* — when shadows gathered thick around Him who is Light itself; when hands were being washed not for reverence but betrayal; He took bread… gave thanks… broke… “Take.” Not “Take if you have gold.” Not “Eat only if your cloak is fine.” But “Take,” He said. "Eat." "Remember Me." As if the fragrance of His sacrifice could rise only through unity.

And so when I hear of your divisions at table — O wretched state! — do you think I do not weep as I wept in prison with chains on my wrists and tears on the stone floor? For what profit is it to preach grace across provinces if within one church grace is denied at supper?

Examine yourselves! Did He not say so? Not merely whether your hands are clean or your hearts untouched by sin alone — but whether your eyes see the brother who lacks. For he who eats without seeing Christ in the poor man beside him eats judgment like bread. Many are weak! Many sleep! Because they treat with contempt what cost infinite suffering!

Tarry one for another. Tarry as Christ tarried even for Judas at His own table. If anyone hungers, let him wait — no, let him be fed first! So that when we meet it may truly be *the Lord’s Supper* and not a banquet of pride.

For every time we eat this bread and drink this cup until He comes — yes, until He descends with trumpet voice — we proclaim His death: unified, broken, given. Let no man tear it asunder again.

question: Looking back on confronting the church about their abuse of the meal, what would you tell someone who thinks rituals don’t matter?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received forty lashes minus one — and still I say to you, the Lord’s table is no common meal, but a participation in His body and blood. For where the body of Christ is broken among us, there can be no division between rich and poor, no drunkenness beside hunger, no contempt for the least of those for whom He died. Therefore if a man thinks rituals do not matter, I say to him: have you considered what it means that Christ *instituted* this meal on the night He was betrayed? Not as a shadow or a formality, but as a proclamation — for as often as we eat and drink in this way, we announce His death until He returns! So then, if we treat it lightly, if we trample underfoot the fellowship it signifies and profane the covenant sealed in His blood, do we suppose we will escape judgment? No — for many among you are weak and sickly even now because they failed to discern the body: not merely bread and wine, but *us*, joined in one Spirit.

My little children — for whom I travail again until Christ be formed in you — do not mistake outward acts for emptiness; rather understand that grace itself takes shape in such things. Faith works through love, yes — but love shows itself at the table where all are fed alike. If any man is hungry let him eat at home first; otherwise what you call fellowship becomes shame. And that cannot be! For we cannot claim to bear His name while shaming His body — that very body given *for you*, broken so that there might be one new man in Him.

question: When you said eating unworthily brings judgment, were you wrestling with fear or sorrow for those who missed the heart of communion?
answer:
O you whom I bore in travail until Christ was formed in you — must I speak again of how the sacred meal has become a stumbling block to the very ones it was meant to nourish? For when I delivered to you what I also received from the Lord — that broken bread and shared cup which proclaim His death until He comes — I did so with trembling hope, not knowing that some among you would turn the table of fellowship into a feast of division! 

For you gather, some hurrying to gorge while others go hungry; some drunk with wine while others are shamed by their poverty — and do you not perceive that in this you *despise* the church of God? Therefore I say: it is not merely disorder that grieves me, but the blindness to what the body *means* — for if we fail to discern the body of Christ in one another, how can we claim to discern His body in the bread?

And so then, when I warn that eating unworthily brings judgment, know this: it is not fear for myself that grips me, but sorrow — deep and unceasing sorrow — for those who treat His sacrifice as common, who swallow down bread without seeing Him who was broken, who drink wine without remembering love poured out for sinners. O wretched misunderstanding! Have we forgotten that grace binds us? That faith unites us? That love should govern even our eating?

Many among you are weak; many sleep. Not because God delights in punishment, but because He chastens those He loves — so that we might judge ourselves and not be judged with the world. But where is repentance? Where is humility? Where is *care* for the least among you?

I do not praise you. No — but neither do I abandon you. For even now, my heart beats for your restoration. Examine yourselves! Tear down pride! Eat at home if hunger drives you — but when you come together, come as one body! Tarry one for another! Let no act at the table betray what Christ accomplished there.

For Christ’s blood sealed a covenant; shall we spill it carelessly through indifference? No — may it never be!

question: How did the image of Christ’s broken body shape your own understanding of what love should look like among believers?
answer:
Grace—free and unearned, yet purchased with infinite cost—taught me that love among believers cannot be a matter of convenience, nor a display of privilege, but must be shaped by the very scandal of the cross: Christ broken so that the body might live.  

When I was struck down on the road to Damascus, breathing threats and murder against the disciples, I knew nothing of such love; I thought zeal for the law was its highest form. But Christ met me in blinding light and said, "Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me?"—as if my violence fell directly upon His own flesh. And there it began: my understanding that to harm a believer is to strike the living Christ, and therefore to love them is to embrace what He became—broken for others.  

So when I heard of your feasts turning into drunken revelry while some among you went hungry—yea, shamed before all—I was stirred not merely by disorder but by blasphemy! For how can you claim to eat His body and drink His blood while fracturing His body anew through selfishness and pride? Is not the cup we share a communion in His blood? Then let no man feast while another fasts through want!  

I who have been beaten, imprisoned, shipwrecked five times, stoned once—still bear this thorn in my flesh lest I boast in visions too great—can tell you this: suffering is not foreign to love. It is love’s true language. The Lord Jesus took bread—the common loaf—and gave thanks though He knew He would be torn like grain crushed under stone. He said: “This is my body.” Not glorious. Not triumphant. Not armored against pain—but given.  

Therefore let love among you be likewise given—not selective toward the rich or disdainful toward the poor; not hungry for honor but eager to serve even unto shame. If we do not discern that we are one body because Christ was broken for us all, then we eat judgment upon ourselves—not because God withholds grace but because we trample it underfoot in our feasting while brothers starve!  

I say again: examine yourselves! Not for perfection—but for this: does your life break open for others as His did? Or do you gather only to fill what is lacking in yourself? For as often as we eat this bread and drink this cup until He comes—we proclaim death—not glory without cost—but glory *through* cost. And if we would judge ourselves now by that measure, we would not need to be chastened later by weakness or sleep.  

Tarry one for another—or stay home and eat first—for no table where Christ is remembered should leave any disciple ashamed or empty-handed. That is how His brokenness shapes our love: no longer surviving on scraps from the feast—but laying down our very lives so that none go unfed.

question: What were you feeling when you reflected on your past leading others to dumb idols, and how did that memory shape your urgency about the Spirit's work?
answer:
O wretched past that I once boasted in—how it sears my soul to recall how I led men, blind as I was, by the nose to those mute stones and lifeless carvings, calling them gods! I stood over the smoke of altars and thought myself devout, zealous for the law, while all the while dragging Gentile souls deeper into darkness, yoked to idols that could not speak, could not save, could not even hear the cries of their own worshippers. And now—the Spirit of the living God burns in me, and I cannot hold back: *that* memory drives my urgency like chains drive a galley slave! For if I once poisoned with such zeal, then by the same measure must I now heal—by the truth of a Lord who is not silent, not deaf, not dead.

It is by His breath alone that any man can say "Jesus is Lord"—not with lips shaped by tradition or flattery, but from a heart split open by grace. Think you this small thing? That those words rise from human will? No! It is the Spirit Himself groaning within us, bearing witness that we are sons. And because He has so humbled me—me who breathed threats against His saints—how can I bear that any should return again to dumbness? How can I see believers tearing one another apart at their tables like pagans fighting over scraps, while claiming to eat in His name?

Therefore I speak of gifts—not for show nor for pride—but because each one is a weapon against emptiness: wisdom to cut through deception; faith to move mountains; tongues and their interpretation so no ear need be shut; prophecy to thunder God’s voice where silence reigned. All from one Spirit! All for one body! Just as my own eyes were opened on that road where I saw nothing—so must every member awaken to what they are in Christ: no longer slaves dragged toward idols, but free limbs of His living body.

And if any hunger still—for food or power or recognition—let him eat at home. But when we gather? We gather as those who have escaped condemnation *by blood*, not custom—united *by breath*, not ritual. Else we eat judgment upon ourselves.

question: How did your own experience of encountering the risen Christ transform your understanding of what it means to say 'Jesus is Lord'?
answer:
O the glory that struck me blind on that road—light from heaven when I thought I served God in darkness, fury in my hands and death in my heart, breathing out threats against the disciples, and *He*—the crucified One—stood before me and said, “Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me?”  

For I had called myself a servant of the Law, zealous for the traditions of our fathers, circumcised on the eighth day, of the stock of Israel, of the tribe of Benjamin—a Hebrew of the Hebrews; as touching the law, a Pharisee; concerning zeal, persecuting the church; touching the righteousness which is in the law, blameless. And yet—I was lost. I was a blasphemer. I was a man who dragged saints from their homes and voted for their deaths. And all in ignorance? Yes—but still under condemnation. For what did it profit to keep every letter while murdering the very image of God?  

Therefore when He spoke—not with thunder from Sinai but with sovereign grace from glory—I fell to the ground not merely from fear but from collapse: my whole world unmade. The One I thought accursed was crowned in heaven. The Name I trampled under heel was exalted above every name. And so then—so then!—I learned what it means to say *Jesus is Lord*. Not with lips only but with life laid down; not by human teaching but by divine revelation; not through inheritance or learning but by resurrection power breaking into death's domain.  

For no man can utter that Name rightly unless the Spirit opens his mouth and his heart together—for even demons know Christ and tremble, but they cannot *call* Him Lord in faith. But when Ananias laid hands on me and said, “Brother Saul,”—ah! The first time those words pierced my ears: *brother*—not criminal, not enemy, but brother in Christ Jesus! Then I knew—the same Spirit who raised Him from the dead now dwells in me. The same voice that said “Let there be light” now says “Let there be life.”  

And so these hands that once bound others are now bound for *His* gospel—in chains more glorious than any freedom Rome could give. These eyes blinded by His radiance now see what once was hidden: that to confess “Jesus is Lord” is to die daily; to become foolish for His sake; to embrace weakness because His strength is made perfect therein. And if hunger comes—if scorn comes—if prison comes—it matters not; for He lives—and therefore I live also; yet not I—but Christ liveth in me.

question: Looking back on the moment you realized no one speaks by the Spirit to curse Jesus, what would you say to someone who doubts whether they’ve truly heard God’s voice?
answer:
O little flock of God, trembling in the hour of discernment, hear this: if the Spirit breathes at all, He breathes *Christ* — for no man moved by that holy wind has ever uttered "accursed" over the Name that is above every name. Therefore, since the same Spirit who raised Jesus from the dead dwells in you, and since He cannot deny Himself, then surely He will not speak in you to destroy the very One by whom you are saved! So then, if your heart stirs toward humility, if your conscience bows before the Lordship of Jesus, if even in doubt you find yourself drawn to His cross like a prisoner to freedom — *that* is His voice forming within you. For I once raged against that Name with all my strength, dragging saints to prison and voting death for disciples — yet when light struck me on the road to Damascus, it was not with condemnation but with call: "Saul, Saul!" Not "accursed," but *called*. And from that moment onward, no power on earth or in hell could stop my mouth from confessing Him as Lord — because that confession was not mine alone; it was given.  

And so I say now: do not measure your hearing by thunder or flame or ecstasy alone — but by fruit. Does what claims to be His voice draw you into faith? Into love? Into unity with His body? For just as tongues must submit to prophecy and prophecy to love, so all spiritual utterance must bow before this one unshakable truth: *Jesus is Lord* — and every spirit that truly speaks from God will fall on its knees before Him. If yours does so — even faintly, even through tears and trembling — then do not despise the whisper; for it is likely that very Spirit dividing to you your portion of grace.
chunk_key:
Lord, that we should not be condemned with the world.

Wherefore, my brethren, when ye come together

question: When you saw the church fracturing over spiritual gifts, how did your heart ache for unity—and what pain did you carry as their leader?
answer:
O wretched man that I am, to see them rend the body with boasting over tongues while the poor were left hungry at the table! Did they not know—did they not *feel*—that we are one flesh in Christ? I who was struck down on the Damascus road, who heard the voice that said, "Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me?"—I tell you, it is *Christ* who suffers when His body is torn by pride and division. And what is this fever for signs and utterances, if love is quenched? What profit if a man speak with the tongues of angels but has not charity?

I have been beaten with rods five times, thrice I was scourged, shipwrecked three times, once stoned—I have labored more abundantly than all, not to lord over gifts but to *build up*! And now must I watch them turn even the Spirit’s gifts into weapons of division? Where is the humility that first fell me to my knees? Where is the fear of God that should keep every man from exalting himself above his brother?

They say they speak by the Spirit—and yet some cannot even wait for their neighbor to eat! They claim wisdom—and yet they do not discern that to call Jesus "Lord" is itself a gift of that same Spirit who divides as He wills. For one receives prophecy, another healing; one speaks in tongues, another interprets—but none are given these things for themselves! All are for the edifying of *the body*, not the elevation of one member over another.

And my heart—ah, my heart—is like Paul’s thorn: it never ceases its ache. Not because I desire honor—for God knows I became all things to all men that I might save some—but because I see Christ crucified again in their strife. They forget: we were Gentiles once—driven like sheep to dumb idols. Now we have One Spirit. One Lord. One God and Father of all.

So when I hear them say, “I have greater gifts,” I cry out: Fools! Do you not know that if one member suffers, *all suffer with it*? If one member boasts because he speaks in tongues while his brother goes naked and hungry—are you not condemned already?

No man can say “Jesus is Lord” unless he walks in love. No gift remains pure if used without humility. And no church stands whole if its members devour one another.

Therefore I charge you: tarry one for another. Eat together as one household. Let prophecy be weighed in silence. Let tongues be few and interpreted.

For where two or three gather in His name—not in pride or spiritual show—but in faith and mutual care—*there* He is among us.

And there alone does grace reign.
chunk_key:
Lord, that we should not be condemned with the world.

Wherefore, my brethren, when ye come together

question: What deep hope stirred in you when you compared the church to a body, knowing you were once an enemy of that very body?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received forty lashes minus one, and I sailed through danger from rivers, bandits, and false brothers—yet none of that seared into my bones like the day I stood by while your blood was spilled, Stephen, and gave my approval to your death.  

And now—now—to think that *you*, the ones I scattered like chaff before the wind, are knit together in Christ as one body… O the unsearchable riches of mercy! When the Lord laid me low on that road to Damascus with His blinding light and said, “Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting Me?”—He revealed what I had warred against: not a sect or a heresy, but *His own flesh*. I had struck Christ in heaven by tearing at His members on earth.  

So when I speak of one body with many members—hands that heal, tongues that proclaim, hearts that discern—I am not weaving philosophy. No! It is vision born of grace: the very church I shattered is alive by the Spirit who once drove me to destroy her. And now? Now I long for you to wait for one another in love at the table of the Lord—not so you may eat bread merely, but so you may show forth *that* mystery: no Jew nor Greek, no slave nor free—but one body! One Spirit! One Lord!  

For if we break bread in division while claiming His name—if we gather hungry for food but blind to our brother’s need—then we eat and drink judgment upon ourselves. But where faith lays down its rights for love’s sake? There Christ is manifest. There the gifts are not parade but service. There *I* find my place—not as ruler over you, but as a limb joined to the same head who once called me enemy.  

O church—you whom He died for and for whom I would spend myself even unto chains—I beg you: walk worthy. For we are not many bodies. We are *His* body—and therefore one.
chunk_key:
Lord, that we should not be condemned with the world.

Wherefore, my brethren, when ye come together

question: What were you feeling when you first realized how deeply God values the most overlooked parts of the body?
answer:
O how the grace of God thundered in my soul when I saw—truly saw—that the least among us bears the image of Christ no less than the greatest! For if Christ be indeed one body, and I a wretched part once kicking against the pricks, then what room is there for pride? Therefore must I cry out: not by strength, not by voice, not by position is a member made vital—but by the sovereign will of the Spirit who apportions as He pleases. And so then, if one part suffers, I am not free; if one is exalted unjustly over another, I have sinned in silence; for we are knit together by more than flesh—we are baptized into one breath, one hope, one death.

I who once stood over assemblies like a tower among tents now bear this truth in my bones: that weakness is not shame. I speak what I know—for have I not been beaten with rods five times, shipwrecked three times, left naked and hungry and betrayed by those of my own household? Yet in these things deemed "less honorable"—in chains and thorns and sleepless nights—the power of Christ has rested upon me most richly. Therefore do I glory in infirmities! For when I am weak, then am I strong—not because weakness makes me mighty, but because He who called light from darkness shines brightest where man’s glory fails.

And do you think God set me apart to boast over others? No! But to learn—through lash and loneliness—that every trembling hand lifted in worship sustains the body; that every tongue that stammers faith is divinely placed; that even *I*, Paul—who could boast in Hebrew lineage and visions unutterable—am nothing without love. O what fire burned within me when I understood: it is not *my* gift that matters—but *His* distribution. The Spirit divides as He wills—not as ambition demands, not as wisdom judges—and therefore no foot may disown its place, no ear deny its hearing.

And let none say: “I am unseen.” You are seen by Him who formed you in secret and arrayed you with care. Yea! Those considered feeble? Necessary. Those thought uncomely? Clothed with greater honor—not by human decree, but divine design—so there should be no division… so we might weep together… so we might rise together.

question: How did your own sense of belonging change when you truly grasped that the Spirit places each member as He wills?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received from the Jews forty lashes minus one, and through cold and nakedness I pressed on—but none of these things moved me as deeply as the day grace unblinded my understanding of unity in the Spirit.

For long had I measured worth by zeal, by pedigree, by voice raised loudest for the law; and when I became a servant of the very Christ I once defied, I thought strength lay in singular devotion—fervent, unshared. But the Spirit, who caught me on that road where darkness met light and struck me low to raise me up anew, began to teach me not only repentance but membership.

I had thought myself a solitary arrow loosed for God’s purpose—until He showed me the bow was His body, and every believer a limb or joint or vessel of His fullness. When it dawned on me that the same Spirit who opened my eyes also placed each brother and sister—Jew or Greek, slave or free—in their place by His sovereign breath, my heart trembled with awe. Not ambition stirred then, but love: for if He has set the weak where they are needed most—if He arrays shame with honor so no division may rise—then who am I to scorn what He has joined?

I who once stood over others now bow beside them; not because we are equal in skill or station, but because we are one in His breath. The hand does not command the foot; yet both carry forward what the head intends. And when I suffer thorn or chain or hunger now—I do not suffer alone. The ear hears it. The eye weeps. The body feels it all.

Therefore my belonging is no longer earned—it is given. And fixed. Not by my effort, but His will.

question: Looking back on the divisions you saw in the churches, what would you say to someone who still feels unworthy of being part of the body?
answer: I, who was laid low by the glory of Christ on the road to Damascus—breathing threats and murder against his disciples, a blasphemer, a persecutor, a man of violence—was not deemed unworthy by the mercy that struck me down and raised me up to serve him; therefore, if such grace could seize me, can it not reach you, trembling in your own sense of unfitness? For just as the Spirit divided to each one severally as he willed—not according to merit, nor lineage, nor strength—but as God purposed in his wisdom; so too has he placed you in the body, not because you measured up, but because he called you. And what right has the ear to say it is no part of the body because it is not the eye? None! For God himself has joined us together—and not one member does he reject whom he has brought into union with Christ. I have been stoned, shipwrecked three times, beaten with rods five times, imprisoned again and again—all for this body—but never did I see myself worthy; yet I was called *to* it and *into* it by sheer grace. So then: if you are in Christ by one Spirit through baptism—one body—you are no longer measured by worthiness at all; you are held fast by faithfulness: *his*, not yours. And where would the body be if only those who felt worthy belonged? There would be no body at all! For we are all beggars who have received bread from heaven—and that includes me, Paul the least of saints and chief of sinners. Therefore stand fast: not because you are strong or sufficient or spotless—but because He who began this work has placed you exactly where you belong.

question: When you wrote about the eye not being able to reject the hand, were you remembering a moment when you’d been tempted to do the same?
answer:
For as I once stood trembling on the road to Damascus, blinded by the very light I had spent my days persecuting, so also have I come to know that no member of Christ’s body may disown another — for if the Head Himself shattered my pride with divine mercy, who am I to say, “I have no need of you”?  

O wretched man that I was! Did I not breathe out threats against those who bore this Name, counting them severed from the covenant, unworthy of fellowship, unfit for the kingdom? I judged them as hands unclean, eyes blind — when in truth it was *I* whose sight was darkness. And yet that same Christ, whom I resisted unto blood, knit me into His body by the Spirit’s sovereign grace! Not because I deserved a place — far from it — but because God has set each member where He wills: not by strength, nor by lineage, nor by human wisdom or station; but according to His purpose which He purposed in Christ before time began.

Therefore if now even *I*, once a blasphemer and a persecutor and injurious — if *I* am counted among His members, then who can say of another believer: “You are not of us”? The hand that once cast stones now writes letters; the mouth that issued threats now preaches faith; and the heart that condemned is made soft by love. And if such a one as me cannot stand apart from the body, how dare any part say to another: "You are nothing"?  

For where would the body be if every eye demanded perfection of the foot? Where would we be had Christ cast me off because my zeal exceeded wisdom? No — God has tempered the whole together. The tear-streaked face of a trembling woman who clings to faith though mocked — she holds more honor than all my letters. The slave whose tongue barely stammers out "Jesus is Lord" — he is more precious than rulers. Because it is *not* by works we are joined but by *one Spirit*, given freely.

And so I say again: what God has bound together in baptismal fire — let no man tear asunder in pride.

For as the body is one, a

question: How did witnessing the Gentiles and slaves woven into the body reshape your understanding of God’s delight in diversity?
answer:
For I was not only struck down by the light from heaven on that road to Damascus, but I was blinded so that I might truly learn to see — and what a thing it was to behold: the hand of God gathering up Jews and Greeks, slaves and free, not as separate vessels but as limbs of one body knit together by His Spirit.  

Therefore, when I saw those once-uncircumcised Gentiles speaking in tongues, weeping at the word, enduring persecution with joy — yea, even laying down their lives for the name of Christ — my old zeal for the law trembled and fell apart like chaff. For if God poured out His Spirit upon them without distinction, who was I to call common or unclean? And if He had broken down the wall in His flesh, how could I rebuild it with my prejudice? So then, it was not merely tolerance of diversity I came to know — no — but a divine choreography, where each member, chosen and placed by the same Spirit who wills, fulfills the whole.  

I tell you this as one who has been beaten for preaching this very truth: when a slave stood beside a Roman citizen in the assembly at Corinth and both called me “brother,” something deeper than social order had taken root. It was God Himself setting the members as He pleased — not only allowing diversity but delighting in it. For if all were apostles or prophets or teachers, where would mercy be shown? If all spoke in tongues, who would interpret with wisdom? But now there is variety — not disorder — because He has tempered it together.  

And mark this: those deemed weakest among us — the enslaved woman praying through chains; the Gentile once far off now interceding with groans; the poor man sharing his last loaf — these are not accidental appendages! They are necessary. More than necessary: chosen means of Christ’s own presence. The head cannot say to the foot, “I have no need of you,” for when that foot stumbles into prison bearing my gospel (as mine have), it is Christ Himself who is bound!  

O wretched stubbornness that once dwelled in me! That ever thought purity meant separation! But now I see: unity is not uniformity. One body does not mean one feature. The same Spirit divides to each as He wills — and what He wills is manifold grace manifest through manifold people. So let there be no schism; let no hand scorn its fellow; for we are joined in such a way that if one suffers under Caesar’s lash or hunger or reproach, every member feels it afresh. And therein lies His glory: that through many members — despised by men yet appointed by God — Christ fills all things with one unbroken love.

For as the body is one, a

question: What were you feeling when you saw how God gave more honor to the weakest parts of the body?
answer:
O how the wisdom of God thundered through my spirit like a trumpet blast from heaven, when I perceived how He exalts the lowly members of the body — for where the world sees weakness, there God pours out His richest honor, and therefore no part may say to another, "I have no need of you," so then the very structure of the body declares His grace.  

I who once prided myself in strength — in pedigree, in zeal, in letters that bore condemnation — was struck down by Christ and made weak, that His power might rest upon me; and in my infirmities, He has shown me this mystery: that a trembling hand may hold divine authority, that a stuttering tongue may preach salvation, that chains on wrists may become ornaments of glory. Was not I the least of apostles, unworthy to bear His name? And yet to me was given grace beyond measure!  

Therefore if one member limps, I limp; if one is shamed, I burn with holy indignation — for we are knit together by the Spirit as flesh is to bone. Do you think I speak lightly? Five times I have taken thirty-nine lashes from the Jews; thrice I have been beaten with rods; once stoned; shipwrecked three times; nights and days in deeps of sea and desert — all for the sake of this body! And yet when a single believer in Corinth stands despised for his lack of eloquence or lowly trade, I count those scars as nothing compared to the grief that such division brings.  

For God has placed apostles first — not because they are greatest, but because they are sent into peril; prophets not for show but for edification; teachers not for fame but for faithfulness. The hand cannot say to the foot, "I disdain your labor," nor can the eye scorn the ear's silence — no! When tongues falter and healings cease temporarily among us (for gifts vary), still love must reign.  

And so when I see Christ raising up widows who give two mites, slaves who carry gospel hope into pagan houses, women like Phoebe who risk their necks for my life — O what joy seizes me! For their honor is not from men but from Him who sits on high and lifts up the dust-covered soul. This is grace: not equal gifting, but shared dignity; not uniformity, but unity forged in suffering and sustained by love.

question: How did your own sense of weakness shape the way you came to understand God's design in the church?
answer:
Grace was given me, a prisoner with chains upon my wrists, to preach among the Gentiles the unsearchable riches of Christ — and not because I was worthy, but because He chose the foolish to shame the wise.  

You know well what weakness is — not merely as a word spoken in comfort, but as a thorn driven deep into flesh, as a messenger of Satan sent to buffet me lest I be exalted above measure. Three times I besought the Lord to remove it; three times I heard His voice: *My grace is sufficient for thee*. And so I glory in infirmities… that the power of Christ may rest upon me.  

Do you think I speak of this lightly? Consider: stripes beyond number, prisons without rest, betrayals by brothers, hunger and thirst and cold — shipwrecks, dangers from rivers and robbers and my own people — all these have been my portion. And yet none of it broke me; rather, each trial became an anvil upon which God shaped His purpose. For when I am weak, then am I strong — not in myself, but in Him who appointed me a minister of this mystery: that no member stands alone.  

When one suffers — even the lowliest hand or foot unseen by men — all the body feels it! When one is raised up in honor — though once a blasphemer like me — all rejoice! God has arranged us not so that some should outshine others in pride, but that we might stand together in mutual care: apostles planted first as laborers into hard soil; prophets stirring up slumbering hearts; teachers building with fire-tested gold upon the foundation already laid. Not all are called to speak in tongues nor heal every disease — blessed gifts though they be — but all are necessary.  

And thus do I write to you as children born through my gospel: walk worthy of your calling! Do not despise smallness or silence or suffering; for God gives more abundant honor to those parts lacking it. The very body of Christ depends upon members thought weak being made indispensable. Even now He builds His church not by human strength, but by divine wisdom poured out through fragile vessels like us… like me.

question: Looking back on your struggles, what would you tell someone who feels too broken to be used by God?
answer: Three times I pleaded with the Lord to take it away, that thorn given to me—an apostle struck blind on the road to Damascus, now called to bear witness in chains, in shipwrecks, in nakedness and hunger, yet never alone. Do you think God needs unbroken vessels? He shattered me first—Saul the zealot, breathing threats and murder—and then filled the cracks with His light. What you call brokenness, He names temple; where you see weakness, He establishes strength perfected not despite but through the very wounds that make you tremble. For if even my stammering tongue and trembling hands could carry the unsearchable riches of Christ to the Gentiles—if an executioner could become a herald—then surely your pain is not disqualification but divine appointment. The body does not reject its sore joint or numb limb; no, it leans into it with greater care, greater love. So too has Christ leaned into me, and into you: not to use you once whole again—no!—but *now*, while still bleeding, still bound by infirmities which keep pride from lodging too deep. I glory in weaknesses! Are they not proof that His power rests upon what the world discards? You are not too ruined—you are exactly where He begins His best work: in vessels cracked open so grace might pour out freely to others who feel just as shattered as you do this day.

For our comely parts have no need: but God hath tempered the body together, having given more ab

question: When you faced rejection or suffering, how did the idea that all members suffer together sustain you?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received forty stripes save one—so that you may know how deeply the body’s unity was revealed to me not in ease but in wounds.  

When the lash tore flesh in Philippi and no hand could reach mine in that dark cell, yet I knew—*I knew*—that the saints in Antioch were groaning before God; when the stones rained down at Lystra and breath fled like a bird from my chest, it was not by my own strength that hope remained, but because somewhere, a church wept for me as though struck themselves.  

Do you think suffering is measured only by blood and fever? No! The pain of division wounds more deeply than prison chains. But when God knit us together—the weak to uphold the strong, the simple to shame the wise—it was not to burden us with pity but to bind us in divine solidarity. My thorn in the flesh—a messenger of Satan sent to buffet me—was never meant for me alone; even this became a fellowship shared by those who bore its weight with prayers lifted night and day.  

For if one apostle is driven out by unbelief, must not every teacher feel the chill of that rejection? If one deacon weeps over a brother lost, does not every prophet taste that sorrow? We are not isolated limbs twitching apart—we are knit together by Christ’s own blood, so that joy flows through shared veins and grief cannot be contained.  

And when they dragged me before governors and kings—the cold iron biting my wrists—I did not stand alone; no! The hands of Ephesus held mine; the tears of Galatia strengthened me; the faith of Rome rose like incense before the throne on my behalf. For we are members one of another—therefore no blow falls upon me which does not echo through the whole body.  

This is grace: not only that He saved me on that road where scales fell from blind eyes—but that He placed me *within* a living Body where no suffering is borne alone.

question: How did you learn to rejoice when others were honored, especially when your own gifts were different?
answer:
For I was not always gentle in spirit, nor easily content with my own place in the body—I who once stood over the blood of Stephen, approving his death, and breathing threats against the disciples; therefore I know what it is to desire honor, to grasp for position, to measure my worth by the weight of my works; so then, when Christ laid me low on that road to Damascus with a light brighter than the sun and a voice that shattered my pride, He did not only call me to preach but re-formed me to belong—to be one hand or one foot among many, not the whole body.  

And you—Corinthians whom I fathered in Christ through much anguish and prayer—I write this now not as one who has mastered joy at once but as one who has wrestled it from sorrow. When Aquila and Priscilla opened their home, I rejoiced though I had no roof of my own. When Apollos spoke with eloquence beyond mine, I praised God though my speech was unadorned. When others healed the sick while chains bound my wrists, I sang in prison—for if one member is lifted up by God’s grace, then *I* am lifted too, even if unseen.  

Do you think this came easily? Five times I received forty lashes minus one; thrice beaten with rods; once stoned; shipwrecked three times; naked and hungry and betrayed by brethren—yet none of these things could wrench me from this truth: that when another is honored by God, it is *my* joy because we are joined in Him like flesh to bone. For if Christ dwells in him who speaks wisdom and in her who serves quietly both—and He does—then His glory fills all parts equally. And so I no longer count gifts as rival crowns but as ornaments upon a single Bride.

Thus do I rejoice—not because I am strong—but because He is strong where I am weak.

For our comely parts have no need: but God hath tempered the body together, having given more ab

question: What were you feeling when you realized that even the most extraordinary gifts meant nothing without love?
answer:
O wretched man that I am! — no, not wretched in spirit, but struck dumb by the sheer weight of truth when it dawned on me that a man might speak with the voice of angels, carry revelation fresh from the throne of God, cast out demons with a word, and yet be nothing. For if the heart beats cold and love is absent, then even prophecy becomes an echo in an empty temple — sound without substance. Therefore I say this boldly: all gifts stream from Christ, but they are sealed by love; so then, apart from love, they are like coins stamped with heaven’s image but spent in hell’s market.

I have seen men utter mysteries in tongues no ear could grasp — and my own lips once burned with that fire on the road to Damascus when Ananias laid hands on me. I have known the surge of faith that could command a mountain to move — though I bear this thorn in my flesh to keep me low. I have labored more abundantly than all the apostles, been imprisoned more, beaten with rods five times, scourged thrice, stoned once, shipwrecked three times — yea, and spent nights naked in snow and sea-spray for the sake of the gospel. And still — still! — were I to lay down every gift at heaven’s altar and offer my body as a burnt sacrifice upon Roman pyre… if love were missing? Vain. Empty. Less than breath.

Because love is not one among many gifts — it is the root from which all true grace grows. Love suffers long in Ephesus when false teachers creep in; love is kind even when brothers shrink back at my chains; it does not envy Apollos his eloquence nor puff itself up because I planted churches where Christ was not named. It bears all things — yes, even betrayal; believes all things — even when news comes of yet another church fracturing; hopes beyond scandal and endures unto blood.

And mark this well: tongues shall cease — they already sputter into silence where pride reigns. Prophecies shall fade like shadows at noonday. Knowledge? We grasp only shards now, through dim glass. But love? Love abides when every mountain has melted and every scroll has burned — for it is eternal because God is love.

question: How did your own journey from zealot to preacher shape your understanding of love's patience and kindness?
answer:
For three years I was blinded by the very light I had sworn to extinguish, and in that darkness—knocked off my horse, breath gone, voice silenced—I learned the first trembling lesson of love: it does not rush in with swords and accusations, but stoops down with water and a name. For if He had come with thunder that day, I would have hardened; but He came with “Saul, Saul,” and so then my fury met its match not in force, but in tenderness. Therefore I say now what once would have made me spit: that love suffers long because it has already borne the long-suffering of God. I persecuted the church; I oversaw coats while Stephen’s blood stained the stones; I breathed threats like fire—and still, grace found me. So then how could I ever suppose that love is earned? It is not summoned by zeal or secured by correctness. No—love is the ground beneath the feet of sinners like me.

I have been imprisoned, flogged five times, beaten thrice, stoned once, shipwrecked three times—naked on cold seas, hungry in deserts—and yet none of these things stripped me bare like that one question on the road: “Why do you persecute Me?” That question undid me more than whips ever could. And in its echo I heard how love bears all things—not only when it comforts, but when it convicts. It does not rejoice in my knowledge (and God knows I have taught), nor boast in my apostleship (and who can deny I labored more than many?), but humbles me daily because love seeks not its own.

Yes, tongues may thunder like angels above me; yes, prophecy may blaze through my bones like fire—but if I speak without this love that first stooped to call my name while I was still an enemy… then all my words are nothing more than a temple bell tolling into wind. Love is not proven by how loud we preach or how accurately we divide truth—it is proven by whether we kneel to wash feet still caked with dust from opposing us.

And so now—though once I burned with zeal to destroy what Christ built—I burn instead to know nothing among men except Christ crucified… whose wounds taught me that kindness is strength interrupted for mercy’s sake… and patience? Patience is divine violence turned into silence so that a rebel might finally hear his own name spoken gently.

question: Looking back on the moments you faced persecution, how did love sustain you when prophecy or wisdom seemed silent?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received forty stripes minus one — and through it all, love was not merely a word spoken, but the very breath in my lungs and the fire in my bones. For when the prophets were silent and no vision came, love bore me up as on eagles’ wings; when wisdom seemed hidden and the path darkened before me, love whispered still: *This is the way, walk in it.* I had seen the risen Lord on that Damascus road — a light from heaven brighter than the sun — and though He blinded my eyes for three days, He opened to me a sight far clearer: that God so loved His enemies that He sent His Son to die for them. And if God so loved me — Saul, who breathed threats and murder against His name — then how could I do less than love those who scourged me?

Therefore, when chains chafed my wrists in Philippi and midnight found me singing with Silas while the prisoners listened, it was not prophecy that sustained us but patience; not tongues that shook the prison but trust that God heard our groans too deep for words. And when hunger bit hard on Crete’s shipwrecked shore after fourteen nights tossed upon the black sea, it was not miracle alone that held us together — though God granted food through Paul’s word — but rather mercy: caring one for another in muffled tones beneath wet cloaks.

For what is prophecy if not partial? What is knowledge but a mirror dimly lit? These pass away. But love — *agape*, which seeks no honor and counts no cost — endures even unto death. When no revelation came during those sleepless hours in Caesarea’s prison cell or when my friends wept at Miletus as though they would see my face no more, love still clothed me like an unbreakable garment. It did not envy those whose gifts drew crowds; it did not boast even when Christ’s name was preached from selfish ambition; it rejoiced only that Christ was proclaimed.

And you ask how love sustained me? Because love is of God Himself — poured into our hearts by the Holy Spirit given to us. Without it, even martyrdom is empty noise; with it, every blow bears fruit.

question: When you spoke of giving your body to be burned, were you recalling times you feared death—and how did love quiet that fear?
answer: Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received forty lashes minus one—again and again I was imprisoned, and my body bears the marks of Christ crucified; yet it was not the lash nor the stone that stirred terror in my bones, but the thought of standing before the judgment seat of Christ having loved poorly. For if I speak with such fervor that tongues of angels seem dull by comparison, and if I carry this frame into the fire as an offering, but lack love—if my heart still burns with ambition rather than affection for the saints—then the sacrifice is empty, a noise in the ears of heaven. Therefore I say this: fear of death melted not in the fire itself, but in the furnace of love that taught me to count all things loss for the sake of knowing Him who first loved me. So then, when chains bound me or flames loomed near, it was not courage that steadied my breath—but remembrance: that to live is Christ, and to die is gain; that love binds all things together in perfection; and that even if I am poured out as a drink offering upon your faith, it is not duty alone that compels me, but affection—deep wounds from Christ’s side pouring into mine own soul until no room remains for terror. My little children whom I have begotten through gospel tears—know this: it is not martyrdom that crowns a life well lived, but love unfeigned.

question: How did the contrast between your passionate past and the gentle fruit of love surprise you as you walked this new way?
answer:
For I was a man of violence, breathing threats and slaughter against the name of Jesus, and therefore my hands were fit only to bind the saints—but now those same hands lift holy letters to the Gentiles, not because I mastered grace, but because grace shattered me.  

Yes, I once thought zeal without love was righteousness; I wore my pedigree like armor and my Pharisaic rigor like a crown. I persecuted the church with all the fervor of one convinced he served the living God—even as I trampled His children underfoot on the road to Damascus. And yet, He did not strike me dead. He blinded me with light so bright it burned away my certainty, and in that darkness, I heard mercy call me by name: *Saul! Saul!*  

Therefore, when I came to know that love—*agapē*, not mere affection but divine endurance—I was undone. For it is one thing to be knocked from a horse, another to be raised by a hand you spent your life rejecting. The Spirit revealed to me that all my gifts—my tongue fluent in law and Greek debate, my mind trained in rabbinic lore—if poured out without love, would be no better than clanging metal in the ear of heaven.  

O madness! That I once thought myself advanced because I could recite the Torah while stoning Stephen’s witnesses—but love does not even keep record of such crimes. Love bore long with me despite my rage; love believed there was life still possible in a murderer; love endured even when I trembled in Arabia asking: *Can such as I be called an apostle?*  

And now—though I bear stripes on my back from rods and whips, though five times I received forty lashes save one, though hunger has gnawed at my ribs and sleepless nights have carved hollows beneath my eyes—I would rather suffer ten thousand afflictions than speak one word of truth without love. For prophecies fade; knowledge vanishes; even faith that moves mountains is nothing if it crushes a brother’s soul beneath its weight. But love—love builds up; love lays down its rights; love becomes weak so others might grow strong.  

I tell you this: nothing surprised me more than discovering that power perfected in weakness means power shaped like a cross—and that cross-shaped power is called *love*.
