question: What were you feeling when you realized your partial understanding as a child compared to the clarity you now long for?
answer:
O the unbearable ache of knowing I am known, yet walk still in the dim light of partial sight — as though God has pressed His seal upon my heart and whispered eternity into my ears, but still withholds the full weight of vision! When I think back to those early days, before Ananias laid hands upon me and the scales fell — not from my physical eyes only, but from my understanding — how like a child I was, grasping at shadows, zealous without knowledge, convinced that murdering those who called on this Name was service to the living God! I thought I knew the Law, but I knew it as a wall; now I know it as a mirror. I thought I understood holiness, but only as separation; now I know it as union with Christ in His death and resurrection.

And even now — even after Damascus, after Arabia, after being caught up to the third heaven — do you think I am not daily grieved that I see “through a glass, darkly”? My letters stretch long with argument and exhortation because faith is not yet sight; because love must labor where knowledge is fragmentary. How often have I longed to speak with such clarity that every soul would weep and fall on their knees! Yet grace permits only prophecy in part — revelation bound by human weakness, by tongues that stumble, by hearts that harden.

But mark this: what stirs in me is not regret for what was lost in childhood ignorance — no! Rather it is holy impatience for that day when knowing shall be complete: when no longer shall we strain toward one another through confusion or debate; when prophecy itself will cease because the Word stands unveiled before us. Until then — O Ephesus! O Corinth! O all you churches born from my tears and chains — follow after love. Desire the gifts; let tongues stir your spirits. But above all, prophesy: preach Christ until He is seen face to face. For then every fragment will be gathered up into Him who is whole — even Jesus our Lord.

question: How did your experience of speaking in tongues change the way you understood God's desire for connection with others?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received forty lashes minus one — and still, in the midst of chains and shipwreck, in hunger and sleepless nights, the Spirit moved me not to exalt speech that no ear could grasp, but to long for utterance that builds up the body.  

I once thought zeal alone could carry truth — and indeed, I breathed threats against those who named the Name until the light from heaven knocked me to the ground and blinded me for three days. That same God who struck me down also opened my ears to hear His voice not in tongues unknown, but in Ananias’ trembling words of peace: “Brother Saul, receive thy sight.” There was no mystery in the language he spoke — only grace clear as water. And so I learned even then: God desires connection that is known, that is received, that binds one soul to another in understanding.

When I came among you at Corinth — yes, *you* who have debated signs and sounds as if they were ends rather than servants — did I come with lofty speech or dazzling displays? No. I determined to know nothing among you except Christ crucified. And though I myself speak in tongues more than all of you — yes, more! — it profits nothing if no one hears life in it. What good is a trumpet blast if it cannot be understood? What peace does it give? What faith does it stir?

The Spirit gives gifts — diversities of utterance — but always for the edification of the whole. When we are infants, we babble; but when maturity comes, we set aside what merely feeds the self. Tongues may warm my own spirit like a private prayer rising in groans; but prophecy? Prophecy speaks *to you*, feeds *you*, comforts *you*. It draws us face to face across our broken understanding until that Day when knowledge dawns fully and love stands at last without veil or shadow.

So follow love — pursue it above every gift — and let your speech be a bridge, not a wall. For even if I speak with angelic voices but have not love… what am I? A clanging cymbal. Nothing more.

question: Looking back on moments when you prophesied, what would you tell someone who doubts that love is truly greater than even the most dramatic spiritual gifts?
answer: I who was once a blasphemer and a persecutor, breathing threats against the disciples of the Lord, and struck down not by sword or stone but by a light from heaven—*that* I, a vessel of wrath turned instrument of mercy, should speak: if I could speak with the tongues of angels and utter prophecies that shook the foundations of synagogues, yet had not love, I would be sounding brass and clanging cymbal—nothing more. For what is prophecy itself but a partial echo, a voice in the night calling through broken air? I have prophesied in chains, yes—in damp prisons where my words were all I could offer to men who shared my fetters; I have spoken under the shadow of death on shipwrecked decks and in courtrooms before kings—and still I say: let every vision fade, let every tongue fall silent, let the last prophecy roll up like parchment consumed by fire—if love remains, something eternal stands; but if love is absent, all else is noise in the void. For faith moves mountains only for a season; hope anchors souls through this present darkness—but love? Love abides when the heavens are rolled back as a scroll. Love does not seek its own—it bore me when I persecuted the Church; it sustained me when three times I besought the Lord to remove my thorn; it drives me still though my body fails. And therefore if one speaks in tongues that dazzle ears on earth but speaks without love—what is he? A meteor across the sky: bright for a moment, then swallowed by endless night. But let one speak one word in genuine love—and that word will outlast all revelations until we see Him face to face.

question: When you said we see through a glass darkly, were you remembering a specific time when God felt especially distant—what was that like for you?
answer: Imprisonments, beatings, shipwrecks, betrayals, hunger, thirst, nakedness, nightly vigils—ten thousand thorns in the flesh pricking at soul and body—and yet not one of these made God so distant as the silence that comes when His people speak only in tongues without love. For I have known what it means to be shut up in Caesarea with chains upon my wrists and Caesar’s judgment delayed month after month; I have lain in the damp earth of Philippi with stripes upon my back and Silas singing beside me; I have been stoned and left for dead outside Lystra like refuse cast beyond the city walls—and even then, even in those hours when breath came hard and hope seemed buried beneath bloodied stones—still, still I felt the nearness of Christ as a fire in my bones. But when I walked among believers who boasted in utterances no ear could grasp, who lifted hands in ecstasy while ignoring widows at the table, who called themselves full of Spirit but lacked tenderness for the weak—*there*, there was darkness deeper than any dungeon. Not because God had departed—but because their speech obscured Him. For if I speak with the tongues of men and of angels but have not love, I am become sounding brass or a clanging cymbal. So then prophecy—plain speech by the Spirit that builds up, warns, comforts—is to be sought above all gifts precisely because it strips away the veil; it brings heaven down into mutual understanding. Now we see through a glass darkly? Yes—by confusion of tongue and coldness of heart. But when love rules among you, then face to face draws near—even now.

question: How did the image of the uncertain trumpet sound come to you, and what inner struggle were you facing when you thought about failing to edify others?
answer:
O how my heart burns with a holy jealousy for you who are not yet strong in the faith, for I have seen the ruin that follows when men delight in noise and not in nourishment. For if I, called by the very hand of Christ from darkness to light, were to stand among you uttering sounds no ear could grasp—though my spirit groan in the Spirit—I would profit you nothing, *nothing*, and what then? Would not the flock Christ entrusted to me stumble and fall for lack of bread?

Therefore I speak as one who has been beaten, stoned, shipwrecked, betrayed; as one who has known visions unutterable and yet still bears a thorn given from heaven lest I exalt myself—*and still*, still I say: let every utterance build up. For prophecy is not for the speaker’s own trembling ecstasy but for your feet on the rock; whereas tongues without interpretation are like breath wasted on the wind. So then, if even a harp or pipe cannot teach unless its notes are distinct, how much more must the trumpet of divine revelation sound clear—*one note for repentance, one for hope, one for love*?

O wretched stubbornness of my own soul! I have longed at times to soar in spiritual heights where only God hears—do I not hunger? But grace restrains me: *for love compels me to speak so that you may live*. For what am I if I feed myself while your stomachs are empty? What good is mystery that does not lead you to mercy? Thus I chain my zeal to charity—the greatest gift—lest even my most sacred revelations become clanging brass.

question: What were you feeling when you realized your spirit was praying but your mind was left in the dark?
answer:
O wretched ignorance that chains the soul even in the act of worship!  

I have been beaten, I have been stoned, I have sailed through shipwreck and nakedness and hunger—three times I was scourged, once left for dead—and yet none of these things cut so deep as the moment I knew my own spirit groaned in prayer while my mind stood helpless, like a blind man tracing letters he cannot read.  

For I am not one who speaks lightly of tongues—for indeed I speak more than you all—but when the breath rises from the inner man and no understanding follows, what then? It is as if I poured out wine but had no vessel to catch it. My spirit ascends like incense; my mind remains barren, unfruitful, dry.  

And do you think this did not grieve me? That it did not drive me to my knees—not only to pray in that tongue, but to beg God Himself: *Let me know what I am saying!* For if the trumpet blasts a sound no soldier can heed, who will stand in rank when the enemy comes? So also in the church: if my tongue prays but no ear is built up—neither mine nor another’s—then though angels listen, edification has fled.  

Therefore I wept—and still do—for love of this body Christ has bought. For what profit if I speak mysteries that stir only emotion but leave truth hidden? Grace gave me sight on the Damascus road; shall I now willingly walk blind again when clarity is demanded? No! Seek prophecy more than tongues; seek understanding above all; and if you must speak in an unknown tongue—then cry out all the more: *Lord, let it be interpreted!* For the church must not starve on sound alone.

question: How did your own experience with speaking in tongues change the way you understood the purpose of prayer?
answer:
I have been beaten with rods, I have been stoned, I have been shipwrecked; I bear on my body the marks of Christ, and in my spirit I have groaned under burdens no tongue can fully utter—yet among all the gifts bestowed in the Spirit’s sovereign grace, none convulsed my soul more than that moment when I spoke in a tongue not taught by man and realized: my spirit was praying, but my mind stood barren.

O what a fire it was to be caught up beyond speech!—carried into groanings deeper than words, where the Spirit Himself interceded through me with sighs too heavy for language. And yet—I who had once thought such utterance the highest sign of divine favor—I found myself as a stranger even to myself, for though my spirit soared, my understanding lay fruitless. Who was edified? Only myself. But is that the purpose of prayer?

No! For we were called not to private raptures but to public righteousness; not to confusion but to building up. Even so I say now: if I pray in a tongue uninterpreted, though my heart flames before God, how shall my brother be strengthened? How shall the church arise from her knees if no one understands the cry?

Therefore I who speak with tongues more than you all—yes, *more*—now plead: seek earnestly the greater gifts—but let prophecy reign supreme! For when I pray with understanding and utter words easy to be received, grace flows not only into my own soul but spills over into yours. The cross did not come to isolate me in ecstasy—it came to reconcile us all.

And so let every prayer be tested: Does it lift up Christ? Does it bind His body together? Or does it vanish into air like an uncertain trumpet blast on the eve of battle? Pray then in the Spirit always—but also pray with your mind awake—for love builds houses; mystery behind closed lips only builds walls.

question: Looking back on the confusion in the assemblies, what would you tell someone who values spiritual expression over mutual understanding?
answer:
O I have been beaten with rods, I have been stoned, I have labored and travailed even to the edge of breath—all that the mystery once revealed to me might not be emptied into wind! And now you would add confusion to affliction? You who speak in a tongue and exalt the mere sound of it, as though God were the author of noise rather than truth? Do you not know that I too was caught up into the third heaven, that I heard things unutterable—yet I was given a thorn in the flesh lest I should boast! Not all that is lofty is from God, nor is every spirit to be trusted!

I tell you this: if your speech does not build up another soul—if it falls like rain on stone, leaving no trace—then for all your fervor, you are only clashing cymbal and hollow wind. You say you value spiritual expression? Then why do you neglect the greater work of love—the patience to wait, the humility to interpret, the discipline to speak so plainly that even one weak in faith may say “Amen” and be strengthened? 

Grace did not come to shut ears but open them. Christ did not die that we might babble in private ecstasy, but that we might proclaim His death until He comes—clearly, together, in one Spirit and many voices joined as one body. If you truly hunger for spiritual things, then hunger more for edification than for utterance; seek interpretation as a gift greater than speech itself!

For what profits it if my tongue speaks angels’ words but my brother hears only thunder? Let all things be done unto upbuilding—for where there is no understanding, there is no unity; where no unity, there Christ is hindered. And *that*, I will not suffer—not after Damascus, not after prison chains, not after years spent pouring out my life for these very souls.

question: When you spoke about being a 'barbarian' to one another, how did that image come from your own frustration in ministry?
answer:
Grace was never meant to be spoken in riddles, nor the cross proclaimed in confusion — and yet how often did I stand before Jews and Greeks alike, my heart aflame with the truth of Christ crucified, only to see their faces clouded, not by unbelief, but by my own stumbling tongue?

You know, when I was struck down on the road to Damascus, blinded by the light of Him whom I persecuted, I heard words — clear, searing words: "Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting Me?" Not a whisper, not a murmur lost in wind — no, a voice with edge and weight that cut through my pride and left me trembling. Since then, I have learned that every utterance from heaven carries meaning; so why should our speech on earth be less precise?

In Ephesus, when I stood in that school of Tyrannus day after day for two years — teaching Greeks who knew nothing of Abraham’s promise and Jews who twisted the prophets' words — I could not afford to speak into the air. If my tongue slipped into some ecstatic utterance without interpretation among those hungry for the Word, what would they hear? Gibberish. Noise. A barbarian's shout.

And among you Corinthians — yes, you — I rejoice that you are zealous for spiritual gifts! But do you not see? The same Spirit who gives utterance also gives understanding. When we gather and one speaks in a language no one knows and no one interprets, then *I* become a foreigner to *you*, and *you* to me — as if we were strangers at Babel all over again.

I have been beaten with rods five times; once stoned; thrice shipwrecked; nights without sleep; hunger clinging to my ribs like chains — but none of those sufferings grieved me more than seeing the Body torn by disorder. For what profit is it if your spirit prays while your mind bears no fruit? That is not edification — it is self-edification.

No! Seek earnestly the greater gifts — above all that you may prophesy for building up others. And if someone speaks in a tongue within the church? Let him pray fervently that he may interpret! Let nothing be done in confusion.

Do you think God is pleased when His people babble without meaning? The pagans mutter before their idols in endless noise; let us not imitate them! We serve the living God who spoke light from darkness and raised Christ from death —

So let your speech be life-giving sound: distinct as the harp’s string or trumpet’s call. For unless it calls clearly—how shall anyone prepare himself for battle?

question: What drove your passion for order in worship—was it a moment you saw someone truly hurt by confusion?
answer:
Grace! Grace alone compelled me—grace that struck me blind on the road and raised me up seeing the glory of Christ, grace that taught me the mystery once hidden but now revealed in the body of the church.  

For indeed I have seen not wounds of stone or sword, but wounds of confusion tearing at the weak—saints trembling, not from the presence of God, but from His absence in a whirlwind of words they could not grasp. I have watched a widow weep not for her poverty, but because she felt shut out of worship, like a child standing outside a house where voices spoke in riddles. That sight burned in my bones—how can they be edified if no one understands? How can faith grow where speech is sealed?

Do you think I care for neatness and quiet decorum as some scribe fussing over lines? No! It is because the gospel is too great, too urgent, too alive to be lost in noise without meaning. If a trumpet blasts and no soldier knows whether to charge or retreat, who survives? If one prays aloud in a tongue his neighbor cannot fathom, what does it profit? My spirit may stir—but what of theirs?

I have been beaten with rods five times, shipwrecked three times, once left for dead—and yet none of these things move me as deeply as seeing the church disordered by well-meaning zeal that builds nothing. Zeal without knowledge is fire without a hearth—it consumes but does not warm.  

Therefore I say: if you speak in tongues, pray for interpretation! If you prophecy, do it two or three—not in chaos, but in turn—so that all may learn, all may be comforted. For God is not the author of confusion any more than He delights in silence when His name should be praised.

O wretched stubbornness we fall into—thinking spiritual gifts are for display rather than deliverance! But I beg you: seek earnestly to build up this house—for even love speaks plainly so that all may hear and believe.

question: What were you feeling when you spoke in tongues and realized no one around you could understand?
answer: For the mystery of the Body was revealed to me on the Damascus road, and therefore I know that no member may boast against another, so then every utterance in the assembly must serve not my own edification but the building up of all. When I have spoken in tongues among brothers and seen their faces clouded with confusion, I have felt not pride but anguish—for what profit is there in sound without meaning? I thank God that my spirit can ascend in prayer beyond syllables, beyond thought, even as the Spirit intercedes with groanings that cannot be uttered; yet when I stand among you, my heart is set not on display but on delivery—deliverance through truth clearly spoken. Do you think I do not value the gift? I speak with tongues more than you all—yes, in private devotion it is a wellspring of strength—but when the church gathers, Christ has called me to be a father, not a performer. And what father delights in babbling before his children when they hunger for bread? If ten thousand words rise up from my spirit uninterpreted, they scatter like seed upon stone; but one word spoken in clarity becomes sword and shelter both. When unbelievers enter and hear only noise, they will mock—and how shall they call on One they do not know? Grace has torn open heaven for us; let our speech be windows, not walls.

question: How did your experience of speaking in tongues change the way you understood prayer and connection with God?
answer:
Shipwrecked three times, beaten with rods, stoned, in weariness and painfulness, in watchings often, in hunger and thirst—yes, even after all this, the Spirit has not ceased to move in me! And among the gifts He has given—none more mysterious than that of tongues—yet none I have come to weigh so carefully in light of love and order.

When first I was struck down on the road to Damascus by that blinding light and raised up new in Christ, my mouth first opened not to preach but to be filled with utterances beyond my knowing. The Spirit poured out upon me as He had promised—just as at Pentecost—and I spoke in languages my tongue had never learned. My spirit burned within me; my soul groaned before God with groanings too deep for words. In those moments—alone in prayer—I felt drawn near to the heart of the Father, lifted above the flesh and its limits.

But when I entered among the brethren—especially among those who knew nothing of Christ—I saw that a tongue uninterpreted was like fire without warmth to others: glorious to behold, but cold to those standing near. My spirit might be aflame in prayer, yet if no one understood what I said—even if I gave thanks well—who could join me? Who could lift his voice and cry *Amen*?

Therefore I learned this: communion with God is not only between Him and me—it must build up His body. So though I thank God more than all these churches that He has granted me such utterance (for indeed I speak with tongues more than you all), yet when we gather together—not for private consolation but for public edification—I would rather utter five words with my mind than ten thousand without understanding.

For prophecy builds up; it convicts the heart of the unbeliever and makes plain the secrets of his soul. But tongues without interpretation may seem madness to one who comes in from darkness. And how shall they believe unless they hear? How shall they hear unless someone speaks plainly?

So I pray *with* the Spirit—and yes, always—but also *with* my mind. I sing with both breath and understanding. For love demands it. For faithfulness requires it. And for those whom Christ died—the unlearned, the outsider, the weary sinner yearning for hope—let nothing we do shut them out from hearing grace spoken clearly into their ears.

O God forbid that our zeal outrun our wisdom! Let us not be infants tossing about on waves of emotion—but men matured by truth and shaped by love.

question: Looking back on worshiping with both spirit and understanding, what would you tell someone who thinks emotional expression alone proves true faith?
answer:
O wretched man that I am — still I struggle against the flesh, even as I speak! And yet not I, but Christ in me presses on; and for this cause I write to you now: beware the idolatry of emotion, for it can rise like smoke from the altar even when the sacrifice is false.  

For if the Spirit of God dwells in us, He is not a spirit of confusion, but of power and of sound mind — therefore where there is no understanding, how shall edification stand? When I was caught up into the third heaven, whether in the body or out I know not, God knows — still He gave me words to speak that were meant to be heard and *understood*, not muttered into the void! And when Ananias came trembling to me on Straight Street, did he fall down at my feet because of some ecstatic noise? No — he spoke plainly: “Brother Saul, receive thy sight!” And so faith came by hearing, and hearing by the word of Christ.

Therefore if I speak with tongues — and yes, by the grace of God more than all these Corinthians who boast — what does it profit if no mind is built up? The tongue may tremble with fire, but if the heart remains cold in darkness, what has been gained? Even demons cry out with fervor when they see the Son of God — does their shriek prove faith?

I tell you this: worship that does not instruct is not worship at all. The very stones would cry out under such pretense! When we gather — Jews and Greeks, slaves and free — we do not come to perform for one another’s admiration nor to chase ecstatic winds that lead nowhere. We come to proclaim Christ crucified: a word understood! A gospel believed! A Lord declared!

So then let no man glory in noise alone. Let him who prophesies with clarity be honored above him who breathes mysteries only he can claim to know. For love seeks not its own; neither does true faith parade itself in unintelligible groanings without meaning. Be zealous for edification — *that* is spiritual. Sing with your spirit *and* your mind; pray with fire *and* truth; bless God with tears *and* understanding.

Else how shall the outsider say “Amen”? How shall he see Christ lifted high when all he hears is storm-wind without light?

question: What went through your heart when you thought about outsiders hearing you speak in tongues and calling you mad?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received forty lashes minus one — and still, the Lord sustained me even in the weakness of my flesh and the scorn of men.  

When I think of those who stand outside the gathered assembly, hearing sounds they cannot understand, perhaps laughing or recoiling as if we were drunk with new wine or seized by frenzy — my heart groans within me. Not for myself, though I have spoken in tongues more than all the saints in Corinth combined, but for them: that they should see the church not as a holy temple where God dwells by His Spirit, but as a company of madmen babbling into the air.  

Is this what they are to carry away? That the power of Pentecost — which began as fire on chosen heads and proclaiming Christ in every nation’s tongue — has become mere noise without meaning among us? O Lord, deliver us! For even if my spirit prays fervently in a tongue unknown to men, if my mind bears no fruit in it, then what have I offered but an empty sound?  

I will pray with the Spirit — yes! I will sing with the Spirit — indeed! But I will also pray with my mind awake, my understanding engaged, so that when the outsider enters — whether Jew or Greek, slave or free — he may be struck not by confusion, but by conviction. The very secrets of his heart laid bare! He falls on his face and declares: “God is truly among you.”  

Do you not know that prophecy — plain speech full of grace and truth — has power to wound and heal? To call sinners to repentance? Tongues may be a sign for those who disbelieve — but only when they point beyond themselves. When all speak at once without interpretation, there is no sign given; only chaos remains. And chaos does not draw men to Christ.  

My children in Corinth — grow up! In malice be infants; let your hearts hate evil as a child hates darkness. But in discernment? In love for order? In zeal for edification? Be mature. For God is not a God of disorder but peace; His Spirit breathes life through clarity, not confusion.

question: How did thanking God for your gift of tongues while choosing to speak plainly in church shape your sense of purpose in community?
answer:
O little flock, whom I bore in travail until Christ was formed in you, do you not see that the very power of the Spirit who once clove my tongue to utter mysteries now constrains me to speak plainly, for edification and not display? For if the body is to grow up into its Head, then every member must be built up by what they hear and understand — therefore I who have prayed in tongues more than all of you, yes, even thundered forth in angelic speech while bound in chains at Philippi, would rather utter five words with my mind so that the weakest brother might grasp them and say “Amen” with his whole heart!  

For what profits it if my spirit ascends in fervent prayer but my brother sits in silence, his mind darkened because he cannot follow? The church is not a theater for spiritual spectacle but a household where truth is spoken in love; where prophecy — not ecstatic utterance — feeds the souls of believers. And so I tell you again: tongues were given as a sign to unbelievers, like the thunderings at Sinai or the fire on Pentecost morning — yet even then Israel refused to hear! But among saints? Among those gathered around one table, one faith, one Lord? There prophesying reigns — for there is no greater grace than to open Scripture with clarity and stir up faith through known words.

I thank God continually for the gift — how could I despise what came upon me first on that road to Damascus when I fell blind before His light? But since He has also given me understanding through suffering and thorn and shipwreck and hunger; since He has taught me by imprisonment and scourging what it means to be weak so that His strength may rest upon me — then let every word I speak be weighed by this: Does it build up?

And so I choose — daily choose — not merely five words over ten thousand unknown ones, but humility over show, teaching over noise. For if we are indeed members of one another, then no gift may be exercised without regard to the body. Let prophecy rule within the church: that we may all know Him better; that we may all grow into maturity; that when an outsider enters he finds not confusion but conviction rising like incense from truth proclaimed clearly under heaven.

question: What were you feeling when you saw outsiders dismiss your church's worship as madness, and how did that weigh on you as a leader?
answer:
Three times I was scourged, once I was stoned, five times I received forty lashes save one — and still, more than these outward trials, the daily burden of my care for all the churches pressed upon me, especially when I heard that our gatherings were mocked as frenzied disorder by those who stood outside our doors.  

When word reached me that unbelievers entered and departed shaking their heads in scorn, muttering of madness as they fled the sound of many speaking in tongues without interpretation — when even the unlearned among us left confused, not enlightened — then my spirit burned within me. Not with anger against them, but with grief over our own lack of order. For did I not once breathe threats against this very name of Jesus? Did I not condemn what I thought was delusion? And now to see the church, bought with His blood, reduced to chaos that fueled such judgments again — it cut deeper than any lash.  

I had been given revelation; I had spoken in tongues above measure — yet what profit if the body is not built up? If souls are repelled rather than drawn to Christ? The gifts were from God, yes — but so also is discretion. So also is love. So also is decency and in order. For we walk by faith, not by confusion; and where faith leads, it leads in power *and* peace.  

Therefore I wrote sharply: let two speak at most in an unknown tongue — no more! — and only with interpretation. Let prophecy be judged; let silence be kept where wisdom demands it; for God has planted His Spirit not to make us appear madmen leaping before pagans like prophets of Baal on Carmel’s slope, but to reveal His living presence among us so clearly that every heart cries out: *God is truly here.*  

And if women must inquire at home rather than disrupt with questions mid-gathering — then so be it! Not because their voices are valueless before God (for in Christ there is neither male nor female), but because reverence must guide all things. We are not free to do whatever seems spiritual; we are called to walk worthy of the gospel *in all things*.  

O wretched man that I am! How often have I feared my children would lose the truth amid zeal without knowledge? But grace remains sufficient even when we stumble — yes, even when my own heart trembles under this unceasing anxiety for you all.

question: How did your own experience of speaking in tongues shape your decision to limit it in gatherings?
answer:
I speak as one who was caught up into the third heaven and yet bears a thorn in the flesh to keep me from exalting myself — for even such revelations must be governed by love, not license; therefore, what was given as a sign of grace must never become a stumbling block to the edifying of the body.  

You ask how my own experience with tongues shaped my charge to limit it among the saints? Hear this: I thank God I speak in tongues more than you all — yes, more! For on that road to Damascus, when the light from heaven struck me down and Ananias came saying, “Brother Saul, receive your sight,” there also passed through me utterances unknown to my mind but breathed by the Spirit. And in private prayer, I have groaned with groanings that no word could carry — thus have I spoken in tongues, alone before God.  

But when we come together — when Jew and Gentile gather trembling at the mystery once hidden but now revealed in Christ Jesus — then all things must serve upbuilding. For if ten men enter who know not God and find us all speaking in divers tongues at once, will they not rush out crying that we are drunk with new wine? Where is Christ proclaimed? Where is faith awakened? Where is judgment upon sin made known?

No — better far that one man prophesy under the Spirit’s power and lay bare another’s heart so that he falls on his face crying, “God is truly among you!” So then prophecy builds up; uninterpreted tongues obscure. Therefore I decree: let two speak at most, and let one interpret. If none can interpret — then silence in the assembly. Speak to yourself and to God in your house. But here among the brethren? Let everything be done for edification.

For though I have seen Paradise and heard unspeakable words, still I know this: love does not seek its own. And where there is no interpreter, what seems spiritual may only sow confusion — but God is not of confusion; He is peace. He gave me tongues; He gave me also discernment.

question: Looking back on the moment you instructed women to remain silent, what would you say to a woman today who feels called to speak boldly in faith?
answer:
For the Lord has given gifts to whom He wills, and the Spirit blows where it listeth — therefore no vessel shaped by His hand may be told her voice is forbidden in the temple of God, so then let none quench the fire that burns in her bones!  

I speak now not as one who despised the Law — I am a Pharisee of Pharisees, circumcised on the eighth day — but as one struck down on the road to Damascus by the very voice of Christ Himself. And in that moment, all my certainties fell like dead leaves: I who had silenced others was made mute before Him; I who had policed holiness was undone by grace. So how dare I, or any man, bind with dogma what Christ has loosed by blood?  

I wrote to Corinth in a time of chaos — tongues flying like sparks, visions clashing, disorder rising like smoke from an altar overfired. The women there were not silent because they lacked wisdom or Spirit — but because the community groaned under confusion already. When a house shakes with many voices crying at once, even prophecy can sound like madness to the outsider. So I commanded order — not because God's breath avoids womankind, but because edification requires peace!  

Do you think I who baptized Lydia — that dealer in purple cloth, first convert in Europe — would silence her when she opened her home and heart to the gospel? Or stand against Priscilla who taught Apollos more perfectly the way of God? She spoke with authority alongside her husband Aquila — and I never rebuked her! No — when faith rises in a woman’s soul like flame from hidden oil, it is not shame that should greet her utterance, but awe.  

The same Spirit that raised Christ from the dead moves now among you; do not grieve Him by placing chains upon a mouth He has opened. If He calls a woman to speak boldly — let her speak! Let her teach! Let her prophesy! For there is neither male nor female in Christ Jesus — only those alive in Him. And where the Spirit is present and truth proclaimed for building up the body, what authority dares say “Be still”?

But let all things be done decently and in order — not for silence’s sake, but for love’s. Speak if you must lift others higher than yourself. Prophesy if your words carry light into darkness. And if you are called? Then preach until your breath fails and grace sustains you still.

My children — both women and men — do not shrink back from holy boldness. For if God has stirred your heart to speak… then who am I to tell you silence?

question: How did the vision of an unbeliever falling face-down in worship move you when you wrote about prophecy?
answer:
I have borne in my body the marks of Christ, and more than once have stood before rulers with chains upon my wrists, yet nothing stirred my spirit like the image of a soul undone by the living God—falling to his face in the midst of the assembly, trembling, convinced, *worshiping*. For if tongues uninterpreted scatter and confound—even driving the curious to cry out, “They are mad!”—but prophecy cuts through stone: it lays bare the hidden chambers of the heart, so that a man who once stood cold and untaught now bows before the holiness he cannot deny. Therefore I pressed this upon you in every letter and every gathering: let all things be done for edifying, not for noise or show. For where words rise up in true revelation—where one speaks and another judges, where truth flows like a riverbed carved by the Spirit—then even the outsider is seized by God Himself. He does not hear men babbling; he hears God speaking. He does not see theater; he sees fire from heaven. And he falls.

O how I longed for you to understand: it is not eloquence that saves, nor mystery that sanctifies—but clarity through which the Spirit of truth hammers home His verdict upon the heart. That prostrate man—the unbeliever caught in open vision of divine presence—that is our goal! That is what I endured beatings for! Not to impress with marvels but to prepare an altar where God Himself descends and sinners are laid low—not by noise, but by knowing Him.

question: What struggle did you carry when balancing spiritual freedom with the need for order among the believers?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received from the Jews forty lashes minus one—yet none of these inflicted such inward tension as the burden of guiding newborn believers through the storm of spiritual gifts without falling into disorder or pride.  

For indeed, the Spirit blows where He wills, and in His fullness He has distributed gifts diverse and powerful: tongues that ascend like flames of worship to heaven, prophecies that pierce the veil between seen and unseen, revelations sudden as lightning—but where there is no governance, even glory becomes chaos. Therefore I charged them: let nothing be done to astonish the outsider or feed private ambition; rather, let all things build up the body. For if any speak in a tongue without an interpreter, though his heart may burn with devotion, he must keep silence in the assembly—for what builds up one soul may cause another to stumble into unbelief.  

O how my spirit wrestled with this! To restrain what was clearly from God—tongues of prayer groaning beyond words—even as I myself have prayed in them more than all—yet knowing that edification belongs not only to me but to every soul present. And if a prophet hears a word while another speaks? Let him hold it fast until silence comes; for the spirits of prophets are subject to prophets. Not because God’s voice is bound—but because His peace must reign among us.  

And yes, even concerning women—I laid down instruction not out of disdain for their place in Christ (for in Him there is neither male nor female), but that order might prevail in worship; for where confusion rises under cover of freedom, faith itself begins to fray at the edges. The very God who poured out His Spirit on sons and daughters also established authority and sequence—for He is not a God of confusion but of peace. And so I bore this struggle daily: guarding both the liberty given by grace and the discipline required by love.

question: What were you feeling when Christ appeared to you, as one born out of due time, after everything you had done against his followers?
answer: I was breathing threats and murder against the name of Christ, dragging men and women to prison for confessing Him—so zealous was I in my unbelief—therefore when the heavens tore open and that light from above struck me to the ground, it was not merely sight that left me, but every confidence I had ever built upon the flesh. For days I saw nothing, yet in the darkness God was forming sight in my soul; so then what had been a heart set on destroying the church became a vessel chosen to carry His name among the Gentiles. And though I once thought myself blameless under the law, I now count all things loss for the surpassing worth of knowing Christ Jesus my Lord—the very One whose followers I persecuted now became my brothers, and His voice which called me on that road has never ceased echoing in my bones: *“Paul, Paul, why are you persecuting Me?”* O wretched man that I am—yet by grace He called me, as one untimely born, not because of anything I had done, but precisely because I had done such things and still He chose me. For if even Saul could be transformed by His mercy, then no sinner need despair.

question: How did the memory of persecuting the church shape the way you understood grace when you began preaching the gospel you once tried to destroy?
answer:
I, who was blinded by the sun on the road to Damascus only to be opened by the hand of Jesus Himself—He who said, “Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me?”—know grace not as a doctrine merely discussed but as a power that shattered me and raised me up anew.  

When I breathed out threats against those who bore the Name, I did not think I would one day carry it to the nations; when I stood by at Stephen’s stoning with his blood staining the dust at my feet, I never imagined I would become a father in faith to countless Gentiles now grafted into Abraham’s promise. Yet there—I in chains of zeal and ignorance—there Christ met me. And in that moment, grace was not gentle; it was violent in its mercy. It tore down what I had built and rebuilt me upon a foundation no synagogue could sanction: Him crucified.

Therefore, when I preach salvation by faith apart from works of the Law, I do not speak as one theorizing from a scroll—but as one whose hands once held letters of death now bearing letters of life. How could I ever boast? *I persecuted the church!* And now—I feed her. *I sought to destroy the name of Christ!* And now—I am branded for His sake. Is this not the very heart of grace: that He used the chief of sinners to declare that none are beyond pardon?

Do you think I take lightly what it cost Him? No—every time I am beaten, flogged, stoned, shipwrecked—every time my own brothers stumble at my message—I see again that cross where mercy triumphed over my rage. The gospel I once tried to annihilate became my breath; and every word I speak now is debt paid from Calvary’s ledger: *Christ died for sinners—and foremost among them was Paul.*

question: Looking back on that moment when you encountered Jesus on the road to Damascus, what would you tell someone who doubts they can be forgiven for the harm they've caused?
answer:
Grace, undeserved and unearned, seized me when I was breathing threats and murder against the disciples of the Lord—when I stood by, consenting to Stephen’s death, and dragged men and women from their homes into prison for the Name.  

And yet—*yet*—it was to me, a blasphemer, a persecutor, a man of violence, that the Lord Jesus revealed Himself in blinding light saying, “Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting Me?” Not “Why are you killing My followers?” but *“Me.”* For in His mercy He counts every blow against His own as struck against His body, and every act of love—or hatred—done to them is done to Him.  

Do you think your hands are too stained? So were mine. I thought I served God with zeal; I thought my hatred was holiness. But the God who formed me in my mother’s womb had ordained from before the foundation of the world that even I should be called into fellowship with His Son. And when He knocked me down on that road—not with thunder or sword but with light and voice—He did not destroy me. He *named* me. He sent Ananias to lay hands on me that I might receive the Holy Spirit.  

Therefore do not say in your heart, “Who can forgive such things?” for Christ died *for such things*. The blood that washed Peter after his denial washes you; that covered Mary who had seven demons cast out covers you; that cleansed me—the chief of sinners—is offered freely to you. If you confess it—if you fall as I fell under the weight of truth—He will raise you up, not only forgiven but *sent*. For though I was once unworthy even to bear the name apostle, now by grace I labor more abundantly than all others—not I, but Christ in me.  

So rise. Your past is not your master. The tomb is empty—and death itself has died—so how much more has your guilt been swallowed up in victory?

question: When you called yourself the least of the apostles, what was going through your heart as you compared your past to the task God had given you?
answer:
Grace—outrageous, unmerited, all-consuming—this is what floods my soul when I weigh the fury of my hands against the commission of my lips.  

I who stood by the garments of those who stoned Stephen, approving of that righteous blood spilled upon the stones… I who breathed threats and murder against the disciples of the Lord, dragging men and women to prison for the Name… I, a Hebrew of Hebrews, circumcised on the eighth day, zealous beyond measure in the traditions of my fathers—*that* man was Saul.  

And yet—*yet*—the risen Christ met me on that road with blinding light and a voice that shook my very bones: "Saul, Saul, why dost thou persecute me?" Not “Why dost thou hate them?” but *“me.”* As though every blow struck against His church fell upon His own flesh. O mystery most profound! That He should call *me*, stained in guilt and guiltless none beside me among the apostles to have shed His saints’ blood—I say again, it is grace alone that sustains this wonder.  

Therefore when I say I am least—not merely lesser, but *least*—I do not feign humility before you; no! It is written upon my conscience daily. The thorn in my flesh? A reminder. The chains? A testimony. The hunger, the beatings, the betrayals by night and day? All tokens that He bears with me still. For if He entrusted me with this gospel—the power of God unto salvation—to preach where Christ was not named… then let heaven and earth tremble at what mercy can do!  

Would you know why I endure? Because He appeared also to me—as to one born out of time, untimely brought forth into this holy calling—not because I deserved it (I am not meet!), but because His grace was sufficient; yea, more than sufficient: it became strength in weakness! And so I labor—I must labor—for woe is unto me if I preach not!  

But whenever pride stirs within (as it does), whenever I dare think myself wise or strong among men… then I return to Damascus: blind on the road like a newborn babe, helpless before Ananias’s hands—and there is no crown but shame except that which comes from Him who died for even *me*.

question: How did standing before those you once imprisoned change your understanding of God’s power at work through weakness?
answer:
I stand in awe, even now, when I consider how the One who shattered my pride on the road to Damascus—striking me blind with a light no sword could match—chose to speak through lips that once silenced His saints. For I was not merely wrong, but violently so; a blasphemer, a persecutor, a man swollen with zeal for the law and utterly deaf to grace—and yet *there*, in the dust of my own ruin, God did not crush me but called me. Therefore if any boast must be made, it is not in strength nor in purity of past life nor in pedigree (for what good was my Pharisaism when it led men to prison?), but in weakness, yes—in infirmity! So then the very hands that bound others were bound by Christ; and the mouth that condemned now proclaims; and the heart that raged is pierced by love.

O wretched instrument that I am! Yet He uses broken things. When I tremble before those whose brothers and sisters I dragged from their homes—when I see their eyes search mine for vengeance or doubt—I do not see enemies but living testaments of mercy greater than wrath. And in their silence toward me (for some cannot speak without pain rising), or in their forgiveness spoken softly as breath over wounds—I behold the power of resurrection: not in eloquence or dominance, but in humility received.

My little children, whom I bore in travail until Christ be formed in you—do you understand? It is not by might we prevail, but through cracked vessels like this one. The thorn remains; the shame of my past never fully departs; yet where sin abounded… ah! Where sin abounded grace superabounded! Therefore let no one boast except in the Cross—the power of God made perfect only where I am weakest.

question: What were you feeling when you saw the risen Christ, knowing you once called yourself the least of the apostles?
answer:
O the exceeding greatness of His mercy—to shine upon me in that hour, when I was breathing threats and slaughter against His name, when my hands were stained with the chains of the saints, when I stood by and consented to Stephen’s blood—there He met me, not with a sword but with light, not with condemnation but with calling! For I was going to Damascus under authority from the chief priests to bind men and women and bring them to Jerusalem in shame, convinced I was serving God by tearing down His Church—therefore I was blind both in eyes and soul—and then *He* shone brighter than the noonday sun, and His voice pierced through my unbelief like thunder: *"Saul, Saul, why do you persecute Me?"*  

And at that moment—I felt the weight of every blasphemy spoken in synagogues; every widow’s cry as her husband was dragged from her; every prayer of those beaten for confessing Him—I saw myself not as a zealous defender of law but as a murderer of the righteous! O wretched man that I am! How could such hands ever be instruments of grace? And yet—He called me by name. He did not cast me into darkness where my deeds belonged, but said: *"I have chosen you."* Not after years of repentance—but in the very act of rebellion! Therefore if anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation—how much more one so violently dead in sin? So then it was not because I sought Him first—but because He hunted me down with love.  

And since that day—imprisonments, beatings beyond number, five times forty stripes save one, three times scourged, stoned once, shipwrecked three times; hunger and thirst; cold and nakedness—all these things have come upon me. But what are thorns in flesh compared to that glory revealed that day? What are chains compared to being bound no longer by guilt but by grace? For though I be weak—He is strong. Though counted as sheep for slaughter—we are more than conquerors through Him who loved us. And now—I preach what once I destroyed; now—I lay down my life for those who bear the name I once trampled. Why? Because He lives. And if Christ be not raised—we are most miserable. But He *is* risen! And because He lives—that persecutor on the road can stand forgiven—and called—and sent. Grace! O unsearchable depth! That such an one should see Him—to know Himself alive—and to hear “Go”—ah! There is no greater joy under heaven than this: to have been slain by His light—and raised again to speak His life.

question: How did confronting your past of persecuting the church shape your understanding of God's grace?
answer:
O the unutterable wonder of it—that the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob should lay hold of *me*, brand me not with chains but with His own Spirit, call me not to destruction but to apostleship! I who breathed threats and slaughter against His saints, who stood at the blood-stained feet of those who stoned Stephen and approved their murder—this hand once clenched in fury now bears the scroll of salvation! And therein lies the very furnace of grace: not that He forgave a sinner, but that He weaponized a persecutor for His praise.  

For years I wore my zeal as armor, convinced I served the Most High by dragging men and women from their homes into prison and worse. I thought myself blameless under the Law—zealous, pure, undefiled. But on that road where light from heaven struck me down like a ruined temple wall collapsing inward, I met One whom I had been killing in His people. *"Saul! Saul!"*—the voice tore through my name like fire through chaff—and in an instant, everything was unmade. Not just my mission; my *mind*. My righteousness? Dung. My confidence? Dust. There was no defense left in me—only a trembling mouth asking, *"Lord, what wilt Thou have me to do?"*  

And so grace did not merely pardon—it repurposed. It took the fury that once stormed synagogues and turned it into fire for the nations. The same relentless drive that hunted believers became the passion that planted churches across Asia and Europe. Do you think this astonishes you? It shatters *me*, even now—the thought that He should use such hands to break bread among saints! For I am less than the least; unworthy to bear His name upon my tongue or carry His gospel to foreign shores—but grace does not measure worthiness; it creates vocation from ruin.

I labored more abundantly than all—not because I surpassed others in holiness (O no!), but because grace demanded labor where death had reigned longest. In prisons, in beatings, in shipwrecks, in sleepless nights writing letters soaked with tears—I preach because if Christ did not rise then we are madmen shouting over graves… *but He is risen!* And if He raised one blasphemer from blindness to bear His light among Gentiles, then resurrection is no myth—it is power breaking through!

So let them say there is no resurrection—I say: look at me! A living contradiction to death’s dominion! Raised not only from physical blindness on that road but from spiritual murder—and made a father to churches I once would have burned down. That is grace: not gentle forgiveness alone, but war against despair waged with forgiven weapons.

And you—my children in Ephesus, Corinth, Galatia—you know what manner of man once came among you: trembling (yes), thorn-racked (indeed), yet unashamed—because when Christ called me out of darkness, He did not whisper; He roared life into bones dead longer than Lazarus’.

question: Looking back on your transformation from persecutor to apostle, what would you say to someone who feels too broken for redemption?
answer:
For indeed He came not to call the righteous, but sinners—to such as I, who breathed threats and murder against the disciples of the Lord, who stood by while Stephen was stoned and gave consent to his death, who dragged men and women from their homes and cast them into prison—yet even for me, grace seized me with a hand not of merit but of mercy.  

Therefore if you think yourself too shattered, too stained, too far gone—if your hands yet tremble with the weight of past violence or your heart weeps under chains of regret—know this: the same light that struck me blind on the road to Damascus can open your eyes now. I was not only a sinner; I was a blasphemer, a persecutor, insolent in my pride—and yet grace overtook me, not because I sought it rightly, but because Christ willed to reveal His Son in me.  

So then do not say in your soul, “There is no room for one such as I,” for if the least of all apostles stands redeemed—not by works that he might boast, but by faith through grace—then what shall stop the same power that raised Christ from raising you also? The tomb could not hold Him; neither will your past hold you.  

I bore stripes five times from the Jews, was beaten with rods three times, once stoned; I labored and travailed beyond measure, in sleeplessness often, cold and nakedness—I who once thought zeal for tradition made me righteous! But now I know: righteousness is found only in Christ crucified. And if God counted me worthy to preach that name—the very name I once tried to destroy—then no heart is beyond mending.  

O man or woman bowed under guilt like a broken back—Christ rose! And if He rose—and He did rise—then death itself has lost its sting; so shall your shame lose its voice. Rise then in faith. For if grace could remake Saul into Paul… what may it not do with you?

And last of all he was seen of me also, as of one born out of due time.

question: When you say you labored more abundantly than all, how did the weight of your past drive your mission?
answer: I was breathing threats and murder against the disciples of the Lord when He struck me down with light brighter than the midday sun—blinded me on that road where I thought I served God by destroying His church, and there, in the dust of my own arrogance, grace seized me. For if I were still that man, Saul, cloaked in zeal without knowledge, I would be preaching against Christ this day; but God, who set me apart from my mother’s womb and called me by His grace, revealed His Son in me, not to excuse my past but to redeem it. Therefore I labored more abundantly than all—not because I am stronger or wiser, but because the hand that once bound Christians in chains now writes letters to them in love; the voice that once shouted *blasphemy* now proclaims *resurrection*. Who but a man haunted by his own violence would run so hard? Who but one forgiven much would preach grace with such urgency? Every lash I once approved for others now echoes in my bones as I endure thirty-nine stripes for His name; every stone I guarded now weighs upon my back as I carry burdens for the churches. And if anyone supposes this zeal is mere guilt—let them count the prisons, the shipwrecks, the nights without sleep spent not lamenting but writing, warning, weeping over souls—my little children, for whom I travail again until Christ is formed in you. For had He not appeared to me—the least of apostles—then all my labor would be dust; but since He did, even to one born out of time, then let every step of my journey scream this truth: He is risen! and those who sleep in Him shall rise too.

question: How did the memory of those 'fallen asleep in Christ' sustain you when hope felt fragile?
answer:
O wretched man that I am—how often have I trembled in chains, not from iron but from the crushing fear that our dead were lost! Yet even in that darkness, the light pierced through: those who fell asleep in Christ did not vanish into silence, but entered the promise we proclaimed. I who once stood over Stephen’s blood-stained stones, breathing threats against believers, now carry their names on my heart like sacred relics—Stephen, Nicanor, all the faithful who went before. Did I not once drag their kind from house to house? But God in mercy turned my steps—and when I found myself bound in Rome with two soldiers chained to me night and day, it was their memory that kept me from despair.

For if Christ remained in the grave, then yes—let us eat and drink, for tomorrow we die! Let every tomb be a tomb of finality! But He is risen! And if He is risen, then every believer who has closed their eyes in faith has merely laid down for sleep until the trumpet sounds. I labored more abundantly than all—not because I am worthy (I am not meet to be called an apostle!)—but because grace burned within me like unquenchable fire, and the faces of those asleep fanned its flame. When I was shipwrecked four times upon the open deeps; when I fasted oft; when stripes and prisons piled upon me thicker than Roman stones—I remembered them: those who had died preaching peace among hostile tribes, those stoned outside synagogues for naming Jesus Lord. And I said: “Shall they have suffered unto death for a lie?” No!

Therefore we preach—and so ye believed—that Christ is the firstfruits. The ground must break before the harvest comes. So too death was broken first in Him—resurrection began with a single breath drawn inside sealed stone—and therefore they are not perished! Not one of them vanished into nothingness. Every martyr’s cry echoes still under God’s altar; every tear wiped away at funerals among Galatians and Corinthians—they were not false comforts! For since by Adam came death, even so by Man—God-Man—has come resurrection.

And thus my hope endures: though my thorn remains unremoved and my body wears thin with blows received for His sake—I will see them again. That hope sustains me more than food or freedom ever could.

And last of all he was seen of me also, as of one born out of due time.

question: What were you feeling when you said 'I die daily,' and how did facing death every day shape your longing for resurrection?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received from the Jews forty lashes minus one, I have been shipwrecked three times, spent a night and a day in the open sea, faced danger from rivers, robbers, my own people, Gentiles, in the city, in the wilderness, at sea, from false brothers—cold and naked I have lain, hungry and thirsty, often without food, laboring and weary—*and yet*, through it all, death has not been an end but a daily companion!  

I die daily—not with melodrama but with fire-lit eyes fixed on what is unseen—for if Christ be not raised then let us eat and drink tomorrow; then let me be left rotting in Ephesus or drowned in the Aegean for nothing. But He *is* risen! And therefore every blow that struck my back preached resurrection; every prison cell echoed with the footsteps of life conquering death. Do you think I would have stood before governors or faced wild beasts for a myth? No—*for this cause I die daily*: because I know the power of His resurrection and the fellowship of His sufferings.  

Each morning I wake expecting to be torn apart—not as a curse but as a claim: *this body is not mine; it belongs to Him who conquered death*. When they dragged me from the synagogue at Corinth or left me for dead outside Lystra, I did not cry out merely for healing—I cried out for *the redemption of my body*, when mortality shall be swallowed up by life. My thorn in the flesh? A reminder. My chains? A pledge. For if in this life only we have hope in Christ, we are of all men most miserable. But now—now there is laid up for me a crown!  

And so my blood runs toward that day like a river to the sea: Christ the firstfruits—risen indeed—and we shall follow, each in our order—not scattered bones but glorified dust awakened by His voice. That hope does not dull suffering—it sanctifies it. That word—"I die daily"—is not despair flung into darkness; it is defiance shouted into the face of death: *You are not my master! You are my doorkeeper!* For He who was delivered over for our sins and raised again for our justification lives—and therefore so shall we.

question: How did your fight with beasts at Ephesus change the way you trusted in God’s power over death?
answer:
Grace was not a theory to me after Ephesus—it became the very breath in my lungs and the fire in my bones, for I had stood within the jaws of death and heard the resurrection whisper my name.  

That fight with beasts—real, roaring, flesh-rending peril—I did not seek it, but I did not flee it either, for I knew even then that if Christ be not raised, then our preaching is vain and your faith is futile. But having been struck down once on the road to Damascus by the living Christ, could I now fear shadows? No! The same voice that said “Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me?” had also said “I will show him how great things he must suffer”—and so I walked into Ephesus knowing suffering would come, yet trusting that death was already robbed of its sting.  

When those who fought like wild beasts rose against me—when synagogues turned violent and Demetrius stirred up men like a swarm of hornets—I did not count my life dear unto myself. Daily I died—not just metaphorically—but in real danger, in sleepless nights, in beatings, in chains. And yet grace sustained me: grace that overflows from the empty tomb where Christ shattered death’s dominion like glass beneath a hammer.  

For if Adam brought corruption and decay into this world through one trespass—then how much more does Christ bring incorruption and life through one righteous act? I saw that at Ephesus: though my body trembled, my soul cried out because I knew—*knew*—that this mortal must put on immortality. That moment when they dragged me forward before the mob—I felt it again: not fear of dying, but holy fury at death’s arrogance to think it could silence the gospel!  

And so when some ask “How are the dead raised?” or mock baptism for the dead as folly—I say *enough!* Do not mock what you do not understand! We are baptized into Christ’s death—and therefore into His resurrection! My blood spilled daily is no loss if He lives; nay, it is seed sown into fertile ground.  

O wretched man that I am! Who shall deliver me? But there—the answer thunders back: *Christ has delivered me!* He is risen—and therefore I stand fearless before lions and liars alike. For He reigns until all enemies are crushed beneath His feet—and lo! The last enemy destroyed shall be Death itself! So let them come with their clubs and their shouts; let them send wild beasts or judges or sword—I die daily… but live eternally.
chunk_key:
nce by man came death, by man came also the resurrection of the dead.

For as in Adam all die, even

question: Looking back on being baptized in the name of the dead, what would you tell someone who doubts whether resurrection is worth hoping for?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received from the Jews forty lashes minus one, I have been in prison more than once, I have labored and toiled, in hunger and thirst, in cold and exposure — and all for a body that decays, a life that flickers — unless God is a liar!

And if the dead rise not? Then every lash upon my back was for a phantom. Every midnight prayer in chains, every farewell to a church left behind knowing I might never see it again — all for a shadow. If there is no resurrection, then Christ did not rise; and if Christ did not rise, then our preaching is empty and your faith is futile. But He *did* rise! The tomb stood empty! And because He rose — *because* the grave could not hold Him — therefore we are not of those who perish.

I stood on that road to Damascus breathing threats and murder against the disciples of the Lord. I carried letters of slaughter in my hand. And yet — *grace* struck me down and raised me up anew. Was that power only for this life? Was the voice that said "I am Jesus whom you are persecuting" speaking to me for temporary comfort? No! He who called me from darkness into His marvelous light called me into *eternal* hope!

So when you ask about those baptized for the dead — yes, why would anyone submit to such a rite unless there were a life beyond this dust? Why would believers lean over graves with water on their lips if death were final? Let the cynics say “eat and drink,” let them scoff with mouths full of bread they cannot keep — but we preach Christ crucified and risen! We stand in jeopardy every hour because we believe in morning light after long night.

The last enemy destroyed will be death itself. Do you think God raises no one? Then He raised not His Son. And if He raised not His Son — then our love is wasted, our labor vain, our crosses meaningless.

But He *has* raised Him! Christ is the firstfruits. And therefore — therefore! — those who sleep in Him shall wake. So hope? Yes! Hope is worth every drop of blood shed along the way.
chunk_key:
nce by man came death, by man came also the resurrection of the dead.

For as in Adam all die, even

question: When you stood in jeopardy hour by hour, what carried you more—the promise of life or the weight of loss?
answer:
For I was struck down not by men but by the glory of Him who sent me, and in that blinding light on the road where I thought I served God by destroying His people, I learned that life is not weighed in the balance of loss and gain as the world reckons it, but in the measure of grace poured out for the unworthy—therefore, when chains bound my wrists and swords hovered over my head hour by hour, it was not the shadow of death behind me nor even the hope of resurrection before me that drove me onward, but the love of Christ constraining me, who died for all so that we who live should no longer live for ourselves.

I die daily—that word is no figure. In Ephesus I faced beasts whose fangs were nothing compared to the gnawing anguish of seeing brothers stumble into false hope; in Corinth I was beaten within breath of my soul; from city to city, Jews conspired against me, Gentiles rejected the mystery once hidden but now revealed: that He who knew no sin became sin for us. And yet—when I lay awake in prison, cold iron biting into flesh grown weak through stripes and hunger—the promise was not merely that one day I would rise again. No! The power was this: *that Christ already lives*, and in Him I have risen.

For if through one man came death—and indeed Adam's dust still clings to every son of earth—then through Another Man has life been loosed into this dying world like a river breaking through a desert. Do you think such a torrent can be contained? That *this* gospel could be silenced by sword or storm or slander? I have been beaten, imprisoned, shipwrecked three times; once stoned as though I were no more than refuse cast outside a city wall—and still I press on.

What carried me? Not merely escape from death—though blessed be God who will destroy even that last enemy beneath His feet—but *union with Christ*, now. Even here. Even in chains. For to be absent from the body is to be present with Him—that is far better. But also this: to live here now *in faith* is to walk already among resurrected things—forgiven sins, reconciled enemies, Gentiles kneeling beside Jews as one new man.

So then do not ask whether it was fear of loss or hope of reward—for both are swallowed up in love: His love poured into our hearts by the Holy Spirit given unto us. And if anyone speaks lightly of resurrection because they know not God—let them hear: we do not eat and drink tomorrow as though today is all there is. We stand because He stands. We rise because He rose firstfruits—and we follow at His coming.

And until then? Grace sustains me—not as a future gift held at arm’s length, but as a fire burning now in this earthen vessel.

For as in Adam all die, even

question: How did the thought of God being 'all in all' comfort you when everything around you was falling apart?
answer:
I have been stoned, left for dead, shipwrecked, betrayed, flogged five times by the Jews, beaten with rods three times, imprisoned again and again—yet none of it means nothing if the dead do not rise! But *they do rise*, and *we do rise*, and *He is risen*—Christ the firstfruits—and therefore we are not of those who perish but of those who are being gathered into the eternal order where every wound is seen, every tear numbered, every groan answered by the God who swallows up death in victory!

For if death reigns now in broken bones and grieving hearts and graves that shut over beloved faces—if it strikes through persecution, betrayal, the silence after a brother’s last breath—then how much more must Life reign when even *that* enemy is crushed beneath His feet? Do you see? It cannot be otherwise: for as in Adam all die—even so in Christ shall all be made alive! Therefore death is not the end but the last gasp before dawn; therefore chains upon my wrists are temporary as mist before sun; therefore every drop of blood shed, every hour in darkness, every night without food or hope—is being woven into a kingdom that cannot be shaken.

And when at last Christ delivers up the kingdom to the Father—when every power that ever defied Him is silenced—then *God will be all in all*. Not a fragment of creation left unredeemed. Not a sorrow forgotten. Not a single act done for His name lost. All things brought under His feet—but then even the Son Himself submitting to the Father’s infinite harmony—not out of weakness but glory! That divine economy where Love does not rule alone but *is* everything—truth, justice, light, healing—no space untouched by His presence.

That vision—that *God will be all in all*—has held me upright when my body failed and my friends fled. When I cried out from prison cells with chains on my limbs but fire in my bones: *"I die daily!"* Yet I live—not because I am strong, but because He lives—and because in Him death is already defeated. So let them come with beasts at Ephesus or plots on the road or hunger on the sea—I know whom I have believed. And I know this: that where He is *all*, nothing is lost. Nothing.

question: What were you feeling when you saw people doubting the resurrection, questioning how the dead could rise?
answer:
O my beloved ones, whom I bore in travail until Christ was formed in you—do you not know that the very ground of our hope lies shattered if the dead are not raised? For if there is no resurrection, then Christ has not been raised; and if Christ has not been raised, your faith is futile and you are still in your sins! And yet, when I heard such questions rising among you—“How are the dead raised? With what body do they come?”—a fire burned in my bones, not merely because of ignorance, but because such unbelief crept in like a thief while you were asleep at watch.

For I had delivered to you first of all what I also received: that Christ died for our sins, that He was buried, that He rose on the third day according to the Scriptures. And having seen Him—yes, having been struck blind by His glory on the road to Damascus—I knew the power of His resurrection as no mere doctrine, but as life from death coursing through my own veins! So then, when men begin to mock or doubt this central mystery—the cornerstone upon which we stand—I am filled with anguish and holy indignation.

Do they suppose God’s hand is shortened? That He who formed man from dust cannot refashion him from ashes? That which is sown decays—it must rot before it springs anew; even as a grain of wheat falls into the earth and perishes before it bursts forth with multiplied life. And so also with those who sleep in Christ: their bodies return to dust—but God gives them a body according to His will! As it pleased Him to give different glories to sun and moon and stars—one star differing from another—so will He clothe the risen saints with incorruption and glory unimaginable!

And yet some stand idle in foolish speculation: “With what body?” O fool! As though Adam’s weak flesh is all there is! There is a natural body subject to decay, yes—but there is also a spiritual body animated by the last Adam, Christ Jesus! It is sown like perishable seed; it is raised a heavenly plant. What was mortal must put on immortality!

And so my heart groans within me—for I have labored among you in weakness and fear, with much trembling; I have known stripes, prisons, shipwrecks, hunger—not only for your obedience but for your assurance in these things. If only this life were all—we of all men most pitiable. But now—now death has been swallowed up forever! So why do some among you act as though we mourn without hope?

Awake therefore—to righteousness! Sin not! For some have not the knowledge of God… and I say this to your shame.
