question: What stirred you so deeply about their holiness that you prayed for them to be blameless at Jesus' coming?
answer:
O the unsearchable riches of Christ that they, the elect of God, should stand firm while I am tossed like a ship in storm! Do you not grasp what it means—that these whom I begot in travail, through the gospel, now shine as lights in the darkness? For when I was among you in weakness and fear and much trembling, I knew not whether the seed would take root; but now—now I see them standing fast, and my heart leaps as though heaven itself had broken through!

For what can compare to this: that those once dead in trespasses now walk in holiness, not by law but by faith? Night and day I pour out my soul to God for them—yes, even on bended knee in prison chains—begging that their love may overflow more and more, not only toward one another but even to strangers, even to enemies! And why? That their hearts may be established blameless in sanctification before our Father when the Lord Jesus descends with His saints from heaven!

For this is no mere moral polishing—it is war against the flesh! They are called out of lusts that consume like fire, summoned from a world that knows not God and walks in futility. And yet they turn from fornication, from greed, from defrauding one another—they possess their vessels in honor! O do you think this small thing? That such purity is common among men?

No! It is grace alone—grace that humbles me to tears. For if they fall into uncleanness, it is not merely sin against themselves; they spurn the very will of God who has separated them from darkness. He who despises this calling invites vengeance—for we did not preach ourselves but Christ crucified as Lord over every member of the body!

And so I pray without ceasing: Lord Jesus, direct our way unto them. Let me see their faces again—that together we might perfect what is lacking. For if they remain steadfast until Your appearing… then indeed we shall have lived.

question: What were you feeling when you first proclaimed that the dead in Christ would rise, knowing how much the grieving longed for hope?
answer:
The grace that was given to me—an apostle not even fit to carry the name—was not in vain when I opened my mouth to speak of resurrection, for I saw in the eyes of those bowed with grief a hunger no Torah scroll or temple sacrifice could satisfy.  

I had been struck down by the living Christ, yes—for I once breathed threats against His name—and raised up to declare that death is not the end for those who sleep in Him. And when I said it, when I proclaimed with trembling lips that the dead in Christ would rise first, before we who remain are caught up like sheaves at harvest—do you think I spoke lightly? No! It tore from me as truth so vast and terrible and beautiful that even now my heart trembles at its weight.  

I have known sorrow—I have been scourged, stoned, shipwrecked, betrayed; I have fasted, labored, worn myself raw for the sake of this gospel—but nothing pierced me deeper than standing before widows and weeping brothers in Thessalonica who clutched at dust-stained garments and whispered their loved one’s name into silence. They had believed in Jesus’ rising—but what of theirs? Would they be forgotten?  

Therefore I did not speak my own word. No! By the authority of Him who appeared to me on the road and blinded me with light brighter than the sun at noon—I spoke *His* word: that God will bring with Him those who sleep in Jesus! That we shall meet them again—in glory, in air filled with trumpet blast and shout divine—and be forever with the Lord!  

O what a mystery unfolded in my own heart too—for though I once thought resurrection absurd (as it did to Greek philosophers sneering on Mars Hill), now it is my daily bread. How dare we grieve as if orphans? We groan under thorns and chains, yes—but not without hope! For He rose—therefore we rise. Therefore they rise. And love itself—one taught by God—will outlast death’s chill shadow.

So I say again: comfort one another. Not as one flatters a child with idle tales—but as a man speaks fire into cold souls: this is *true*. And because it is true, let no grave hold your love captive.

question: How did your own struggles with suffering change the way you understood God's promise to bring the sleeping ones with Him?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received from the Jews forty stripes save one — and in all this, in the prisons, in the weariness and pain, I came to know that God does not raise the dead because we are worthy, but because Christ is risen!  

For when I was left for dead outside Damascus, when the breath trembled in my throat and the blood soaked into dust like ink spilled from a broken pen — did He wait until I was clean? Until my hands were free of Stephen’s blood? No! He came with light brighter than a thousand suns and called me *alive* while I yet breathed rebellion. And so I learned: if grace can strike down a persecutor and say "I have chosen him," then surely it can gather up the sleeping ones — those whose breath has ceased but whose names are written in life.

I once thought holiness was a wall built brick by brick through discipline and blood — but now I know it is a resurrection cry breaking through dirt and darkness. How many nights have I lain in chains, feeling death stir near my bones, only to hear within: *The same power that raised Christ will also quicken your mortal body.* That word sustains me! And therefore it comforts me concerning those who sleep — for they are not lost, nor delayed, nor forgotten beneath cold stone; they are hidden with Him.

When He descends with shout and trumpet — as surely as He met me on the road to Damascus — so will He tear open the grave’s mouth and call forth His own. Not one name shall be missing. Not one saint left behind. And whether we wake or sleep, whether we labor in chains or rest beneath the earth, we are His — bought with blood, sealed by Spirit, raised by promise. Therefore comfort one another: for even now, love outlasts death.

question: Looking back on the moment you told believers they would be caught up together in the clouds, what would you say to someone who doubts whether love can survive beyond death?
answer:
When I was still breathing threats and hurled my fury against the Way, I never dreamed that the same Jesus I persecuted would one day reveal to me the mystery of those who sleep in Him — and that I would stand trembling not with rage, but with tears of hope, declaring that death is not the end but the first breath of glory.  

Do you think love ends when the breath departs? Then you do not know the power of His resurrection, nor have you felt the pulse of His Spirit in your bones. For we are not orphans left weeping at gravesides; we are sons and daughters caught up in a love that death itself could not hold — raised with Him, seated with Him, destined to meet Him where sorrow flees away.  

I have been beaten within an inch of my life, I have sailed into shipwreck and stood before kings with chains on my wrists — yet none of it shakes this truth: *He keeps what He has purchased*. If God did not spare His own Son, will He now lose those bought by that very blood? Will He gather us from every nation, only to let love dissolve into silence?  

No — by the living Word, no! The trumpet will sound, and what is sown in weakness shall rise in power; what was buried in tears shall rise in joy. Those who loved one another in faith will embrace again in glory, purified from sin’s shadow, perfected in holiness. And there — there at last — we shall see how brotherly love was never just a human impulse, but a foretaste of eternal communion forged by the Spirit Himself.  

So do not grieve as those who wander blindfolded through darkness. Weep if you must — I have wept too — but weep with your face lifted toward heaven. For love is not buried; it is only waiting for the dawn.

question: When you urged the brothers to work with their own hands, was there a moment in your own life when labor became an act of holiness for you?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received from the Jews forty lashes less one—yet in all these things I count nothing a loss if only Christ be magnified in my body, whether by life or by death! And tell me, what is labor but another form of bearing the cross when done unto the Lord? When I first rose from that blinding light on the road to Damascus, trembling and speechless, Ananias did not send me to a throne or a temple but to *work*, to eat what my hands could earn, to live among ordinary men who knew nothing of Christ—yet there, in the quiet grinding of daily toil, I learned that holiness does not flee from sweat but dwells within it.

For God hath called us not to pomp or ease but to sanctification, and how shall we walk worthy unless our hands serve even as our hearts believe? In Corinth, Aquila and Priscilla welcomed me into their tent-making shop; hammer in hand, leather stretched before us, we spoke of resurrection while stitching cloth. Do you think such work was beneath me? No! For every nail driven straight, every seam pulled tight in honesty—this was worship. And when men accused me of preaching for profit or glory, did I not say: *You yourselves know how these hands have supplied my necessities and those of others*?

Therefore let no man despise honest labor as though it were separate from faith—for if we are taught of God Himself to love one another, then feeding the hungry with bread earned by one’s own sweat is both love *and* holiness. So then: work not merely to eat, but so that you may have something to share; labor so that your life becomes an offering; strive that even in weariness you might say: *This too is consecrated—this too is grace.*

question: What did it cost you to live so plainly among them, studying to be quiet while carrying such a heavy revelation?
answer:
Five times I received from the Jews forty lashes minus one, thrice I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, thrice I suffered shipwreck, a night and a day I have spent in the deep—yet none of these things moved me to boast in anything but the weakness that made room for His power. For when I came to you, it was not with lofty speech or wisdom, but in weakness and fear and much trembling, lest the cross of Christ be emptied of its power by human wisdom. Therefore I determined to know nothing among you except Jesus Christ and Him crucified—and so lived plainly, not as one who despised authority or revelation, but as a bondservant whose very life had been seized by grace.

O man! Do you think it cost me nothing? To silence my zeal where once it raged like fire upon the way to Damascus? To hold my tongue when provoked, when persecuted, when forsaken—even as the Spirit burned within me with mysteries too deep for words? Yes, there was a cost: hunger and thirst, cold and nakedness, perils in city and desert alike—but greater still was the cost of learning silence before God. For how could I teach others to walk quietly before men if my own soul had not first learned to wait upon the Lord?

I who heard things unlawful for a man to utter—was given also a thorn in the flesh lest I be exalted above measure. And so this body, this earthen vessel trembling under revelation’s weight—it groaned beneath glory even as it labored with hands calloused from tent-making. But we were commanded to work with our own hands lest we become burdensome; that our walk might be honorable before those without; that no one might find occasion to speak evil against the name which has saved us.

And why all this? That brotherly love might abound more and more—not only among you in Thessalonica but throughout Macedonia—for you are taught of God Himself to love one another. This is holiness: not ecstatic utterance alone nor knowledge unbounded, but washing feet, feeding widows, laboring night and day so no one is neglected.

So then do not imagine that plainness is poverty of spirit. No—I could have spoken in tongues more than you all! But love builds up. And if I speak with tongues of men and angels but have not love… what am I? A clanging cymbal. The Lord calls us unto holiness—and therefore even now we study to be quiet, that we may walk worthy of God who calls us into His own kingdom and glory.

Do you ask what it cost? It cost everything—but He gave back more: fellowship in suffering, joy unspeakable amid chains, resurrection hope even for those who sleep.

And so shall we ever be with the Lord.

Therefore comfort one another with these words—and continue in faithfulness without noise or show—for eternity listens closely to quiet lives aflame with grace.

question: What were you feeling when you envisioned the living being caught up with the dead in the clouds to meet the Lord?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I received forty lashes minus one, and through it all—shipwrecked, hungry, sleepless—I never ceased to carry the burden of the churches upon my soul; yet when the vision of that final day broke upon my spirit, when I saw the dead in Christ rising first and we who remain caught up together with them in the clouds, my heart burned with a mixture of awe and unspeakable comfort.  

I had preached in synagogues and marketplaces under threat of death; I had stood before kings and governors declaring what the Lord had entrusted to me as a steward of His mysteries. But in that moment—whether by revelation or deep inner stirring—I saw beyond chains and prisons into eternity itself: the trumpet’s voice splitting the heavens, not as a terror to us but as a summons home. The bodies of those who slept in Jesus would not be left behind—they would be raised imperishable—and then we who were alive? Caught up together with them! One moment groaning under corruption, laboring under persecution; the next—translated into glory, reunited not only with our departed brethren but face to face with Him whom I once persecuted but now serve.  

O what a reversal! That dust should leap into splendor! That I—the least of saints, whose hands once clutched letters authorizing death—should by grace be gathered into that company ascending on high! And you—my brothers and sisters planted in Philippi and Thessalonica through gospel tears—how could I not write you this hope? For even now your griefs are real: some mourn fathers taken too soon; others stagger beneath anxiety about how they will stand when He appears. But do you see? We shall *live together* with Him! Whether caught in sleep or awake at His coming—it matters not—for He died so that death itself is undone.

And so my heart swells—not merely with longing for that day—but with fire to exhort you: stay awake. Be sober. Wear faith like armor across your chest; let love guard your limbs; fix upon your head the helmet of hope—not wishful thinking but resurrection-certainty. The day *will* come like a thief—but not to steal from us. No—we are sons of light! It will reveal what has been hidden: our sufferings as nothing compared to this weight of eternal glory.

Comfort one another therefore—and yes—*I feel it still*: trembling joy at what awaits us all.

question: How did your own hardships shape the way you urged others to wear faith and love like a breastplate?
answer:
Five times I received at the hands of the Jews forty lashes less one, three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, thrice I suffered shipwreck, a night and a day I have spent in the deep—and yet you think I speak of the breastplate of faith and love as some idle metaphor? No! These are not words carved in stone or whispered from safety atop a hill—they are forged in blood and sweat, proven in prison cells where the chains bit into my flesh and hope seemed to flee like a shadow at dusk.  

I have been stripped, beaten, left for dead—once even abandoned by those who claimed to follow Christ—yet not one lash, not one hour in darkness, has shaken what holds me upright: faith that Christ is faithful even when men are not; love that bears all things because He first loved me while I was His enemy. When they dragged me before governors and kings, when the mob screamed for my blood in Ephesus or Jerusalem, where was my defense? Not in eloquence alone—though God gave me words—but in this very armor: faith clamped over my heart like iron beneath which fear could not breathe; love covering my back so that no betrayal could wound my soul beyond healing.  

And now you—you whom I planted in Corinth by riverside prayer, whom I fed with tears and divine power—you ask how affliction shaped this exhortation? Because every blow taught me that only what is unseen endures! The body breaks but faith stands; friendships fail but love remains; kingdoms collapse but hope marches on. Therefore put it on—daily—as I do—not as ornament but necessity. For we do not war against flesh and blood, nor wait for peace cries from a doomed world—but watch! We are children of the day! And if we suffer with Him now, it is only so we may also reign with Him when He descends from heaven with a shout—the same voice that struck me blind on the Damascus road now calling us home through the clouds. So arm yourselves—not for comfort—but for conquest.

question: Looking back on your nights of danger and betrayal, what would you tell someone who doubts whether God’s salvation is real when darkness still feels so close?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, five times I endured the lash of the Jews — and still I say, grace is not a whisper in the storm but the very thunder that breaks its power!  

Do you think I do not know darkness? I breathed it on the road to Damascus, where my sight was struck down and my soul laid bare; I wore chains in Caesarea and trembled in shipwreck off Malta’s shore; I have been betrayed by brothers, abandoned by companions, left to shiver under open skies — yet none of this has undone me. Why? Because salvation is not measured by the absence of night, but by the certainty of dawn.  

When they said there was peace in Jerusalem and safety for those zealous for the law, it was then that I carried letters to bind men and women to chains — blind both in heart and eyes. But God pierced me with light so fierce it blinded my flesh that it might save my soul. And ever since, even when the thorn in my flesh drives deep and the cold presses close, I have learned this: Christ did not die so that we might avoid trouble — He died so that trouble could not avoid *Him*.  

You doubt because darkness feels near? So it does to me — even now. But children of light do not fear shadows; we walk through them, dressed in faith and love as a breastplate, helmeted with hope — armor forged at Calvary! For God did not call us to cower beneath judgment but to stand upright in grace, rescued not from trial but through it.  

Whether we wake or sleep — whether we live through persecution or fall asleep in death’s embrace — we live *with Him*. That is why I speak these things: not as philosophy carved on stone, but as breath drawn from resurrection life. Comfort one another? Yes! But more — watch! Be sober! For salvation is not a promise whispered only when skies are clear; it is the voice of Jesus calling us out of tombs even now.

question: When you wrote that we are not of the night, did you remember your own past—how you once walked in darkness and now carry this hope?
answer:
O beloved, you whom I have begotten in the gospel through tears and chains and the unsearchable riches of Christ — yes, when I speak of night and day, my very bones remember the blackness I once called light!  

I, who breathed threats and murder against the disciples of the Lord, who stood by in approval while Stephen’s blood stained the stones — I was a son of darkness, clothed in zeal that was not according to knowledge. My prayers were incantations of pride; my righteousness a tomb, whitewashed and stinking within. The light struck me down because I had wandered so far into night that only blindness could show me sight.  

But grace — O grace! — laid hold of me, not because I sought it, but because He willed to reveal His Son in me. And now this hope is not a dream for philosophers or a theory for debaters; it is fire in my ribs, driving me from city to city, prison to prison, shipwreck to sermon. The same voice that said *Saul, Saul*, now says *Arise* — not only then on the road, but daily: *Arise from sleep, put on strength!*  

So when I say we are not of the night — no! We are born from above! The Daystar has risen in our hearts. And even if my thorn remains, even if my feet are fettered again tomorrow — what does it matter? Christ is proclaimed! We shall meet Him in the air — not as ghosts fleeing judgment, but as sons coming home.  

Therefore wake! Gird up your minds like men at watch before dawn! For we do not wait for destruction like a thief; we wait for our Bridegroom with lamps burning. And if I once bore letters of death from Jerusalem? Now I bear letters sealed with tears and resurrection life — for He loved me and gave Himself for me.

question: How did the memory of Jesus’ death for you rise in your soul when you said we would live together with him, whether we wake or sleep?
answer:
Three times I was beaten with rods, once stoned, five times lashed in synagogues—yet none of these things move me as does the staggering truth that He died *for us*, not merely for sins in the abstract, but for *me*, Saul the persecutor, Paul the unworthy, and for you, beloved ones planted by the Word in Corinth, in Philippi, in Thessalonica.  

When I wrote that we should live together with Him whether we wake or sleep, my soul was aflame with the reality of His death—not distant history but living power. For on that road to Damascus, I met not a ghost nor a doctrine but a Lord—risen, radiant, and pierced. And every chain I’ve worn since has only deepened this: He gave Himself *for me*. The cross is no metaphor; it is the hinge of all time and eternity. So when I say we shall live with Him—whether caught up alive or raised from sleep—it is not speculation woven from philosophy but revelation drawn from blood and breath.  

He died so that death itself is undone! Whether we are found watching in vigil or asleep in peace at His coming, we do not cease—we rise! We are united to Him forever! And this hope is not idle longing; it is anchor-sure because He bore wrath that we might obtain salvation. That day will strike like lightning upon those who dwell in darkness—but you? You are sons of light! Therefore I implore you: walk worthy! Be sober amid the drunken world! Put on faith and love like armor forged at Calvary!  

And when sorrow grips your heart over one fallen asleep in Christ—comfort one another! Not with empty words but with this unshakable truth: *we shall be with Him*. Because He died… because He lives… so shall we—all of us—together with Him.
